Monitor access key usage with AWS CloudTrail and implement alerts for multiple active keys to enforce this control via IaC and regular audits.

cis_aws_1.14.description: Access keys consist of an access key ID and secret access key, which are used to sign programmatic requests that you make to AWS. AWS users need their own access keys to make programmatic calls to AWS from the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI), Tools for Windows PowerShell, the AWS SDKs, or direct HTTP calls using the APIs for individual AWS services. It is recommended that all access keys be rotated regularly.
cis_aws_1.14.remediation: Rotate access keys every 90 days using AWS IAM key rotation features or automation scripts. Implement policies to enforce rotation and use AWS Secrets Manager for secure storage. Monitor key age with AWS Config and set up notifications for keys nearing expiration to ensure timely updates.

cis_aws_1.15.description: IAM users are granted access to services, functions, and data through IAM policies. There are four ways to define policies for a user: 1) Edit the user policy directly, also known as an inline or user policy; 2) attach a policy directly to a user; 3) add the user to an IAM group that has an attached policy; 4) add the user to an IAM group that has an inline policy. Only the third implementation is recommended.
cis_aws_1.15.remediation: Assign IAM users to groups with attached policies instead of using inline or direct user policies. Use AWS Organizations or IaC tools like Terraform to manage group memberships centrally. Regularly audit IAM configurations to ensure compliance and remove any direct policy attachments for better access control.

cis_aws_1.16.description: IAM policies are the means by which privileges are granted to users, groups, or roles. It is recommended and considered standard security advice to grant least privilege—that is, granting only the permissions required to perform a task. Determine what users need to do, and then craft policies for them that allow the users to perform only those tasks, instead of granting full administrative privileges.
cis_aws_1.16.remediation: Review and remove IAM policies with full "*:*" administrative privileges. Implement least privilege principles by creating custom policies with specific actions and resources. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to identify over-permissive policies and automate policy management with IaC to enforce granular permissions.

cis_aws_1.17.description: AWS provides a support center that can be used for incident notification and response, as well as technical support and customer services. Create an IAM Role, with the appropriate policy assigned, to allow authorized users to manage incidents with AWS Support.
cis_aws_1.17.remediation: Create an IAM role with the AWSSupportAccess policy attached for managing AWS Support incidents. Assign this role to specific users or groups using RBAC. Document the process for role assumption and regularly review access to ensure only authorized personnel can interact with AWS Support for security incidents.

cis_aws_1.18.description: AWS access from within AWS instances can be done by either encoding AWS keys into AWS API calls or by assigning the instance to a role which has an appropriate permissions policy for the required access. "AWS Access" means accessing the APIs of AWS in order to access AWS resources or manage AWS account resources.
cis_aws_1.18.remediation: Use IAM instance roles instead of hardcoding access keys in EC2 instances. Attach IAM roles with least privilege policies to instances during launch. Enforce this with AWS Config rules and use IaC tools like CloudFormation to define roles, ensuring secure and manageable access from instances.

cis_aws_1.19.description: To enable HTTPS connections to your website or application in AWS, you need an SSL/TLS server certificate. You can use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) or IAM to store and deploy server certificates. Use IAM as a certificate manager only when you must support HTTPS connections in a region that is not supported by ACM. IAM securely encrypts your private keys and stores the encrypted version in IAM SSL certificate storage. IAM supports deploying server certificates in all regions, but you must obtain
cis_aws_1.19.remediation: Regularly audit IAM-stored SSL/TLS certificates and remove expired ones. Use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) where possible for automatic renewal. Implement automation scripts or AWS Config rules to monitor certificate expiration and schedule removals, ensuring only valid certificates are in use for secure connections.

cis_aws_1.2.description: AWS provides customers with the option of specifying the contact information for account's security team. It is recommended that this information be provided.
cis_aws_1.2.remediation: Register security contact information in AWS Account Settings under Alternate Contacts. Establish a procedure to update these details annually or when team changes occur. Use AWS Organizations to manage contacts across multiple accounts and document this in security policies for accountability.

cis_aws_1.20.description: Enable the IAM Access Analyzer for IAM policies regarding all resources in each active AWS region. IAM Access Analyzer is a technology introduced at AWS reinvent 2019. After the Analyzer is enabled in IAM, scan results are displayed on the console showing the accessible resources. Scans show resources that other accounts and federated users can access, such as KMS keys and IAM roles. The results allow you to determine whether an unintended user is permitted, making it easier for administrators t
cis_aws_1.20.remediation: Enable IAM Access Analyzer in all active AWS regions via the IAM console or API. Use AWS Organizations to apply this centrally. Configure regular scans and review findings to identify and remediate unintended resource access. Automate with CloudFormation or Terraform to ensure continuous enforcement.

cis_aws_1.21.description: In multi-account environments, IAM user centralization facilitates greater user control. User access beyond the initial account is then provided via role assumption. Centralization of users can be accomplished through federation with an external identity provider or through the use of AWS Organizations.
cis_aws_1.21.remediation: Centralize IAM user management using AWS Organizations or federate with an external identity provider (e.g., Active Directory). Use IAM roles for cross-account access instead of creating users in each account. Implement SSO solutions like AWS IAM Identity Center and document procedures for user provisioning and deprovisioning.

cis_aws_1.22.description: AWS CloudShell is a convenient way of running CLI commands against AWS services; a managed IAM policy ('AWSCloudShellFullAccess') provides full access to CloudShell, which allows file upload and download capability between a user's local system and the CloudShell environment. Within the CloudShell environment, a user has sudo permissions and can access the internet. Therefore, it is feasible to install file transfer software, for example, and move data from CloudShell to external internet server
cis_aws_1.22.remediation: Restrict access to the AWSCloudShellFullAccess policy by applying it only to necessary IAM roles or users. Use custom policies with least privilege for CloudShell access. Monitor policy attachments with AWS Config and implement RBAC to limit usage, preventing data exfiltration risks through CloudShell.

cis_aws_1.3.description: The AWS support portal allows account owners to establish security questions that can be used to authenticate individuals calling AWS customer service for support. It is recommended that security questions be established.
cis_aws_1.3.remediation: Set up security questions in the AWS Support Center for account recovery and verification. Document the questions and answers securely, ensuring they are known to multiple authorized personnel. Review and update these questions annually as part of security governance procedures to maintain account integrity.

cis_aws_1.4.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. AWS Access Keys provide programmatic access to a given AWS account. It is recommended that all access keys associated with the 'root' user account be deleted.
cis_aws_1.4.remediation: Delete any access keys associated with the root user via the AWS Management Console, CLI, or SDK. Use IAM users or roles with least privilege for programmatic access. Implement automated checks in IaC tools like Terraform or CloudFormation to prevent root access key creation.

cis_aws_1.5.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. Multi-factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to an AWS website, they will be prompted for their username and password as well as for an authentication code from their AWS MFA device. Note: When virtual MFA is used for 'root' accounts, it is recommended that the device used is NOT a personal device, but rather a dedicated mobile de
cis_aws_1.5.remediation: Enable MFA for the root user using a hardware or virtual MFA device via the AWS Management Console. Enforce MFA through IAM policies and use AWS Organizations SCPs to require MFA for root actions. Regularly audit MFA status with automated tools.

cis_aws_1.6.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. MFA adds an extra layer of protection on top of a user name and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to an AWS website, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their AWS MFA device. For Level 2, it is recommended that the 'root' user account be protected with a hardware MFA.
cis_aws_1.6.remediation: Configure a hardware MFA device for the root user via the AWS Management Console. Use IAM policies to enforce hardware MFA for sensitive operations. Implement monitoring to detect and alert on root login attempts without MFA.

cis_aws_1.7.description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure passwords are at least a given length. It is recommended that the password policy require a minimum password length 14.
cis_aws_1.7.remediation: Set the minimum password length to 14 or greater in the IAM password policy using AWS CLI, SDK, or Management Console. Enforce this in IaC templates like CloudFormation or Terraform. Regularly review and update the policy to meet organizational standards.

cis_aws_1.8.description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure passwords are at least a given length. It is recommended that the password policy require a minimum password length 14.
cis_aws_1.8.remediation: Set the minimum password length to 14 or greater in the IAM password policy using AWS CLI, SDK, or Management Console. Enforce this in IaC templates like CloudFormation or Terraform. Regularly review and update the policy to meet organizational standards.

cis_aws_1.9.description: IAM password policies can prevent the reuse of a given password by the same user. It is recommended that the password policy prevent the reuse of passwords.
cis_aws_1.9.remediation: Configure the IAM password policy to prevent password reuse, typically by setting a history of 24 previous passwords. Use AWS CLI or Management Console to update the policy. Integrate this into IaC for consistent enforcement across accounts.

cis_aws_10.1.description: Ensure secure data ingestion for Amazon Timestream by using encrypted protocols and private endpoints to prevent unauthorized access during data transfer.
cis_aws_10.1.remediation: Configure Timestream to use secure protocols like HTTPS and VPC endpoints for data ingestion. Use IAM policies to restrict access to authorized users and services. Implement network security groups to control traffic flow.

cis_aws_10.2.description: Enable encryption at rest for Amazon Timestream to protect your data while it is stored. Utilize AWS Key Management Service (KMS) to manage and control the encryption keys used for data encryption. Configure Timestream to encrypt your data using AWS-managed keys or customer-managed keys.
cis_aws_10.2.remediation: Enable encryption at rest in Timestream using AWS KMS keys via the AWS Console, CLI, or SDK. Use customer-managed keys for greater control and auditability. Ensure IaC templates specify encryption settings for all Timestream databases.

cis_aws_10.3.description: Configure your applications or tools to use secure communication protocols when interacting with Amazon Timestream. Utilize endpoints to establish private and secure connections to Timestream.
cis_aws_10.3.remediation: Enforce TLS encryption for all data in transit to Timestream by configuring clients to use HTTPS. Set up VPC endpoints to keep traffic within the AWS network. Use security policies to block unencrypted connections.

cis_aws_10.4.description: Utilize AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) to control access to your Amazon Timestream resources. Define IAM policies that grant or deny permissions for specific Timestream actions and resources.
cis_aws_10.4.remediation: Create IAM policies with least privilege for Timestream access, attaching them to users, roles, or groups. Enable MFA for sensitive operations. Use AWS Organizations SCPs to enforce access controls across accounts.

cis_aws_10.5.description: Leverage Timestream's fine-grained access control capabilities to control table or row level access. Define access policies that limit access to specific tables, columns, or rows based on user roles or conditions. Implement data filtering and row-level security to restrict access to sensitive information.
cis_aws_10.5.remediation: Configure fine-grained access control in Timestream using IAM policies and resource-level permissions. Implement row-level security through query conditions. Regularly audit access policies to ensure they align with data sensitivity.

cis_aws_10.6.description: Enable AWS CloudTrail to capture and log API calls and activities related to Amazon Timestream. Configure CloudTrail to store the logs in a secure location and regularly review the logs for any unauthorized or suspicious activities.
cis_aws_10.6.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging for Timestream in all regions, storing logs in an encrypted S3 bucket with restricted access. Use CloudWatch Logs for real-time monitoring and set up alerts for critical events. Integrate with SIEM tools for analysis.

cis_aws_10.7.description: Stay updated with the latest security patches and updates provided by AWS for Amazon Timestream. Follow AWS security best practices and recommendations to ensure your Timestream implementation remains secure.
cis_aws_10.7.remediation: Subscribe to AWS security bulletins and apply updates promptly. Use AWS Config rules to monitor Timestream configuration compliance. Establish a patch management process that includes regular reviews and testing.

cis_aws_10.8.description: Utilize Amazon CloudWatch to monitor key metrics, events, and logs related to Amazon Timestream. Set up appropriate alarms and notifications to detect security incidents or abnormal behavior proactively.
cis_aws_10.8.remediation: Create CloudWatch alarms for Timestream metrics like error rates and latency. Configure SNS topics for alert notifications. Use dashboards to visualize performance and security trends, integrating with incident response tools.

cis_aws_10.9.description: Conduct regular security reviews and assessments of your Amazon Timestream implementation. Evaluate access permissions, encryption settings, and security controls to ensure they align with your organization's security requirements.
cis_aws_10.9.remediation: Schedule periodic security audits using AWS Security Hub or third-party tools. Review IAM policies, encryption configurations, and network settings. Update security configurations based on audit findings and evolving threats.

cis_aws_11.1.description: This control is important because by having IAM roles implemented in the database it only allows certain people who are authenticated into the database to modify the database and would not give access to unauthorized personnel. This ensures that the data is being protected from any threat actor.
cis_aws_11.1.remediation: Implement IAM roles with least privilege for QLDB access, using policies to restrict actions to necessary permissions. Use AWS Organizations to enforce role-based access across accounts. Regularly review and update IAM configurations to prevent privilege creep.

cis_aws_11.2.description: By applying certain network access such as Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) it protects the private network that has been established from any external networks from interfering. It allows internal networks to communicate with one another with the network that has been established. The Access Control List (ACLs) allows only specific individuals to access the resources. Also, by monitoring and logging the activity within the database it helps the individual know what is being logged within the activit
cis_aws_11.2.remediation: Configure QLDB within a VPC and use security groups and NACLs to restrict network access. Enable VPC endpoints for private connectivity. Monitor network traffic with CloudWatch and VPC Flow Logs to detect anomalies.

cis_aws_11.3.description: This helps ensure that the data is kept secure and protected when at rest. The user must choose from two key options which then determine when the data is encrypted at rest.
cis_aws_11.3.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for QLDB using AWS KMS, selecting either AWS-managed or customer-managed keys. Configure encryption during ledger creation via CLI or SDK. Ensure IaC templates enforce encryption settings for all QLDB resources.

cis_aws_11.4.description: Use Transport Layer Security (TLS) to encrypt communications between clients and your QLDB instance. QLDB provides TLS support by default, allowing secure communication over the network. Configure your client applications to use TLS when connecting to QLDB.
cis_aws_11.4.remediation: Enforce TLS 1.2 or higher for all client connections to QLDB. Configure applications to use HTTPS endpoints. Use security policies to block unencrypted traffic and regularly update TLS configurations to meet compliance standards.

cis_aws_11.5.description: Utilize QLDB's built-in authentication and access control mechanisms. Define IAM policies to control which users or roles can perform specific actions on QLDB resources. Leverage IAM roles for cross-service access, securely integrating QLDB with other AWS services.
cis_aws_11.5.remediation: Create IAM policies with least privilege for QLDB actions, attaching them to roles for cross-service access. Enable MFA for sensitive operations. Use AWS Config to monitor policy compliance and audit access logs regularly.

cis_aws_11.6.description: Enable QLDB's built-in logging to capture important system events and database activity. Monitor the logs for any suspicious activities or errors. Leverage Amazon CloudWatch to collect and analyze logs, set up alarms, and receive notifications for potential security incidents.
cis_aws_11.6.remediation: Enable CloudTrail and QLDB audit logs, storing them in an encrypted S3 bucket. Use CloudWatch to set up alarms for critical events like unauthorized access. Integrate logs with SIEM tools for comprehensive monitoring and analysis.

cis_aws_11.7.description: Having the data backed up ensures that all the crucial information is stored securely it defends against any human errors and system errors that resulted in data loss. An organization that has a disaster recovery plan is prepared for any disruption that would impact business operations.
cis_aws_11.7.remediation: Configure QLDB journal exports to S3 for backups, enabling encryption and versioning. Test restore procedures regularly. Develop a disaster recovery plan that includes backup schedules and recovery time objectives, integrating with AWS Backup for automation.

cis_aws_12.1.description: With AWS Config, you can track configuration changes to the Lambda functions (including deleted functions), runtime environments, tags, handler name, code size, memory allocation, timeout settings, and concurrency settings, along with Lambda IAM execution role, subnet, and security group associations.
cis_aws_12.1.remediation: Enable AWS Config for Lambda in all regions, configuring rules to monitor function settings. Use Config rules to enforce compliance policies. Store configuration history in an encrypted S3 bucket and set up notifications for changes.

cis_aws_12.10.description: Ensure that all your Amazon Lambda functions are configured to allow access only to trusted AWS accounts in order to protect against unauthorized cross-account access.
cis_aws_12.10.remediation: Review and restrict Lambda resource policies to allow access only from trusted accounts. Use IAM conditions to limit cross-account permissions. Implement automated scans with tools like AWS Config or third-party CSPM to detect and remediate overly permissive policies.

cis_aws_12.11.description: Always using a recent version of the execution environment configured for your Amazon Lambda functions adheres to best practices for the newest software features, the latest security patches and bug fixes, and performance and reliability.
cis_aws_12.11.remediation: Regularly update Lambda runtime environments to supported versions, avoiding end-of-life runtimes. Use AWS Lambda layers or container images to manage dependencies. Implement automated checks in CI/CD pipelines to enforce runtime version policies.

cis_aws_12.12.description: As you can set your own environmental variables for Lambda it is important to also encrypt them for in transit protection.
cis_aws_12.12.remediation: Enable encryption in transit for Lambda environment variables by using AWS KMS to encrypt them. In Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like AWS CloudFormation or Terraform, specify KMS keys for environment variables. Ensure IAM roles have necessary permissions to use the KMS key for encryption and decryption.

cis_aws_12.2.description: Ensure that Amazon CloudWatch Lambda Insights is enabled for your Amazon Lambda functions for enhanced monitoring.
cis_aws_12.2.remediation: Enable CloudWatch Lambda Insights for Lambda functions via AWS Management Console, CLI, or IaC tools. In Terraform or CloudFormation, set the 'tracing_config' mode to 'Active' and attach the necessary IAM policies for CloudWatch permissions. Regularly review logs and metrics for anomalies.

cis_aws_12.3.description: Lambda functions often have to access a database or other services within your environment.
cis_aws_12.3.remediation: Configure Lambda functions to use AWS Secrets Manager for storing and retrieving secrets like database credentials. In IaC, reference secrets from Secrets Manager instead of hardcoding them. Ensure IAM roles grant least privilege access to Secrets Manager and enable encryption at rest.

cis_aws_12.4.description: Lambda is fully integrated with IAM, allowing you to control precisely what each Lambda function can do within the AWS Cloud. As you develop a Lambda function, you expand the scope of this policy to enable access to other resources. For example, for a function that processes objects put into an S3 bucket, it requires read access to objects stored in that bucket. Do not grant the function broader permissions to write or delete data, or operate in other buckets.
cis_aws_12.4.remediation: Apply least privilege principles by crafting IAM policies that grant only necessary permissions for each Lambda function. Use IaC to define precise IAM roles and policies, avoiding wildcard permissions. Regularly audit and update policies based on function requirements.

cis_aws_12.5.description: Every Lambda function should have a one to one IAM execution role and the roles should not be shared between functions.
cis_aws_12.5.remediation: Assign a unique IAM execution role to each Lambda function. In IaC like AWS CloudFormation, create separate IAM roles for each function definition. Avoid reusing roles across functions to minimize blast radius and enforce granular access control.

cis_aws_12.6.description: A publicly accessible Amazon Lambda function is open to the public and can be reviewed by anyone. To protect against unauthorized users that are sending requests to invoke these functions they need to be changed so they are not exposed to the public.
cis_aws_12.6.remediation: Restrict public access to Lambda functions by configuring resource-based policies to allow only specific IAM roles or accounts. Use AWS IAM and VPC settings to limit invocations. In IaC, explicitly set permissions and avoid public access configurations.

cis_aws_12.7.description: In order to have the necessary permissions to access the AWS cloud services and resources Amazon Lambda functions should be associated with active(available) execution roles.
cis_aws_12.7.remediation: Ensure Lambda functions reference existing and active IAM execution roles. In IaC, verify role existence before deployment. Regularly audit functions to detect and remediate any references to deleted or inactive roles.

cis_aws_12.8.description: Ensure that all your Amazon Lambda functions are configured to use the Code Signing feature in order to restrict the deployment of unverified code.
cis_aws_12.8.remediation: Enable Code Signing for Lambda functions by creating a code signing configuration in AWS Signer. In IaC, specify the signing profile and attach it to functions. Ensure IAM roles have permissions for code signing and regularly update signing keys.

cis_aws_12.9.description: Ensure that your Amazon Lambda functions don't have administrative permissions potentially giving the function access to all AWS cloud services and resources.
cis_aws_12.9.remediation: Audit IAM roles attached to Lambda functions and remove any policies with full administrative privileges like '*:*'. Use IaC to enforce least privilege policies. Implement regular reviews to detect and revoke excessive permissions.

cis_aws_16.1.description: SimSpace Weaver doesn't manage communications between your apps and the clients.
cis_aws_16.1.remediation: Enable encryption in transit for application communications using TLS/HTTPS. Configure services like DynamoDB, S3, and EC2 to require encrypted connections. In IaC, set policies to enforce HTTPS and use KMS for encryption where applicable.

cis_aws_2.1.description: To allow users to administer Amazon WorkSpaces, IAM policies must be created and attached with the required permissions to an IAM Principal used for administration of Amazon WorkSpaces. An IAM Principal may be a IAM Role or an IAM User, or an IAM User Group with Users within the User Group. AWS has an AWS Managed Policy, AmazonWorkSpacesAdmin that grants permissions to administer Amazon WorkSpaces. A custom managed policy or inline policy may be used to grant WorkSpaces permissions to the IAM Pr
cis_aws_2.1.remediation: Define WorkSpaces administration using IAM by attaching the AmazonWorkSpacesAdmin managed policy or custom policies with least privilege to IAM roles or groups. In IaC, create IAM roles with specific permissions and avoid inline policies. Regularly review and update policies.

cis_aws_2.1.1.description: At the Amazon S3 bucket level, you can configure permissions through a bucket policy, making the objects accessible only through HTTPS.
cis_aws_2.1.1.remediation: Configure S3 bucket policies to deny HTTP requests and require TLS/HTTPS. In IaC like Terraform, set the 'policy' attribute to include a condition that enforces 'aws:SecureTransport'. Apply this to all buckets to ensure encrypted data in transit.

cis_aws_2.1.2.description: Ensure that audit logs are collected and managed in accordance with the enterprise’s audit log management process across all Kubernetes components.
cis_aws_2.1.2.remediation: Enable and centralize audit logging using services like AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, or GCP Logging. In IaC, configure log trails and sinks with encryption and retention policies. Ensure IAM roles have necessary permissions and regularly review logs for compliance.

cis_aws_2.1.3.description: Amazon S3 buckets can contain sensitive data that, for security purposes, should be discovered, monitored, classified, and protected. Macie, along with other third-party tools, can automatically provide an inventory of Amazon S3 buckets.
cis_aws_2.1.3.remediation: Use AWS Macie or third-party tools to discover, classify, and secure sensitive data in S3 buckets. In IaC, enable Macie jobs and configure bucket policies for encryption and access control. Regularly review classification results and apply security measures as needed.

cis_aws_2.1.4.description: Amazon S3 provides Block public access (bucket settings) and Block public access (account settings) to help you manage public access to Amazon S3 resources. By default, S3 buckets and objects are created with public access disabled. However, an IAM principal with sufficient S3 permissions can enable public access at the bucket and/or object level. While enabled, Block public access (bucket settings) prevents an individual bucket and its contained objects from becoming publicly accessible. Simila
cis_aws_2.1.4.remediation: Enable S3 Block Public Access at both bucket and account levels. In IaC, set 'block_public_acls', 'ignore_public_acls', 'block_public_policy', and 'restrict_public_buckets' to true. Regularly audit S3 configurations to ensure no public access is inadvertently granted.

cis_aws_2.1.5.description: EC2 allows you to make an AMI public, sharing it with all AWS accounts.
cis_aws_2.1.5.remediation: Ensure EC2 AMIs are not publicly available by setting visibility to private or shared only with specific accounts. In IaC, configure AMI permissions to restrict public access. Regularly scan and update AMI settings to prevent accidental exposure.

cis_aws_2.10.description: In order for Windows updates to occur auto-stop WorkSpaces must be utilized and the default for maintenance mode must be set to enabled.
cis_aws_2.10.remediation: Configure WorkSpaces to use auto-stop and enable maintenance mode for operating system updates. In IaC, set directory settings to enforce these configurations. Establish a patch management process to regularly apply updates and monitor compliance.

cis_aws_2.11.description: AWS IAM users can access AWS resources using different types of credentials, such as passwords or access keys. It is recommended that all credentials that have been unused for 45 days or more be deactivated or removed.
cis_aws_2.11.remediation: Implement IAM credential lifecycle policies to disable unused credentials after 45 days. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer or custom scripts to audit and deactivate inactive credentials. In IaC, enforce policies and regularly review user activity.

cis_aws_2.12.description: Access keys are long-term credentials for an IAM user or the AWS account 'root' user. You can use access keys to sign programmatic requests to the AWS CLI or AWS API (directly or using the AWS SDK)
cis_aws_2.12.remediation: Ensure each IAM user has only one active access key at a time. Use IAM policies and monitoring tools to audit and enforce this. In IaC, implement checks and rotate keys regularly to maintain security.

cis_aws_2.13.description: Access keys consist of an access key ID and secret access key, which are used to sign programmatic requests that you make to AWS. AWS users need their own access keys to make programmatic calls to AWS from the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI), Tools for Windows PowerShell, the AWS SDKs, or direct HTTP calls using the APIs for individual AWS services. It is recommended that all access keys be rotated regularly.
cis_aws_2.13.remediation: Rotate IAM access keys every 90 days or less using automated tools or AWS IAM policies. In IaC, integrate key rotation scripts and monitor key age. Ensure users update their configurations with new keys promptly.

cis_aws_2.14.description: IAM users are granted access to services, functions, and data through IAM policies. There are four ways to define policies for a user: 1) Edit the user policy directly, also known as an inline or user policy; 2) attach a policy directly to a user; 3) add the user to an IAM group that has an attached policy; 4) add the user to an IAM group that has an inline policy. Only the third implementation is recommended.
cis_aws_2.14.remediation: Grant IAM user permissions only through groups by adding users to IAM groups with attached policies. In IaC, define groups and policies separately, avoiding inline or direct user attachments. Regularly audit group memberships and policies for compliance.

cis_aws_2.15.description: IAM policies are the means by which privileges are granted to users, groups, or roles. It is recommended and considered standard security advice to grant least privilege—that is, granting only the permissions required to perform a task. Determine what users need to do, and then craft policies for them that allow the users to perform only those tasks, instead of granting full administrative privileges.
cis_aws_2.15.remediation: Avoid IAM policies with full '*:*' administrative privileges. Use IaC to define least privilege policies and regularly audit existing policies. Implement IAM Access Analyzer to identify and remediate overly permissive policies.

cis_aws_2.16.description: AWS provides a support center that can be used for incident notification and response, as well as technical support and customer services. Create an IAM Role, with the appropriate policy assigned, to allow authorized users to manage incidents with AWS Support.
cis_aws_2.16.remediation: Create an IAM role with the AWSSupportAccess policy attached for managing AWS Support incidents. In IaC, define the role and policy attachment. Ensure only authorized users assume this role and regularly review its usage.

cis_aws_2.17.description: AWS access from within AWS instances can be done by either encoding AWS keys into AWS API calls or by assigning the instance to a role which has an appropriate permissions policy for the required access. "AWS Access" means accessing the APIs of AWS in order to access AWS resources or manage AWS account resources.
cis_aws_2.17.remediation: Use IAM instance roles for AWS resource access from EC2 instances instead of embedding access keys. In IaC, attach IAM roles to instances with least privilege policies. Regularly audit instance configurations to ensure no hardcoded credentials are present.

cis_aws_2.18.description: To enable HTTPS connections to your website or application in AWS, you need an SSL/TLS server certificate. You can use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) or IAM to store and deploy server certificates. Use IAM as a certificate manager only when you must support HTTPS connections in a region that is not supported by ACM. IAM securely encrypts your private keys and stores the encrypted version in IAM SSL certificate storage. IAM supports deploying server certificates in all regions, but you must obtain
cis_aws_2.18.remediation: Remove expired SSL/TLS certificates stored in AWS IAM by regularly auditing and deleting them. Use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) for certificate management where possible. In IaC, automate certificate lifecycle management and monitor expiration dates.

cis_aws_2.19.description: Enable the IAM External Access Analyzer regarding all resources in each active AWS region. IAM Access Analyzer is a technology introduced at AWS reinvent 2019. After the Analyzer is enabled in IAM, scan results are displayed on the console showing the accessible resources. Scans show resources that other accounts and federated users can access, such as KMS keys and IAM roles. The results allow you to determine whether an unintended user is permitted, making it easier for administrators to monito
cis_aws_2.19.remediation: Enable IAM Access Analyzer in all active AWS regions via the AWS Management Console, CLI, or SDK. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to automate deployment and ensure continuous monitoring. Regularly review findings to remediate unintended external access and update IAM policies accordingly.

cis_aws_2.2.description: AWS provides customers with the option of specifying the contact information for account's security team. It is recommended that this information be provided.
cis_aws_2.2.remediation: Set security contact information in AWS Organizations or the AWS Management Console under 'Account Settings'. Use AWS Config or custom scripts to automate compliance checks. Ensure contact details are updated regularly and integrated with incident response procedures.

cis_aws_2.2.1.description: Amazon RDS encrypted DB instances use the industry-standard AES-256 encryption algorithm to encrypt your data on the server that hosts your Amazon RDS DB instances. After your data is encrypted, Amazon RDS handles the authentication of access and the decryption of your data transparently, with minimal impact on performance.
cis_aws_2.2.1.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for all RDS instances during creation via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools like CloudFormation. Use AWS KMS keys for encryption and enforce policies to block unencrypted instance creation. Regularly audit existing instances and re-encrypt if necessary.

cis_aws_2.2.2.description: Ensure that RDS database instances have the Auto Minor Version Upgrade flag enabled to automatically receive minor engine upgrades during the specified maintenance window. This way, RDS instances can obtain new features, bug fixes, and security patches for their database engines.
cis_aws_2.2.2.remediation: Enable Auto Minor Version Upgrade for RDS instances via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Configure maintenance windows to minimize downtime. Use AWS Config rules to monitor compliance and automate remediation for non-compliant instances.

cis_aws_2.2.3.description: Ensure and verify that the RDS database instances provisioned in your AWS account restrict unauthorized access in order to minimize security risks. To restrict access to any RDS database instance, you must disable the Publicly Accessible flag for the database and update the VPC security group associated with the instance.
cis_aws_2.2.3.remediation: Disable the 'Publicly Accessible' flag for all RDS instances and configure VPC security groups to restrict access to authorized IPs or VPCs. Use IaC to enforce private subnet placement and regular audits with AWS Config to detect and remediate public exposure.

cis_aws_2.2.4.description: Amazon RDS offers Multi-AZ deployments that provide enhanced availability and durability for your databases, using synchronous replication to replicate data to a standby instance in a different Availability Zone (AZ). In the event of an infrastructure failure, Amazon RDS automatically fails over to the standby to minimize downtime and ensure business continuity.
cis_aws_2.2.4.remediation: Enable Multi-AZ deployment for production RDS instances via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools. Configure failover settings and monitor replication status. Use AWS Cost Explorer to manage expenses and ensure non-critical instances align with business requirements.

cis_aws_2.20.description: In multi-account environments, IAM user centralization facilitates greater user control. User access beyond the initial account is then provided via role assumption. Centralization of users can be accomplished through federation with an external identity provider or through the use of AWS Organizations.
cis_aws_2.20.remediation: Implement identity federation using AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) or integrate with external IdPs like Okta. Use AWS Organizations for centralized management and assign roles via cross-account access. Enforce least privilege and audit user access regularly.

cis_aws_2.21.description: AWS CloudShell is a convenient way of running CLI commands against AWS services; a managed IAM policy ('AWSCloudShellFullAccess') provides full access to CloudShell, which allows file upload and download capability between a user's local system and the CloudShell environment. Within the CloudShell environment, a user has sudo permissions and can access the internet. Therefore, it is feasible to install file transfer software, for example, and move data from CloudShell to external internet server
cis_aws_2.21.remediation: Restrict attachment of the AWSCloudShellFullAccess policy to only necessary IAM roles or users. Implement least privilege by creating custom policies with specific permissions. Use AWS Config to monitor policy attachments and automate removal of excessive privileges.

cis_aws_2.3.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. AWS Access Keys provide programmatic access to a given AWS account. It is recommended that all access keys associated with the 'root' user account be deleted.
cis_aws_2.3.remediation: Delete any access keys associated with the root user via the AWS Management Console or CLI. Use IAM users or roles for programmatic access with MFA and least privilege. Enable AWS Config rules to detect and alert on root access key usage.

cis_aws_2.3.1.description: EFS data should be encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (Key Management Service).
cis_aws_2.3.1.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for all EFS file systems during creation using AWS KMS. Use IaC tools like Terraform to enforce encryption policies. Audit existing file systems and re-encrypt if necessary, ensuring KMS key policies restrict unauthorized access.

cis_aws_2.4.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. Multi-factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to an AWS website, they will be prompted for their username and password as well as for an authentication code from their AWS MFA device. Note: When virtual MFA is used for 'root' accounts, it is recommended that the device used is NOT a personal device, but rather a dedicated mobile de
cis_aws_2.4.remediation: Enable MFA for the root user using a hardware or virtual MFA device via the AWS Console. Store backup codes securely and avoid using personal devices. Use AWS IAM policies to enforce MFA for all privileged actions and monitor via CloudTrail logs.

cis_aws_2.5.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. MFA adds an extra layer of protection on top of a username and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to an AWS website, they will be prompted for their username and password as well as for an authentication code from their AWS MFA device. For Level 2, it is recommended that the 'root' user account be protected with a hardware MFA. Where an AWS Organization is using centralized root access, root credentials can be r
cis_aws_2.5.remediation: Enable hardware MFA for the root user using devices like YubiKey or Gemalto. Follow AWS documentation for setup and store recovery options securely. Use AWS Organizations for centralized root management and enforce MFA via IAM policies for all console logins.

cis_aws_2.6.description: WorkSpaces access should be restricted to trusted operating systems and clients
cis_aws_2.6.remediation: Disable web access for Amazon WorkSpaces via the AWS Management Console or CLI. Configure directory settings to allow only trusted clients and enforce network policies. Use AWS Config to monitor compliance and automate remediation if web access is enabled.

cis_aws_2.7.description: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure passwords are at least a given length. It is recommended that the password policy require a minimum password length 14.
cis_aws_2.7.remediation: Set IAM password policy to require a minimum length of 14 characters using AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Enforce this policy across all IAM users and integrate with identity federation. Regularly audit compliance using AWS Config or custom scripts.

cis_aws_2.8.description: IAM password policies can prevent the reuse of a given password by the same user. It is recommended that the password policy prevent the reuse of passwords.
cis_aws_2.8.remediation: Configure IAM password policy to prevent password reuse, typically setting a history of 24 previous passwords. Use AWS Management Console or CLI to apply the policy. Monitor compliance with AWS Config and ensure synchronization with external identity providers.

cis_aws_2.9.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional credentials. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to the AWS Console, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their physical or virtual MFA token. It is recommended that MFA be enabled for all accounts that have a console password.
cis_aws_2.9.remediation: Enable MFA for all IAM users with console passwords using virtual or hardware MFA devices. Use AWS IAM policies to enforce MFA for sensitive actions. Automate compliance checks with AWS Config and provide user training on MFA setup.

cis_aws_3.1.description: AWS CloudTrail is a web service that records AWS API calls for your account and delivers log files to you. The recorded information includes the identity of the API caller, the time of the API call, the source IP address of the API caller, the request parameters, and the response elements returned by the AWS service. CloudTrail provides a history of AWS API calls for an account, including API calls made via the Management Console, SDKs, command line tools, and higher-level AWS services (such as
cis_aws_3.1.remediation: Enable CloudTrail in all AWS regions and configure a multi-region trail to centralize logs in an S3 bucket. Use IaC to automate deployment and enable log file validation. Encrypt logs with KMS and set up alerts for suspicious activities via CloudWatch.

cis_aws_3.1.1.description: If kubelet is running, and if it is configured by a kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_aws_3.1.1.remediation: Manually check and set kubeconfig file permissions to 644 using commands like 'chmod 644 /path/to/kubeconfig'. Implement regular audits via scripts or security tools. Document procedures and train administrators on proper file permission management in Kubernetes environments.

cis_aws_3.1.2.description: If kubelet is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
cis_aws_3.1.2.remediation: Manually set kubeconfig file ownership to root:root using 'chown root:root /path/to/kubeconfig'. Schedule periodic reviews with audit scripts. Update operational runbooks to include ownership checks as part of deployment and maintenance processes.

cis_aws_3.1.3.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_aws_3.1.3.remediation: Set kubelet configuration file permissions to 644 using IaC tools for EKS node groups or manual commands. Use AWS Config rules or third-party tools to automate compliance checks. Regularly audit file permissions across all Kubernetes clusters to prevent unauthorized modifications.

cis_aws_3.1.4.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file is owned by root:root.
cis_aws_3.1.4.remediation: Manually set ownership of kubelet configuration files to root:root using 'chown root:root /path/to/config'. Implement automated checks with security scanning tools. Include ownership validation in CI/CD pipelines and operational checklists for cluster deployments.

cis_aws_3.10.description: EFS access points serve as gateways to your EFS file system, allowing applications to interact with the file system across various resources. Proper configuration of these access points within your applications is crucial to ensure seamless and secure access. By configuring EFS access points, you can control and manage which users have access to specific resources in your EFS environment, enhancing security and operational efficiency.
cis_aws_3.10.remediation: Configure EFS access points with appropriate IAM policies and root directory settings using AWS Console or IaC. Enforce least privilege by restricting access to specific users or roles. Use AWS Config to monitor access point configurations and automate remediation for deviations.

cis_aws_3.11.description: You can use IAM policies to control access to your EFS access points. To achieve this, utilize the elasticfilesystem:AccessPointArn IAM condition key. The AccessPointArn represents the Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the access point that the file system is mounted with.
cis_aws_3.11.remediation: Create IAM policies using the elasticfilesystem:AccessPointArn condition key to restrict access to EFS access points. Apply these policies to IAM roles or users via AWS Console or IaC. Regularly review and update policies based on least privilege principles and audit access logs.

cis_aws_3.12.description: Before installing the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery client, you need to configure AWS IAM permissions and users for both the AWS Replication and AWS Failback Client.
cis_aws_3.12.remediation: Configure IAM permissions for AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery by creating custom policies with least privilege for replication and failback clients. Use AWS Console or IaC to assign these policies to IAM roles. Test permissions in a staging environment and monitor usage with CloudTrail.

cis_aws_3.13.description: Ensure that only trusted container images from verified sources or private repositories are used with Amazon ECS to maintain the integrity and security of workloads. Note: This recommendation assumes that only the latest active revision of a task definition is in use. If older revisions are in use, apply the audit and remediation procedures to those revisions as needed.
cis_aws_3.13.remediation: Use private ECR repositories or trusted public sources for ECS container images. Implement image scanning with Amazon ECR or third-party tools. Enforce policies in task definitions to block untrusted images and regularly audit all active revisions for compliance.

cis_aws_3.14.description: Ensure that assignPublicIp is set to DISABLED for Amazon ECS task sets, to prevent task sets from being publicly accessible.
cis_aws_3.14.remediation: In AWS ECS, configure task sets to disable assignPublicIp via the AWS Management Console, CLI, or IaC tools like Terraform or CloudFormation. Use IAM policies to restrict public IP assignments and regularly audit ECS configurations to ensure no public exposure.

cis_aws_3.2.description: CloudTrail log file validation creates a digitally signed digest file containing a hash of each log that CloudTrail writes to S3. These digest files can be used to determine whether a log file was changed, deleted, or remained unchanged after CloudTrail delivered the log. It is recommended that file validation be enabled for all CloudTrails.
cis_aws_3.2.remediation: Enable log file validation in CloudTrail settings through the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Ensure all trails have validation turned on to detect tampering. Use automated checks to monitor and enforce this configuration across accounts.

cis_aws_3.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
cis_aws_3.2.1.remediation: Configure Kubelet settings to disable anonymous authentication by setting --anonymous-auth=false in the kubelet configuration file. Use IaC tools like Terraform for Kubernetes clusters to enforce this, and apply RBAC policies to restrict access to authenticated users only.

cis_aws_3.2.2.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
cis_aws_3.2.2.remediation: Set --authorization-mode to a value other than AlwaysAllow, such as Webhook or RBAC, in the kubelet configuration. Use Kubernetes admission controllers and IaC to enforce this setting, and regularly audit cluster configurations to prevent overly permissive authorization.

cis_aws_3.2.3.remediation: Manually configure a Client CA file for Kubelet by setting --client-ca-file in the kubelet configuration. Establish a procedure to generate and manage certificates using a PKI system, and ensure regular rotation and auditing of certificates for security compliance.

cis_aws_3.2.4.remediation: Manually set --read-only-port=0 in the kubelet configuration to disable the read-only port. Implement a process to verify this setting across all nodes, using configuration management tools and regular audits to ensure compliance and prevent unauthorized access.

cis_aws_3.2.5.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
cis_aws_3.2.5.remediation: Ensure --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to a non-zero value, such as 30m, in the kubelet configuration. Use IaC for Kubernetes to enforce this setting, and monitor configurations to prevent security risks from indefinite connections.

cis_aws_3.2.6.remediation: Manually set --make-iptables-util-chains=true in the kubelet configuration. Follow a documented procedure to configure this on all nodes, and use configuration audits to ensure Kubelet can properly manage network rules for security.

cis_aws_3.2.7.description: Security relevant information should be captured. The eventRecordQPS on the Kubelet configuration can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered and sets the maximum event creations per second. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
cis_aws_3.2.7.remediation: Manually configure --eventRecordQPS to a balanced value, such as 5, to capture security events without causing denial of service. Establish a process to review and adjust this setting based on cluster load and security needs, using monitoring tools to track event logging.

cis_aws_3.2.8.remediation: Set --rotate-certificates=true in the kubelet configuration using IaC tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests. Automate certificate management with tools like cert-manager, and regularly audit to ensure certificates are rotating as intended to maintain security.

cis_aws_3.2.9.remediation: Configure RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true in the kubelet configuration via IaC. Use automated tools to manage certificate lifecycle and enforce rotation policies, ensuring server certificates are regularly updated to prevent security vulnerabilities.

cis_aws_3.3.description: AWS Config is a web service that performs configuration management of supported AWS resources within your account and delivers log files to you. The recorded information includes the configuration items (AWS resources), relationships between configuration items (AWS resources), and any configuration changes between resources. It is recommended that AWS Config be enabled in all regions.
cis_aws_3.3.remediation: Enable AWS Config in all regions using the AWS Management Console, CLI, or IaC like CloudFormation. Set up multi-region recorders and ensure continuous monitoring. Use automated policies to enforce this configuration and alert on any disabled regions.

cis_aws_3.3.1.description: EFS data should be encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (Key Management Service).
cis_aws_3.3.1.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for all EFS file systems via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools such as Terraform. Use KMS CMKs for encryption and apply IAM policies to restrict access. Regularly audit EFS configurations to ensure encryption is enabled.

cis_aws_3.4.description: Server access logging generates a log that contains access records for each request made to your S3 bucket. An access log record contains details about the request, such as the request type, the resources specified in the request worked, and the time and date the request was processed. It is recommended that server access logging be enabled on the CloudTrail S3 bucket.
cis_aws_3.4.remediation: Enable server access logging on the S3 bucket used for CloudTrail logs through AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Configure logging to a separate bucket for isolation, and use automated checks to ensure logging remains enabled and logs are monitored for suspicious activity.

cis_aws_3.5.description: AWS CloudTrail is a web service that records AWS API calls for an account and makes those logs available to users and resources in accordance with IAM policies. AWS Key Management Service (KMS) is a managed service that helps create and control the encryption keys used to encrypt account data, and uses Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) to protect the security of encryption keys. CloudTrail logs can be configured to leverage server side encryption (SSE) and KMS customer-created master keys (CMK) t
cis_aws_3.5.remediation: Encrypt CloudTrail logs at rest using KMS CMKs by enabling SSE-KMS in CloudTrail settings via AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Use customer-managed keys for better control, and apply IAM policies to restrict key access. Regularly audit encryption settings to ensure compliance.

cis_aws_3.6.description: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) allows customers to rotate the backing key, which is key material stored within the KMS that is tied to the key ID of the customer-created customer master key (CMK). The backing key is used to perform cryptographic operations such as encryption and decryption. Automated key rotation currently retains all prior backing keys so that decryption of encrypted data can occur transparently. It is recommended that CMK key rotation be enabled for symmetric keys. Key rotat
cis_aws_3.6.remediation: Enable automatic key rotation for symmetric CMKs in KMS via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools like CloudFormation. Set rotation periods (e.g., annually) and use IAM policies to enforce rotation. Monitor key usage and rotation status with automated alerts.

cis_aws_3.7.description: VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. After you've created a flow log, you can view and retrieve its data in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. It is recommended that VPC Flow Logs be enabled for packet "Rejects" for VPCs.
cis_aws_3.7.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for all VPCs, focusing on reject traffic, using AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Configure logs to send to CloudWatch or S3 for analysis. Use automated tools to ensure flow logs are active and set up alarms for unusual network activity.

cis_aws_3.8.description: S3 object-level API operations, such as GetObject, DeleteObject, and PutObject, are referred to as data events. By default, CloudTrail trails do not log data events, so it is recommended to enable object-level logging for S3 buckets.
cis_aws_3.8.remediation: Enable object-level logging for write events in CloudTrail by configuring data events for S3 buckets via AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Use IAM policies to restrict logging to sensitive buckets, and monitor logs with SIEM tools for unauthorized access attempts.

cis_aws_3.9.description: S3 object-level API operations, such as GetObject, DeleteObject, and PutObject, are referred to as data events. By default, CloudTrail trails do not log data events, so it is recommended to enable object-level logging for S3 buckets.
cis_aws_3.9.remediation: Enable object-level logging for read events in CloudTrail by setting up data events for S3 buckets using AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Ensure logging covers all critical buckets and use automated checks to verify configuration. Analyze logs regularly for security insights.

cis_aws_4.1.description: AWS CloudTrail is a web service that records AWS API calls for your account and delivers log files to you. The recorded information includes the identity of the API caller, the time of the API call, the source IP address of the API caller, the request parameters, and the response elements returned by the AWS service. CloudTrail provides a history of AWS API calls for an account, including API calls made via the Management Console, SDKs, command line tools, and higher-level AWS services (such as
cis_aws_4.1.remediation: Enable CloudTrail in all regions using a multi-region trail via AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Ensure trails are configured to log management and data events. Use automated monitoring to detect any disabled trails and enforce compliance with organizational policies.

cis_aws_4.1.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
cis_aws_4.1.1.remediation: Restrict use of the cluster-admin role by applying least privilege principles in Kubernetes RBAC. Use IaC to define role bindings and regularly audit permissions with tools like kubeaudit. Ensure only necessary users or service accounts have this role.

cis_aws_4.1.5.description: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
cis_aws_4.1.5.remediation: Avoid using default service accounts in Kubernetes pods. Create dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions using IaC like Helm or Kustomize. Regularly review and audit service account usage to enforce this policy and reduce security risks.

cis_aws_4.1.6.description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
cis_aws_4.1.6.remediation: Configure pods to mount service account tokens only when necessary by setting automountServiceAccountToken to false in pod specifications. Use IaC to enforce this and audit pod configurations regularly to prevent unnecessary token exposure and enhance security.

cis_aws_4.10.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Security groups are stateful packet filters that control ingress and egress traffic within a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established to detect changes to security groups.
cis_aws_4.10.remediation: Set up CloudTrail logs to CloudWatch and create metric filters and alarms for security group changes. Use IaC to automate this configuration and ensure alarms notify relevant teams. Regularly test alarms to maintain effective monitoring of network security changes.

cis_aws_4.11.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. NACLs are used as a stateless packet filter to control ingress and egress traffic for subnets within a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for any changes made to NACLs.
cis_aws_4.11.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to log NACL changes and set up metric filters and alarms in CloudWatch. Use IaC to deploy these monitoring tools consistently. Ensure alarms are integrated with incident response procedures to quickly address unauthorized network changes.

cis_aws_4.12.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Network gateways are required to send and receive traffic to a destination outside of a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to network gateways.
cis_aws_4.12.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for events like CreateVpnGateway or DeleteVpnGateway and set up a CloudWatch alarm to notify on changes. Use AWS Config rules or IaC tools like Terraform to automate this setup across accounts.

cis_aws_4.13.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Routing tables are used to route network traffic between subnets and to network gateways. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to route tables.
cis_aws_4.13.remediation: Set up CloudTrail integration with CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Define a metric filter for API calls such as CreateRoute or ReplaceRoute and configure a CloudWatch alarm for alerts. Implement this using AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to ensure consistency in multi-region deployments.

cis_aws_4.14.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is possible to have more than one VPC within an account; additionally, it is also possible to create a peer connection between two VPCs, enabling network traffic to route between them. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes made.
cis_aws_4.14.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for VPC-related events like CreateVpc or ModifyVpcAttribute and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config or IaC scripts to automate this monitoring across all VPCs and regions.

cis_aws_4.15.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes made to AWS Organizations in the master AWS account.
cis_aws_4.15.remediation: Configure CloudTrail in the master account to log Organizations API calls. Set up a metric filter for events like CreateOrganization or InviteAccountToOrganization and create a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Organizations policies and IaC to enforce this monitoring setup.

cis_aws_4.16.description: Security Hub collects security data from various AWS accounts, services, and supported third-party partner products, helping you analyze your security trends and identify the highest-priority security issues. When you enable Security Hub, it begins to consume, aggregate, organize, and prioritize findings from the AWS services that you have enabled, such as Amazon GuardDuty, Amazon Inspector, and Amazon Macie. You can also enable integrations with AWS partner security products.
cis_aws_4.16.remediation: Enable AWS Security Hub in all regions using the AWS Management Console, CLI, or SDK. Integrate it with services like GuardDuty and Inspector. Use AWS Organizations to automate enabling Security Hub across member accounts and set up compliance checks via AWS Config.

cis_aws_4.2.description: CloudTrail log file validation creates a digitally signed digest file containing a hash of each log that CloudTrail writes to S3. These digest files can be used to determine whether a log file was changed, deleted, or remained unchanged after CloudTrail delivered the log. It is recommended that file validation be enabled for all CloudTrails.
cis_aws_4.2.remediation: Enable log file validation when creating or updating CloudTrail trails via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools like CloudFormation. Ensure S3 bucket policies restrict write access to prevent tampering. Regularly audit trails using AWS Config rules to verify validation is active.

cis_aws_4.3.description: AWS Config is a web service that performs configuration management of supported AWS resources within your account and delivers log files to you. The recorded information includes the configuration items (AWS resources), relationships between configuration items (AWS resources), and any configuration changes between resources. It is recommended that AWS Config be enabled in all regions.
cis_aws_4.3.remediation: Enable AWS Config in all regions using the AWS Management Console, CLI, or automation scripts. Configure it to record all resource types and deliver logs to an S3 bucket. Use AWS Organizations to deploy Config rules across accounts and set up compliance alerts.

cis_aws_4.3.1.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
cis_aws_4.3.1.remediation: Select a CNI plugin that supports Kubernetes Network Policies, such as Calico or Cilium. Configure your EKS, AKS, GKE, or OKE cluster to enable network policy enforcement during cluster creation. Use IaC tools like Terraform to automate this setup and apply network policies to restrict traffic.

cis_aws_4.3.2.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
cis_aws_4.3.2.remediation: Define and apply Kubernetes Network Policies for all namespaces to control ingress and egress traffic. Use tools like kubectl or Helm charts to deploy policies. Regularly audit namespaces using kube-bench or similar tools to ensure no namespace is without a network policy.

cis_aws_4.4.description: Server access logging generates a log that contains access records for each request made to your S3 bucket. An access log record contains details about the request, such as the request type, the resources specified in the request worked, and the time and date the request was processed. It is recommended that server access logging be enabled on the CloudTrail S3 bucket.
cis_aws_4.4.remediation: Enable server access logging on the S3 bucket used for CloudTrail logs via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Configure logging to a separate S3 bucket with appropriate retention policies. Use bucket policies to ensure logging cannot be disabled and monitor access logs for suspicious activity.

cis_aws_4.5.description: AWS CloudTrail is a web service that records AWS API calls for an account and makes those logs available to users and resources in accordance with IAM policies. AWS Key Management Service (KMS) is a managed service that helps create and control the encryption keys used to encrypt account data, and uses Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) to protect the security of encryption keys. CloudTrail logs can be configured to leverage server side encryption (SSE) and KMS customer-created master keys (CMK) to encrypt logs at rest.
cis_aws_4.5.remediation: Enable KMS encryption for CloudTrail logs by specifying a customer-managed CMK when creating or updating the trail. Use IAM policies to restrict key usage and enable key rotation. Implement this via CloudFormation or Terraform to ensure encryption is applied across all trails and regions.

cis_aws_4.6.description: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) allows customers to rotate the backing key, which is key material stored within the KMS that is tied to the key ID of the customer-created customer master key (CMK). The backing key is used to perform cryptographic operations such as encryption and decryption. Automated key rotation currently retains all prior backing keys so that decryption of encrypted data can occur transparently. It is recommended that CMK key rotation be enabled for symmetric keys.
cis_aws_4.6.remediation: Enable automatic key rotation for symmetric CMKs in KMS via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Set a rotation schedule (e.g., annually) and monitor key usage with CloudTrail. Use AWS Config rules to detect non-compliant keys and automate remediation scripts.

cis_aws_4.7.description: VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. After you've created a flow log, you can view and retrieve its data in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. It is recommended that VPC Flow Logs be enabled for packet "Rejects" for VPCs.
cis_aws_4.7.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for all VPCs, focusing on reject traffic, using the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Configure logs to be sent to CloudWatch Logs or S3 for analysis. Use AWS Config to ensure flow logs are enabled and set up alerts for unusual traffic patterns.

cis_aws_4.8.description: S3 object-level API operations, such as GetObject, DeleteObject, and PutObject, are referred to as data events. By default, CloudTrail trails do not log data events, so it is recommended to enable object-level logging for S3 buckets.
cis_aws_4.8.remediation: Enable data event logging in CloudTrail for S3 write operations (e.g., PutObject, DeleteObject). Configure this via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC tools. Limit logging to specific buckets to manage costs and use CloudWatch alarms to monitor for unauthorized write activities.

cis_aws_4.9.description: S3 object-level API operations, such as GetObject, DeleteObject, and PutObject, are referred to as data events. By default, CloudTrail trails do not log data events, so it is recommended to enable object-level logging for S3 buckets.
cis_aws_4.9.remediation: Enable data event logging in CloudTrail for S3 read operations (e.g., GetObject). Use the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC to set this up. Apply logging selectively to sensitive buckets and monitor logs with SIEM tools to detect unauthorized access attempts.

cis_aws_5.1.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for unauthorized API calls.
cis_aws_5.1.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for error codes like "AccessDenied" or "UnauthorizedOperation" and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config or IaC to automate this monitoring and integrate with incident response tools.

cis_aws_5.1.1.description: Scan images being deployed to Amazon EKS for vulnerabilities.
cis_aws_5.1.1.remediation: Enable image scanning on Amazon ECR repositories to scan on push. Use third-party tools like Trivy or Clair for additional scanning. Integrate scanning into CI/CD pipelines and set up alerts for high-severity vulnerabilities to block deployment of insecure images.

cis_aws_5.1.2.description: Common Internet File System (CIFS) is a network file-sharing protocol that allows systems to share files over a network. However, unrestricted CIFS access can expose your data to unauthorized users, leading to potential security risks. It is important to restrict CIFS access to only trusted networks and users to prevent unauthorized access and data breaches.
cis_aws_5.1.2.remediation: Configure security groups and NACLs to restrict CIFS traffic (ports 139/445) to trusted IP ranges. Use IAM policies and network segmentation to limit access. Regularly audit network configurations with AWS Config and VPC Flow Logs to detect and remediate open CIFS ports.

cis_aws_5.10.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Security groups are stateful packet filters that control ingress and egress traffic within a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established to detect changes to security groups.
cis_aws_5.10.remediation: Set up CloudTrail logging to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for security group events like AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress and configure a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config rules to monitor security group configurations and automate alerts for unauthorized changes.

cis_aws_5.11.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. NACLs are used as a stateless packet filter to control ingress and egress traffic for subnets within a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for any changes made to NACLs.
cis_aws_5.11.remediation: Enable CloudTrail integration with CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Define a metric filter for NACL-related API calls such as CreateNetworkAclEntry and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use IaC tools to enforce NACL policies and regularly review changes with AWS Config.

cis_aws_5.12.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Network gateways are required to send and receive traffic to a destination outside of a VPC. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to network gateways.
cis_aws_5.12.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for events like CreateVpnGateway or DeleteVpnGateway and set up a CloudWatch alarm to notify on changes. Use AWS Config rules or IaC tools like Terraform to automate this setup across accounts.

cis_aws_5.13.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. Routing tables are used to route network traffic between subnets and to network gateways. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to route tables.
cis_aws_5.13.remediation: Set up CloudTrail integration with CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Define a metric filter for API calls such as CreateRoute or ReplaceRoute and configure a CloudWatch alarm for alerts. Implement this using AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to ensure consistency in multi-region deployments.

cis_aws_5.14.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is possible to have more than one VPC within an account; additionally, it is also possible to create a peer connection between two VPCs, enabling network traffic to route between them. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes made.
cis_aws_5.14.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging to CloudWatch Logs or a SIEM. Create a metric filter for VPC-related events like CreateVpc or ModifyVpcAttribute and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config or IaC scripts to automate this monitoring across all VPCs and regions.

cis_aws_5.15.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms.
