It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes made to AWS Organizations in the master AWS account.
cis_aws_5.15.remediation: Configure CloudTrail in the master account to log Organizations API calls. Set up a metric filter for events like CreateOrganization or InviteAccountToOrganization and create a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Organizations policies and IaC to enforce this monitoring setup.

cis_aws_5.16.description: Security Hub collects security data from various AWS accounts, services, and supported third-party partner products, helping you analyze your security trends and identify the highest-priority security issues. When you enable Security Hub, it begins to consume, aggregate, organize, and prioritize findings from the AWS services that you have enabled, such as Amazon GuardDuty, Amazon Inspector, and Amazon Macie. You can also enable integrations with AWS partner security products.
cis_aws_5.16.remediation: Enable AWS Security Hub in all regions using the AWS Management Console, CLI, or SDK. Integrate it with services like GuardDuty and Inspector. Use AWS Organizations to automate enabling Security Hub across member accounts and set up compliance checks via AWS Config.

cis_aws_5.2.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for console logins that are not protected by multi-factor authentication (MFA).
cis_aws_5.2.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs and create a metric filter for console sign-in events without MFA. Set up a CloudWatch alarm to trigger notifications for such events. Use AWS Config rules or third-party CSPM tools to automate detection and enforce this monitoring across all accounts.

cis_aws_5.3.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for 'root' login attempts to detect unauthorized use or attempts to use the root account.
cis_aws_5.3.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging and integrate with CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter to capture root account usage events and configure a CloudWatch alarm for alerts. Implement IAM policies to restrict root access and use AWS Organizations SCPs to enforce monitoring across all member accounts.

cis_aws_5.3.1.description: Encrypt Kubernetes secrets, stored in etcd, using secrets encryption feature during Amazon EKS cluster creation.
cis_aws_5.3.1.remediation: When creating an EKS cluster, enable encryption for secrets using AWS KMS Customer Master Keys (CMKs). Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or CloudFormation to enforce this setting. Regularly audit existing clusters and re-create with encryption if not enabled, ensuring KMS key policies restrict unauthorized access.

cis_aws_5.4.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes made to Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies.
cis_aws_5.4.remediation: Set up CloudTrail to log IAM policy changes and forward logs to CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter for IAM policy modification events and configure a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config managed rules or CSPM platforms to continuously monitor and alert on unauthorized IAM changes.

cis_aws_5.4.2.description: Disable access to the Kubernetes API from outside the node network if it is not required.
cis_aws_5.4.2.remediation: Create EKS clusters with private endpoint enabled and public access disabled using AWS CLI, Console, or IaC. Update existing clusters via AWS API if possible, or re-create with proper settings. Implement network policies and security groups to restrict API access to trusted IP ranges only.

cis_aws_5.4.3.description: Disable public IP addresses for cluster nodes, so that they only have private IP addresses. Private Nodes are nodes with no public IP addresses.
cis_aws_5.4.3.remediation: Configure EKS node groups to use private subnets without public IP assignment. Use Terraform or CloudFormation to enforce this during cluster creation. For existing clusters, modify node group settings or migrate nodes to private subnets, ensuring proper NAT gateway configuration for outbound internet access.

cis_aws_5.4.4.description: Amazon EKS provides two ways to implement network policy. You choose a network policy option when you create an EKS cluster. The policy option can't be changed after the cluster is created: Calico Network Policies, an open-source network and network security solution founded by Tigera. Both implementations use Linux IPTables to enforce the specified policies. Policies are translated into sets of allowed and disallowed IP pairs. These pairs are then programmed as IPTable filter rules.
cis_aws_5.4.4.remediation: Enable network policy during EKS cluster creation by selecting Calico or AWS VPC CNI plugin with network policy support. Use IaC to enforce this configuration. For existing clusters, consider re-creating with network policy enabled and apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources to define least-privilege pod-to-pod communication.

cis_aws_5.5.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be used to detect changes to CloudTrail's configurations.
cis_aws_5.5.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to log its own configuration changes and send logs to CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter for CloudTrail modification events and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config rules to monitor CloudTrail settings and enforce compliance via automated remediation scripts or CSPM tools.

cis_aws_5.6.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for failed console authentication attempts.
cis_aws_5.6.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging for console sign-in events and integrate with CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter to capture authentication failures and configure a CloudWatch alarm. Implement IAM policies with strong password requirements and MFA to reduce failed attempts, and use SIEM tools for advanced threat detection.

cis_aws_5.7.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for customer-created CMKs that have changed state to disabled or are scheduled for deletion.
cis_aws_5.7.remediation: Set up CloudTrail to log KMS key state changes and forward logs to CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter for key disabling or deletion events and configure a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Config rules to monitor KMS key status and enforce key rotation policies, ensuring backup keys are available to prevent data loss.

cis_aws_5.8.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to S3 bucket policies.
cis_aws_5.8.remediation: Enable CloudTrail logging for S3 bucket policy changes and send logs to CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter for these events and set up a CloudWatch alarm. Use S3 bucket policies with least-privilege access and AWS Config rules to detect and revert unauthorized policy modifications automatically.

cis_aws_5.9.description: Real-time monitoring of API calls can be achieved by directing CloudTrail Logs to CloudWatch Logs or an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) environment, and establishing corresponding metric filters and alarms. It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for detecting changes to AWS Config's configurations.
cis_aws_5.9.remediation: Configure CloudTrail to log AWS Config configuration changes and integrate with CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter for Config modification events and configure a CloudWatch alarm. Use AWS Organizations to enforce Config rules across all accounts and implement automated remediation via Lambda functions or CSPM platforms.

cis_aws_6.1.description: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery is a service that enables you to create and maintain backups of your workloads on AWS, particularly your servers. This service is crucial for ensuring high resilience for your AWS workloads. It operates by establishing and maintaining backups in selected AWS regions, guaranteeing that your data is safe, durable, and highly available in the event of issues in the primary availability zone or region where your AWS server is located.
cis_aws_6.1.remediation: Enable AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery for critical EC2 instances and configure replication to a secondary region. Use IaC to define recovery plans and automate failover testing. Regularly test disaster recovery procedures and monitor replication status via CloudWatch metrics to ensure RPO and RTO objectives are met.

cis_aws_6.1.1.description: Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) supports encryption at rest when using the Elastic Block Store (EBS) service. While disabled by default, forcing encryption at EBS volume creation is supported.
cis_aws_6.1.1.remediation: Enable EBS default encryption in all AWS regions using the AWS Management Console, CLI, or IaC tools like Terraform. Use AWS KMS CMKs for encryption and enforce this via SCPs. For existing unencrypted volumes, create encrypted snapshots and restore them, or use third-party tools to encrypt in place.

cis_aws_6.1.2.description: Common Internet File System (CIFS) is a network file-sharing protocol that allows systems to share files over a network. However, unrestricted CIFS access can expose your data to unauthorized users, leading to potential security risks. It is important to restrict CIFS access to only trusted networks and users to prevent unauthorized access and data breaches.
cis_aws_6.1.2.remediation: Configure security groups and NACLs to restrict inbound traffic on TCP port 445 to trusted IP ranges only. Use IAM policies and network segmentation to limit access. Regularly audit CIFS configurations and use AWS Network Firewall or third-party solutions to monitor and block unauthorized access attempts.

cis_aws_6.10.description: Execute a comprehensive disaster recovery failover to transition operations from the primary system to a backup system during disruptions. This process includes ensuring all critical data and applications are accurately replicated to the backup site for seamless operational continuity. Regularly test and document the failover process to identify and resolve any issues, maintaining readiness to minimize downtime and data loss during real disasters.
cis_aws_6.10.remediation: Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery or similar services to automate failover execution. Schedule regular failover tests in a non-production environment and document procedures. Monitor replication health and update recovery plans based on test results to ensure quick recovery during actual disasters.

cis_aws_6.11.description: This method involves transitioning operations back from the backup or recovery system to the primary system after the resolution of a disruption or disaster. You can execute a failback either to the original server, ensuring continuity and restoring the previous state, or to a new server, which might be necessary if the original server is compromised or no longer functional. The failback process ensures that all updated data and configurations are transferred back, maintaining the integrity and
cis_aws_6.11.remediation: Develop and document a manual failback procedure that includes steps to validate data integrity and synchronize changes from the recovery environment. Train staff on executing failback during disaster recovery drills. Use AWS services like Elastic Disaster Recovery to automate parts of the process, but ensure manual oversight to prevent data loss.

cis_aws_6.12.description: Set up and monitor AWS CloudWatch metrics for Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) to track and analyze the performance and security of your AWS environment. This involves configuring CloudWatch to collect detailed logs and metrics on EDR activities, such as threat detections, response actions, and system health. Regularly review these metrics to identify trends, anomalies, and potential security issues, enabling proactive management and timely responses to ensure the effectiveness of your EDR
cis_aws_6.12.remediation: Configure EDR solutions to send logs to CloudWatch Logs and create custom metrics for key events like threat detections. Set up CloudWatch dashboards and alarms for anomalous activity. Use AWS Config or third-party SIEM tools to correlate EDR data with other security logs for comprehensive monitoring.

cis_aws_6.13.description: Ensure Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions are operational and effectively monitoring endpoints for threats.
cis_aws_6.13.remediation: Deploy EDR agents on all EC2 instances and ensure they are updated and communicating with the management console. Use AWS Systems Manager to automate agent deployment and health checks. Regularly test EDR functionality by simulating threats and verifying alerts and response actions are triggered as expected.

cis_aws_6.2.description: The Network Access Control List (NACL) function provides stateless filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that no NACL allows unrestricted ingress access to remote server administration ports, such as SSH on port 22 and RDP on port 3389, using either the TCP (6), UDP (17), or ALL (-1) protocols.
cis_aws_6.2.remediation: Review all NACL rules and remove any that allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to ports 22 and 3389. Use IaC to enforce least-privilege NACL configurations. Implement security groups for stateful filtering and regularly audit NACLs using AWS Config or CSPM tools to detect and remediate misconfigurations automatically.

cis_aws_6.3.description: Security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to remote server administration ports, such as SSH on port 22 and RDP on port 3389, using either the TCP (6), UDP (17), or ALL (-1) protocols.
cis_aws_6.3.remediation: Audit all security groups and remove rules that allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to ports 22 and 3389. Use IaC to define security groups with least-privilege access. Implement AWS Security Hub or third-party CSPM platforms to continuously monitor and alert on open admin ports, enabling quick remediation.

cis_aws_6.4.description: Security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to remote server administration ports, such as SSH on port 22 and RDP on port 3389.
cis_aws_6.4.remediation: Check security group rules for IPv6 ingress from ::/0 to ports 22 and 3389, and remove any such rules. Use IaC to enforce IPv6 restrictions. Regularly scan security groups with AWS Config or CSPM tools to detect and fix misconfigurations, ensuring both IPv4 and IPv6 traffic are properly controlled.

cis_aws_6.5.description: A VPC comes with a default security group whose initial settings deny all inbound traffic, allow all outbound traffic, and allow all traffic between instances assigned to the security group. If a security group is not specified when an instance is launched, it is automatically assigned to this default security group. Security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that the default security group restrict all traffic, both inbound a
cis_aws_6.5.remediation: Modify the default security group in each VPC to deny all inbound and outbound traffic. Use AWS CLI, Console, or IaC to apply these rules. Ensure new instances are assigned to custom security groups with least-privilege rules. Use AWS Config rules to monitor and enforce this configuration across all VPCs.

cis_aws_6.6.description: Once a VPC peering connection is established, routing tables must be updated to enable any connections between the peered VPCs. These routes can be as specific as desired, even allowing for the peering of a VPC to only a single host on the other side of the connection.
cis_aws_6.6.remediation: Configure VPC peering routing tables with specific CIDR blocks or host routes instead of allowing entire VPC ranges. Use IaC to define least-access routes and regularly audit routing tables. Implement network segmentation and use AWS Transit Gateway for centralized routing management to minimize exposure.

cis_aws_6.7.description: When enabling the Metadata Service on AWS EC2 instances, users have the option of using either Instance Metadata Service Version 1 (IMDSv1; a request/response method) or Instance Metadata Service Version 2 (IMDSv2; a session-oriented method).
cis_aws_6.7.remediation: Enforce IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances by setting the metadata version to v2-only during launch. Use IaC templates like AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to apply this configuration. For existing instances, use AWS Systems Manager to update IMDS settings and disable IMDSv1 to prevent SSRF attacks and unauthorized metadata access.

cis_aws_6.8.description: To ensure your organization is prepared for a disaster, it's crucial to verify that your disaster recovery services function as expected. Your IT team should conduct regular recovery drills on your AWS Elastic Recovery Instance to confirm everything operates smoothly and according to plan.
cis_aws_6.8.remediation: Schedule and execute regular recovery drills using AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) or similar services. Automate these drills via AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to ensure consistency and test failover processes. Document outcomes and update recovery plans based on drill results to maintain readiness.

cis_aws_6.9.description: Maintain ongoing disaster recovery operations to ensure that systems and data can be swiftly restored in the event of a disruption. This involves regularly updating and testing recovery plans, monitoring replication processes, and verifying the integrity and accessibility of backups. Continuously evaluate and improve disaster recovery strategies to adapt to evolving threats and organizational changes, ensuring resilience and minimal downtime during incidents.
cis_aws_6.9.remediation: Enable automated backup replication in AWS Backup and set up monitoring with Amazon CloudWatch to track replication status. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like AWS CloudFormation to define and manage backup policies. Regularly review and test recovery procedures to ensure they meet business continuity requirements.

cis_aws_7.1.description: Plan the network architecture to isolate your DocumentDB instances within a secure Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). Configure appropriate security groups and network access control lists (ACLs) to control inbound and outbound traffic to your DocumentDB instances.
cis_aws_7.1.remediation: Deploy DocumentDB clusters within a VPC using AWS CloudFormation or Terraform, ensuring they are placed in private subnets. Configure security groups to restrict access to specific IP ranges and ports, and enable VPC flow logs for monitoring. Regularly audit network configurations to prevent exposure to the internet.

cis_aws_7.10.description: Configure a backup window for DocumentDB clusters to schedule automated backups during low-traffic periods, ensuring data durability without impacting performance.
cis_aws_7.10.remediation: Set the preferred backup window in AWS DocumentDB via the AWS Management Console, CLI, or IaC tools like CloudFormation. Align the window with off-peak hours to minimize disruption. Monitor backup completion using Amazon CloudWatch alarms to ensure reliability.

cis_aws_7.11.description: Periodically perform security assessments, including vulnerability assessments and penetration testing, to identify and address any security weaknesses. Review your security configuration against best practices and industry standards.
cis_aws_7.11.remediation: Establish a schedule for manual security assessments using tools like AWS Inspector or third-party scanners. Document findings and remediation steps in a risk register. Implement a process for regular configuration reviews and update security policies based on assessment results.

cis_aws_7.2.description: Creating a VPC, configuring subnets, and creating security groups help isolate your DocumentDB instances within your virtual network and control inbound and outbound traffic.
cis_aws_7.2.remediation: Use AWS CloudFormation or Terraform to provision VPCs, subnets, and security groups for DocumentDB. Ensure instances are in private subnets with strict security group rules. Enable VPC flow logs and regularly review network configurations to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_aws_7.3.description: Enable encryption at rest for DocumentDB clusters to protect stored data from unauthorized access using AWS Key Management Service (KMS).
cis_aws_7.3.remediation: Enable encryption at rest during DocumentDB cluster creation via the AWS Console, CLI, or IaC. Use AWS KMS customer-managed keys for greater control. Ensure all existing clusters are encrypted and audit encryption settings regularly with AWS Config.

cis_aws_7.4.description: Enable encryption in transit for DocumentDB clusters to secure data during transmission between clients and the database using TLS/SSL.
cis_aws_7.4.remediation: Configure DocumentDB to enforce TLS/SSL encryption for all connections using AWS parameters. Update client applications to use encrypted endpoints. Monitor for unencrypted connections with AWS CloudTrail and Amazon GuardDuty alerts.

cis_aws_7.5.description: Configure authentication mechanisms for your DocumentDB instances, such as using AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) users or database users. Define appropriate user roles and permissions to control access to the DocumentDB instances and databases.
cis_aws_7.5.remediation: Enable IAM database authentication for DocumentDB and assign least-privilege IAM roles. Use AWS Secrets Manager to manage database credentials. Regularly audit IAM policies and user permissions with AWS IAM Access Analyzer.

cis_aws_7.6.description: Enable audit logging to capture database activities, including login attempts, queries, and modifications. Send the logs to Amazon CloudWatch or a centralized log management system for analysis and monitoring.
cis_aws_7.6.remediation: Enable audit logging in DocumentDB and configure export to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Use AWS CloudFormation to automate logging setup. Set up alerts for suspicious activities and retain logs according to compliance requirements.

cis_aws_7.7.description: Stay informed about the latest security updates and patches released by Amazon for DocumentDB. Regularly apply updates and patches to your DocumentDB instances to protect against known vulnerabilities.
cis_aws_7.7.remediation: Enable automatic minor version upgrades for DocumentDB clusters. Schedule maintenance windows for major updates using AWS Management Console or CLI. Monitor AWS security bulletins and use AWS Systems Manager for patch management.

cis_aws_7.8.description: This helps by alerting the system if any unusual event has occurred or if a particular threshold has been achieved because the user is able to set a desired interval or the cluster. This then allows system administrators to swiftly correct the situation and avoid subsequent complications if something unusual is happening.
cis_aws_7.8.remediation: Configure Amazon CloudWatch alarms for DocumentDB metrics like CPU utilization and connection counts. Use AWS EventBridge to trigger automated responses. Set up notifications via Amazon SNS for critical alerts to enable quick remediation.

cis_aws_7.9.description: Set up automated backups for your DocumentDB instances to ensure data durability and recoverability. Consider implementing a disaster recovery plan that includes data replication across different availability zones or regions.
cis_aws_7.9.remediation: Enable automated backups with a retention period in DocumentDB using AWS CloudFormation. Implement cross-region replication for disaster recovery. Regularly test backup restoration processes to ensure data recoverability.

cis_aws_8.1.description: To access Amazon Keyspaces, the user would be required to log in with their AWS credentials. Once logged in the user can access the AWS resources and can explore the resources that Amazon Keyspaces offers. Amazon Keyspaces offers a lot of security that can mitigate a potential attack.
cis_aws_8.1.remediation: Configure IAM policies with least privilege for Amazon Keyspaces access. Use AWS CloudTrail to log API calls. Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for IAM users and regularly review access permissions.

cis_aws_8.2.description: In order to access Amazon Keyspaces the user is required to set specific networking parameters and security measurements without these extra steps they will not be able to access it. Users are required to create or select a virtual private cloud (VPC) and define their inbound and outbound rules accordingly.
cis_aws_8.2.remediation: Deploy Amazon Keyspaces within a VPC using private subnets. Configure security groups and network ACLs to restrict traffic. Use AWS CloudFormation to automate network setup and enable VPC flow logs for monitoring.

cis_aws_8.3.description: Once a user is logged in to their AWS account and has access to their Amazon Keyspaces they are encouraged to choose from the following two options to encrypt their data. Depending on which key they select for encryption at rest would store the data according to their preference. For encryption in transit the user is also encouraged to choose from two options depending on if the data needs to be encrypted during transit.
cis_aws_8.3.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for Amazon Keyspaces using AWS KMS customer-managed keys. Enforce TLS/SSL for encryption in transit. Use AWS Config rules to audit encryption settings and ensure compliance.

cis_aws_9.1.description: This helps ensure that all the necessary security measurements are taken to prevent a cyber-attack. Such as utilizing VPC, creating certain inbound and outbound rules, and ACLs.
cis_aws_9.1.remediation: Deploy Neptune clusters within a VPC in private subnets. Configure security groups to allow only necessary traffic. Use AWS CloudFormation to automate network security setup and enable VPC flow logs.

cis_aws_9.2.description: This helps ensure that the data is kept secure and protected when at rest. The user must choose from two key options which then determine when the data is encrypted at rest.
cis_aws_9.2.remediation: Enable encryption at rest for Neptune clusters using AWS KMS during creation. Use customer-managed keys for enhanced control. Audit encryption settings with AWS Config and ensure all clusters are encrypted.

cis_aws_9.3.description: Enabling encryption in transit helps that the data is protected when it is moving from one location to another.
cis_aws_9.3.remediation: Configure Neptune to enforce SSL/TLS encryption for all client connections. Update applications to use encrypted endpoints. Monitor for unencrypted traffic with AWS CloudTrail and set up alerts.

cis_aws_9.4.description: This helps ensure that there are specific IAM roles and policies that are given the necessary information within a Neptune DB cluster to operate as needed.
cis_aws_9.4.remediation: Enable IAM database authentication for Neptune and assign least-privilege IAM roles. Use AWS Secrets Manager for credential management. Regularly review IAM policies with AWS IAM Access Analyzer.

cis_aws_9.5.description: This control is important because it helps ensure activity within the cluster and identifies who has last modified the document and who has access to it, in case of breaches. It also ensures compliance with regulation requirements.
cis_aws_9.5.remediation: Enable audit logging for Neptune and export logs to Amazon CloudWatch or S3. Use AWS CloudFormation to automate logging configuration. Set up alerts for suspicious activities and retain logs as per compliance needs.

cis_aws_9.6.description: This helps by removing or updating any IAM roles, security networks, encryption settings, audit logging, and authentication. By updating or removing a few things from these lists it helps tighten security and ensures that the users do not have excessive permissions.
cis_aws_9.6.remediation: Schedule regular security reviews using AWS Config and IAM Access Analyzer. Automate checks with AWS Security Hub. Update configurations based on findings and enforce least privilege principles across all settings.

cis_aws_9.7.description: Enable monitoring and alerting for Neptune clusters to detect performance issues and security threats using Amazon CloudWatch.
cis_aws_9.7.remediation: Configure CloudWatch alarms for Neptune metrics like CPU and memory usage. Use AWS EventBridge for automated responses. Set up SNS notifications for critical alerts to enable prompt incident response.

cis_azure_10.1.description: Customer-managed keys (CMK) for Azure NetApp Files volume encryption enable organizations to use their own keys instead of platform-managed ones, providing full control over encryption.
cis_azure_10.1.remediation: Configure Azure NetApp Files to use customer-managed keys via Azure Key Vault. Use Azure Policy to enforce CMK usage. Regularly audit encryption settings with Azure Monitor and ensure key rotation policies are in place.

cis_azure_10.2.description: Ensure that no SQL Databases allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 (ANY IP).
cis_azure_10.2.remediation: Restrict SQL Database firewall rules to specific IP ranges and remove any 0.0.0.0/0 entries. Use Azure Policy to enforce network security. Enable Azure Defender for SQL to detect and alert on open access configurations.

cis_azure_10.3.description: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) with Customer-managed key support provides increased transparency and control over the TDE Protector, increased security with an HSM-backed external service, and promotion of separation of duties. With TDE, data is encrypted at rest with a symmetric key (called the database encryption key) stored in the database or data warehouse distribution. To protect this data encryption key (DEK) in the past, only a certificate that the Azure SQL Service managed could be us
cis_azure_10.3.remediation: Configure TDE to use a customer-managed key (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault instead of the default service-managed key. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates or Terraform to enforce this setting across all SQL servers. Ensure proper RBAC permissions for key access and enable key rotation policies in Key Vault.

cis_azure_10.4.description: Use Microsoft Entra authentication for authentication with SQL Database to manage credentials in a single place.
cis_azure_10.4.remediation: Enable Microsoft Entra authentication on SQL servers by configuring an Entra admin and disabling SQL authentication. Use IaC to automate this setup and enforce RBAC roles for database access. Regularly audit authentication methods to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_10.5.description: Enable Transparent Data Encryption on every SQL server.
cis_azure_10.5.remediation: Turn on TDE for all SQL databases via the Azure portal, PowerShell, or CLI. Implement IaC policies to automatically enable TDE on new databases. Monitor encryption status using Azure Policy or CSPM tools to ensure no databases are unencrypted.

cis_azure_10.6.description: SQL Server Audit Retention should be configured to be greater than 90 days.
cis_azure_10.6.remediation: Set audit retention to at least 91 days in SQL Server auditing settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce this retention period across all SQL servers. Store audit logs in a secure storage account with encryption and access controls.

cis_azure_10.7.description: Disabling public network access restricts the service from accessing public networks.
cis_azure_10.7.remediation: Disable public network access on SQL servers via the Azure portal or IaC. Use private endpoints and VNet integration for secure connectivity. Implement network security groups and firewall rules to block public internet access.

cis_azure_11.1.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
cis_azure_11.1.remediation: Configure SAS tokens to allow only HTTPS protocols. Use Azure Policy or storage account settings to enforce this. In IaC, specify HTTPS-only for SAS generation and regularly audit SAS configurations.

cis_azure_11.2.description: Shared access signature (SAS) tokens provide restricted access to Azure Storage resources (such as blobs, files, queues, or tables) for a defined time period with specific permissions. It enables users to interact with the resources without exposing account keys, offering precise control over the permitted actions (e.g., read, write) and the duration of access. To minimize security risks, SAS tokens should be configured with the shortest possible lifespan, ideally lasting no longer than an hour.
cis_azure_11.2.remediation: Set SAS token expiration to one hour or less when generating tokens. Use stored access policies to manage expiration centrally. Implement automated scripts or CSPM tools to monitor and alert on long-lived SAS tokens.

cis_azure_11.3.description: Blobs in Azure storage accounts may contain sensitive or personal data, such as ePHI or financial information. Data that is erroneously modified or deleted by an application or a user can lead to data loss or unavailability. It is recommended that soft delete be enabled on Azure storage accounts with blob storage to allow for the preservation and recovery of data when blobs or blob snapshots are deleted.
cis_azure_11.3.remediation: Enable soft delete for blobs in storage account settings. Configure retention periods appropriate for compliance needs. Use IaC to enforce soft delete on all storage accounts and regularly test data recovery processes.

cis_azure_11.4.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
cis_azure_11.4.remediation: Create stored access policies for containers and apply them to SAS tokens instead of ad-hoc settings. Use Azure CLI or PowerShell to manage policies. Enforce this via Azure Policy and audit SAS usage to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_11.5.description: Enabling blob versioning allows for the automatic retention of previous versions of objects. With blob versioning enabled, earlier versions of a blob are accessible for data recovery in the event of modifications or deletions.
cis_azure_11.5.remediation: Enable blob versioning on storage accounts through the Azure portal or IaC. Set lifecycle management policies to manage version retention costs. Monitor versioning status and ensure it's applied to all critical storage accounts.

cis_azure_11.6.description: Require locked immutability policies for all containers that store business-critical blob data. This measure protects the data from modifications or deletions, ensuring that critical information remains intact and unaltered, regardless of user actions or access permissions.
cis_azure_11.6.remediation: Configure locked immutability policies on containers holding critical data using Azure Blob Storage immutable storage. Use IaC to apply policies during container creation. Ensure proper RBAC to prevent unauthorized policy changes and regularly audit compliance.

cis_azure_12.1.description: Enabling double encryption on Azure Data Box applies an additional layer of encryption to safeguard data during physical transfer. This approach enhances confidentiality and integrity, ensuring that sensitive information remains secure against unauthorized access if the device is lost, stolen, or intercepted.
cis_azure_12.1.remediation: Enable double encryption for Azure Data Box orders in high-security environments via the Azure portal. Use customer-managed keys for both encryption layers. Document and enforce this procedure for all data transfer operations.

cis_azure_15.1.description: Azure Elastic SAN is a scalable, high-performance cloud-based storage solution. Disabling public network access at the SAN level ensures that Elastic SAN resources are accessible only through private networks.
cis_azure_15.1.remediation: Disable public network access on Elastic SAN resources using Azure portal or IaC. Configure private endpoints for secure access. Use network security controls to restrict traffic to approved virtual networks.

cis_azure_15.2.description: Azure Elastic SAN volume groups offer two encryption options: Microsoft-managed keys, which provide automatic encryption without user intervention, and customer- managed keys (CMK), which allow organizations to retain full control over their encryption keys for enhanced security and compliance.
cis_azure_15.2.remediation: Configure Elastic SAN volume groups to use customer-managed keys (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault. Use IaC to enforce CMK encryption on all volume groups. Implement key rotation and access policies in Key Vault.

cis_azure_15.3.description: This recommendation disables local authentication and ensures that a centralized identity provider is used.
cis_azure_15.3.remediation: Disable local authentication methods on Batch accounts and enable Microsoft Entra authentication. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration. Regularly review authentication settings to ensure only centralized identity providers are used.

cis_azure_15.4.description: Private endpoints for Azure Batch accounts ensure all network communication occurs over private networks rather than the public internet.
cis_azure_15.4.remediation: Create private endpoints for Batch accounts and connect them to your VNet. Use IaC to automate private endpoint deployment. Configure network security groups to restrict access and validate connectivity.

cis_azure_15.5.description: Disabling public network access ensures all connectivity occurs through private endpoints or approved virtual networks.
cis_azure_15.5.remediation: Disable public network access on Batch accounts via Azure portal or IaC. Enforce private endpoint usage and block public IP access. Use Azure Policy to audit and remediate any public access configurations.

cis_azure_15.6.description: Private DNS zones for Azure Batch private endpoints provide secure internal name resolution, preventing public internet exposure. When a private endpoint is created for a Batch account, Azure requires a private DNS zone (privatelink..batch.azure.com) to map the Batch service’s domain name to a private IP address within your virtual network (VNet).
cis_azure_15.6.remediation: Configure private DNS zones for Batch private endpoints in your VNet. Use Azure DNS private zones or on-premises DNS integration. Automate this setup with IaC and verify DNS resolution to prevent exposure.

cis_azure_15.7.description: Azure Batch resource logs give important operational data such as job scheduling, pool management, and node communication. Having these logs enabled is necessary for monitoring, troubleshooting, and compliance auditing.
cis_azure_15.7.remediation: Enable diagnostics settings for Batch accounts to send logs to Log Analytics or storage. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging across all Batch accounts. Configure log retention and encryption for audit compliance.

cis_azure_16.1.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
cis_azure_16.1.remediation: Set SAS tokens to allow only HTTPS protocols in storage account configurations. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Regularly audit SAS usage and revoke any tokens allowing HTTP.

cis_azure_16.2.description: Shared access signature (SAS) tokens provide restricted access to Azure Storage resources (such as blobs, files, queues, or tables) for a defined time period with specific permissions. It enables users to interact with the resources without exposing account keys, offering precise control over the permitted actions (e.g., read, write) and the duration of access. To minimize security risks, SAS tokens should be configured with the shortest possible lifespan, ideally lasting no longer than an hour.
cis_azure_16.2.remediation: Configure SAS token expiration to one hour or less. Use stored access policies to manage expiration centrally. Implement monitoring to detect and alert on long-lived SAS tokens.

cis_azure_16.3.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
cis_azure_16.3.remediation: Create and use stored access policies for SAS tokens instead of ad-hoc settings. Apply policies via Azure CLI or PowerShell. Enforce this with Azure Policy and regularly audit SAS configurations.

cis_azure_17.1.1.description: Access Keys authenticate application access requests to data contained in Storage Accounts. A periodic rotation of these keys is recommended to ensure that potentially compromised keys cannot result in a long-term exploitable credential. The "Rotation Reminder" is an automatic reminder feature for a manual procedure.
cis_azure_17.1.1.remediation: Enable key rotation reminders in storage account settings. Establish a manual rotation procedure and schedule regular key rotations. Use Azure Monitor or CSPM tools to track reminders and ensure compliance.

cis_azure_17.1.2.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
cis_azure_17.1.2.remediation: Configure SAS tokens to allow only HTTPS protocols in storage account settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce this. Audit SAS configurations regularly and revoke any non-compliant tokens.

cis_azure_17.1.3.description: For increased security, regenerate storage account access keys periodically.
cis_azure_17.1.3.remediation: Implement a key rotation policy to regenerate storage account access keys at regular intervals (e.g., every 90 days). Use Azure Key Vault or automation scripts to manage rotation. Monitor key age and enforce rotation via Azure Policy.

cis_azure_17.1.4.description: Shared access signature (SAS) tokens provide restricted access to Azure Storage resources (such as blobs, files, queues, or tables) for a defined time period with specific permissions. It enables users to interact with the resources without exposing account keys, offering precise control over the permitted actions (e.g., read, write) and the duration of access. To minimize security risks, SAS tokens should be configured with the shortest possible lifespan, ideally lasting no longer than an hour.
cis_azure_17.1.4.remediation: Configure SAS tokens with an expiration time of one hour or less using Azure CLI, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Implement stored access policies for centralized management and revocation. Use Azure Policy to enforce token expiration limits across storage accounts.

cis_azure_17.1.5.description: Every secure request to an Azure Storage account must be authorized. By default, requests can be authorized with either Microsoft Entra credentials or by using the account access key for Shared Key authorization.
cis_azure_17.1.5.remediation: Disable 'Allow storage account key access' via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates to enforce Microsoft Entra ID authentication. Use Azure Policy to audit and enforce this setting. Implement RBAC roles for access control instead of shared keys.

cis_azure_17.1.6.description: Enable sensitive data encryption at rest using Customer Managed Keys (CMK) rather than Microsoft Managed keys.
cis_azure_17.1.6.remediation: Configure storage accounts to use CMK for encryption via Azure Key Vault. Use ARM templates or Azure CLI to enable this setting. Implement Azure Policy to enforce CMK usage and regularly audit key rotation and access policies.

cis_azure_17.10.description: Azure Table storage is a service that stores structured NoSQL data in the cloud, providing a key/attribute store with a schema-less design. Storage Logging happens server-side and allows details for both successful and failed requests to be recorded in the storage account. These logs allow users to see the details of read, write, and delete operations against the tables. Storage Logging log entries contain the following information about individual requests: timing information such as start time
cis_azure_17.10.remediation: Enable storage logging for Table service with read, write, and delete operations via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure log retention policies and integrate with Azure Monitor for analysis. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging across all storage accounts.

cis_azure_17.11.description: In some cases, Azure Storage sets the minimum TLS version to be version 1.0 by default. TLS 1.0 is a legacy version and has known vulnerabilities. This minimum TLS version can be configured to be later protocols such as TLS 1.2.
cis_azure_17.11.remediation: Set the minimum TLS version to 1.2 for storage accounts using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration. Regularly audit TLS settings and disable older protocols to prevent downgrade attacks.

cis_azure_17.12.description: Cross Tenant Replication in Azure allows data to be replicated across multiple Azure tenants. While this feature can be beneficial for data sharing and availability, it also poses a significant security risk if not properly managed. Unauthorized data access, data leakage, and compliance violations are potential risks. Disabling Cross Tenant Replication ensures that data is not inadvertently replicated across different tenant boundaries without explicit authorization.
cis_azure_17.12.remediation: Disable Cross Tenant Replication in storage account settings via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to audit and enforce this configuration. Implement network rules and private endpoints to restrict data access within the tenant.

cis_azure_17.13.description: The Azure Storage setting ‘Allow Blob Anonymous Access’ (aka "allowBlobPublicAccess") controls whether anonymous access is allowed for blob data in a storage account. When this property is set to True, it enables public read access to blob data, which can be convenient for sharing data but may carry security risks. When set to False, it disallows public access to blob data, providing a more secure storage environment.
cis_azure_17.13.remediation: Set 'Allow Blob Anonymous Access' to Disabled via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting across all storage accounts. Implement private endpoints and network access controls to restrict blob access to authorized users only.

cis_azure_17.14.description: Azure Resource Manager CannotDelete (Delete) locks can prevent users from accidentally or maliciously deleting a storage account. This feature ensures that while the Storage account can still be modified or used, deletion of the Storage account resource requires removal of the lock by a user with appropriate permissions. This feature is a protective control for the availability of data. By ensuring that a storage account or its parent resource group cannot be deleted without first removing the l
cis_azure_17.14.remediation: Apply CannotDelete locks to critical storage accounts using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce lock application. Ensure RBAC permissions are configured so only authorized users can remove locks.

cis_azure_17.15.description: Adding an Azure Resource Manager ReadOnly lock can prevent users from accidentally or maliciously deleting a storage account, modifying its properties and containers, or creating access assignments. The lock must be removed before the storage account can be deleted or updated. It provides more protection than a CannotDelete-type of resource manager lock. This feature prevents POST operations on a storage account and containers to the Azure Resource Manager control plane, management.azure.com. Bl
cis_azure_17.15.remediation: Consider applying ReadOnly locks to storage accounts for enhanced protection via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to audit lock configurations. Balance security with operational needs by reviewing lock usage regularly.

cis_azure_17.16.description: Geo-redundant storage (GRS) in Azure replicates data three times within the primary region using locally redundant storage (LRS) and asynchronously copies it to a secondary region hundreds of miles away. This setup ensures high availability and resilience by providing 16 nines (99.99999999999999%) durability over a year, safeguarding data against regional outages.
cis_azure_17.16.remediation: Configure redundancy to GRS for critical storage accounts via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Regularly test failover procedures and monitor replication health to ensure data availability.

cis_azure_17.2.1.description: Use private endpoints for your Azure Storage accounts to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote
cis_azure_17.2.1.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for storage accounts using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure network security groups and route tables to restrict access. Use Azure Policy to enforce private endpoint usage and disable public network access.

cis_azure_17.2.2.description: Disallowing public network access for a storage account overrides the public access settings for individual containers in that storage account for Azure Resource Manager Deployment Model storage accounts. Azure Storage accounts that use the classic deployment model will be retired on August 31, 2024.
cis_azure_17.2.2.remediation: Disable public network access for storage accounts via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Implement private endpoints and network rules to allow access only from trusted networks.

cis_azure_17.2.3.description: Restricting default network access helps to provide a new layer of security, since storage accounts accept connections from clients on any network. To limit access to selected networks, the default action must be changed.
cis_azure_17.2.3.remediation: Set the default network access rule to Deny for storage accounts using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure specific IP ranges or virtual networks for allowed access. Use Azure Policy to audit and enforce this rule across all storage accounts.

cis_azure_17.4.remediation: Enable 'Secure transfer required' for storage accounts via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce HTTPS-only traffic. Configure TLS settings and disable insecure protocols to ensure all data transfers are encrypted.

cis_azure_17.5.description: Enabling encryption at the hardware level on top of the default software encryption for Storage Accounts accessing Azure storage solutions.
cis_azure_17.5.remediation: Enable infrastructure encryption for storage accounts using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Ensure CMK is configured for additional control over encryption keys and access policies.

cis_azure_17.6.description: NOTE: This recommendation assumes that the Public network access parameter is set to Enabled from selected virtual networks and IP addresses. Please ensure the prerequisite recommendation has been implemented before proceeding: • Ensure Default Network Access Rule for Storage Accounts is Set to Deny Some Azure services that interact with storage accounts operate from networks that can't be granted access through network rules. To help this type of service work as intended, allow the set of trust
cis_azure_17.6.remediation: Enable 'Allow Azure services on the trusted services list to access this storage account' via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates after setting default network access to Deny. Use Azure Policy to audit this configuration. Regularly review trusted services list to minimize attack surface.

cis_azure_17.7.description: The Azure Storage blobs contain data like ePHI or Financial, which can be secret or personal. Data that is erroneously modified or deleted by an application or other storage account user will cause data loss or unavailability. It is recommended that both Azure Containers with attached Blob Storage and standalone containers with Blob Storage be made recoverable by enabling the soft delete configuration. This is to save and recover data when blobs or blob snapshots are deleted.
cis_azure_17.7.remediation: Enable soft delete for containers and blob storage via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure retention periods appropriate for data sensitivity. Use Azure Policy to enforce soft delete settings and regularly test recovery procedures.

cis_azure_17.8.description: The Storage Queue service stores messages that may be read by any client who has access to the storage account. A queue can contain an unlimited number of messages, each of which can be up to 64KB in size using version 2011-08-18 or newer. Storage Logging happens server-side and allows details for both successful and failed requests to be recorded in the storage account. These logs allow users to see the details of read, write, and delete operations against the queues. Storage Logging log entrie
cis_azure_17.8.remediation: Enable storage logging for Queue service with read, write, and delete operations via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Set up log retention and integration with Azure Monitor. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging across all storage accounts for compliance monitoring.

cis_azure_17.9.description: The Storage Blob service provides scalable, cost-efficient object storage in the cloud. Storage Logging happens server-side and allows details for both successful and failed requests to be recorded in the storage account. These logs allow users to see the details of read, write, and delete operations against the blobs. Storage Logging log entries contain the following information about individual requests: timing information such as start time, end-to-end latency, and server latency; authenticat
cis_azure_17.9.remediation: Enable storage logging for Blob service with read, write, and delete operations via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure log analytics and alerting for suspicious activities. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging and ensure logs are retained for audit purposes.

cis_azure_18.1.description: Shared access signature (SAS) tokens provide restricted access to Azure Storage resources (such as blobs, files, queues, or tables) for a defined time period with specific permissions. It enables users to interact with the resources without exposing account keys, offering precise control over the permitted actions (e.g., read, write) and the duration of access. To minimize security risks, SAS tokens should be configured with the shortest possible lifespan, ideally lasting no longer than an hour.
cis_azure_18.1.remediation: Configure SAS tokens with an expiration time of one hour or less using Azure CLI, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Implement stored access policies for centralized management and revocation. Use Azure Policy to enforce token expiration limits across storage accounts.

cis_azure_18.2.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
cis_azure_18.2.remediation: Generate SAS tokens linked to stored access policies via Azure Portal, CLI, or SDKs. Use ARM templates to define policies for consistency. Implement Azure Policy to enforce SAP usage and regularly review and revoke unnecessary policies.

cis_azure_18.3.description: Ensure all users accessing Azure Storage resources with Storage Explorer are using the latest version of the software, applying updates promptly to safeguard against new vulnerabilities and benefit from the latest security enhancements.
cis_azure_18.3.remediation: Establish a process to regularly update Storage Explorer to the latest version. Use endpoint management tools to enforce updates. Provide training and documentation to users on update procedures and security benefits.

cis_azure_2.1.description: Ensuring that Microsoft Entra Authentication is ‘Enabled’ provides a natively integrated use of identities already defined with Microsoft Entra ID.
cis_azure_2.1.remediation: Enable Microsoft Entra Authentication for Azure Cache for Redis via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Configure RBAC roles and conditional access policies to secure authentication flows.

cis_azure_2.1.1.description: Networking for Azure Databricks can be set up in a few different ways. Using a customer-managed Virtual Network (VNet) (also known as VNet Injection) ensures that compute clusters and control planes are securely isolated within the organization’s network boundary. By default, Databricks creates a managed VNet, which provides limited control over network security policies, firewall configurations, and routing.
cis_azure_2.1.1.remediation: Deploy Azure Databricks in a customer-managed VNet using Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Configure network security groups and route tables for isolation. Use Azure Policy to enforce VNet injection and regularly audit network configurations.

cis_azure_2.1.1.1.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
cis_azure_2.1.1.1.remediation: Set 'Allowed Protocols' to HTTPS Only when generating SAS tokens via Azure Portal, CLI, or SDKs. Use stored access policies to enforce this setting centrally. Implement Azure Policy to audit and enforce HTTPS-only protocols for all SAS tokens.

cis_azure_2.1.1.2.description: Shared access signature (SAS) tokens provide restricted access to Azure Storage resources (such as blobs, files, queues, or tables) for a defined time period with specific permissions. It enables users to interact with the resources without exposing account keys, offering precise control over the permitted actions (e.g., read, write) and the duration of access. To minimize security risks, SAS tokens should be configured with the shortest possible lifespan, ideally lasting no longer than an hour.
cis_azure_2.1.1.2.remediation: Configure SAS tokens with an expiration time of one hour or less using Azure Storage APIs or SDKs. Implement automation in Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or ARM templates to enforce this policy. Regularly audit SAS token usage and revoke any tokens that exceed the allowed lifespan.

cis_azure_2.1.1.3.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
cis_azure_2.1.1.3.remediation: Create stored access policies in Azure Storage accounts to define permissions and expiration centrally. Use these policies when generating SAS tokens via Azure Portal, CLI, or SDKs. In IaC, define SAPs in templates to ensure consistent enforcement and simplify revocation by updating the policy instead of individual tokens.

cis_azure_2.1.10.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_2.1.10.remediation: Set 'Allow Public Network Access' to 'Disabled' in Azure resource configurations, such as for Databricks workspaces. Use private endpoints and network security groups to restrict access to trusted IPs. Implement this in IaC by setting public network access flags to false and deploying private endpoints for secure connectivity.

cis_azure_2.1.11.description: Use private endpoints for Azure Databricks workspaces to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote
cis_azure_2.1.11.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for Azure Databricks workspaces using Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC tools like Terraform. Configure virtual networks and private DNS zones to route traffic securely. Ensure network security groups are applied to restrict access and monitor connectivity to prevent exposure to the public internet.

cis_azure_2.1.12.description: App Service authentication can prevent anonymous HTTP requests from reaching an app, or authenticate those with tokens before they reach the app. If an anonymous request is received from a browser, App Service will redirect to a login page. To handle the login process, a choice from a set of identity providers can be made, or a custom authentication mechanism can be implemented.
cis_azure_2.1.12.remediation: Enable App Service authentication in the Azure Portal under Authentication settings. Configure identity providers like Microsoft Entra ID for token-based access. Use IaC to set authentication flags and define RBAC roles to manage user permissions, ensuring all requests are authenticated before reaching the application.

cis_azure_2.1.13.description: Managed identities from Microsoft Entra ID allow App Service apps to securely access other Azure services without the need to provision or rotate any secrets.
cis_azure_2.1.13.remediation: Assign a system-assigned or user-assigned managed identity to App Service apps via Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC. Grant necessary permissions to the managed identity using RBAC roles. This eliminates the need for storing credentials in code or configuration files, enhancing security by leveraging Azure's identity management.

cis_azure_2.1.14.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_2.1.14.remediation: Disable public network access for App Service apps by setting IP security restrictions to block all public IPs. Use private endpoints and virtual network integration for secure access. Implement this in IaC by configuring network settings to restrict inbound traffic and deploying private endpoints to keep traffic within Azure's backbone.

cis_azure_2.1.15.description: Ensure that your App Service plan SKU supports private endpoints. Private endpoints provide secure access over Azure Private Link, which keeps traffic on the Microsoft backbone network and eliminates exposure to the public internet. Note that not all SKUs support private endpoints.
cis_azure_2.1.15.remediation: Select an App Service plan SKU that supports private endpoints, such as Premium V2 or higher. Use Azure Portal or CLI to verify SKU compatibility before deployment. In IaC, specify the appropriate SKU in templates and validate configurations to ensure private endpoint functionality is available for secure connectivity.

cis_azure_2.1.16.description: Use private endpoints to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote infrastructures together. This
cis_azure_2.1.16.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for App Service apps using Azure Private Link. Configure virtual networks and network security groups to control access. Use IaC tools to automate private endpoint creation and ensure all app traffic is routed through secure, encrypted connections, minimizing exposure to the public internet.

cis_azure_2.1.17.description: Use private DNS zones to override DNS resolution for a private endpoint. A private DNS zone links a virtual network to an App Service app. This recommendation assumes application of, and should be applied in conjunction with, the recommendation titled "Ensure private endpoints are used to access App Service apps."
cis_azure_2.1.17.remediation: Create private DNS zones in Azure DNS and link them to virtual networks used by App Service private endpoints. Configure DNS records to resolve app names to private IP addresses. Use IaC to define private DNS zones and associations, ensuring seamless and secure name resolution without relying on public DNS.

cis_azure_2.1.18.description: Integrate App Service apps with a virtual network to enable access to resources in or through a non-internet-routable virtual network.
cis_azure_2.1.18.remediation: Enable virtual network integration for App Service apps via the Azure Portal or CLI. Select the appropriate virtual network and subnet. Use IaC to configure integration settings, ensuring apps can securely access on-premises or other cloud resources without exposing traffic to the internet.

cis_azure_2.1.19.description: By default, configuration traffic for App Service apps goes directly over the public route. Container image pulls and content sharing can be routed through the virtual network integration. This recommendation should be applied after integrating an App Service app with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.1.19.remediation: Configure App Service apps to route configuration traffic, such as container image pulls, through the integrated virtual network. Use Azure Portal settings or IaC to enable this feature. This ensures that sensitive operations are performed over secure, private connections, reducing the risk of data interception.

cis_azure_2.1.2.description: Network Security Groups (NSGs) should be implemented to control inbound and outbound traffic to Azure Databricks subnets, ensuring only authorized communication. NSGs operate using a rule-based model that includes both explicit allow/deny rules and an implicit deny at the end of the rule list. This means that any traffic not explicitly allowed is automatically denied. To ensure secure and predictable behavior, NSGs should be configured with explicit deny rules for known unwanted traffic, in addi
cis_azure_2.1.2.remediation: Associate NSGs with Databricks subnets to enforce traffic rules. Define allow rules for necessary ports and deny all others. Use IaC to deploy NSGs with least-privilege principles, regularly review rules for compliance, and monitor traffic logs to detect and block unauthorized access attempts.

cis_azure_2.1.2.1.1.description: Microsoft Managed Keys (MMK) [also known as Platform-managed keys (PMK)] provides a very low overhead method of encrypting data at rest and implementing encryption key management. Keys maintained in an MMK implementation are automatically managed by Azure and require no customer interaction.
cis_azure_2.1.2.1.1.remediation: Enable encryption at rest using Microsoft Managed Keys for Azure Storage accounts. This can be configured via Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC by setting the encryption type to Microsoft-managed. Ensure all critical data is encrypted by default, and regularly audit storage accounts to verify encryption status and compliance with security policies.

cis_azure_2.1.2.2.1.description: Customer Managed Keys introduce additional depth to security by providing a means to manage access control for encryption keys. Where compliance and security frameworks indicate the need, and organizational capacity allows, sensitive data at rest can be encrypted using Customer Managed Keys (CMK) rather than Microsoft Managed keys.
cis_azure_2.1.2.2.1.remediation: Configure Azure Storage accounts to use Customer Managed Keys from Azure Key Vault. Set up key vault policies for access control and rotation. Use IaC to define CMK encryption settings, ensuring keys are managed by the organization to meet compliance requirements and enhance data security through granular key management.

cis_azure_2.1.20.description: Enable vnetRouteAllEnabled to ensure all outbound traffic is routed through the integrated virtual network. This recommendation should be applied after integrating an App Service app with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.1.20.remediation: Set vnetRouteAllEnabled to true for App Service apps integrated with a virtual network. Use Azure Portal or CLI to enable this setting. In IaC, configure this property to force all outbound traffic through the VNet, ensuring consistent security monitoring and preventing data leakage over public networks.

cis_azure_2.1.21.description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how applications interact with resources hosted on different domains.
cis_azure_2.1.21.remediation: Configure CORS settings in App Service to specify allowed origins explicitly, rather than allowing all origins. Use Azure Portal or IaC to define trusted domains. Regularly review and update CORS policies to prevent cross-site scripting attacks and ensure only authorized domains can access resources, enhancing application security.

cis_azure_2.1.3.description: By default, data exchanged between worker nodes in an Azure Databricks cluster is not encrypted. To ensure that data is encrypted at all times, whether at rest or in transit, you can create an initialization script that configures your clusters to encrypt traffic between worker nodes using AES 256-bit encryption over a TLS 1.3 connection.
cis_azure_2.1.3.remediation: Create and apply initialization scripts to Databricks clusters that enable encryption for inter-node traffic using AES-256 and TLS 1.3. Use cluster policies in Databricks to enforce this configuration. In IaC, define scripts and policies to automate deployment, ensuring all data in transit is encrypted to protect against eavesdropping and data breaches.

cis_azure_2.1.4.description: To ensure centralized identity and access management, users and groups from Microsoft Entra ID should be synchronized with Azure Databricks. This is achieved through SCIM provisioning, which automates the creation, update, and deactivation of users and groups in Databricks based on Entra ID assignments. Enabling this integration ensures that access controls in Databricks remain consistent with corporate identity governance policies, reducing the risk of orphaned accounts, stale permissions, and
cis_azure_2.1.4.remediation: Enable SCIM provisioning in Azure Databricks to sync users and groups from Microsoft Entra ID. Configure provisioning settings in the Azure Portal or using Databricks APIs. Use IaC to automate this setup, ensuring identity management is centralized and access permissions are automatically updated based on Entra ID changes.

cis_azure_2.1.5.description: Unity Catalog is a centralized governance model for managing and securing data in Azure Databricks. It provides fine-grained access control to databases, tables, and views using Microsoft Entra ID identities. Unity Catalog also enhances data lineage, audit logging, and compliance monitoring, making it a critical component for security and governance.
cis_azure_2.1.5.remediation: Configure Unity Catalog for Azure Databricks workspaces to enable centralized data governance. Set up access controls and audit logging via Databricks admin console. Use IaC to define Unity Catalog settings and policies, ensuring data is protected with role-based access and compliance with regulatory requirements through detailed monitoring.

cis_azure_2.1.6.description: Databricks personal access tokens (PATs) provide API-based authentication for users and applications. By default, users can generate API tokens without expiration, leading to potential security risks if tokens are leaked, improperly stored, or not rotated regularly. To mitigate these risks, administrators should: • Restrict token creation to approved users and service principals. • Enforce expiration policies to prevent long-lived tokens. • Monitor token usage and revoke unused or compromised to
cis_azure_2.1.6.remediation: Implement policies in Databricks to restrict PAT creation and enforce expiration dates. Use Databricks admin settings to configure token lifetimes and monitor usage. In IaC, define token policies and automate revocation processes. Regularly audit token activity and rotate tokens to minimize the risk of unauthorized access from leaked credentials.

cis_azure_2.1.7.description: Azure Databricks Diagnostic Logging provides insights into system operations, user activities, and security events within a Databricks workspace. Enabling diagnostic logs helps organizations: • Detect security threats by logging access, job executions, and cluster activities. • Ensure compliance with industry regulations such as SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR. • Monitor operational performance and troubleshoot issues proactively.
cis_azure_2.1.7.remediation: Enable diagnostic logging for Azure Databricks workspaces and configure log delivery to a storage account, Log Analytics, or Event Hubs. Use Azure Portal or CLI to set up logging. In IaC, automate log configuration to ensure continuous monitoring. Regularly review logs for security incidents and compliance audits, enhancing visibility and threat detection.

cis_azure_2.1.8.description: Customer-managed keys introduce additional depth to security by providing a means to manage access control for encryption keys. Where compliance and security frameworks indicate the need, and organizational capacity allows, sensitive data at rest can be encrypted using customer-managed keys (CMK) rather than Microsoft-managed keys.
cis_azure_2.1.8.remediation: Configure Azure Databricks workspaces to use Customer Managed Keys for encryption at rest. Set up keys in Azure Key Vault with proper access policies. Use IaC to enable CMK encryption in Databricks settings, ensuring sensitive data is protected with organization-controlled keys to meet compliance and enhance security through key management.

cis_azure_2.1.9.description: Enable secure cluster connectivity (also known as no public IP) on Azure Databricks workspaces to ensure that clusters do not have public IP addresses and communicate with the control plane over a secure connection.
cis_azure_2.1.9.remediation: Enable 'No Public IP' setting for Azure Databricks workspaces to prevent clusters from having public IP addresses. Use private endpoints and virtual networks for secure connectivity. Configure this in the Azure Portal or via IaC to enforce that all cluster communication occurs over private links, reducing exposure to internet-based attacks.

cis_azure_2.10.description: App Service Environment supports changing the cipher suite from the default. The default set of ciphers is the same set that is used in the multi-tenant App Service. Changing the cipher suite is only possible with App Service Environment, the single- tenant offering, not the multi-tenant offering, because changing it affects the entire App Service deployment. There are two cipher suites that are required for an App Service Environment: TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 and TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH
cis_azure_2.10.remediation: Configure TLS cipher suite ordering in App Service Environment to use strong ciphers like TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384. Use Azure Portal or CLI to set cipher preferences. In IaC, define cipher suite configurations to enforce encryption standards, ensuring secure communication and compliance with security best practices for data in transit.

cis_azure_2.2.description: Setting 'Allow access only via SSL' to 'Yes' ensures that data in transit to and from Azure Cache for Redis is encrypted using TLS.
cis_azure_2.2.remediation: Enable SSL-only access in Azure Cache for Redis configuration via Azure Portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to enforce this setting across all Redis instances, ensuring TLS encryption for all connections to prevent data interception.

cis_azure_2.2.1.description: Periodically, older versions of Java may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Java for App Service deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.2.1.remediation: Update Java version to a currently supported release in App Service deployment slot settings. Automate version checks with IaC tools like Terraform or Azure Policy to enforce patch management and reduce exposure to known vulnerabilities.
