cis_azure_2.2.1.1.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints and Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_2.2.1.1.remediation: Disable public network access in Azure Storage Account settings and configure private endpoints for secure connectivity. Implement RBAC to restrict access, and use IaC to enforce this configuration across all storage resources to minimize attack surface.

cis_azure_2.2.1.2.description: Restricting default network access provides a foundational level of security to networked resources. To limit access to selected networks, the default action must be changed.
cis_azure_2.2.1.2.remediation: Set network access rules to deny-by-default in Azure Storage Account firewall settings. Use IaC to define allowed IP ranges or virtual networks, ensuring only authorized traffic can access storage resources and enhancing network security.

cis_azure_2.2.10.description: Remote debugging allows an App Service deployment slot to be debugged in real-time directly in the Azure environment. When remote debugging is enabled, it opens a communication channel that could potentially be exploited by unauthorized users if not properly secured.
cis_azure_2.2.10.remediation: Disable remote debugging in App Service deployment slot configuration settings. Use secure development practices and local debugging tools instead. Enforce this via Azure Policy or IaC to prevent accidental exposure of debugging interfaces.

cis_azure_2.2.11.description: Client certificates allow for the deployment slot to request a certificate for incoming requests. Only clients that have a valid certificate will be able to reach the deployment slot.
cis_azure_2.2.11.remediation: Enable and require incoming client certificates in App Service deployment slot settings. Configure certificate validation and use IaC to automate this setup, ensuring mutual TLS authentication for enhanced access control.

cis_azure_2.2.12.description: Managed identities from Microsoft Entra ID allow App Service deployment slots to securely access other Azure services without the need to provision or rotate any secrets.
cis_azure_2.2.12.remediation: Configure managed identities for App Service deployment slots in Azure Portal or via CLI. Assign appropriate RBAC roles to these identities and use IaC to automate identity management, eliminating secret storage risks.

cis_azure_2.2.13.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_2.2.13.remediation: Disable public network access for App Service deployment slots and set up private endpoints for internal connectivity. Implement network security groups and IaC to enforce this, reducing internet-facing attack vectors.

cis_azure_2.2.14.description: Integrate App Service deployment slots with a virtual network to enable access to resources in or through a non-internet-routable virtual network.
cis_azure_2.2.14.remediation: Enable virtual network integration for App Service deployment slots via Azure networking settings. Use IaC to automate VNet peering and subnet assignments, ensuring secure communication with on-premises or cloud resources.

cis_azure_2.2.15.description: By default, configuration traffic for App Service deployment slots goes directly over the public route. Container image pulls and content sharing can be routed through the virtual network integration. This recommendation should be applied after integrating an App Service deployment slot with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.2.15.remediation: Route configuration traffic through the virtual network integration in App Service settings. Configure private endpoints for container registries and use IaC to enforce this, enhancing security by keeping traffic within trusted networks.

cis_azure_2.2.16.description: Enable vnetRouteAllEnabled to ensure all outbound traffic is routed through the integrated virtual network. This recommendation should be applied after integrating an App Service deployment slot with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.2.16.remediation: Set vnetRouteAllEnabled to true in App Service deployment slot configuration. Use IaC tools like ARM templates to automate this setting, ensuring all outbound traffic is secured through the VNet and monitored for anomalies.

cis_azure_2.2.17.description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how applications interact with resources hosted on different domains.
cis_azure_2.2.17.remediation: Restrict CORS settings in App Service to specific allowed origins instead of wildcards. Configure this via Azure Portal or IaC, and regularly audit CORS policies to prevent unauthorized cross-domain requests and potential data leaks.

cis_azure_2.2.2.description: Periodically, older versions of Python may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Python for App Service deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.2.2.remediation: Update Python version to a supported release in App Service deployment slot settings. Automate version management with IaC and Azure Policy to enforce security patches and reduce vulnerability exposure.

cis_azure_2.2.2.1.description: Use private endpoints to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote infrastructures together.
cis_azure_2.2.2.1.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for Azure services like Storage Accounts to enable secure Private Link connections. Configure VNet integration and use IaC to automate endpoint creation, ensuring encrypted traffic and reduced public exposure.

cis_azure_2.2.3.description: Periodically, older versions of PHP may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of PHP for App Service deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.2.3.remediation: Update PHP version to a currently supported release in App Service deployment slot configuration. Implement IaC to manage version settings and use Azure Policy to enforce patch compliance, mitigating security risks from outdated software.

cis_azure_2.2.4.description: Basic Authentication Publishing Credentials provides the ability to publish—or deploy to—an App Service deployment slot without a centralized Identity Provider. For a more effective, capable, and secure solution for Identity, Authentication, Authorization, and Accountability, a centralized Identity Provider such as Entra ID is strongly advised.
cis_azure_2.2.4.remediation: Disable basic authentication publishing credentials in App Service deployment slot settings. Migrate to Entra ID or other centralized identity providers for authentication. Use IaC to enforce this and implement RBAC for secure access management.

cis_azure_2.2.5.description: By default, App Service supports deployment over FTP. If FTP is essential for a deployment workflow, FTPS should be enforced for all App Service deployment slots. If FTPS is not explicitly required, the recommended setting is Disabled.
cis_azure_2.2.5.remediation: Set FTP state to 'FTPS only' or 'Disabled' in App Service deployment slot configuration. Use secure deployment methods like Git or DevOps pipelines, and enforce this via IaC to prevent unencrypted file transfers.

cis_azure_2.2.6.description: Periodically, newer versions are released for HTTP, either due to security flaws or to include additional functionalities. Using the latest HTTP version allows apps to take advantage of security fixes, if any, and/or new functionalities of the newer version.
cis_azure_2.2.6.remediation: Set HTTP version to 2.0 in App Service deployment slot settings if supported. Use IaC to configure this and regularly update configurations to leverage security enhancements and performance improvements in newer protocols.

cis_azure_2.2.7.description: Azure App Service allows deployment slots to run under both HTTP and HTTPS by default. Deployment slots can be accessed by anyone using non-secure HTTP links by default. Non-secure HTTP requests can be restricted and all HTTP requests redirected to the secure HTTPS port. It is recommended to enforce HTTPS-only traffic.
cis_azure_2.2.7.remediation: Enable 'HTTPS Only' in App Service deployment slot configuration. Use TLS certificates from Azure Key Vault and enforce redirects from HTTP to HTTPS via IaC, ensuring all traffic is encrypted in transit.

cis_azure_2.2.8.description: The TLS (Transport Layer Security) protocol secures the transmission of data over the internet using standard encryption technology. App Service deployment slots use TLS 1.2 for the Minimum Inbound TLS Version by default and allow for the use of TLS versions 1.0, 1.1, and 1.3. NIST strongly suggests the use of TLS 1.2 and recommends the adoption of TLS 1.3.
cis_azure_2.2.8.remediation: Set minimum inbound TLS version to 1.2 or higher in App Service deployment slot settings. Use IaC to enforce this configuration and regularly audit TLS settings to prevent use of deprecated protocols and enhance encryption security.

cis_azure_2.2.9.description: End-to-end (E2E) TLS encryption ensures that front-end to worker communication within App Service deployment slots is encrypted using TLS. Without this feature, while incoming HTTPS requests are encrypted to the front ends, the traffic from front ends to workers running the application workloads would travel unencrypted inside Azure’s infrastructure.
cis_azure_2.2.9.remediation: Enable end-to-end TLS encryption in App Service deployment slot configuration. Use IaC to automate this setting and ensure TLS certificates are properly configured, securing internal traffic and preventing data exposure within Azure.

cis_azure_2.3.description: Setting the 'Minimum TLS version' helps reduce (but not eliminate) TLS protocol vulnerabilities by preventing the use of significantly outdated versions of TLS.
cis_azure_2.3.remediation: Configure minimum TLS version to 1.2 or higher in Azure Redis settings. Use Azure Policy or IaC to enforce this across all Redis instances, reducing risks associated with older TLS versions and enhancing data encryption.

cis_azure_2.3.1.description: Periodically, older versions of Java may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Java for function apps is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.3.1.remediation: Update Java version to a supported release in Azure Function App settings. Automate version management with IaC and implement patch policies to ensure compliance and reduce vulnerability exposure from outdated runtimes.

cis_azure_2.3.10.description: Client certificates allow for the function app to request a certificate for incoming requests. Only clients that have a valid certificate will be able to reach the app.
cis_azure_2.3.10.remediation: Enable and require incoming client certificates in Azure Function App configuration. Use IaC to set up certificate validation and enforce mutual TLS authentication, enhancing access control and security for client connections.

cis_azure_2.3.11.description: App Service authentication can prevent anonymous HTTP requests from reaching an app, or authenticate those with tokens before they reach the app. If an anonymous request is received from a browser, App Service will redirect to a login page. To handle the login process, a choice from a set of identity providers can be made, or a custom authentication mechanism can be implemented.
cis_azure_2.3.11.remediation: Enable App Service authentication in Azure Function App settings. Configure identity providers like Entra ID and use IaC to automate authentication setup, ensuring all requests are authenticated and reducing unauthorized access risks.

cis_azure_2.3.12.description: Managed identities from Microsoft Entra ID allow function apps to securely access other Azure services without the need to provision or rotate any secrets.
cis_azure_2.3.12.remediation: Enable managed identities for Azure Function Apps via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Assign appropriate roles (e.g., Contributor, Reader) to the managed identity using Azure RBAC to limit permissions. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Bicep to automate this configuration and ensure consistency across environments.

cis_azure_2.3.13.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_2.3.13.remediation: Disable public network access for Azure resources (e.g., storage accounts, function apps) via Azure portal or CLI. Configure private endpoints to allow access only from within virtual networks. Implement network security groups (NSGs) and firewall rules to restrict inbound traffic, and use IaC to enforce these settings in deployment pipelines.

cis_azure_2.3.14.description: Integrate function apps with a virtual network to enable access to resources in or through a non-internet-routable virtual network. This recommendation does not apply to function apps created on the consumption hosting plan, which does not support virtual networking.
cis_azure_2.3.14.remediation: Enable virtual network integration for Azure Function Apps on supported plans (Premium or Dedicated) via the Azure portal or CLI. Configure subnet delegation and network settings to allow secure communication. Use IaC templates to automate VNet integration and ensure it's applied during resource provisioning.

cis_azure_2.3.15.description: By default, configuration traffic for function apps goes directly over the public route. Container image pulls and content sharing can be routed through the virtual network integration. This recommendation should be applied after integrating a function app with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.3.15.remediation: After VNet integration, enable routing of configuration traffic through the virtual network in the Azure Function App settings. Use Azure CLI commands or ARM templates to set 'vnetRouteAllEnabled' to true. Ensure network policies allow traffic flow and monitor with Azure Monitor for any connectivity issues.

cis_azure_2.3.16.description: Enable vnetRouteAllEnabled to ensure all outbound traffic is routed through the integrated virtual network. This recommendation should be applied after integrating a function app with a virtual network.
cis_azure_2.3.16.remediation: Set 'vnetRouteAllEnabled' to true in the Azure Function App configuration via the portal, CLI, or IaC. Verify that the VNet has proper routes and security groups to handle outbound traffic. Regularly audit network configurations using Azure Policy to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_2.3.17.description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how applications interact with resources hosted on different domains.
cis_azure_2.3.17.remediation: Configure CORS settings in Azure Function Apps to specify allowed origins explicitly, avoiding wildcards like '*'. Use the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates to set restricted origins. Implement regular reviews of CORS policies to remove unused domains and prevent unauthorized cross-domain access.

cis_azure_2.3.2.description: Periodically, older versions of Python may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Python for function apps is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.3.2.remediation: Update Python runtime to a currently supported version in Azure Function App settings. Use Azure CLI or IaC to enforce version policies across deployments. Monitor for end-of-life announcements and schedule regular updates as part of patch management processes.

cis_azure_2.3.3.description: Basic Authentication Publishing Credentials provides the ability to publish—or deploy to—a function app without a centralized Identity Provider. For a more effective, capable, and secure solution for Identity, Authentication, Authorization, and Accountability, a centralized Identity Provider such as Entra ID is strongly advised.
cis_azure_2.3.3.remediation: Disable basic authentication publishing credentials in Azure Function App settings via the portal or CLI. Enable integration with Microsoft Entra ID for secure authentication and authorization. Use role-based access control (RBAC) to manage deployment permissions and audit access logs regularly.

cis_azure_2.3.4.description: By default, App Service supports deployment over FTP. If FTP is essential for a deployment workflow, FTPS should be enforced for all function apps. If FTPS is not explicitly required, the recommended setting is Disabled.
cis_azure_2.3.4.remediation: Set FTP state to 'FTPS Only' or 'Disabled' in Azure Function App configuration. Use Azure CLI or ARM templates to enforce this setting. If FTPS is needed, ensure strong TLS encryption and restrict IP addresses. Prefer secure deployment methods like Git or DevOps pipelines over FTP.

cis_azure_2.3.5.description: Periodically, newer versions are released for HTTP, either due to security flaws or to include additional functionalities. Using the latest HTTP version allows apps to take advantage of security fixes, if any, and/or new functionalities of the newer version.
cis_azure_2.3.5.remediation: Configure HTTP version to 2.0 in Azure Function App settings via the portal or CLI. Use IaC to standardize this across all function apps. Test compatibility with existing applications and monitor for performance improvements or issues after enabling HTTP/2.

cis_azure_2.3.6.description: Azure App Service allows function apps to run under both HTTP and HTTPS by default. Function apps can be accessed by anyone using non-secure HTTP links by default. Non-secure HTTP requests can be restricted and all HTTP requests redirected to the secure HTTPS port. It is recommended to enforce HTTPS-only traffic.
cis_azure_2.3.6.remediation: Enable 'HTTPS Only' in Azure Function App configuration to force all traffic over TLS. Use Azure CLI or ARM templates to apply this setting. Ensure SSL certificates are valid and configured, and use Azure Front Door or Application Gateway for additional SSL termination and security.

cis_azure_2.3.7.description: The TLS (Transport Layer Security) protocol secures the transmission of data over the internet using standard encryption technology. Function apps use TLS 1.2 for the Minimum Inbound TLS Version by default and allow for the use of TLS versions 1.0, 1.1, and 1.3. NIST strongly suggests the use of TLS 1.2 and recommends the adoption of TLS 1.3.
cis_azure_2.3.7.remediation: Set minimum inbound TLS version to 1.2 or higher in Azure Function App settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce this across subscriptions. Regularly update to newer TLS versions as they become available and disable older, insecure versions to protect against vulnerabilities.

cis_azure_2.3.8.description: End-to-end (E2E) TLS encryption ensures that front-end to worker communication within function apps is encrypted using TLS. Without this feature, while incoming HTTPS requests are encrypted to the front ends, the traffic from front ends to workers running the application workloads would travel unencrypted inside Azure’s infrastructure.
cis_azure_2.3.8.remediation: Enable end-to-end TLS encryption in Azure Function App configuration via the portal or CLI. Use IaC to ensure this is applied during deployment. Verify that certificates are properly configured and monitor for any encryption gaps using Azure Security Center or network monitoring tools.

cis_azure_2.3.9.description: Remote debugging allows a function app to be debugged in real-time directly in the Azure environment. When remote debugging is enabled, it opens a communication channel that could potentially be exploited by unauthorized users if not properly secured.
cis_azure_2.3.9.remediation: Disable remote debugging in Azure Function App settings after development phases. Use Azure CLI or ARM templates to enforce this in production environments. If debugging is needed, enable it temporarily with strict IP restrictions and disable it immediately after use.

cis_azure_2.4.description: Access Policies provide an Access Control List (ACL) functionality allowing administrators to define which identities or identity groups have access to what data and commands. This is an implementation of the Role Based Access Control (RBAC) concept and will require careful consideration to deploy and maintain.
cis_azure_2.4.remediation: Implement least-privilege access policies using Azure RBAC. Regularly review and audit IAM roles and permissions via Azure Policy or third-party tools. Use IaC to define and enforce access controls, and schedule periodic access reviews to remove unnecessary permissions and ensure compliance.

cis_azure_2.4.1.description: Periodically, older versions of Java may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Java for function app deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
cis_azure_2.4.1.remediation: Update Java runtime to a supported version in Azure Function App deployment slot settings. Use Azure CLI or IaC to manage versions. Monitor for security advisories and plan upgrades as part of regular maintenance to mitigate risks from outdated software.

cis_azure_2.4.15.description: Enable vnetRouteAllEnabled to ensure all outbound traffic is routed through the integrated virtual network. This recommendation should be applied after integrating a function app deployment slot with a virtual network.

cis_azure_2.4.16.description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how applications interact with resources hosted on different domains. Regularly review and update CORS configurations to align with application requirements and prevent unauthorized cross-domain requests.

cis_azure_2.4.2.remediation: Update Python runtime to a supported version in deployment slot settings. Use Azure CLI or IaC to manage version consistency. Schedule regular updates based on vendor support timelines and test compatibility to avoid disruptions in application functionality.

cis_azure_2.4.3.remediation: Disable basic authentication publishing credentials for deployment slots. Enable integration with Microsoft Entra ID for secure authentication. Use RBAC to control deployment access and implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for additional security. Regularly audit access logs to detect unauthorized attempts.

Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform or Bicep to enforce this setting across all deployment slots. Regularly audit function apps to ensure compliance and disable FTP if not needed to reduce attack surface.

Implement IaC to automate this setting and ensure it's applied during deployment. Monitor for updates and adjust as newer versions become available to maintain security and performance.

cis_azure_2.5.description: System Assigned Managed Identities provide the Azure Cache for Redis instance with a unique account like a service principle but automatically assigned and managed by Azure. These identities are unique to the resource instance they are created for, and removed when the resource is deleted.
cis_azure_2.5.remediation: Enable System Assigned Managed Identity for Azure Cache for Redis instances via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate this configuration and assign necessary RBAC roles for secure access. Regularly audit identities to ensure they are properly managed and not over-permissioned.

cis_azure_2.6.description: Disabling public network access restricts the service from accessing public networks.
cis_azure_2.6.remediation: Disable public network access for Azure Cache for Redis using the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this setting and configure private endpoints or VNet integration for secure access. Regularly review network configurations to prevent accidental exposure to the internet.

cis_azure_2.7.description: Ensure App Service Environment is provisioned with v3 or higher to benefit from the latest enhancements.
cis_azure_2.7.remediation: Provision App Service Environment with version v3 or higher via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate deployment and ensure compliance with version requirements. Regularly update to newer versions as they become available to leverage security and performance improvements.

cis_azure_2.8.description: The App Service Environment operates as a black box system where you cannot see the internal components or the communication within the system. To enable higher throughput, encryption is not enabled by default between internal components. The system is secure as the traffic is inaccessible to being monitored or accessed. However, if you have a compliance requirement that requires complete encryption of the data path from end to end, you can enable encryption of the complete data path with a clus
cis_azure_2.8.remediation: Enable internal encryption for App Service Environment via the Azure portal or CLI if compliance requires end-to-end encryption. Use IaC to configure this setting and ensure it aligns with organizational policies. Monitor encryption status and adjust as needed to meet regulatory requirements.

cis_azure_2.9.description: The TLS (Transport Layer Security) protocol secures the transmission of data over the internet using standard encryption technology. TLS versions 1.0 and 1.1 have been deprecated, and their use is generally discouraged. Disable all inbound TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 traffic for all the apps in an App Service Environment.
cis_azure_2.9.remediation: Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1 in App Service Environment configuration using the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this setting and set Minimum TLS Version to 1.2 or higher. Regularly audit TLS configurations to prevent use of deprecated protocols.

cis_azure_20.1.description: Migrate blob-based VHDs to Managed Disks on Virtual Machines to exploit the default features of this configuration. The features include: 1. Default Disk Encryption 2. Resilience, as Microsoft will managed the disk storage and move around if underlying hardware goes faulty 3. Reduction of costs over storage accounts
cis_azure_20.1.remediation: Migrate Virtual Machines to use Managed Disks via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate deployment with Managed Disks and enable default encryption features. Regularly review VM configurations to ensure all disks are managed and encrypted.

cis_azure_20.10.description: When Secure Boot and vTPM are enabled together, they provide a strong foundation for protecting your VM from boot attacks. For example, if an attacker attempts to replace the bootloader with a malicious version, Secure Boot will prevent the VM from booting. If the attacker is able to bypass Secure Boot and install a malicious bootloader, vTPM can be used to detect the intrusion and alert you.
cis_azure_20.10.remediation: Enable Trusted Launch with Secure Boot and vTPM for Virtual Machines during creation via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this configuration and ensure it's applied to all new VMs. Regularly audit VMs to verify Trusted Launch is enabled and functioning.

cis_azure_20.11.description: Encryption at host enhances Azure Disk Storage Server-Side Encryption to ensure that all temporary disks and disk caches are encrypted at rest and flow encrypted to the storage clusters.
cis_azure_20.11.remediation: Enable encryption at host for Virtual Machines via the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate this setting and ensure it's applied to all VMs, especially those handling sensitive data. Regularly check encryption status to protect data at rest.

cis_azure_20.2.description: Ensure that OS disks (boot volumes) and data disks (non-boot volumes) are encrypted with CMK (Customer Managed Keys). Customer Managed keys can be either ADE or Server Side Encryption (SSE).
cis_azure_20.2.remediation: Encrypt OS and data disks with Customer Managed Keys using Azure Disk Encryption or Server-Side Encryption via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to configure CMK and manage key rotation in Azure Key Vault. Regularly audit disk encryption to ensure compliance with security policies.

cis_azure_20.3.description: Ensure that unattached disks in a subscription are encrypted with a Customer Managed Key (CMK).
cis_azure_20.3.remediation: Encrypt unattached disks with Customer Managed Keys via the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate encryption settings and regularly scan for unattached disks to apply encryption. Implement policies to delete or secure unused disks to reduce risk.

cis_azure_20.4.description: Virtual Machine Disks and snapshots can be configured to allow access from different network resources.
cis_azure_20.4.remediation: Configure disk network access to restrict public access by setting it to private or selected networks via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce network policies and implement private endpoints for secure access. Regularly review network settings to prevent exposure to the internet.

cis_azure_20.5.description: Data Access Authentication Mode provides a method of uploading or exporting Virtual Machine Disks.
cis_azure_20.5.remediation: Enable Data Access Authentication Mode for Virtual Machine Disks via the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to configure this setting and ensure authentication is required for disk operations. Regularly audit access logs to monitor for unauthorized activities.

cis_azure_20.6.description: For added security, only install organization-approved extensions on VMs.
cis_azure_20.6.remediation: Implement a policy to allow only approved extensions on Virtual Machines using Azure Policy or IaC templates. Use RBAC to restrict installation permissions and regularly audit installed extensions. Remove or update non-compliant extensions to maintain security.

cis_azure_20.7.description: Install endpoint protection for all virtual machines.
cis_azure_20.7.remediation: Deploy endpoint protection extensions like Microsoft Defender for Cloud to all Virtual Machines via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate installation and ensure coverage across all VMs. Regularly update and monitor endpoint protection for threats.

cis_azure_20.9.description: Verify identities without MFA that can log in to a privileged virtual machine using separate login credentials. An adversary can leverage the access to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. Make sure the virtual machine only has necessary permissions, and revoke the admin-level permissions according to the least privileges principal
cis_azure_20.9.remediation: Enforce MFA for all identities accessing privileged Virtual Machines using Azure AD Conditional Access policies. Use RBAC to assign minimal necessary permissions and regularly review access controls. Implement logging and alerts for unauthorized login attempts.

cis_azure_3.1.description: Limiting your Cosmos DB to only communicate on whitelisted networks lowers its attack footprint.
cis_azure_3.1.remediation: Configure Cosmos DB network restrictions to allow only selected networks via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce firewall rules and implement private endpoints for secure access. Regularly audit network settings to prevent public exposure.

cis_azure_3.1.1.description: Verify identities without MFA that can log in to a privileged virtual machine using separate login credentials. An adversary can leverage the access to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. Make sure the virtual machine only has necessary permissions, and revoke the admin-level permissions according to the principle of least privilege.
cis_azure_3.1.1.remediation: Enforce MFA for identities accessing privileged Virtual Machines using Azure AD Conditional Access policies. Use RBAC to apply least privilege and regularly audit permissions. Implement monitoring for login activities and revoke unnecessary access.

cis_azure_3.1.2.description: If kubelet is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
cis_azure_3.1.2.remediation: Manually check and set the kubelet kubeconfig file ownership to root:root on each node in the Kubernetes cluster. Use SSH or management tools to verify permissions and adjust as needed. Document this procedure and include it in regular security audits to maintain compliance.

cis_azure_3.1.3.description: The azure.json file in an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster is a configuration file used by the Kubernetes cloud provider integration for Azure. This file contains essential details that allow the Kubernetes cluster to interact with Azure resources effectively. It's part of the Azure Cloud Provider configuration, enabling Kubernetes components to communicate with Azure services for features like load balancers, storage, and networking. Ensure the file has permissions of 644 or more restrict
cis_azure_3.1.3.remediation: Set permissions for the azure.json file to 644 or more restrictive using IaC tools like Ansible or scripts during AKS cluster deployment. Use RBAC to control access and regularly audit file permissions. Implement monitoring to detect and correct any deviations from the required settings.

cis_azure_3.1.4.description: The azure.json file in an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster is a configuration file used by the Kubernetes cloud provider integration for Azure. This file contains essential details that allow the Kubernetes cluster to interact with Azure resources effectively. It's part of the Azure Cloud Provider configuration, enabling Kubernetes components to communicate with Azure services for features like load balancers, storage, and networking. Ensure that the file is owned by root:root.
cis_azure_3.1.4.remediation: Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Azure Resource Manager templates to set file ownership to root:root during AKS cluster deployment. Regularly audit file permissions with CSPM tools and enforce RBAC policies to restrict access to sensitive configuration files.

cis_azure_3.2.description: Private endpoints limit network traffic to approved sources.
cis_azure_3.2.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for Azure services like Cosmos DB using IaC to ensure network isolation. Configure network security groups and Azure Firewall to restrict access, and use CSPM tools to monitor for public endpoints and enforce compliance policies.

cis_azure_3.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
cis_azure_3.2.1.remediation: Manually update Kubelet configuration files to set --anonymous-auth=false. Establish a procedure for reviewing and applying configuration changes in AKS clusters, and use RBAC to limit access to Kubelet settings.

cis_azure_3.2.2.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
cis_azure_3.2.2.remediation: Configure Kubelet with --authorization-mode=Webhook or other explicit modes using IaC during AKS setup. Use CSPM tools to scan for misconfigurations and enforce RBAC policies to ensure proper authorization mechanisms are in place.

cis_azure_3.2.3.remediation: Manually set up and manage certificate authorities and client certificates for Kubelet authentication. Implement a process for certificate rotation and validation, and use RBAC to control access to certificate management tasks.

cis_azure_3.2.4.remediation: Manually configure Kubelet to disable the read-only port by setting appropriate flags. Establish security procedures for AKS node configurations and use network policies to restrict unnecessary ports.

cis_azure_3.2.5.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
cis_azure_3.2.5.remediation: Set --streaming-connection-idle-timeout to a non-zero value using IaC for AKS clusters. Monitor configurations with CSPM tools and enforce policies to prevent insecure timeout settings that could lead to resource exhaustion.

cis_azure_3.2.6.remediation: Manually configure Kubelet with --make-iptables-util-chains=true to ensure proper network rule management. Follow documented procedures for AKS security settings and review configurations regularly to maintain compliance.

cis_azure_3.2.7.description: Security relevant information should be captured. The --eventRecordQPS flag on the Kubelet can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
cis_azure_3.2.7.remediation: Manually set --eventRecordQPS to a balanced value that captures security events without causing denial of service. Implement logging and monitoring procedures, and use RBAC to control access to Kubelet configuration changes.

cis_azure_3.2.8.remediation: Configure --rotate-certificates=true in AKS clusters using IaC to automate certificate management. Use CSPM tools to detect misconfigurations and enforce encryption policies for secure certificate handling.

cis_azure_3.2.9.remediation: Set RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true in AKS configurations via IaC. Regularly audit certificate settings with CSPM tools and apply RBAC policies to restrict modifications to security-sensitive parameters.

cis_azure_3.3.description: When using either a user-assigned or system-assigned managed identity, those identities may require a role or privilege assignment to perform a desired function. The roles or privileges assigned to that identity should be assigned with the principle of least privilege in mind - the identity is given the minimum levels of access or permissions needed to perform the job.
cis_azure_3.3.remediation: Assign minimal RBAC roles to managed identities using IaC templates. Use CSPM tools to scan for excessive permissions and enforce least privilege policies through regular access reviews and automated compliance checks.

cis_azure_4.1.description: Enable customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) for Azure Managed Lustre file systems to enhance data security and provide greater control over encryption processes. By using CMEK, organizations can manage their own encryption keys within Azure Key Vault, allowing them to rotate, revoke, or otherwise control access to these keys in accordance with their security policies.
cis_azure_4.1.remediation: Configure CMEK for Azure Managed Lustre file systems using IaC to integrate with Azure Key Vault. Enforce encryption policies with CSPM tools and use RBAC to restrict key management access, ensuring keys are rotated regularly.

cis_azure_4.1.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
cis_azure_4.1.1.remediation: Limit cluster-admin role assignments using RBAC policies and IaC to define minimal permissions. Use CSPM tools to audit role usage and enforce compliance by regularly reviewing and revoking unnecessary admin access.

cis_azure_4.1.5.description: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
cis_azure_4.1.5.remediation: Create and use custom service accounts with specific permissions instead of the default, configured via IaC. Use CSPM tools to detect default service account usage and enforce RBAC policies to restrict their application in pods.

cis_azure_4.1.6.description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
cis_azure_4.1.6.remediation: Configure pods to disable automatic token mounting using IaC, setting automountServiceAccountToken to false where not needed. Use CSPM tools to scan for misconfigurations and enforce RBAC policies to limit token access.

cis_azure_4.4.1.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
cis_azure_4.4.1.remediation: Deploy AKS clusters with the latest CNI version using IaC to ensure support for network policies. Use CSPM tools to monitor CNI versions and enforce update policies through automated compliance checks and RBAC for configuration management.

cis_azure_4.4.2.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
cis_azure_4.4.2.remediation: Define and apply network policies for all namespaces using IaC tools like Kubernetes manifests. Use CSPM tools to audit policy coverage and enforce RBAC to control network policy modifications, ensuring traffic isolation.

cis_azure_5.1.1.description: Soft delete provides additional protection for Backup vault data. With soft delete enabled, deleted backup data can be recovered within the retention period.
cis_azure_5.1.1.remediation: Enable soft delete on Backup vaults using IaC during deployment. Use CSPM tools to monitor compliance and enforce RBAC policies to prevent disabling of soft delete, ensuring data recovery capabilities.

cis_azure_5.1.2.description: Immutable vaults safeguard backup data by preventing any operations that could result in the loss of recovery points. The immutable vault setting can be locked, making it irreversible and preventing malicious actors from disabling it and deleting backups.
cis_azure_5.1.2.remediation: Configure immutability for Backup vaults using IaC to lock settings. Enforce compliance with CSPM tools and use RBAC to restrict access to vault configurations, preventing unauthorized changes.

cis_azure_5.1.3.description: Backup vaults offer two encryption options: Microsoft-managed keys, which provide automatic encryption without user intervention, and customer-managed keys (CMK), which allow organizations to retain full control over their encryption keys for enhanced security and compliance.
cis_azure_5.1.3.remediation: Enable CMK encryption for Backup vaults using IaC to integrate with Azure Key Vault. Use CSPM tools to scan for unencrypted vaults and enforce encryption policies with RBAC controls for key management.

cis_azure_5.1.4.description: In addition to using customer-managed keys for encryption at rest in the Backup vault, you can enable an additional layer of platform-managed infrastructure encryption. This dual-layer approach enhances the protection of your backup data.
cis_azure_5.1.4.remediation: Enable infrastructure encryption on Backup vaults using IaC configurations. Monitor with CSPM tools to ensure dual-layer encryption is applied and enforce RBAC policies to maintain encryption settings.

cis_azure_5.1.5.description: Cross region restore enables data restoration in a secondary Azure paired region, even when the primary region is fully operational. This allows organizations to conduct drills and validate regional resiliency, thereby ensuring preparedness for potential outages.
cis_azure_5.1.5.remediation: Enable cross region restore on Backup vaults using IaC to ensure disaster recovery readiness. Use CSPM tools to audit settings and enforce RBAC policies to control restore capabilities, maintaining compliance with resiliency standards.

cis_azure_5.1.6.description: Disable cross subscription restore for Backup vaults to ensure that backup data can only be restored within the same subscription as the Backup vault, preventing restoration to targets in other subscriptions.
cis_azure_5.1.6.remediation: Configure Backup vaults to disable cross subscription restore using IaC. Use CSPM tools to detect misconfigurations and enforce RBAC policies to restrict restore settings, ensuring data isolation.

cis_azure_5.10.description: Ensure that users are notified on their primary and alternate emails on password resets.
cis_azure_5.10.remediation: Enable password reset notifications in Azure AD using IaC or management consoles. Use CSPM tools to monitor compliance and enforce RBAC policies to ensure notification settings are maintained for user awareness.

cis_azure_5.11.description: Ensure that all Global Administrators are notified if any other administrator resets their password.
cis_azure_5.11.remediation: In Azure Entra ID, configure the password reset notification setting to 'Yes' via the Azure portal under Entra ID > Password reset > Notification settings. Use Azure Policy or Terraform to enforce this configuration across your tenant, ensuring automated monitoring and compliance.

cis_azure_5.12.description: Require administrators to provide consent for applications before use.
cis_azure_5.12.remediation: Set 'User consent for applications' to 'Do not allow user consent' in Azure Entra ID under Enterprise applications > Consent and permissions. Implement this via Azure CLI or PowerShell scripts, and use RBAC to restrict application registration permissions to administrators only.

cis_azure_5.13.description: Allow users to provide consent for selected permissions when a request is coming from a verified publisher.
cis_azure_5.13.remediation: Configure 'User consent for applications' to 'Allow user consent for apps from verified publishers, for selected permissions' in Azure Entra ID. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and regularly audit application permissions to ensure compliance with least privilege principles.

cis_azure_5.14.description: Require administrators or appropriately delegated users to register third-party applications.
cis_azure_5.14.remediation: Disable 'Users can register applications' by setting it to 'No' in Azure Entra ID under User settings. Use Azure RBAC to assign application registration roles only to trusted administrators, and monitor for unauthorized registrations with Azure Monitor or CSPM tools.

cis_azure_5.15.remediation: Set 'Guest users access restrictions' to 'Guest user access is restricted to properties and memberships of their own directory objects' in Azure Entra ID under External Identities > Guest user access. Enforce this with Azure Policy and review guest user assignments regularly to minimize exposure.

cis_azure_5.16.description: Restrict invitations to either users with specific administrative roles or no one.
cis_azure_5.16.remediation: Configure 'Guest invite restrictions' to 'Only users assigned to specific admin roles' or 'No one' in Azure Entra ID. Use Azure RBAC to limit invitation permissions and implement automated checks via Azure CLI or PowerShell to ensure compliance across the tenant.

cis_azure_5.17.description: Restrict access to the Microsoft Entra ID administration center to administrators only. NOTE: This only affects access to the Entra ID administrator's web portal. This setting does not prohibit privileged users from using other methods such as Rest API or Powershell to obtain sensitive information from Microsoft Entra ID.
cis_azure_5.17.remediation: Enable 'Restrict access to Microsoft Entra admin center' to 'Yes' in Azure Entra ID settings. Complement this with RBAC to limit administrative roles and use Conditional Access policies to enforce MFA for all admin access, reducing risk from alternative access methods.

cis_azure_5.18.description: Restrict access to group web interface in the Access Panel portal.
cis_azure_5.18.remediation: Set 'Restrict user ability to access groups features in My Groups' to 'Yes' in Azure Entra ID under User settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration and regularly audit user permissions to ensure only authorized personnel can manage group features.

cis_azure_5.19.description: Restrict security group creation to administrators only.
cis_azure_5.19.remediation: Disable 'Users can create security groups in Azure portals, API or PowerShell' by setting it to 'No' in Azure Entra ID. Implement RBAC to assign group creation permissions only to administrators and use Azure Monitor to alert on unauthorized creation attempts.

cis_azure_5.2.1.description: Soft delete provides additional protection for Recovery Services vault data. With soft delete enabled, deleted backup data can be recovered within the retention period.
cis_azure_5.2.1.remediation: Enable soft delete on all Recovery Services vaults via the Azure portal under Backup center > Vaults > Properties. Use Azure Policy or Terraform to enforce this setting and set appropriate retention periods to ensure data recoverability in case of accidental deletion.

cis_azure_5.2.2.description: Immutable vaults safeguard backup data by preventing any operations that could result in the loss of recovery points. The immutable vault setting can be locked, making it irreversible and preventing malicious actors from disabling it and deleting backups.
cis_azure_5.2.2.remediation: Enable immutability on Recovery Services vaults and lock the setting to prevent changes. Configure this via Azure portal under Vault > Security > Immutability. Use Azure Policy to enforce immutability across all vaults and regularly audit compliance to protect against data loss.

cis_azure_5.2.3.description: Recovery Services vaults offer two encryption options: Microsoft-managed keys, which provide automatic encryption without user intervention, and customer-managed keys (CMK), which allow organizations to retain full control over their encryption keys for enhanced security and compliance.
cis_azure_5.2.3.remediation: Configure Recovery Services vaults to use customer-managed keys (CMK) for encryption at rest. Set this up in Azure portal under Vault > Encryption. Use Azure Key Vault to manage keys and implement RBAC and monitoring to ensure key access is restricted and compliant.

cis_azure_5.2.4.description: In addition to using customer-managed keys for encryption at rest in the Recovery Services vault, you can enable an additional layer of platform-managed infrastructure encryption. This dual-layer approach enhances the protection of your backup data.
cis_azure_5.2.4.remediation: Enable 'Use infrastructure encryption for this vault' on Recovery Services vaults via Azure portal under Vault > Encryption. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and combine with CMK for a defense-in-depth strategy to secure backup data.

cis_azure_5.2.5.description: Disable public network access on Recovery Services vaults to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints and Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to securely manage access within trusted networks.
cis_azure_5.2.5.remediation: Disable public network access on Recovery Services vaults by setting it to 'Disabled' in Azure portal under Vault > Networking. Implement private endpoints for secure access and use RBAC to restrict vault permissions. Enforce this with Azure Policy to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_azure_5.2.6.description: Cross region restore enables data restoration in a secondary Azure paired region, even when the primary region is fully operational. This allows organizations to conduct drills and validate regional resiliency, thereby ensuring preparedness for potential outages.
cis_azure_5.2.6.remediation: Enable 'Cross Region Restore' on Recovery Services vaults via Azure portal under Vault > Properties. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and regularly test restore procedures to ensure business continuity and compliance with disaster recovery plans.

cis_azure_5.2.7.description: Disable cross subscription restore for Recovery Services vaults to ensure that backup data can only be restored within the same subscription as the Recovery Services vault, preventing restoration to targets in other subscriptions.
cis_azure_5.2.7.remediation: Set 'Cross Subscription Restore' to 'Disabled' or 'Permanently Disabled' on Recovery Services vaults in Azure portal under Vault > Properties. Use Azure Policy to enforce this restriction and implement RBAC to control restore permissions within the subscription.

cis_azure_5.2.8.description: This recommendation ensures that issued tokens are only issued to the intended device.
cis_azure_5.2.8.remediation: Create a Token Protection Conditional Access policy in Azure Entra ID under Protection > Conditional Access. Configure device compliance and token issuance rules. Use Azure Monitor to audit token usage and ensure policies are applied to protect against token theft or misuse.

cis_azure_5.20.description: Restrict security group management to administrators only.
cis_azure_5.20.remediation: Set 'Owners can manage group membership requests in My Groups' to 'No' in Azure Entra ID under Group settings. Use RBAC to assign group management permissions exclusively to administrators and implement regular access reviews to maintain control over group memberships.

cis_azure_5.21.description: Restrict Microsoft 365 group creation to administrators only.
cis_azure_5.21.remediation: Disable 'Users can create Microsoft 365 groups in Azure portals, API or PowerShell' by setting it to 'No' in Azure Entra ID. Use RBAC to limit creation permissions to administrators and monitor group creation activities with Azure Log Analytics to detect unauthorized attempts.

cis_azure_5.22.description: NOTE: This recommendation is only relevant if your subscription is using Per-User MFA. If your organization is licensed to use Conditional Access, the preferred method of requiring MFA to join devices to Entra ID is to use a Conditional Access policy (see additional information below for link). Joining or registering devices to Microsoft Entra ID should require multi-factor authentication.
cis_azure_5.22.remediation: Enable 'Require Multifactor Authentication to register or join devices with Microsoft Entra' to 'Yes' in Azure Entra ID under Devices > Device settings. Alternatively, use Conditional Access policies to enforce MFA for device registration. Implement this via Azure portal and monitor compliance with Azure AD reports.

cis_azure_5.23.description: The principle of least privilege should be followed and only necessary privileges should be assigned instead of allowing full administrative access.
cis_azure_5.23.remediation: Audit and remove any custom subscription administrator roles in Azure under Subscriptions > Access control (IAM). Use built-in roles with least privilege and assign permissions via RBAC. Implement Azure Policy to prohibit custom admin roles and regularly review role assignments.

cis_azure_5.24.description: Resource locking is a powerful protection mechanism that can prevent inadvertent modification or deletion of resources within Azure subscriptions and resource groups, and it is a recommended NIST configuration.
cis_azure_5.24.remediation: Create a custom RBAC role with permissions for administering resource locks in Azure. Assign this role to authorized users via IAM. Use Azure Policy to enforce lock configurations and monitor lock status with Azure Monitor to ensure critical resources are protected from accidental changes.

cis_azure_5.25.description: Users who are set as subscription owners are able to make administrative changes to the subscriptions and move them into and out of Microsoft Entra ID.
cis_azure_5.25.remediation: Set 'Subscription leaving Microsoft Entra tenant' and 'Subscription entering Microsoft Entra tenant' to 'Permit no one' in Azure under Management groups > Settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce these restrictions and implement RBAC to limit subscription management permissions, reducing the risk of unauthorized tenant transfers.

cis_azure_5.26.description: This recommendation aims to maintain a balance between security and operational efficiency by ensuring that a minimum of 2 and a maximum of 4 users are assigned the Global Administrator role in Microsoft Entra ID. Having at least two Global Administrators ensures redundancy, while limiting the number to four reduces the risk of excessive privileged access.
cis_azure_5.26.remediation: Review and adjust Global Administrator assignments in Azure Entra ID under Roles and administrators. Ensure there are between 2 and 4 Global Administrators. Use RBAC to assign alternative privileged roles where possible and implement regular access reviews to maintain compliance with least privilege.

cis_azure_5.27.description: The Owner role in Azure grants full control over all resources in a subscription, including the ability to assign roles to others.
cis_azure_5.27.remediation: Ensure there are between 2 and 3 subscription owners by reviewing assignments in Azure under Subscriptions > Access control (IAM). Adjust as needed to maintain redundancy without excessive privileges. Use Azure Policy to enforce owner count limits and implement RBAC with least privilege for other administrative tasks.

cis_azure_5.28.description: Passwordless authentication methods improve security and user experience by replacing passwords with something you have (e.g., a hardware key), something you are (biometrics), or something you know, offering a convenient and secure way to access resources. Microsoft Entra ID and Azure Government integrate the following passwordless authentication options: Windows Hello for Business, Platform Credential for macOS, Platform single sign-on (PSSO) for macOS with smart card authentication, Micro.
cis_azure_5.28.remediation: Enable passwordless authentication in Microsoft Entra ID by configuring policies for methods like Windows Hello for Business or FIDO2 security keys. Use Azure Portal or IaC tools like Terraform to set up authentication methods and enforce them for user accounts. Regularly review and update these policies to align with security best practices.

cis_azure_5.3.1.description: Microsoft Azure admin accounts should not be used for routine, non-administrative tasks.
cis_azure_5.3.1.remediation: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) to assign least privilege roles for daily operations, using standard user accounts instead of admin accounts. Use Azure Policy to enforce separation of duties and monitor admin account usage with Azure Monitor logs. Automate this with IaC to ensure consistent configuration across environments.

cis_azure_5.3.2.description: Microsoft Entra ID has native and extended identity functionality allowing you to invite people from outside your organization to be guest users in your cloud account and sign in with their own work, school, or social identities. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Evaluating the appropriateness of guest users requires a manual review, as it depends on the specific needs and context of each organization and environment.
cis_azure_5.3.2.remediation: Establish a manual review process to periodically audit guest users in Microsoft Entra ID, checking for unnecessary or outdated accounts. Use Azure AD access reviews to automate part of this process, but ensure human oversight for context-specific decisions. Document and enforce policies for guest user lifecycle management.

cis_azure_5.3.3.description: The User Access Administrator role grants the ability to view all resources and manage access assignments at any subscription or management group level within the tenant. Due to its high privilege level, this role assignment should be removed immediately after completing the necessary changes at the root scope to minimize security risks.
cis_azure_5.3.3.remediation: Limit assignments of the User Access Administrator role to specific, time-bound tasks using Azure Privileged Identity Management (PIM). Implement RBAC with least privilege principles and use IaC to manage role assignments. Regularly audit role assignments with Azure Monitor to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_5.3.4.description: Periodic review of privileged role assignments is performed to ensure that the privileged roles assigned to users are accurate and appropriate.
cis_azure_5.3.4.remediation: Set up automated reviews for privileged role assignments using Azure AD access reviews or third-party CSPM tools. Configure alerts for unusual assignments and enforce policies with Azure Policy. Use IaC to define and manage role assignments consistently.

cis_azure_5.3.5.description: Ensure that any roles granting read, write, or owner permissions are removed from disabled Azure user accounts. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Removing role assignments from disabled user accounts depends on the context and requirements of each organization and environment.
cis_azure_5.3.5.remediation: Automate the removal of permissions from disabled accounts using scripts or CSPM tools, but include manual validation for context. Implement RBAC policies that automatically revoke access upon account disablement. Regularly audit disabled accounts with Azure AD reports to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_5.3.6.description: Perform a periodic review of the Tenant Creator role assignment to ensure that the assignments are accurate and appropriate. This recommendation should be applied alongside the recommendation "Ensure that 'Restrict non-admin users from creating tenants' is set to 'Yes'".
cis_azure_5.3.6.remediation: Use Azure AD access reviews to periodically audit Tenant Creator role assignments and restrict this role to necessary administrators. Implement RBAC and Azure Policy to enforce restrictions on tenant creation. Automate reviews with CSPM tools to maintain continuous compliance.

cis_azure_5.3.7.description: Perform a periodic review of non-privileged role assignments to ensure that the non- privileged roles assigned to users are appropriate. Note: Determining 'appropriate' assignments requires a clear understanding of your organization's personnel, systems, policies, and security requirements. This cannot be effectively prescribed in a procedure.
cis_azure_5.3.7.remediation: Conduct regular manual reviews of non-privileged role assignments using Azure AD access reviews, supplemented by automated checks with CSPM tools. Implement least privilege principles in RBAC and use IaC to manage role definitions. Document and enforce role assignment policies based on organizational needs.

cis_azure_5.4.description: Require administrators or appropriately delegated users to create new tenants.
cis_azure_5.4.remediation: Configure Microsoft Entra ID policies to restrict tenant creation to admin users only, using Azure Portal or IaC tools like ARM templates. Implement RBAC to delegate this permission cautiously and monitor creation events with Azure Activity Logs. Enforce this setting across all subscriptions with Azure Policy.

cis_azure_5.4.2.description: Disable access to the Kubernetes API from outside the node network if it is not required.
cis_azure_5.4.2.remediation: Create AKS clusters with private endpoints enabled and public access disabled using Azure CLI, PowerShell, or IaC tools like Terraform. Configure network security groups and Azure Firewall to restrict inbound traffic. Regularly audit cluster configurations with CSPM tools to ensure compliance.

cis_azure_5.4.3.description: Disable public IP addresses for cluster nodes, so that they only have private IP addresses. Private Nodes are nodes with no public IP addresses.
cis_azure_5.4.3.remediation: Enable private clusters in AKS during creation or update, using Azure Portal or IaC. Configure virtual networks and subnets to isolate nodes and use private endpoints for internal communication. Monitor network settings with Azure Network Watcher to enforce private node configurations.

cis_azure_5.4.4.description: When you run modern, microservices-based applications in Kubernetes, you often want to control which components can communicate with each other. The principle of least privilege should be applied to how traffic can flow between pods in an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster. Let's say you likely want to block traffic directly to back-end applications. The Network Policy feature in Kubernetes lets you define rules for ingress and egress traffic between pods in a cluster.
cis_azure_5.4.4.remediation: Enable and configure Network Policy in AKS using Azure CNI or Calico, defining rules to restrict pod-to-pod traffic based on least privilege. Use IaC tools like Terraform to deploy and manage network policies. Regularly review and update policies to align with application requirements and security best practices.

cis_azure_5.5.description: Ensures that two alternate forms of identification are provided before allowing a password reset.
cis_azure_5.5.remediation: Configure Microsoft Entra ID authentication methods policy to require two methods for password reset, using Azure Portal or IaC. Enable MFA and other verification options like email or phone. Enforce this policy across all users and monitor compliance with Azure AD reports.

cis_azure_5.6.description: The account lockout threshold determines how many failed login attempts are permitted prior to placing the account in a locked-out state and initiating a variable lockout duration.
cis_azure_5.6.remediation: Set the account lockout threshold to 10 or fewer failed attempts in Microsoft Entra ID authentication policies, using Azure Portal or IaC. Implement monitoring for failed login attempts with Azure AD Identity Protection. Regularly review and adjust thresholds based on security needs.

cis_azure_5.7.description: The account lockout duration value determines how long an account retains the status of lockout, and therefore how long before a user can continue to attempt to login after passing the lockout threshold.
cis_azure_5.7.remediation: Configure the account lockout duration to at least 60 seconds in Microsoft Entra ID authentication policies, using Azure Portal or IaC tools. Combine this with smart lockout settings to prevent brute-force attacks. Monitor lockout events with Azure AD logs to fine-tune durations.

cis_azure_5.8.description: Microsoft Azure applies a default global banned password list to all user and admin accounts that are created and managed directly in Microsoft Entra ID. The Microsoft Entra password policy does not apply to user accounts that are synchronized from an on-premises Active Directory environment, unless Microsoft Entra ID Connect is used and EnforceCloudPasswordPolicyForPasswordSyncedUsers is enabled. Review the Default Value section for more detail on the password policy. For increased password sec
cis_azure_5.8.remediation: Enable and configure a custom banned password list in Microsoft Entra ID password protection policies, using Azure Portal or IaC. Extend this to hybrid environments by enabling EnforceCloudPasswordPolicyForPasswordSyncedUsers in Azure AD Connect. Regularly update the banned list based on common weak passwords and organizational policies.

cis_azure_5.9.description: Ensure that the number of days before users are asked to re-confirm their authentication information is not set to 0.
cis_azure_5.9.remediation: Set the re-confirmation period to a positive number (e.g., 90 days) in Microsoft Entra ID authentication methods policy, using Azure Portal or IaC. This ensures users periodically verify their contact information. Monitor and enforce this setting with Azure Policy to maintain account security.

cis_azure_6.1.description: Enable SSL connection on MYSQL Servers.
cis_azure_6.1.remediation: Configure Azure Database for MySQL servers to enforce SSL connections by setting 'Enforce SSL connection' to Enabled, using Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC tools like ARM templates. Use TLS 1.2 or higher and rotate certificates regularly. Monitor compliance with CSPM tools to ensure all connections are encrypted.

cis_azure_6.1.1.1.description: Enable Diagnostic settings for exporting activity logs. Diagnostic settings are available for each individual resource within a subscription. Settings should be configured for all appropriate resources for your environment.
cis_azure_6.1.1.1.remediation: Create diagnostic settings for subscription activity logs to export to Log Analytics, Storage Account, or Event Hubs, using Azure Portal or IaC tools like Terraform. Configure retention policies and ensure all critical resources are covered. Automate this with Azure Policy to enforce logging across subscriptions.

cis_azure_6.1.1.10.description: Ensure that Intune logs are captured and fed into a central log analytics workspace.
cis_azure_6.1.1.10.remediation: Enable diagnostic settings for Intune to send logs to a Log Analytics workspace, using Azure Portal or IaC. Configure log categories and retention periods for compliance. Use Azure Monitor to analyze logs and set up alerts for security events.

cis_azure_6.1.1.2.description: Prerequisite: A Diagnostic Setting must exist. If a Diagnostic Setting does not exist, the navigation and options within this recommendation will not be available. Please review the recommendation at the beginning of this subsection titled: "Ensure that a 'Diagnostic Setting' exists." The diagnostic setting should be configured to log the appropriate activities from the control/management plane.
cis_azure_6.1.1.2.remediation: After creating diagnostic settings, configure them to capture all relevant categories like Administrative, Security, and Policy logs. Use Azure Portal or IaC to define these settings and ensure they align with compliance requirements. Regularly audit diagnostic settings with CSPM tools to maintain coverage.

cis_azure_6.1.1.3.description: Customer-managed keys introduce additional depth to security by providing a means to manage access control for encryption keys. Where compliance and security frameworks indicate the need, and organizational capacity allows, sensitive data at rest can be encrypted using customer-managed keys (CMK) rather than Microsoft-managed keys.
cis_azure_6.1.1.3.remediation: Encrypt storage accounts containing activity logs with customer-managed keys (CMK) using Azure Key Vault, configured via Azure Portal or IaC. Implement RBAC for key access and rotate keys periodically. Monitor encryption status with Azure Policy to ensure compliance with data protection standards.

cis_azure_6.1.1.4.description: Enable AuditEvent logging for key vault instances to ensure interactions with key vaults are logged and available.
cis_azure_6.1.1.4.remediation: Enable diagnostic logging for Azure Key Vault to capture AuditEvent logs, sending them to Log Analytics or Storage Account, using Azure Portal or IaC. Configure retention policies and monitor logs for unauthorized access. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging across all key vaults.

cis_azure_6.1.1.5.description: Ensure that network flow logs are captured and fed into a central log analytics workspace. Retirement Notice On September 30, 2027, network security group (NSG) flow logs will be retired. As of June 30, 2025, creating new NSG flow logs is no longer possible. Azure recommends migrating to virtual network flow logs. Review https://azure.microsoft.com/en- us/updates?id=Azure-NSG-flow-logs-Retirement for more information. For virtual network flow logs, consider applying the recommendation, Ensure th
cis_azure_6.1.1.5.remediation: Migrate to virtual network flow logs and enable them to send data to Log Analytics, using Azure Portal or IaC tools. Configure retention and analysis for network traffic. Monitor with Azure Network Watcher and update configurations to comply with Azure's retirement timeline for NSG flow logs.

cis_azure_6.1.1.6.description: Enable AppServiceHTTPLogs diagnostic log category for Azure App Service instances to ensure all http requests are captured and centrally logged.
cis_azure_6.1.1.6.remediation: Enable HTTP logs for Azure App Service by configuring diagnostic settings to include AppServiceHTTPLogs, sending to Log Analytics or Storage Account, using Azure Portal or IaC. Set retention policies and monitor logs for security incidents. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging across all app services.

cis_azure_6.1.1.7.description: Ensure that virtual network flow logs are captured and fed into a central log analytics workspace.
cis_azure_6.1.1.7.remediation: Enable flow logs for each virtual network in Azure and configure them to send to a Log Analytics workspace. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration across all virtual networks, and set up alerts for any deviations.

cis_azure_6.1.1.8.description: Ensure that a Microsoft Entra diagnostic setting is configured to send Microsoft Graph activity logs to a suitable destination, such as a Log Analytics workspace, storage account, or event hub. This enables centralized monitoring and analysis of all HTTP requests that the Microsoft Graph service receives and processes for a tenant.
cis_azure_6.1.1.8.remediation: Configure diagnostic settings in Microsoft Entra to export Microsoft Graph activity logs to a Log Analytics workspace or other secure destination. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and regularly audit logs for compliance.

cis_azure_6.1.1.9.description: Ensure that a Microsoft Entra diagnostic setting is configured to send Microsoft Entra activity logs to a suitable destination, such as a Log Analytics workspace, storage account, or event hub. This enables centralized monitoring and analysis of Microsoft Entra activity logs.
cis_azure_6.1.1.9.remediation: Set up diagnostic settings in Microsoft Entra to forward activity logs to a centralized logging solution. Apply Azure Policy to ensure this is enabled across all tenants and monitor for any gaps in log collection.

cis_azure_6.1.2.1.description: Create an activity log alert for the Create Policy Assignment event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.1.remediation: Create an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to trigger notifications when a policy assignment is created. Use Azure CLI or ARM templates to automate this setup and ensure alerts are sent to appropriate teams for review.

cis_azure_6.1.2.10.description: Create an activity log alert for the Delete Public IP Address rule.
cis_azure_6.1.2.10.remediation: Configure an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to detect deletions of public IP addresses. Automate this with Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform and set up notifications to security teams for immediate response.

cis_azure_6.1.2.11.description: Create an activity log alert for Service Health.
cis_azure_6.1.2.11.remediation: Set up an activity log alert in Azure Monitor for Service Health events to stay informed about Azure service issues. Use Azure Policy to enforce this alert creation and integrate notifications with incident management systems.

cis_azure_6.1.2.2.description: Create an activity log alert for the Delete Policy Assignment event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.2.remediation: Implement an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to monitor deletions of policy assignments. Automate this configuration using ARM templates and ensure alerts are routed to administrators for verification.

cis_azure_6.1.2.3.description: Create an Activity Log Alert for the Create or Update Network Security Group event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.3.remediation: Create an activity log alert in Azure Monitor for any changes to network security groups. Use Azure Policy to enforce this alert and configure it to notify security operations for review of network changes.

cis_azure_6.1.2.4.description: Create an activity log alert for the Delete Network Security Group event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.4.remediation: Set up an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to detect deletions of network security groups. Automate this with Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and ensure alerts are sent to network administrators for immediate action.

cis_azure_6.1.2.5.description: Create an activity log alert for the Create or Update Security Solution event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.5.remediation: Configure an activity log alert in Azure Monitor for modifications to security solutions. Use Azure CLI or PowerShell scripts to deploy this alert and integrate it with security information and event management (SIEM) systems.

cis_azure_6.1.2.6.description: Create an activity log alert for the Delete Security Solution event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.6.remediation: Create an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to monitor deletions of security solutions. Automate this setup with ARM templates and set up notifications to security teams to prevent unauthorized removals.

cis_azure_6.1.2.7.description: Create an activity log alert for the Create or Update SQL Server Firewall Rule event.
cis_azure_6.1.2.7.remediation: Set up an activity log alert in Azure Monitor for changes to SQL Server firewall rules. Use Azure Policy to enforce this alert and configure it to send alerts to database administrators for review.

cis_azure_6.1.2.8.description: Create an activity log alert for the "Delete SQL Server Firewall Rule."
cis_azure_6.1.2.8.remediation: Implement an activity log alert in Azure Monitor to detect deletions of SQL Server firewall rules. Automate this with Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and ensure alerts are routed to security teams for investigation.

cis_azure_6.1.2.9.description: Create an activity log alert for the Create or Update Public IP Addresses rule.
cis_azure_6.1.2.9.remediation: Configure an activity log alert in Azure Monitor for modifications to public IP address rules. Use Azure CLI or ARM templates to automate this and set up notifications to network administrators for oversight.

cis_azure_6.1.3.1.description: Application Insights within Azure act as an Application Performance Monitoring solution providing valuable data into how well an application performs and additional information when performing incident response. The types of log data collected include application metrics, telemetry data, and application trace logging data providing organizations with detailed information about application activity and application transactions. Both data sets help organizations adopt a proactive and retroactive m
cis_azure_6.1.3.1.remediation: Enable Application Insights for all critical applications in Azure to monitor performance and collect telemetry data. Use Azure Policy to enforce Application Insights configuration and integrate logs with central monitoring tools for analysis.

cis_azure_6.1.4.description: Resource Logs capture activity to the data access plane while the Activity log is a subscription-level log for the control plane. Resource-level diagnostic logs provide insight into operations that were performed within that resource itself; for example, reading or updating a secret from a Key Vault. Currently, 95 Azure resources support Azure Monitoring (See the more information section for a complete list), including Network Security Groups, Load Balancers, Key Vault, AD, Logic Apps, and Cosmo
cis_azure_6.1.4.remediation: Enable resource-level diagnostic logging for all Azure services that support it, such as Key Vault and Network Security Groups. Use Azure Policy to enforce logging settings and forward logs to a Log Analytics workspace for centralized analysis.
