cis_azure_6.1.5.description: The use of Basic or Free SKUs in Azure whilst cost effective have significant limitations in terms of what can be monitored and what support can be realized from Microsoft. Typically, these SKUs do not have a service SLA and Microsoft may refuse to provide support for them. Consequently Basic/Free SKUs should never be used for production workloads. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Determining appropriate SKUs depends on
cis_azure_6.1.5.remediation: Avoid using Basic or Free SKUs for production workloads; upgrade to Standard or higher SKUs that support monitoring and SLAs. Use Azure Policy to enforce SKU requirements and regularly audit resources to ensure compliance with production standards.

cis_azure_6.2.description: Resource Manager Locks provide a way for administrators to lock down Azure resources to prevent deletion of, or modifications to, a resource. These locks sit outside of the Role Based Access Controls (RBAC) hierarchy and, when applied, will place restrictions on the resource for all users. These locks are very useful when there is an important resource in a subscription that users should not be able to delete or change. Locks can help prevent accidental and malicious changes or deletion. While a
cis_azure_6.2.remediation: Apply Resource Manager locks (CanNotDelete or ReadOnly) to mission-critical Azure resources to prevent accidental or malicious changes. Use Azure Policy to enforce lock application and regularly review locks to ensure they are appropriately set for high-priority assets.

cis_azure_6.3.remediation: Configure the audit_log_enabled parameter to 'ON' for all MySQL Database Servers in Azure. Use Azure Policy or CLI commands to enforce this setting and monitor compliance through regular audits of server configurations.

cis_azure_6.4.description: Set audit_log_enabled to include CONNECTION on MySQL Servers.
cis_azure_6.4.remediation: Ensure the audit_log_events parameter includes 'CONNECTION' for MySQL Database Servers to log connection attempts. Apply this configuration via Azure Database for MySQL settings and use policy enforcement to maintain logging standards.

cis_azure_7.1.description: Network security groups should be periodically evaluated for port misconfigurations. Where RDP is not explicitly required and narrowly configured for resources attached to a network security group, Internet-level access to Azure resources should be restricted or eliminated.
cis_azure_7.1.remediation: Review and restrict RDP access from the Internet by configuring network security groups to allow only from specific IP ranges or using VPN/private endpoints. Use Azure Policy to enforce NSG rules and regularly audit for open RDP ports to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_azure_7.10.description: Azure Web Application Firewall helps protect applications from common exploits and attacks by inspecting and filtering incoming traffic.
cis_azure_7.10.remediation: Enable Web Application Firewall (WAF) on Azure Application Gateway for all web applications. Configure WAF policies to block common threats and use Azure Policy to enforce WAF deployment across all application gateways.

cis_azure_7.11.description: Protect subnet resources by ensuring subnets are associated with network security groups, which can filter inbound and outbound traffic using security rules.
cis_azure_7.11.remediation: Associate all subnets with network security groups (NSGs) to control traffic flow. Use Azure Policy to enforce NSG associations and define security rules to restrict unnecessary access, regularly reviewing configurations for compliance.

cis_azure_7.12.description: The TLS (Transport Layer Security) protocol secures the transmission of data over the internet using standard encryption technology. Application gateways use TLS 1.2 for the Min protocol version by default and allow for the use of TLS versions 1.0, 1.1, and 1.3. NIST strongly suggests the use of TLS 1.2 and recommends the adoption of TLS 1.3.
cis_azure_7.12.remediation: Set the SSL policy's minimum protocol version to 'TLSv1_2' or higher on Azure Application Gateway. Configure this via the gateway settings and use Azure Policy to enforce strong TLS versions across all application gateways to enhance encryption security.

cis_azure_7.13.description: Enable HTTP/2 for improved performance, efficiency, and security. HTTP/2 protocol support is available to clients that connect to application gateway listeners only. Communication with backend server pools is always HTTP/1.1.
cis_azure_7.13.remediation: Enable HTTP/2 on Azure Application Gateway listeners to benefit from enhanced performance and security features. Configure this setting in the gateway configuration and use policy enforcement to ensure HTTP/2 is enabled for all relevant application gateways.

cis_azure_7.14.description: Enable request body inspection so that the Web Application Firewall evaluates the contents of HTTP message bodies for potential threats.
cis_azure_7.14.remediation: In Azure, configure the Web Application Firewall (WAF) policy on Application Gateway to enable request body inspection via the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform or Bicep to enforce this setting across environments, ensuring all WAF policies inspect HTTP bodies for threats like SQL injection or cross-site scripting.

cis_azure_7.15.description: Enable bot protection on the Web Application Firewall to block or log requests from known malicious IP addresses identified through the Microsoft Threat Intelligence feed.
cis_azure_7.15.remediation: Enable bot protection in the WAF policy on Azure Application Gateway through the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Implement this via IaC tools to automate deployment and ensure consistent configuration, reducing the risk of automated attacks from malicious bots.

cis_azure_7.16.description: Azure Network Security Perimeter creates a logical boundary around Azure platform-as-a-service (PaaS) resources outside of virtual networks. By default, the network security perimeter denies public access to associated PaaS resources, with the ability to define explicit rules for inbound and outbound traffic. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Determining appropriate network security perimeter profiles and resource assig
cis_azure_7.16.remediation: Deploy Azure Network Security Perimeter for PaaS resources using Azure Policy or manual configuration in the Azure portal. Define explicit inbound and outbound rules to restrict access, and use IaC to manage these perimeters consistently. Regularly review and update rules to align with least-privilege principles.

cis_azure_7.2.description: Network security groups should be periodically evaluated for port misconfigurations. Where SSH is not explicitly required and narrowly configured for resources attached to a network security group, Internet-level access to Azure resources should be restricted or eliminated.
cis_azure_7.2.remediation: Review and restrict SSH access from the Internet in Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) by removing unnecessary rules. Use Azure Policy to enforce restrictions and IaC to define NSG rules, ensuring SSH is only allowed from trusted IP ranges or via VPN/private endpoints.

cis_azure_7.3.description: Network security groups should be periodically evaluated for port misconfigurations. Where UDP is not explicitly required and narrowly configured for resources attached to a network security group, Internet-level access to Azure resources should be restricted or eliminated.
cis_azure_7.3.remediation: Audit NSG rules for UDP ports and remove any that allow Internet access unless explicitly required. Implement Azure Policy to block unnecessary UDP rules and use IaC to manage NSG configurations, limiting UDP traffic to specific sources and ports.

cis_azure_7.4.description: Network security groups should be periodically evaluated for port misconfigurations. Where HTTP(S) is not explicitly required and narrowly configured for resources attached to a network security group, Internet-level access to Azure resources should be restricted or eliminated.
cis_azure_7.4.remediation: Evaluate and restrict HTTP(S) access from the Internet in NSGs by configuring rules to allow only necessary traffic. Use Azure Policy to enforce restrictions and IaC to automate NSG management, ensuring web services are protected from unauthorized access.

cis_azure_7.5.description: Network security group flow logs should be enabled and the retention period set to greater than or equal to 90 days. Retirement Notice On September 30, 2027, network security group (NSG) flow logs will be retired. As of June 30, 2025, creating new NSG flow logs is no longer possible. Azure recommends migrating to virtual network flow logs. Review https://azure.microsoft.com/en- us/updates?id=Azure-NSG-flow-logs-Retirement for more information. For virtual network flow logs, consider applying the
cis_azure_7.5.remediation: Enable NSG flow logs with a retention period of at least 90 days via Azure Monitor or migrate to virtual network flow logs as recommended. Use Azure Policy to enforce retention settings and IaC to configure logging, ensuring compliance with audit requirements before the retirement date.

cis_azure_7.6.description: Enable Network Watcher for physical regions in Azure subscriptions.
cis_azure_7.6.remediation: Enable Network Watcher in all used Azure regions through the Azure portal, CLI, or ARM templates. Use IaC to automate deployment and ensure continuous monitoring for network diagnostics and troubleshooting across your subscription.

cis_azure_7.7.description: Public IP Addresses provide tenant accounts with Internet connectivity for resources contained within the tenant. During the creation of certain resources in Azure, a Public IP Address may be created. All Public IP Addresses within the tenant should be periodically reviewed for accuracy and necessity. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Evaluating the appropriateness of public IP addresses requires a manual review, as it d
cis_azure_7.7.remediation: Regularly review and audit public IP addresses in Azure using tools like Azure Policy or manual checks. Remove unnecessary public IPs and use private endpoints or VPNs where possible. Implement governance processes to approve new public IP allocations.

cis_azure_7.8.description: Ensure that virtual network flow logs are retained for greater than or equal to 90 days.
cis_azure_7.8.remediation: Configure virtual network flow logs in Azure Monitor with a retention period of at least 90 days. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and IaC to automate log configuration, ensuring network traffic is logged for security analysis and compliance.

cis_azure_7.9.description: Enable only 'Azure Active Directory' (Microsoft Entra ID) authentication for Azure VPN Gateway point-to-site connections.
cis_azure_7.9.remediation: Set the authentication type to 'Azure Active Directory' only in Azure VPN Gateway point-to-site configurations via the Azure portal or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this setting and disable other authentication methods like certificates to enhance security.

cis_azure_8.1.description: Azure Files offers soft delete for file shares, allowing you to easily recover your data when it is mistakenly deleted by an application or another storage account user.
cis_azure_8.1.remediation: Enable soft delete for Azure File Shares in the storage account settings through the Azure portal, PowerShell, or ARM templates. Use IaC to configure this feature across all storage accounts, ensuring data recovery capabilities and protection against accidental deletions.

cis_azure_8.1.1.1.description: Enable Microsoft Defender CSPM to continuously assess cloud resources for security misconfigurations, compliance risks, and exposure to threats.
cis_azure_8.1.1.1.remediation: Set Microsoft Defender CSPM to 'On' in the Azure Security Center via the portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration and IaC to automate deployment, ensuring continuous security posture management across your Azure environment.

cis_azure_8.1.10.description: Ensure that the latest OS patches for all virtual machines are applied.
cis_azure_8.1.10.remediation: Configure Microsoft Defender for Cloud to check VM operating systems for updates by enabling the update management feature. Use Azure Policy to enforce patch compliance and IaC to automate VM configurations, ensuring regular patching to mitigate vulnerabilities.

cis_azure_8.1.11.description: The Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark (or "MCSB") is an Azure Policy Initiative containing many security policies to evaluate resource configuration against best practice recommendations. If a policy in the MCSB is set with effect type Disabled, it is not evaluated and may prevent administrators from being informed of valuable security recommendations.
cis_azure_8.1.11.remediation: Review Azure Policy assignments to ensure no MCSB policies are set to 'Disabled'. Use the Azure portal or CLI to audit and update policy effects, and implement IaC to manage policy assignments, ensuring all security benchmarks are actively evaluated.

cis_azure_8.1.12.description: Enable security alert emails to subscription owners.
cis_azure_8.1.12.remediation: Configure Microsoft Defender for Cloud to send email notifications to subscription owners by setting 'All users with the following roles' to 'Owner'. Use the Azure portal or ARM templates to enable this, and implement IaC to automate notification settings for consistent alerting.

cis_azure_8.1.13.description: Microsoft Defender for Cloud emails the subscription owners whenever a high-severity alert is triggered for their subscription. You should provide a security contact email address as an additional email address.
cis_azure_8.1.13.remediation: Add a security contact email address in Microsoft Defender for Cloud's notification settings via the Azure portal or CLI. Use IaC to configure this setting and ensure multiple contacts receive alerts for timely incident response.

cis_azure_8.1.14.description: Enables emailing security alerts to the subscription owner or other designated security contact.
cis_azure_8.1.14.remediation: Enable 'Notify about alerts with the following severity (or higher)' in Microsoft Defender for Cloud, setting an appropriate threshold like 'High'. Configure this via the Azure portal or ARM templates, and use IaC to enforce notification policies across subscriptions.

cis_azure_8.1.15.description: Enables emailing attack paths to the subscription owner or other designated security contact.
cis_azure_8.1.15.remediation: Enable 'Notify about attack paths with the following risk level (or higher)' in Microsoft Defender for Cloud, such as 'Medium' or higher. Use the Azure portal or CLI to configure this, and implement IaC to automate settings for consistent attack path notifications.

cis_azure_8.1.16.description: An organization's attack surface is the collection of assets with a public network identifier or URI that an external threat actor can see or access from outside your cloud. It is the set of points on the boundary of a system, a system element, system component, or an environment where an attacker can try to enter, cause an effect on, or extract data from, that system, system element, system component, or environment. The larger the attack surface, the harder it is to protect. This tool can be c
cis_azure_8.1.16.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender External Attack Surface Monitoring (EASM) in the Azure Security Center via the portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this and IaC to automate deployment, helping identify and reduce exposed assets to minimize attack vectors.

cis_azure_8.1.2.1.description: Microsoft Defender for APIs offers full lifecycle protection, detection, and response coverage for APIs. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Due to its potentially high cost, Microsoft Defender for APIs may not be suitable for all environments and should be evaluated carefully before implementation.
cis_azure_8.1.2.1.remediation: Set Microsoft Defender for APIs to 'On' in Azure Security Center after evaluating cost and suitability. Use the Azure portal or CLI to enable it, and consider IaC for configuration management to protect API endpoints from threats and misconfigurations.

cis_azure_8.1.3.1.description: The Defender for Servers plan in Microsoft Defender for Cloud reduces security risk by providing actionable recommendations to improve and remediate machine security posture. Defender for Servers also helps to protect machines against real-time security threats and attacks. Defender for Servers offers two paid plans: Plan 1 The following components are enabled by default: • Log Analytics agent (deprecated) • Endpoint protection Plan 1 also offers the following components, disabled by default: •
cis_azure_8.1.3.1.remediation: Enable Defender for Servers by setting it to 'On' in Microsoft Defender for Cloud via the Azure portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this and IaC to automate deployment, ensuring servers are protected with threat detection and security recommendations.

cis_azure_8.1.3.2.description: Enable vulnerability assessment for machines on both Azure and hybrid (Arc enabled) machines.
cis_azure_8.1.3.2.remediation: Set the 'Vulnerability assessment for machines' component to 'On' in Microsoft Defender for Cloud through the Azure portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration and IaC to automate settings, ensuring regular vulnerability scans across all machines.

cis_azure_8.1.3.3.description: The Endpoint protection component enables Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (formerly 'Advanced Threat Protection' or 'ATP' or 'WDATP' - see additional info) to communicate with Microsoft Defender for Cloud. IMPORTANT: When enabling integration between DfE & DfC it needs to be taken into account that this will have some side effects that may be undesirable. 1. For server 2019 & above if defender is installed (default for these server SKUs) this will trigger a deployment of the new unified agent an
cis_azure_8.1.3.3.remediation: Enable the 'Endpoint protection' component in Microsoft Defender for Cloud by setting it to 'On' via the Azure portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this and IaC to manage configurations, ensuring endpoint security integration and protection against threats.

cis_azure_8.1.3.4.description: Using disk snapshots, the agentless scanner scans for installed software, vulnerabilities, and plain text secrets.
cis_azure_8.1.3.4.remediation: Set the 'Agentless scanning for machines' component to 'On' in Microsoft Defender for Cloud through the Azure portal or CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting and IaC to automate deployment, enabling vulnerability and secret scanning without installing agents on machines.

cis_azure_8.1.3.5.description: File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) is a feature that monitors critical system files in Windows or Linux for potential signs of attack or compromise.
cis_azure_8.1.3.5.remediation: Enable File Integrity Monitoring in Microsoft Defender for Cloud via the Azure Portal or Azure Policy. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or ARM templates to enforce this setting across all subscriptions and resources, ensuring continuous monitoring and alerting for unauthorized file changes.

cis_azure_8.1.4.1.description: Microsoft Defender for Containers helps improve, monitor, and maintain the security of containerized assets—including Kubernetes clusters, nodes, workloads, container registries, and images—across multi-cloud and on-premises environments. By default, when enabling the plan through the Azure Portal, Microsoft Defender for Containers automatically configures the following components: • Agentless scanning for machines • Defender sensor for runtime protection • Azure Policy for enforcing security be
cis_azure_8.1.4.1.remediation: Activate Microsoft Defender for Containers in the Azure Portal under Defender for Cloud settings. Apply Azure Policy definitions to enforce this across all container registries and Kubernetes clusters, and integrate with CI/CD pipelines to scan images for vulnerabilities before deployment.

cis_azure_8.1.5.1.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for Storage enables threat detection for Storage, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in the Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.5.1.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for Storage via the Azure Portal or Azure Policy to protect all storage accounts. Use IaC to automate this configuration, and set up alerts in Microsoft Sentinel or email notifications to monitor for suspicious activities like unauthorized access attempts.

cis_azure_8.1.5.2.description: After enabling Microsoft Defender for Storage, configure an alert monitoring and response process to ensure that alerts are actioned in a timely manner. Integrate with SIEM solutions like Microsoft Sentinel, or configure email/webhook notifications to security teams.
cis_azure_8.1.5.2.remediation: Set up alert export from Microsoft Defender for Storage to SIEM tools like Microsoft Sentinel using Azure Monitor. Configure email or webhook notifications for critical alerts, and establish an incident response process to investigate and remediate threats promptly.

cis_azure_8.1.6.1.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for App Service enables threat detection for App Service, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in the Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.6.1.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for App Service through the Azure Portal or Azure Policy. Use IaC to enforce this setting across all App Service plans, and regularly review threat detection alerts to identify and mitigate attacks like SQL injection or DDoS attempts.

cis_azure_8.1.7.1.description: Microsoft Defender for Azure Cosmos DB scans all incoming network requests for threats to your Azure Cosmos DB resources.
cis_azure_8.1.7.1.remediation: Activate Microsoft Defender for Azure Cosmos DB in the Azure Portal under Defender for Cloud. Apply Azure Policy to ensure all Cosmos DB accounts are protected, and monitor alerts for anomalous access patterns or potential data exfiltration attempts.

cis_azure_8.1.7.2.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for Open-source relational databases enables threat detection for Open-source relational databases, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in the Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.7.2.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for Open-source relational databases via the Azure Portal or Azure Policy. Use IaC to automate this for all database instances, and configure logging to track and respond to threats like brute-force attacks or data breaches.

cis_azure_8.1.7.3.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for Azure SQL Databases enables threat detection for Managed Instance Azure SQL databases, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.7.3.remediation: Activate Microsoft Defender for Azure SQL Databases in the Azure Portal under Defender for Cloud settings. Apply Azure Policy to enforce this across all SQL databases, and set up alerting for suspicious activities such as SQL injection or unauthorized data access.

cis_azure_8.1.7.4.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines enables threat detection for SQL servers on machines, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.7.4.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for SQL servers on machines through the Azure Portal or Azure Policy. Use IaC to ensure all virtual machines with SQL Server are protected, and monitor alerts for potential threats like credential theft or malware infections.

cis_azure_8.1.8.1.description: Turning on Microsoft Defender for Key Vault enables threat detection for Key Vault, providing threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and behavior analytics in the Microsoft Defender for Cloud.
cis_azure_8.1.8.1.remediation: Activate Microsoft Defender for Key Vault in the Azure Portal under Defender for Cloud. Apply Azure Policy to enforce this across all key vaults, and configure alerts for unusual access patterns or attempts to tamper with keys and secrets.

cis_azure_8.1.9.1.description: Microsoft Defender for Resource Manager scans incoming administrative requests to change your infrastructure from both CLI and the Azure portal.
cis_azure_8.1.9.1.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for Resource Manager via the Azure Portal or Azure Policy. Use IaC to automate this configuration, and monitor alerts for suspicious administrative actions that could indicate insider threats or compromised credentials.

cis_azure_8.2.description: Permissions for NFS file shares are enforced by the client OS rather than by the Azure Files service. Root squash is an administrative security feature in NFS that prevents unauthorized root-level access to the NFS server by client machines. This functionality is an important part of protecting user data and system settings from manipulation by untrusted or compromised clients.
cis_azure_8.2.remediation: Configure root squash for NFS file shares in Azure Storage accounts via the Azure Portal or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this setting, and regularly audit NFS configurations to ensure root access is restricted and only authorized users can modify files.

cis_azure_8.2.1.description: Microsoft Defender for IoT acts as a central security hub for IoT devices within your organization.
cis_azure_8.2.1.remediation: Enable Microsoft Defender for IoT Hub in the Azure Portal under Defender for Cloud settings. Apply Azure Policy to protect all IoT hubs, and integrate with security monitoring tools to detect and respond to threats like device tampering or data exfiltration from IoT networks.

cis_azure_8.3.description: Ensure that SMB file shares are configured to use the latest supported SMB protocol version. Keeping the SMB protocol updated helps mitigate risks associated with older SMB versions, which may contain vulnerabilities and lack essential security controls.
cis_azure_8.3.remediation: Set SMB protocol version to SMB 3.1.1 or higher for all Azure Storage accounts via the Azure Portal or ARM templates. Use IaC to enforce this configuration, and regularly update storage settings to leverage the latest security features like encryption and authentication improvements.

cis_azure_8.3.1.description: Ensure that all Keys in Role Based Access Control (RBAC) Azure Key Vaults have an expiration date set.
cis_azure_8.3.1.remediation: Configure expiration dates for all keys in RBAC-enabled Azure Key Vaults using the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce key expiration policies, and implement automated key rotation to replace expired keys without disrupting services.

cis_azure_8.3.10.description: Azure Key Vault Managed HSM is a fully managed, highly available, single-tenant cloud service that safeguards cryptographic keys using FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs. Note: While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual, as this recommendation to use Managed HSM applies only to scenarios where specific regulatory and compliance requirements mandate the use of a dedicated hardware security module.
cis_azure_8.3.10.remediation: Evaluate regulatory requirements and use Azure Key Vault Managed HSM for scenarios needing FIPS 140-2 Level 3 compliance. Provision Managed HSM via the Azure Portal or ARM templates, and migrate keys from standard key vaults using Azure Key Vault backup and restore procedures.

cis_azure_8.3.11.description: Restrict the validity period of certificates stored in Azure Key Vault to 12 months or less.
cis_azure_8.3.11.remediation: Set certificate validity periods to 12 months or less in Azure Key Vault using the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce this limit, and implement automated certificate renewal processes to avoid service disruptions due to expired certificates.

cis_azure_8.3.2.description: Ensure that all Keys in Non Role Based Access Control (RBAC) Azure Key Vaults have an expiration date set.
cis_azure_8.3.2.remediation: Configure expiration dates for all keys in non-RBAC Azure Key Vaults via the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Apply Azure Policy to enforce key expiration, and use monitoring tools to alert on keys nearing expiration to facilitate timely rotation.

cis_azure_8.3.3.description: Ensure that all Secrets in Role Based Access Control (RBAC) Azure Key Vaults have an expiration date set.
cis_azure_8.3.3.remediation: Set expiration dates for all secrets in RBAC-enabled Azure Key Vaults using the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Use Azure Policy to enforce secret expiration policies, and automate secret rotation to maintain security without manual intervention.

cis_azure_8.3.4.description: Ensure that all Secrets in Non Role Based Access Control (RBAC) Azure Key Vaults have an expiration date set.
cis_azure_8.3.4.remediation: Configure expiration dates for all secrets in non-RBAC Azure Key Vaults via the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Apply Azure Policy to enforce this, and implement processes to regularly review and update secrets to prevent unauthorized access from stale credentials.

cis_azure_8.3.5.description: Key vaults contain object keys, secrets, and certificates. Deletion of a key vault can cause immediate data loss or loss of security functions (authentication, validation, verification, non-repudiation, etc.) supported by the key vault objects. It is recommended the key vault be made recoverable by enabling the "purge protection" function. This is to prevent the loss of encrypted data, including storage accounts, SQL databases, and/or dependent services provided by key vault objects (keys, secre
cis_azure_8.3.5.remediation: Enable purge protection for all Azure Key Vaults via the Azure Portal or ARM templates during creation. Use IaC to enforce this setting, and ensure soft delete is also enabled to allow recovery of deleted vaults, protecting against accidental or malicious deletions.

cis_azure_8.3.6.description: The recommended way to access Key Vaults is to use the Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions model. Azure RBAC is an authorization system built on Azure Resource Manager that provides fine-grained access management of Azure resources. It allows users to manage Key, Secret, and Certificate permissions. It provides one place to manage all permissions across all key vaults.
cis_azure_8.3.6.remediation: Enable RBAC for Azure Key Vault in the Azure Portal under access policies. Use Azure Policy to enforce RBAC across all key vaults, and assign least-privilege roles to users and applications to minimize the risk of unauthorized access to sensitive data.

cis_azure_8.3.7.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks. When a private endpoint is configured on a key vault, connections from Azure resources within the same subnet will use its private IP address. However, network traffic from the public internet can still connect to the key vault's public endpoint (mykeyvault.vault.azure.net) using its public IP address unless public
cis_azure_8.3.7.remediation: Disable public network access for Azure Key Vault via the Azure Portal or ARM templates. Configure private endpoints to allow secure access from within your VNet, and use network security groups to restrict traffic, ensuring key vaults are only accessible through private IP addresses.

cis_azure_8.3.8.description: Use private endpoints to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote infrastructures together. This
cis_azure_8.3.8.remediation: Configure private endpoints for Azure Key Vault using the Azure Portal or ARM templates. Integrate with Azure Private Link to ensure encrypted communication, and use IaC to automate this setup across all key vaults, reducing exposure to public internet threats.

cis_azure_8.3.9.description: Automated cryptographic key rotation in Key Vault allows users to configure Key Vault to automatically generate a new key version at a specified frequency. A key rotation policy can be defined for each individual key.
cis_azure_8.3.9.remediation: Enable automatic key rotation in Azure Key Vault via the Azure Portal or Azure CLI. Define rotation policies for each key based on compliance requirements, and use Azure Policy to enforce rotation schedules, ensuring keys are regularly updated to mitigate cryptographic attacks.

cis_azure_8.4.description: Implement SMB channel encryption with AES-256-GCM for SMB file shares to ensure data confidentiality and integrity in transit. This method offers strong protection against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks, safeguarding sensitive information.
cis_azure_8.4.remediation: Enable SMB channel encryption with AES-256-GCM or higher in Azure Storage Account settings for SMB file shares. Use Azure Policy or Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or ARM templates to enforce this configuration across all storage accounts. Regularly audit and monitor for compliance to prevent data exposure in transit.

cis_azure_8.4.1.description: The Azure Bastion service allows secure remote access to Azure Virtual Machines over the Internet without exposing remote access protocol ports and services directly to the Internet. The Azure Bastion service provides this access using TLS over 443/TCP, and subscribes to hardened configurations within an organization's Azure Active Directory service.
cis_azure_8.4.1.remediation: Deploy an Azure Bastion Host in your virtual network to provide secure RDP/SSH access to VMs without exposing them to the internet. Configure it using IaC tools like Azure Resource Manager templates or Terraform. Ensure Bastion is integrated with Azure AD for authentication and monitor its usage to maintain secure access controls.

cis_azure_8.5.description: Azure DDoS Network Protection defends resources in virtual networks against distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks. While an automated assessment procedure exists for this recommendation, the assessment status remains manual. Determining the appropriateness of enabling Azure DDoS Network Protection depends on the context and requirements of each organization and environment.
cis_azure_8.5.remediation: Enable Azure DDoS Network Protection on critical virtual networks to mitigate DDoS attacks. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting across all VNets. Regularly review and adjust protection levels based on organizational risk assessments and network traffic patterns to ensure resilience against attacks.

cis_azure_9.1.1.description: Azure Files offers soft delete for file shares, allowing you to easily recover your data when it is mistakenly deleted by an application or another storage account user.
cis_azure_9.1.1.remediation: Enable soft delete for Azure File Shares in storage account settings with a retention period (e.g., 7-14 days). Use Azure Policy or IaC to apply this configuration uniformly. Regularly test recovery procedures to ensure data can be restored in case of accidental deletion.

cis_azure_9.1.2.description: Ensure that SMB file shares are configured to use the latest supported SMB protocol version. Keeping the SMB protocol updated helps mitigate risks associated with older SMB versions, which may contain vulnerabilities and lack essential security controls.
cis_azure_9.1.2.remediation: Set the SMB protocol version to SMB 3.1.1 or higher in Azure Storage Account settings for SMB file shares. Enforce this via Azure Policy or IaC tools. Monitor for updates and adjust configurations as new versions are released to maintain security against known vulnerabilities.

cis_azure_9.1.3.description: Implement SMB channel encryption with AES-256-GCM for SMB file shares to ensure data confidentiality and integrity in transit. This method offers strong protection against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks, safeguarding sensitive information.
cis_azure_9.1.3.remediation: Configure SMB channel encryption to use AES-256-GCM or higher in Azure Storage Account settings. Use Azure Policy to enforce encryption standards across all file shares. Regularly audit configurations to ensure no gaps in data protection during transit.

cis_azure_9.2.1.description: Blobs in Azure storage accounts may contain sensitive or personal data, such as ePHI or financial information. Data that is erroneously modified or deleted by an application or a user can lead to data loss or unavailability. It is recommended that soft delete be enabled on Azure storage accounts with blob storage to allow for the preservation and recovery of data when blobs or blob snapshots are deleted.
cis_azure_9.2.1.remediation: Enable soft delete for blobs in Azure Blob Storage with a defined retention period. Apply this setting via Azure Policy or IaC. Test recovery processes periodically to ensure data integrity and availability in case of accidental deletions.

cis_azure_9.2.2.description: Containers in Azure storage accounts may contain sensitive or personal data, such as ePHI or financial information. Data that is erroneously modified or deleted by an application or a user can lead to data loss or unavailability. It is recommended that soft delete for containers be enabled on Azure storage accounts with blob storage to allow for the preservation and recovery of data when containers are deleted.
cis_azure_9.2.2.remediation: Enable soft delete for containers in Azure Blob Storage to protect against accidental deletion. Use Azure Policy to enforce this configuration. Set appropriate retention policies and verify recovery capabilities through regular testing.

cis_azure_9.2.3.description: Enabling blob versioning allows for the automatic retention of previous versions of objects. With blob versioning enabled, earlier versions of a blob are accessible for data recovery in the event of modifications or deletions.
cis_azure_9.2.3.remediation: Enable blob versioning on Azure Blob Storage accounts to retain previous versions of objects. Configure this via Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC tools. Monitor storage costs and set lifecycle policies to manage version retention effectively.

cis_azure_9.3.1.1.description: Access Keys authenticate application access requests to data contained in Storage Accounts. A periodic rotation of these keys is recommended to ensure that potentially compromised keys cannot result in a long-term exploitable credential. The "Rotation Reminder" is an automatic reminder feature for a manual procedure.
cis_azure_9.3.1.1.remediation: Enable key rotation reminders in Azure Storage Account settings to prompt manual key regeneration. Establish a procedure for rotating keys every 90 days or as per organizational policy. Use Azure Monitor or alerts to track reminders and ensure compliance.

cis_azure_9.3.1.2.description: For increased security, regenerate storage account access keys periodically.
cis_azure_9.3.1.2.remediation: Implement a process to regenerate storage account access keys regularly (e.g., every 90 days). Use Azure Automation or scripts to automate key rotation where possible. Update applications and services with new keys to avoid disruptions.

cis_azure_9.3.1.3.description: Every secure request to an Azure Storage account must be authorized. By default, requests can be authorized with either Microsoft Entra credentials or by using the account access key for Shared Key authorization.
cis_azure_9.3.1.3.remediation: Disable shared key access for Azure Storage Accounts to enforce Microsoft Entra ID-based authentication. Use Azure Policy to block shared key authorization. Migrate applications to use managed identities or service principals for secure access.

cis_azure_9.3.10.description: Adding an Azure Resource Manager ReadOnly lock can prevent users from accidentally or maliciously deleting a storage account, modifying its properties and containers, or creating access assignments. The lock must be removed before the storage account can be deleted or updated. It provides more protection than a CannotDelete-type of resource manager lock. This feature prevents POST operations on a storage account and containers to the Azure Resource Manager control plane, management.azure.com. Bl
cis_azure_9.3.10.remediation: Apply Azure Resource Manager ReadOnly locks to critical storage accounts to prevent unauthorized modifications. Use IaC or Azure CLI to set locks. Ensure proper RBAC permissions are in place so only authorized users can remove locks when necessary.

cis_azure_9.3.11.description: Geo-redundant storage (GRS) in Azure replicates data three times within the primary region using locally redundant storage (LRS) and asynchronously copies it to a secondary region hundreds of miles away. This setup ensures high availability and resilience by providing 16 nines (99.99999999999999%) durability over a year, safeguarding data against regional outages.
cis_azure_9.3.11.remediation: Configure critical Azure Storage Accounts with geo-redundant storage (GRS) for high availability. Use Azure Policy to enforce GRS on storage accounts containing sensitive data. Regularly test failover procedures to ensure data recovery in case of regional failures.

cis_azure_9.3.2.1.description: Use private endpoints for your Azure Storage accounts to allow clients and services to securely access data located over a network via an encrypted Private Link. To do this, the private endpoint uses an IP address from the VNet for each service. Network traffic between disparate services securely traverses encrypted over the VNet. This VNet can also link addressing space, extending your network and accessing resources on it. Similarly, it can be a tunnel through public networks to connect remote
cis_azure_9.3.2.1.remediation: Deploy private endpoints for Azure Storage Accounts to restrict access to trusted virtual networks. Configure private endpoints using Azure Portal, CLI, or IaC. Integrate with network security groups and Azure Private Link to ensure encrypted, private connectivity.

cis_azure_9.3.2.2.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks. Disallowing public network access for a storage account overrides the public access settings for individual containers in that storage account.
cis_azure_9.3.2.2.remediation: Disable public network access on Azure Storage Accounts to limit exposure. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Rely on private endpoints or VNet service endpoints for secure access, and monitor for any misconfigurations that might re-enable public access.

cis_azure_9.3.2.3.description: Restricting default network access helps to provide a new layer of security, since storage accounts accept connections from clients on any network. To limit access to selected networks, the default action must be changed.
cis_azure_9.3.2.3.remediation: Set the default network access rule for storage accounts to 'Deny' to block all traffic by default. Use Azure Policy to apply this rule. Then, explicitly allow access from specific IP ranges or virtual networks using network rules to maintain least-privilege access.

cis_azure_9.3.3.1.description: When this property is enabled, the Azure portal authorizes requests to blobs, files, queues, and tables with Microsoft Entra ID by default.
cis_azure_9.3.3.1.remediation: Enable 'Default to Microsoft Entra authorization in the Azure portal' for storage accounts to enforce Entra ID-based authentication. Configure this via Azure Portal or CLI. Ensure users and applications have appropriate Entra ID permissions to access storage resources securely.

cis_azure_9.3.4.remediation: Enable 'Secure transfer required' on Azure Storage Accounts to enforce HTTPS for all data transfers. Use Azure Policy to mandate this setting across all storage accounts. Monitor for non-compliant accounts and remediate to prevent unencrypted data exposure.

cis_azure_9.3.5.description: NOTE: This recommendation assumes that the Public network access parameter is set to Enabled from selected virtual networks and IP addresses. Please ensure the prerequisite recommendation has been implemented before proceeding: • Ensure Default Network Access Rule for Storage Accounts is Set to Deny If the Allow Azure services on the trusted services list to access this storage account exception is enabled, the following services are granted access to the storage account: • Azure Backup, • Azure
cis_azure_9.3.5.remediation: Enable 'Allow Azure services on the trusted services list to access this storage account' only after setting default network access to deny. Use Azure Policy to manage this exception. Regularly review the trusted services list and adjust as needed to maintain secure access for authorized Azure services.

cis_azure_9.3.6.description: In some cases, Azure Storage sets the minimum TLS version to be version 1.0 by default. TLS 1.0 is a legacy version and has known vulnerabilities. This minimum TLS version can be configured to be later protocols such as TLS 1.2.
cis_azure_9.3.6.remediation: Set the minimum TLS version to 1.2 or higher for Azure Storage Accounts to protect against weak encryption. Enforce this via Azure Policy or IaC. Regularly audit configurations to ensure compliance and disable older TLS versions to mitigate security risks.

cis_azure_9.3.7.description: Cross Tenant Replication in Azure allows data to be replicated across multiple Azure tenants. While this feature can be beneficial for data sharing and availability, it also poses a significant security risk if not properly managed. Unauthorized data access, data leakage, and compliance violations are potential risks. Disabling Cross Tenant Replication ensures that data is not inadvertently replicated across different tenant boundaries without explicit authorization.
cis_azure_9.3.7.remediation: Disable Cross Tenant Replication on Azure Storage Accounts unless explicitly required for business purposes. Use Azure Policy to block this feature. If enabled, implement strict RBAC and audit logging to monitor replication activities and prevent unauthorized data access across tenants.

cis_azure_9.3.8.description: The Azure Storage setting ‘Allow Blob Anonymous Access’ (aka "allowBlobPublicAccess") controls whether anonymous access is allowed for blob data in a storage account. When this property is set to True, it enables public read access to blob data, which can be convenient for sharing data but may carry security risks. When set to False, it disallows public access to blob data, providing a more secure storage environment.
cis_azure_9.3.8.remediation: Disable 'Allow Blob Anonymous Access' on all Azure Storage Accounts to prevent public exposure of blob data. Use Azure Policy to enforce this setting. Regularly scan for misconfigured accounts and implement network restrictions to block anonymous access, ensuring data is only accessible via authenticated methods.

cis_azure_9.3.9.description: Azure Resource Manager CannotDelete (Delete) locks can prevent users from accidentally or maliciously deleting a storage account. This feature ensures that while the Storage account can still be modified or used, deletion of the Storage account resource requires removal of the lock by a user with appropriate permissions. This feature is a protective control for the availability of data. By ensuring that a storage account or its parent resource group cannot be deleted without first removing the l
cis_azure_9.3.9.remediation: Apply Azure Resource Manager Delete locks to critical storage accounts to prevent accidental deletion. Use IaC or Azure CLI to set locks. Ensure RBAC permissions are configured so only authorized administrators can remove locks, and regularly review lock status to maintain protection.

cis_gcp_1.1.description: Use corporate login credentials instead of consumer accounts, such as Gmail accounts.
cis_gcp_1.1.remediation: Enforce the use of corporate credentials (e.g., Google Workspace) for all GCP access by configuring Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies. Use Google Cloud Identity to manage users and groups. Regularly audit IAM roles to ensure no consumer accounts have access, and implement SSO integration for centralized authentication.

cis_gcp_1.10.description: Google Cloud Key Management Service stores cryptographic keys in a hierarchical structure designed for useful and elegant access control management. The format for the rotation schedule depends on the client library that is used. For the gcloud command-line tool, the next rotation time must be in ISO or RFC3339 format, and the rotation period must be in the form INTEGER[UNIT], where units can be one of seconds (s), minutes (m), hours (h) or days (d).
cis_gcp_1.10.remediation: Enable automatic rotation for KMS encryption keys with a period of 90 days or less using Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or Terraform. Configure the rotation schedule via the cryptoKey resource, ensuring it aligns with security policies. Regularly audit key rotation status via Cloud Monitoring or CSPM tools to enforce compliance.

cis_gcp_1.11.description: It is recommended that the principle of 'Separation of Duties' is enforced while assigning KMS related roles to users.
cis_gcp_1.11.remediation: Assign KMS roles (e.g., Cloud KMS Admin, CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter) to users based on least privilege, avoiding combined admin and user permissions. Use IAM conditions or custom roles to restrict access. Implement RBAC policies in Terraform or Google Cloud Console, and regularly review IAM assignments with tools like Policy Analyzer.

cis_gcp_1.12.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. Unused keys with their permissions in tact may still exist within a project. Keys are insecure because they can be viewed publicly, such as from within a browser, or they can be accessed on a device where the key resides. It is recommended to use standard authentication flow instead.
cis_gcp_1.12.remediation: Delete unused API keys via Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or Terraform. Prefer service accounts with IAM roles or OAuth for authentication. Regularly audit API key usage with Cloud Monitoring or CSPM scans, and enforce policies to restrict key creation to necessary services only.

cis_gcp_1.13.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. In this case, unrestricted keys are insecure because they can be viewed publicly, such as from within a browser, or they can be accessed on a device where the key resides. It is recommended to restrict API key usage to trusted hosts, HTTP referrers and apps. It is recommended to use the more secure standard authentication flow instead.
cis_gcp_1.13.remediation: Configure API key restrictions to allow only specific hosts, HTTP referrers, or apps via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Use Terraform to enforce these restrictions in IaC. Regularly review and update restrictions, and consider migrating to service accounts or OAuth for enhanced security.

cis_gcp_1.14.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. API keys are always at risk because they can be viewed publicly, such as from within a browser, or they can be accessed on a device where the key resides. It is recommended to restrict API keys to use (call) only APIs required by an application.
cis_gcp_1.14.remediation: Limit API keys to specific APIs needed by the application using Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Define these restrictions in Terraform for consistency. Audit API key permissions regularly and revoke unnecessary access, preferring service accounts with scoped IAM roles for better security.

cis_gcp_1.15.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. If they are in use it is recommended to rotate API keys every 90 days.
cis_gcp_1.15.remediation: Implement a key rotation policy to rotate API keys every 90 days using automation scripts, Google Cloud Console, or CSPM tools. Document the rotation process and monitor compliance via Cloud Monitoring. Consider replacing API keys with more secure authentication methods like service accounts to reduce risk.

cis_gcp_1.16.description: It is recommended that Essential Contacts is configured to designate email addresses for Google Cloud services to notify of important technical or security information.
cis_gcp_1.16.remediation: Configure Essential Contacts in Google Cloud Console or via gcloud CLI to set email addresses for notifications on security and technical issues. Use Terraform to manage this configuration in IaC. Regularly verify contact details are up-to-date and that notifications are being received to ensure timely response to alerts.

cis_gcp_1.17.description: Google Cloud Functions allow you to host serverless code that is executed when an event is triggered, without the requiring the management a host operating system. These functions can also store environment variables to be used by the code that may contain authentication or other information that needs to remain confidential.
cis_gcp_1.17.remediation: Use Google Secret Manager to store sensitive data instead of plaintext environment variables in Cloud Functions. Update function code to fetch secrets at runtime via the Secret Manager API. Implement this in Terraform by configuring secret resources and IAM permissions, and audit functions for compliance regularly.

cis_gcp_1.2.description: Setup multi-factor authentication for Google Cloud Platform accounts.
cis_gcp_1.2.remediation: Enable MFA for all non-service accounts via Google Admin Console or IAM settings. Enforce MFA through organizational policies and monitor compliance with CSPM tools. Use Terraform to set IAM conditions requiring MFA, and regularly review user accounts to ensure MFA is active and not bypassed.

cis_gcp_1.3.description: Setup Security Key Enforcement for Google Cloud Platform admin accounts.
cis_gcp_1.3.remediation: Enforce security keys (e.g., FIDO2) for admin accounts using Google Admin Console or IAM conditions. Configure this via Terraform by setting IAM policies that require security keys. Regularly audit admin accounts to ensure compliance and consider using Context-Aware Access to add additional layers of security.

cis_gcp_1.4.description: User-managed service accounts should not have user-managed keys.
cis_gcp_1.4.remediation: Use only GCP-managed keys for service accounts, avoiding user-managed keys. Configure service accounts to use short-lived credentials or workload identity. In Terraform, specify key types as 'SYSTEM_MANAGED' and audit existing keys via gcloud CLI or CSPM tools to remove any user-managed keys.

cis_gcp_1.5.description: A service account is a special Google account that belongs to an application or a VM, instead of to an individual end-user. The application uses the service account to call the service's Google API so that users aren't directly involved. It's recommended not to use admin access for ServiceAccount.
cis_gcp_1.5.remediation: Assign least privilege IAM roles to service accounts, avoiding admin roles like 'Owner' or 'Editor'. Use custom roles with minimal permissions in Terraform or Google Cloud Console. Regularly review service account permissions with IAM Recommender and Policy Analyzer to enforce compliance.

cis_gcp_1.6.description: It is recommended to assign the Service Account User (iam.serviceAccountUser) and Service Account Token Creator (iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator) roles to a user for a specific service account rather than assigning the role to a user at project level.
cis_gcp_1.6.remediation: Assign Service Account User and Token Creator roles at the service account level, not project-wide, using IAM conditions in Google Cloud Console or Terraform. Implement RBAC policies to restrict access, and audit IAM assignments regularly to ensure roles are scoped appropriately to individual service accounts.

cis_gcp_1.7.description: Service Account keys consist of a key ID (Private_key_Id) and Private key, which are used to sign programmatic requests users make to Google cloud services accessible to that particular service account. It is recommended that all Service Account keys are regularly rotated.
cis_gcp_1.7.remediation: Rotate user-managed or external service account keys every 90 days or less using automation scripts, Google Cloud Console, or CSPM tools. Define key rotation policies in Terraform and monitor key age via Cloud Monitoring. Prefer using GCP-managed keys or workload identity to minimize key management overhead.

cis_gcp_1.8.description: It is recommended that the principle of 'Separation of Duties' is enforced while assigning service-account related roles to users.
cis_gcp_1.8.remediation: Enforce separation of duties by assigning service account roles (e.g., Service Account Admin, User) to different users based on least privilege. Use IAM conditions and custom roles in Terraform or Google Cloud Console to restrict combined permissions. Regularly audit role assignments with Policy Analyzer to ensure compliance.

cis_gcp_1.9.description: It is recommended that the IAM policy on Cloud KMS cryptokeys should restrict anonymous and/or public access.
cis_gcp_1.9.remediation: Restrict IAM policies on KMS cryptokeys to specific users or service accounts, removing 'allUsers' or 'allAuthenticatedUsers' permissions. Configure this via Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or Terraform. Regularly audit key policies with CSPM tools and use IAM Recommender to identify and fix overly permissive access.

cis_gcp_2.1.description: It is recommended that Cloud Audit Logging is configured to track all admin activities and read, write access to user data.
cis_gcp_2.1.remediation: Enable Cloud Audit Logging for all services (Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event) via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Configure log sinks to export logs to a SIEM or Cloud Storage. Use Terraform to set up audit configurations and regularly review logs to ensure comprehensive coverage and compliance.

cis_gcp_2.10.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for Cloud Storage Bucket IAM changes.
cis_gcp_2.10.remediation: Create a log metric filter for Cloud Storage IAM permission changes and set up alerts via Cloud Monitoring. Use Terraform to define the metric and alerting policies. Configure notifications to email or Pub/Sub, and regularly test alerts to ensure they trigger on unauthorized IAM modifications.

cis_gcp_2.11.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for SQL instance configuration changes.
cis_gcp_2.11.remediation: Set up a log metric filter for SQL instance configuration changes and create alerts in Cloud Monitoring. Implement this in Terraform for consistency. Ensure alerts are sent to relevant teams via email or Slack, and review alert logs periodically to detect and respond to suspicious changes quickly.

cis_gcp_2.12.description: Cloud DNS logging records the queries from the name servers within your VPC to Stackdriver. Logged queries can come from Compute Engine VMs, GKE containers, or other GCP resources provisioned within the VPC.
cis_gcp_2.12.remediation: Enable Cloud DNS logging for all VPC networks via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Use Terraform to configure logging in managed zones. Regularly review DNS logs for anomalies and ensure logs are exported to a SIEM for analysis to enhance network security monitoring.

cis_gcp_2.13.description: GCP Cloud Asset Inventory is services that provides a historical view of GCP resources and IAM policies through a time-series database. The information recorded includes metadata on Google Cloud resources, metadata on policies set on Google Cloud projects or resources, and runtime information gathered within a Google Cloud resource. Cloud Asset Inventory Service (CAIS) API enablement is not required for operation of the service, but rather enables the mechanism for searching/exporting CAIS asset
cis_gcp_2.13.remediation: Enable Cloud Asset Inventory via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Configure export schedules to Cloud Storage or BigQuery for analysis. Use Terraform to manage asset inventory settings and regularly review asset data to track resource changes and ensure compliance with security policies.

cis_gcp_2.14.description: GCP Access Transparency provides audit logs for all actions that Google personnel take in your Google Cloud resources.
cis_gcp_2.14.remediation: Enable Access Transparency in Google Cloud Console or via gcloud CLI. Configure log exports to a SIEM for monitoring. Use Terraform to set up Access Transparency settings and regularly review logs to track Google support access, ensuring transparency and compliance with internal security policies.

cis_gcp_2.15.description: GCP Access Approval enables you to require your organizations' explicit approval whenever Google support try to access your projects. You can then select users within your organization who can approve these requests through giving them a security role in IAM. All access requests display which Google Employee requested them in an email or Pub/Sub message that you can choose to Approve. This adds an additional control and logging of who in your organization approved/denied these requests.
cis_gcp_2.15.remediation: Enable Access Approval via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI. Assign approver roles to trusted users in IAM. Use Terraform to configure Access Approval settings and set up notifications via email or Pub/Sub. Regularly review approval logs to ensure only authorized access is granted by Google support.

cis_gcp_2.16.description: Logging enabled on a HTTPS Load Balancer will show all network traffic and its destination.
cis_gcp_2.16.remediation: Enable logging for HTTP(S) Load Balancers in Google Cloud Console or via gcloud CLI. Configure log exports to Cloud Storage or a SIEM for analysis. Use Terraform to set logging options in backend service configurations and regularly review logs to monitor traffic patterns and detect potential security issues.

cis_gcp_2.2.description: It is recommended to create a sink that will export copies of all the log entries. This can help aggregate logs from multiple projects and export them to a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM).
cis_gcp_2.2.remediation: Create log sinks for all log entries to export to destinations like Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub. Use Terraform to define sink configurations across projects. Ensure sinks are encrypted and regularly verify that logs are being exported correctly to support SIEM integration and comprehensive security monitoring.

cis_gcp_2.3.description: Enabling retention policies on log buckets will protect logs stored in cloud storage buckets from being overwritten or accidentally deleted. It is recommended to set up retention policies and configure Bucket Lock on all storage buckets that are used as log sinks.
cis_gcp_2.3.remediation: Configure retention policies and Bucket Lock on Cloud Storage buckets used for log exports via Terraform or GCP Console. Use gsutil commands to set retention periods and lock policies to prevent deletion. Regularly audit buckets to ensure compliance with data retention requirements.

cis_gcp_2.4.description: In order to prevent unnecessary project ownership assignments to users/service-accounts and further misuses of projects and resources, all roles/Owner assignments should be monitored. Members (users/Service-Accounts) with a role assignment to primitive role roles/Owner are project owners. The project owner has all the privileges on the project the role belongs to. These are summarized below: • All viewer permissions on all GCP Services within the project • Permissions for actions that modify the project.
cis_gcp_2.4.remediation: Create log metric filters and alerts in Cloud Monitoring for project ownership changes. Use IAM policies to restrict Owner role assignments and implement least privilege. Automate alerts via Cloud Functions or third-party tools to notify security teams of any changes.

cis_gcp_2.5.description: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) services write audit log entries to the Admin Activity and Data Access logs to help answer the questions of, "who did what, where, and when?" within GCP projects. Cloud audit logging records information includes the identity of the API caller, the time of the API call, the source IP address of the API caller, the request parameters, and the response elements returned by GCP services. Cloud audit logging provides a history of GCP API calls for an account, including API calls made through the Cloud Console, Cloud SDKs, command-line tools, and other GCP services.
cis_gcp_2.5.remediation: Set up log metric filters and alerts for audit configuration changes in Cloud Logging. Use Terraform to define alerts and ensure audit logs are enabled for all services. Integrate with SIEM tools for continuous monitoring and incident response.

cis_gcp_2.6.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for changes to Identity and Access Management (IAM) role creation, deletion and updating activities.
cis_gcp_2.6.remediation: Configure log metric filters and alerts in Cloud Monitoring for IAM custom role changes. Implement IAM policies to restrict who can create or modify roles. Use automated scripts or CSPM tools to detect and remediate unauthorized role changes.

cis_gcp_2.7.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Network Firewall rule changes.
cis_gcp_2.7.remediation: Create log metric filters and alerts for VPC firewall rule changes in Cloud Logging. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform to manage firewall rules and enforce change controls. Regularly review firewall configurations to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_gcp_2.8.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network route changes.
cis_gcp_2.8.remediation: Set up log metric filters and alerts for VPC network route changes using Cloud Monitoring. Implement network security policies to restrict route modifications. Automate route management with IaC and conduct periodic audits to ensure compliance.

cis_gcp_2.9.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network changes.
cis_gcp_2.9.remediation: Configure log metric filters and alerts for VPC network changes in Cloud Logging. Use Terraform to define VPC networks and enforce change management processes. Monitor network configurations continuously to detect unauthorized modifications.

cis_gcp_3.1.description: To prevent use of default network, a project should not have a default network.
cis_gcp_3.1.remediation: Delete the default network in GCP projects using gcloud commands or Terraform. Create custom VPC networks with proper security controls. Implement organizational policies to block creation of default networks in new projects.

cis_gcp_3.1.1.description: If kubelet is running, and if it is configured by a kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_gcp_3.1.1.remediation: Manually check and set kubeconfig file permissions to 644 using chmod commands. Establish a process for regular file permission audits on Kubernetes nodes. Document procedures and train administrators on secure configuration management.

cis_gcp_3.1.2.description: If kubelet is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
cis_gcp_3.1.2.remediation: Use automated tools or scripts to set kubeconfig file ownership to root:root. Implement configuration management with tools like Ansible or Chef. Regularly audit file ownership across all nodes to maintain security compliance.

cis_gcp_3.1.3.description: Ensure that if the kubelet configuration file exists, it has permissions of 644.
cis_gcp_3.1.3.remediation: Automate setting kubelet configuration file permissions to 644 using IaC or configuration management tools. Use CSPM platforms to continuously monitor and enforce permissions. Apply security policies across all cloud providers consistently.

cis_gcp_3.1.4.description: Ensure that if the kubelet configuration file exists, it is owned by root:root.
cis_gcp_3.1.4.remediation: Configure automated checks to ensure kubelet configuration file ownership is set to root:root. Use tools like Terraform or cloud-native automation to enforce ownership settings. Regularly scan nodes for deviations and remediate promptly.

cis_gcp_3.2.description: In order to prevent use of legacy networks, a project should not have a legacy network configured. As of now, Legacy Networks are gradually being phased out, and you can no longer create projects with them. This recommendation is to check older projects to ensure that they are not using Legacy Networks.
cis_gcp_3.2.remediation: Identify and migrate any legacy networks to modern VPC networks using gcloud commands or Terraform. Update network configurations in IaC templates. Conduct periodic reviews of older projects to ensure no legacy networks remain.

cis_gcp_3.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
cis_gcp_3.2.1.remediation: Configure Kubelet to disable anonymous authentication by setting --anonymous-auth=false in kubelet configuration files. Use automated tools to enforce this setting across all nodes. Regularly audit Kubelet configurations to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_gcp_3.2.2.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
cis_gcp_3.2.2.remediation: Set --authorization-mode to a value other than AlwaysAllow, such as Webhook or RBAC, in Kubelet configuration. Use IaC to define and deploy secure configurations. Monitor authorization settings continuously to ensure compliance with security policies.

cis_gcp_3.2.3.remediation: Manually configure a Client CA file for Kubelet authentication by setting --client-ca-file in kubelet configuration. Establish a certificate management process for issuing and rotating client certificates. Train staff on proper certificate handling and update documentation regularly.

cis_gcp_3.2.4.remediation: Manually disable the read-only port by setting --read-only-port=0 in kubelet configuration. Implement a process for verifying this setting during node provisioning. Conduct regular security audits to ensure the port remains disabled across all environments.

cis_gcp_3.2.5.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
cis_gcp_3.2.5.remediation: Ensure --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is not set to 0 in kubelet configuration files. Use automated configuration management to enforce appropriate timeout values. Regularly review and update timeout settings to balance security and performance.

cis_gcp_3.2.6.remediation: Set --make-iptables-util-chains=true in kubelet configuration using automated tools. Use IaC to deploy consistent configurations across all nodes. Monitor iptables management to ensure Kubelet can enforce network policies effectively.

cis_gcp_3.2.7.description: Security relevant information should be captured. The eventRecordQPS on the Kubelet configuration can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered and sets the maximum event creations per second. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
cis_gcp_3.2.7.remediation: Configure --eventRecordQPS to a value that ensures appropriate event capture without causing denial of service, such as 10 or higher. Use automated configuration management to set and monitor this parameter. Regularly adjust based on node performance and security requirements.

cis_gcp_3.2.8.remediation: Set --rotate-certificates=true in kubelet configuration to enable client certificate rotation. Use automated tools to manage certificate lifecycle and ensure timely rotation. Implement monitoring to detect and remediate any failures in certificate rotation processes.

cis_gcp_3.2.9.remediation: Configure RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true in kubelet configuration to enable server certificate rotation. Automate certificate management with tools like cert-manager or cloud-native solutions. Regularly audit certificate expiration and rotation status to maintain security.

cis_gcp_3.3.description: Cloud Domain Name System (DNS) is a fast, reliable and cost-effective domain name system that powers millions of domains on the internet. Domain Name System Security Extensions (DNSSEC) in Cloud DNS enables domain owners to take easy steps to protect their domains against DNS hijacking and man-in-the-middle and other attacks.
cis_gcp_3.3.remediation: Enable DNSSEC for Cloud DNS managed zones via Terraform or GCP Console. Use gcloud commands to configure DNSSEC settings and ensure key signing algorithms are secure. Regularly monitor DNSSEC status and update configurations as needed to protect against attacks.

cis_gcp_3.4.description: NOTE: Currently, the SHA1 algorithm has been removed from general use by Google, and, if being used, needs to be whitelisted on a project basis by Google and will also, therefore, require a Google Cloud support contract. DNSSEC algorithm numbers in this registry may be used in CERT RRs. Zone signing (DNSSEC) and transaction security mechanisms (SIG(0) and TSIG) make use of particular subsets of these algorithms. The algorithm used for key signing should be a recommended one and it should be strong.
cis_gcp_3.4.remediation: Configure Cloud DNS DNSSEC to use stronger algorithms like RSASHA256 or ECDSAP256SHA256 instead of RSASHA1 for key-signing keys. Use Terraform or GCP Console to update DNSSEC settings. Regularly audit DNS configurations to ensure compliance with security best practices.

cis_gcp_3.5.description: NOTE: Currently, the SHA1 algorithm has been removed from general use by Google, and, if being used, needs to be whitelisted on a project basis by Google and will also, therefore, require a Google Cloud support contract. DNSSEC algorithm numbers in this registry may be used in CERT RRs. Zone signing (DNSSEC) and transaction security mechanisms (SIG(0) and TSIG) make use of particular subsets of these algorithms. The algorithm used for key signing should be a recommended one and it should be strong.
cis_gcp_3.5.remediation: Set Cloud DNS DNSSEC to use secure algorithms like RSASHA256 for zone-signing keys, avoiding RSASHA1. Implement automated checks via CSPM tools to detect weak algorithms. Update IaC templates to enforce strong cryptographic standards across all DNS zones.

cis_gcp_3.6.description: GCP Firewall Rules are specific to a VPC Network. Each rule either allows or denies traffic when its conditions are met. Its conditions allow the user to specify the type of traffic, such as ports and protocols, and the source or destination of the traffic, including IP addresses, subnets, and instances. Firewall rules are defined at the VPC network level and are specific to the network in which they are defined. The rules themselves cannot be shared among networks. Firewall rules only support I
cis_gcp_3.6.remediation: Review and modify GCP firewall rules to restrict SSH (port 22) access from the internet (0.0.0.0/0) to specific IP ranges or internal networks only. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Deployment Manager to enforce these rules, and regularly audit firewall configurations to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_gcp_3.7.description: GCP Firewall Rules are specific to a VPC Network. Each rule either allows or denies traffic when its conditions are met. Its conditions allow users to specify the type of traffic, such as ports and protocols, and the source or destination of the traffic, including IP addresses, subnets, and instances. Firewall rules are defined at the VPC network level and are specific to the network in which they are defined. The rules themselves cannot be shared among networks. Firewall rules only support IPv4
cis_gcp_3.7.remediation: Review and modify GCP firewall rules to restrict RDP (port 3389) access from the internet (0.0.0.0/0) to specific IP ranges or internal networks only. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Deployment Manager to enforce these rules, and implement network security groups to limit exposure.

cis_gcp_3.8.description: Flow Logs is a feature that enables users to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in the organization's VPC Subnets. Once a flow log is created, the user can view and retrieve its data in Stackdriver Logging. It is recommended that Flow Logs be enabled for every business-critical VPC subnet.
cis_gcp_3.8.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for all subnets in GCP VPC networks to monitor and log network traffic. Use IaC tools like Terraform to automate this configuration, and integrate logs with Cloud Logging for analysis and alerting on suspicious activities.

cis_gcp_3.9.description: Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) policies determine what port Transport Layer Security (TLS) features clients are permitted to use when connecting to load balancers. To prevent usage of insecure features, SSL policies should use (a) at least TLS 1.2 with the MODERN profile; or (b) the RESTRICTED profile, because it effectively requires clients to use TLS 1.2 regardless of the chosen minimum TLS version; or (3) a CUSTOM profile that does not support any of the following features: TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_G
cis_gcp_3.9.remediation: Configure SSL policies for HTTPS or SSL proxy load balancers to use at least TLS 1.2 with the MODERN or RESTRICTED profile, avoiding weak cipher suites. Use IaC tools to enforce these policies, and regularly audit load balancer configurations to ensure compliance with security standards.

cis_gcp_4.1.description: It is recommended to configure your instance to not use the default Compute Engine service account because it has the Editor role on the project.
cis_gcp_4.1.remediation: Assign custom service accounts with minimal permissions to Compute Engine instances instead of the default service account. Use IAM roles to enforce least privilege, and automate this with IaC tools like Terraform to prevent misconfigurations.
