cis_gcp_4.1.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
cis_gcp_4.1.1.remediation: Restrict the use of the cluster-admin role in GKE to only essential users or service accounts. Implement RBAC policies to assign specific, limited roles, and use IaC tools to manage role bindings, regularly reviewing permissions for unnecessary access.

cis_gcp_4.1.10.description: Avoid non-default ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings with the group system:authenticated, except the ClusterRoleBindings system:basic-user, system:discovery, and system:public-info-viewer. Google's approach to authentication is to make authenticating to Google Cloud and GKE as simple and secure as possible without adding complex configuration steps. The group system:authenticated includes all users with a Google account, which includes all Gmail accounts. Consider your authorization controls w
cis_gcp_4.1.10.remediation: Audit and remove any non-default bindings to the system:authenticated group in GKE, allowing only the default ClusterRoleBindings. Use RBAC policies to enforce this, and implement automated checks with IaC tools to prevent unauthorized bindings.

cis_gcp_4.1.2.description: The Kubernetes API stores secrets, which may be service account tokens for the Kubernetes API or credentials used by workloads in the cluster. Access to these secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
cis_gcp_4.1.2.remediation: Limit access to Kubernetes secrets by using RBAC to assign specific roles only to necessary users or service accounts. Implement secret management tools like HashiCorp Vault, and use IaC to automate role assignments, ensuring least privilege.

cis_gcp_4.1.3.description: Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles provide access to resources based on sets of objects and actions that can be taken on those objects. It is possible to set either of these to be the wildcard "*", which matches all items. Use of wildcards is not optimal from a security perspective as it may allow for inadvertent access to be granted when new resources are added to the Kubernetes API either as CRDs or in later versions of the product.
cis_gcp_4.1.3.remediation: Avoid using wildcards (*) in Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles; instead, specify explicit resources and actions. Use RBAC policies to define precise permissions, and automate role management with IaC tools to reduce the risk of over-permissioning.

cis_gcp_4.1.4.description: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
cis_gcp_4.1.4.remediation: Disable the use of default service accounts in GKE namespaces by configuring pods to use custom service accounts with minimal permissions. Use IaC tools to enforce this setting, and regularly audit service account usage to ensure compliance.

cis_gcp_4.1.5.description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
cis_gcp_4.1.5.remediation: Configure GKE clusters to disable automatic mounting of service account tokens in pods unless explicitly required. Use pod specifications to control token mounting, and implement IaC policies to enforce this configuration across all workloads.

cis_gcp_4.1.6.description: The special group system:masters should not be used to grant permissions to any user or service account, except where strictly necessary (e.g. bootstrapping access prior to RBAC being fully available)
cis_gcp_4.1.6.remediation: Avoid assigning permissions to the system:masters group in GKE; use specific RBAC roles instead. Implement strict access controls and use IaC tools to manage role bindings, limiting this group to essential administrative tasks only.

cis_gcp_4.1.7.description: Cluster roles and roles with the impersonate, bind or escalate permissions should not be granted unless strictly required. Each of these permissions allow a particular subject to escalate their privileges beyond those explicitly granted by cluster administrators
cis_gcp_4.1.7.remediation: Restrict the use of impersonate, bind, and escalate permissions in GKE RBAC roles to only necessary users or service accounts. Implement role-based access controls and use IaC to automate permission assignments, regularly auditing for excessive privileges.

cis_gcp_4.1.8.description: Avoid ClusterRoleBindings nor RoleBindings with the user system:anonymous.
cis_gcp_4.1.8.remediation: Ensure no ClusterRoleBindings or RoleBindings are assigned to the system:anonymous user in GKE. Use RBAC policies to enforce this, and implement automated checks with IaC tools to prevent unauthorized bindings and maintain security.

cis_gcp_4.1.9.description: Avoid non-default ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings with the group system:unauthenticated, except the ClusterRoleBinding system:public-info- viewer.
cis_gcp_4.1.9.remediation: Audit and remove any non-default bindings to the system:unauthenticated group in GKE, allowing only the default ClusterRoleBinding. Use RBAC policies to enforce this, and automate configuration management with IaC tools to prevent security gaps.

cis_gcp_4.10.description: In order to maintain the highest level of security all connections to an application should be secure by default.
cis_gcp_4.10.remediation: Configure App Engine applications to enforce HTTPS connections by setting the appropriate security headers and redirects. Use IaC tools like Terraform to automate this configuration, and regularly test for compliance with encryption standards.

cis_gcp_4.11.description: Google Cloud encrypts data at-rest and in-transit, but customer data must be decrypted for processing. Confidential Computing is a breakthrough technology that encrypts data in-use while it is being processed. Confidential Computing environments keep data encrypted in memory and elsewhere outside the central processing unit (CPU). Confidential VMs leverage hardware-based memory encryption technologies, such as AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV), AMD SEV-SNP, and Intel Trust Domain Extensi
cis_gcp_4.11.remediation: Enable Confidential Computing on Compute Engine instances by selecting confidential VM options during creation. Use IaC tools to automate this configuration, and ensure that sensitive workloads are deployed on instances with hardware-based encryption for enhanced data protection.

cis_gcp_4.12.description: Google Cloud Virtual Machines have the ability via an OS Config agent API to periodically (about every 10 minutes) report OS inventory data. A patch compliance API periodically reads this data, and cross references metadata to determine if the latest updates are installed. This is not the only Patch Management solution available to your organization and you should weigh your needs before committing to using this method.
cis_gcp_4.12.remediation: Implement OS patch management for GCP VMs using OS Config policies to ensure the latest updates are installed. Use IaC tools to define and enforce patch policies, and integrate with monitoring tools to track compliance and automate remediation.

cis_gcp_4.2.description: To support principle of least privileges and prevent potential privilege escalation it is recommended that instances are not assigned to default service account Compute Engine default service account with Scope Allow full access to all Cloud APIs.
cis_gcp_4.2.remediation: Avoid assigning the default service account with full Cloud API access to Compute Engine instances; use custom service accounts with limited scopes instead. Implement IAM roles to enforce least privilege, and automate with IaC tools to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_gcp_4.2.1.description: The Pod Security Standard Baseline profile defines a baseline for container security. You can enforce this by using the built-in Pod Security Admission controller.
cis_gcp_4.2.1.remediation: Configure GKE clusters to enforce the Pod Security Standard Baseline profile or stricter for all namespaces using the Pod Security Admission controller. Use IaC tools to automate this policy, and regularly audit pod configurations to ensure compliance with security standards.

cis_gcp_4.3.description: It is recommended to use Instance specific SSH key(s) instead of using common/shared project-wide SSH key(s) to access Instances.
cis_gcp_4.3.remediation: Enable 'Block Project-Wide SSH Keys' on Compute Engine instances to enforce the use of instance-specific SSH keys. Use IaC tools like Terraform to set this configuration, and manage SSH keys through IAM for better access control and auditing.

cis_gcp_4.3.1.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
cis_gcp_4.3.1.remediation: Ensure the CNI plugin used in GKE clusters supports Network Policies, such as Calico or Cilium. Configure network policies to control traffic flow, and use IaC tools to automate CNI selection and policy enforcement for enhanced network security.

cis_gcp_4.3.2.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in the cluster network.
cis_gcp_4.3.2.remediation: Define and apply Network Policies for all namespaces in GKE clusters to restrict pod-to-pod communication. Use IaC tools to automate policy creation, and regularly review policies to ensure they align with security requirements and minimize attack surfaces.

cis_gcp_4.4.description: Enabling OS login binds SSH certificates to IAM users and facilitates effective SSH certificate management.
cis_gcp_4.4.remediation: Enable OS login for GCP projects to bind SSH access to IAM users, improving authentication and management. Use IaC tools to configure this setting, and enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for IAM users to enhance security.

cis_gcp_4.4.1.description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
cis_gcp_4.4.1.remediation: Prefer mounting secrets as files in Kubernetes pods rather than as environment variables to reduce exposure. Use IaC tools to configure pod specifications accordingly, and implement secret management best practices to protect sensitive data.

cis_gcp_4.4.2.description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if more complex secret management is required. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
cis_gcp_4.4.2.remediation: Implement an external secrets manager like AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or Google Cloud Secret Manager. Configure authentication and RBAC policies to restrict access, enable audit logging for all secret operations, and ensure encryption at rest and in transit. Use IaC tools like Terraform to automate deployment and enforce encryption settings.

cis_gcp_4.5.description: Interacting with a serial port is often referred to as the serial console, which is similar to using a terminal window, in that input and output is entirely in text mode and there is no graphical interface or mouse support. If you enable the interactive serial console on an instance, clients can attempt to connect to that instance from any IP address. Therefore interactive serial console support should be disabled.
cis_gcp_4.5.remediation: Disable the 'Enable Connecting to Serial Ports' setting for all VM instances in Google Compute Engine. Use IaC templates like Terraform or Deployment Manager to enforce this configuration. Apply organization policies to prevent re-enablement and regularly audit instances for compliance.

cis_gcp_4.5.1.description: Configure Image Provenance for the deployment.
cis_gcp_4.5.1.remediation: Manually set up the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller in your Kubernetes cluster to verify image signatures and enforce policies. Define admission policies that require images from trusted registries and have valid signatures. Document the process and train administrators on maintaining and updating these policies.

cis_gcp_4.6.description: Compute Engine instance cannot forward a packet unless the source IP address of the packet matches the IP address of the instance. Similarly, GCP won't deliver a packet whose destination IP address is different than the IP address of the instance receiving the packet. However, both capabilities are required if you want to use instances to help route packets. Forwarding of data packets should be disabled to prevent data loss or information disclosure.
cis_gcp_4.6.remediation: Ensure IP forwarding is disabled on all Compute Engine instances unless explicitly required for routing purposes. Use IaC tools to set 'canIpForward' to false in instance configurations. Implement network security policies and VPC firewalls to control traffic flow and regularly audit instances for compliance.

cis_gcp_4.6.1.description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
cis_gcp_4.6.1.remediation: Create and use namespaces in Kubernetes to segregate resources by environment, team, or application. Apply RBAC policies to restrict access between namespaces and use network policies to control traffic. Automate namespace creation with IaC tools like Helm or Kubernetes manifests to ensure consistent enforcement.

cis_gcp_4.6.2.description: Enable RuntimeDefault seccomp profile in the pod definitions.
cis_gcp_4.6.2.remediation: Configure pod security contexts to set the seccomp profile to 'RuntimeDefault' in Kubernetes pod definitions. Use admission controllers or policy engines like OPA Gatekeeper to enforce this across clusters. Update existing deployments and CI/CD pipelines to include this setting in all new pod creations.

cis_gcp_4.6.3.description: Apply Security Context to Pods and Containers
cis_gcp_4.6.3.remediation: Define security contexts in Kubernetes pod and container specifications to set privileges, capabilities, and user IDs. Use tools like Pod Security Standards or admission controllers to enforce security policies. Implement in CI/CD pipelines to validate configurations before deployment and audit existing workloads for compliance.

cis_gcp_4.6.4.description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
cis_gcp_4.6.4.remediation: Avoid using the default namespace for any workloads; instead, create and use specific namespaces. Implement RBAC policies to restrict access to the default namespace and use admission controllers to block resource creation there. Regularly audit clusters to ensure no resources are deployed in the default namespace.

cis_gcp_4.7.description: Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) are a feature in Google Cloud Storage and Google Compute Engine. If you supply your own encryption keys, Google uses your key to protect the Google-generated keys used to encrypt and decrypt your data. By default, Google Compute Engine encrypts all data at rest. Compute Engine handles and manages this encryption for you without any additional actions on your part. However, if you wanted to control and manage this encryption yourself, you can provide your
cis_gcp_4.7.remediation: Enable Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) for critical VM disks in Google Compute Engine. Use Cloud KMS to manage encryption keys and apply them via IaC tools like Terraform. Ensure key rotation policies are in place and audit disk encryption settings regularly to maintain compliance.

cis_gcp_4.8.description: To defend against advanced threats and ensure that the boot loader and firmware on your VMs are signed and untampered, it is recommended that Compute instances are launched with Shielded VM enabled.
cis_gcp_4.8.remediation: Enable Shielded VM features (secure boot, vTPM, integrity monitoring) for all Compute Engine instances. Use IaC templates to enforce this setting during instance creation. Apply organization policies to require Shielded VM and monitor compliance through CSPM tools.

cis_gcp_4.9.description: Compute instances should not be configured to have external IP addresses.
cis_gcp_4.9.remediation: Avoid assigning public IP addresses to Compute Engine instances; use private IPs and access via VPN, Cloud NAT, or load balancers. Configure VPC networks and firewall rules to restrict external access. Use IaC to enforce no public IPs and regularly audit instances for compliance.

cis_gcp_5.1.description: It is recommended that IAM policy on Cloud Storage bucket does not allows anonymous or public access.
cis_gcp_5.1.remediation: Disable anonymous and public access on Cloud Storage buckets by setting IAM policies and bucket permissions. Enable uniform bucket-level access and use tools like Terraform to enforce these settings. Regularly audit buckets for public exposure and implement alerts for any changes.

cis_gcp_5.1.1.description: Note: GCR is now deprecated, being superseded by Artifact Registry starting 15th May 2024. Runtime Vulnerability scanning is available via GKE Security Posture Scan images stored in Google Container Registry (GCR) or Artifact Registry (AR) for vulnerabilities.
cis_gcp_5.1.1.remediation: Enable vulnerability scanning for container images in Artifact Registry or Container Registry. Configure scanning policies to automatically scan images on push and block deployments if critical vulnerabilities are found. Use IaC to enable scanning and integrate with CI/CD pipelines for continuous monitoring.

cis_gcp_5.1.2.description: Note: GCR is now deprecated, see the references for more details. Restrict user access to GCR or AR, limiting interaction with build images to only authorized personnel and service accounts.
cis_gcp_5.1.2.remediation: Apply IAM roles with least privilege to restrict access to container image repositories. Use Artifact Registry IAM conditions and RBAC to limit permissions. Automate access reviews and audit logs to monitor and revoke unnecessary access regularly.

cis_gcp_5.1.3.description: Note: GCR is now deprecated, see the references for more details. Configure the Cluster Service Account with Artifact Registry Viewer Role to only allow read-only access to AR repositories. Configure the Cluster Service Account with Storage Object Viewer Role to only allow read-only access to GCR.
cis_gcp_5.1.3.remediation: Assign minimal IAM roles (e.g., Artifact Registry Viewer) to cluster service accounts for read-only access to image repositories. Use Workload Identity to manage service account credentials. Enforce these roles via IaC and audit service account permissions regularly.

cis_gcp_5.1.4.description: Use Binary Authorization to allowlist (whitelist) only approved container registries.
cis_gcp_5.1.4.remediation: Implement Binary Authorization policies in GKE to only allow images from trusted registries. Define attestation policies and use Cloud KMS for signing. Enforce these policies via IaC and integrate with CI/CD to block unauthorized images during deployment.

cis_gcp_5.10.1.description: Note: The Kubernetes web UI (Dashboard) does not have admin access by default in GKE 1.7 and higher. The Kubernetes web UI is disabled by default in GKE 1.10 and higher. In GKE 1.15 and higher, the Kubernetes web UI add-on KubernetesDashboard is no longer supported as a managed add-on. The Kubernetes Web UI (Dashboard) has been a historical source of vulnerability and should only be deployed when necessary.
cis_gcp_5.10.1.remediation: Ensure the Kubernetes Dashboard is disabled in GKE clusters. Use IaC tools to verify and enforce this setting. If required, deploy a secure alternative with RBAC and network policies, and regularly audit cluster configurations for compliance.

cis_gcp_5.10.2.description: Alpha clusters are not covered by an SLA and are not production-ready.
cis_gcp_5.10.2.remediation: Avoid using Alpha clusters for production workloads; use stable or regular release channels instead. Enforce this via organization policies and IaC templates. Monitor cluster configurations and alert on any Alpha feature usage in production environments.

cis_gcp_5.10.3.description: Use GKE Sandbox to restrict untrusted workloads as an additional layer of protection when running in a multi-tenant environment.
cis_gcp_5.10.3.remediation: Enable GKE Sandbox for node pools running untrusted workloads. Configure sandboxed pods using gVisor and apply via Kubernetes manifests. Use IaC to automate deployment and enforce isolation policies for multi-tenant clusters.

cis_gcp_5.10.4.description: Binary Authorization helps to protect supply-chain security by only allowing images with verifiable cryptographically signed metadata into the cluster.
cis_gcp_5.10.4.remediation: Enable Binary Authorization in GKE clusters to enforce image signing and attestation. Set up policies using Cloud KMS and integrate with CI/CD pipelines. Use IaC to deploy and maintain these policies, and regularly audit for compliance.

cis_gcp_5.10.5.remediation: Enable Security Posture features in GKE, such as automated vulnerability scanning and policy enforcement. Configure via Google Cloud Console or IaC tools like Terraform. Regularly review security posture reports and update policies to address identified risks.

cis_gcp_5.2.description: It is recommended that uniform bucket-level access is enabled on Cloud Storage buckets.
cis_gcp_5.2.remediation: Enable uniform bucket-level access on all Cloud Storage buckets to simplify IAM management and prevent ACL misconfigurations. Use IaC tools to enforce this setting and migrate existing buckets. Regularly audit buckets to ensure uniform access is applied and monitor for any deviations.

cis_gcp_5.2.1.description: Create and use minimally privileged Service accounts to run GKE cluster nodes instead of using the Compute Engine default Service account. Unnecessary permissions could be abused in the case of a node compromise.
cis_gcp_5.2.1.remediation: Create custom service accounts with minimal permissions for GKE node pools and disable the default service account. Use IaC to define and assign these service accounts. Regularly review and audit service account permissions to ensure least privilege.

cis_gcp_5.2.2.description: Kubernetes workloads should not use cluster node service accounts to authenticate to Google Cloud APIs. Each Kubernetes Workload that needs to authenticate to other Google services using Cloud IAM should be provisioned a dedicated Service account. Enabling Workload Identity manages the distribution and rotation of Service account keys for the workloads to use.
cis_gcp_5.2.2.remediation: Enable Workload Identity on GKE clusters to associate Kubernetes service accounts with Google Cloud IAM. Create dedicated service accounts for workloads and assign minimal roles. Use IaC to configure Workload Identity and audit configurations regularly.

cis_gcp_5.3.1.description: Encrypt Kubernetes secrets, stored in etcd, at the application-layer using a customer- managed key in Cloud KMS.
cis_gcp_5.3.1.remediation: Enable application-layer encryption for Kubernetes secrets using customer-managed keys in Cloud KMS. Configure this via GKE cluster settings and use IaC to enforce encryption. Regularly rotate encryption keys and audit secret encryption status to ensure compliance.

cis_gcp_5.4.1.description: Running the GKE Metadata Server prevents workloads from accessing sensitive instance metadata and facilitates Workload Identity.
cis_gcp_5.4.1.remediation: Enable the GKE Metadata Server during cluster creation or update via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or Terraform. Ensure Workload Identity is configured to use the metadata server for secure authentication, and verify in existing clusters using gcloud container clusters describe.

cis_gcp_5.4.2.description: Enable Control Plane Authorized Networks to restrict access to the cluster's control plane to only an allowlist of authorized IPs.
cis_gcp_5.4.2.remediation: Configure authorized networks in the GKE cluster settings using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --master-authorized-networks flag, or Terraform. Regularly review and update the IP allowlist to limit exposure and enforce least privilege access.

cis_gcp_5.4.3.description: Disable access to the Kubernetes API from outside the node network if it is not required.
cis_gcp_5.4.3.remediation: Create GKE clusters with private endpoint enabled and public access disabled via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-private-endpoint and --disable-public-endpoint flags, or Terraform. Use VPC peering or VPN for secure internal access to the control plane.

cis_gcp_5.4.4.description: Private Nodes are nodes with no public IP addresses. Disable public IP addresses for cluster nodes, so that they only have private IP addresses.
cis_gcp_5.4.4.remediation: Enable private nodes during cluster creation using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-private-nodes flag, or Terraform. Ensure nodes use private IPs only and configure Cloud NAT or proxies for outbound internet access if needed.

cis_gcp_5.4.5.description: Encrypt traffic to HTTPS load balancers using Google-managed SSL certificates.
cis_gcp_5.4.5.remediation: Use Google-managed SSL certificates for GKE Ingress resources by specifying them in the Ingress manifest or via Terraform. This automates certificate provisioning and renewal, reducing manual errors and ensuring encryption for external traffic.

cis_gcp_5.5.1.description: Use Container-Optimized OS (cos_containerd) as a managed, optimized and hardened base OS that limits the host's attack surface.
cis_gcp_5.5.1.remediation: Specify cos_containerd as the node image during GKE node pool creation using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --image-type flag, or Terraform. Regularly update to the latest version to benefit from security patches and hardening features.

cis_gcp_5.5.2.description: Nodes in a degraded state are an unknown quantity and so may pose a security risk.
cis_gcp_5.5.2.remediation: Enable node auto-repair in GKE node pool settings via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-autorepair flag, or Terraform. This automatically detects and repairs unhealthy nodes, maintaining cluster integrity and reducing manual intervention.

cis_gcp_5.5.3.description: Node auto-upgrade keeps nodes at the current Kubernetes and OS security patch level to mitigate known vulnerabilities.
cis_gcp_5.5.3.remediation: Enable node auto-upgrade in GKE node pool settings using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-autoupgrade flag, or Terraform. Combine with release channels to automate updates and ensure nodes are patched against security vulnerabilities.

cis_gcp_5.5.4.description: Subscribe to the Regular or Stable Release Channel to automate version upgrades to the GKE cluster and to reduce version management complexity to the number of features and level of stability required.
cis_gcp_5.5.4.remediation: Configure GKE clusters to use a release channel (Regular or Stable) during creation or update via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --release-channel flag, or Terraform. This automates version management and ensures timely security updates with minimal manual effort.

cis_gcp_5.5.5.description: Shielded GKE Nodes provides verifiable integrity via secure boot, virtual trusted platform module (vTPM)-enabled measured boot, and integrity monitoring.
cis_gcp_5.5.5.remediation: Enable shielded nodes during GKE node pool creation using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --shielded-secure-boot flag, or Terraform. Ensure integrity monitoring and secure boot are also enabled to protect against firmware and boot-level attacks.

cis_gcp_5.5.6.description: Enable Integrity Monitoring for Shielded GKE Nodes to be notified of inconsistencies during the node boot sequence.
cis_gcp_5.5.6.remediation: Enable integrity monitoring for shielded GKE nodes via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --shielded-integrity-monitoring flag, or Terraform. Configure alerts in Cloud Monitoring to detect and respond to boot integrity issues promptly.

cis_gcp_5.5.7.description: Enable Secure Boot for Shielded GKE Nodes to verify the digital signature of node boot components.
cis_gcp_5.5.7.remediation: Enable secure boot for shielded GKE nodes using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --shielded-secure-boot flag, or Terraform. This ensures only signed boot components are loaded, protecting against rootkits and unauthorized modifications.

cis_gcp_5.6.1.description: Enable VPC Flow Logs and Intranode Visibility to see pod-level traffic, even for traffic within a worker node.
cis_gcp_5.6.1.remediation: Enable VPC flow logs and intranode visibility in GKE cluster settings via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-intranode-visibility flag, or Terraform. Configure logs to be sent to Cloud Logging for monitoring and analysis of network traffic.

cis_gcp_5.6.2.description: Create Alias IPs for the node network CIDR range in order to subsequently configure IP-based policies and firewalling for pods. A cluster that uses Alias IPs is called a VPC-native cluster.
cis_gcp_5.6.2.remediation: Create GKE clusters as VPC-native by enabling alias IPs during cluster creation using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-ip-alias flag, or Terraform. This allows for better network segmentation and pod-level firewall rules.

cis_gcp_5.6.3.description: Enable Control Plane Authorized Networks to restrict access to the cluster's control plane to only an allowlist of authorized IPs.
cis_gcp_5.6.3.remediation: Configure authorized networks in the GKE cluster settings using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --master-authorized-networks flag, or Terraform. Regularly review and update the IP allowlist to limit exposure and enforce least privilege access.

cis_gcp_5.6.4.description: Disable access to the Kubernetes API from outside the node network if it is not required.
cis_gcp_5.6.4.remediation: Create GKE clusters with private endpoint enabled and public access disabled via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-private-endpoint and --disable-public-endpoint flags, or Terraform. Use VPC peering or VPN for secure internal access to the control plane.

cis_gcp_5.6.5.description: Private Nodes are nodes with no public IP addresses. Disable public IP addresses for cluster nodes, so that they only have private IP addresses.
cis_gcp_5.6.5.remediation: Enable private nodes during cluster creation using the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --enable-private-nodes flag, or Terraform. Ensure nodes use private IPs only and configure Cloud NAT or proxies for outbound internet access if needed.

cis_gcp_5.6.6.description: Reduce the network attack surface of GKE nodes by using Firewalls to restrict ingress and egress traffic.
cis_gcp_5.6.6.remediation: Apply firewall rules to GKE worker nodes using Google Cloud Firewall policies via the Console, gcloud CLI, or Terraform. Use network tags and service accounts to enforce least privilege, blocking unnecessary ports and limiting traffic to trusted sources.

cis_gcp_5.6.7.description: Encrypt traffic to HTTPS load balancers using Google-managed SSL certificates.
cis_gcp_5.6.7.remediation: Use Google-managed SSL certificates for GKE Ingress resources by specifying them in the Ingress manifest or via Terraform. This automates certificate provisioning and renewal, reducing manual errors and ensuring encryption for external traffic.

cis_gcp_5.7.1.description: Send logs and metrics to a remote aggregator to mitigate the risk of local tampering in the event of a breach.
cis_gcp_5.7.1.remediation: Enable logging and Cloud Monitoring for GKE clusters via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --logging and --monitoring flags, or Terraform. Configure log sinks to export logs to a secure destination like Cloud Storage or BigQuery for long-term retention and analysis.

cis_gcp_5.7.2.description: Run the auditd logging daemon to obtain verbose operating system logs from GKE nodes running Container-Optimized OS (COS).
cis_gcp_5.7.2.remediation: Enable Linux auditd logging on GKE nodes by configuring the node OS image or using startup scripts. Ensure logs are forwarded to Cloud Logging for centralized monitoring and set up alerts for critical audit events to detect unauthorized activities.

cis_gcp_5.8.1.description: Do not make use of client certificate authentication in GKE, as the credentials cannot be revoked. Instead, use another authentication method like OpenID Connect.
cis_gcp_5.8.1.remediation: Disable client certificate authentication in GKE clusters by setting --issue-client-certificate=false during creation or update via gcloud CLI or Terraform. Migrate to OpenID Connect (OIDC) or IAM-based authentication for revocable and manageable access controls.

cis_gcp_5.8.2.description: Cluster Administrators should leverage G Suite Groups and Cloud IAM to assign Kubernetes user roles to a collection of users, instead of to individual emails using only Cloud IAM.
cis_gcp_5.8.2.remediation: Use Google Groups for GKE RBAC by binding Kubernetes roles to groups in the cluster RBAC configuration. Integrate with Cloud IAM to manage group memberships centrally, ensuring scalable and consistent access management across users.

cis_gcp_5.8.3.description: Legacy Authorization, also known as Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) has been superseded by Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and is not under active development. RBAC is the recommended way to manage permissions in Kubernetes.
cis_gcp_5.8.3.remediation: Disable ABAC in GKE clusters by setting --no-enable-legacy-authorization during creation or update via gcloud CLI or Terraform. Migrate to RBAC by defining roles and role bindings in Kubernetes manifests, ensuring fine-grained and auditable access controls.

cis_gcp_5.9.1.description: Use Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) to encrypt dynamically-provisioned attached Google Compute Engine Persistent Disks (PDs) using keys managed within Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS).
cis_gcp_5.9.1.remediation: Enable CMEK for GKE persistent disks by specifying a Cloud KMS key during cluster or node pool creation via the Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI with --disk-encryption-key flag, or Terraform. Ensure proper IAM permissions for KMS key usage and regularly rotate keys as per security policy.

cis_gcp_5.9.2.description: Use Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) to encrypt node boot disks using keys managed within Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS).
cis_gcp_5.9.2.remediation: Enable CMEK for GKE node boot disks via Cloud KMS. In Terraform, set the 'disk_encryption_key' block in the 'google_container_node_pool' resource. Use IAM roles like 'roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter' for service accounts. Ensure keys are rotated and access is restricted.

cis_gcp_6.1.1.description: It is recommended to set a password for the administrative user (root by default) to prevent unauthorized access to the SQL database instances. This recommendation is applicable only for MySQL Instances. PostgreSQL does not offer any setting for No Password from the cloud console.
cis_gcp_6.1.1.remediation: Set a strong password for the MySQL root user during instance creation or via the Cloud SQL console. Use IAM policies to restrict access and enable Cloud Audit Logs. In IaC, specify the 'root_password' parameter in resources like 'google_sql_database_instance'.

cis_gcp_6.1.2.description: It is recommended to set skip_show_database database flag for Cloud SQL Mysql instance to on
cis_gcp_6.1.2.remediation: Configure the 'skip_show_database' flag to 'on' in Cloud SQL MySQL instances via the GCP console, gcloud CLI, or IaC. In Terraform, set it in the 'database_flags' of 'google_sql_database_instance'. This restricts users from viewing databases they don't have privileges for.

cis_gcp_6.1.3.description: It is recommended to set the local_infile database flag for a Cloud SQL MySQL instance to off.
cis_gcp_6.1.3.remediation: Disable the 'local_infile' flag by setting it to 'off' in Cloud SQL MySQL instances. Use the Cloud Console, gcloud commands, or IaC tools like Terraform with 'database_flags'. This prevents local file access, reducing attack surface.

cis_gcp_6.2.1.description: The log_error_verbosity flag controls the verbosity/details of messages logged. Valid values are: • TERSE • DEFAULT • VERBOSE TERSE excludes the logging of DETAIL, HINT, QUERY, and CONTEXT error information. VERBOSE output includes the SQLSTATE error code, source code file name, function name, and line number that generated the error. Ensure an appropriate value is set to 'DEFAULT' or stricter.
cis_gcp_6.2.1.remediation: Set 'log_error_verbosity' to 'DEFAULT' or 'TERSE' in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC (e.g., 'database_flags' in Terraform). This ensures sufficient logging without excessive detail.

cis_gcp_6.2.2.description: Enabling the log_connections setting causes each attempted connection to the server to be logged, along with successful completion of client authentication. This parameter cannot be changed after the session starts.
cis_gcp_6.2.2.remediation: Enable 'log_connections' by setting it to 'on' for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Use the Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or IaC tools. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This aids in auditing and detecting unauthorized access attempts.

cis_gcp_6.2.3.description: Enabling the log_disconnections setting logs the end of each session, including the session duration.
cis_gcp_6.2.3.remediation: Set 'log_disconnections' to 'on' in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, include it in 'database_flags'. This provides audit trails for session management and security monitoring.

cis_gcp_6.2.4.description: The value of log_statement flag determined the SQL statements that are logged. Valid values are: • none • ddl • mod • all The value ddl logs all data definition statements. The value mod logs all ddl statements, plus data-modifying statements. The statements are logged after a basic parsing is done and statement type is determined, thus this does not logs statements with errors. When using extended query protocol, logging occurs after an Execute message is received and values of the Bind paramet
cis_gcp_6.2.4.remediation: Configure 'log_statement' appropriately (e.g., 'ddl' or 'mod') for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set it in 'database_flags'. This balances logging detail with performance and security needs.

cis_gcp_6.2.5.description: The log_min_messages flag defines the minimum message severity level that is considered as an error statement. Messages for error statements are logged with the SQL statement. Valid values include (from lowest to highest severity) DEBUG5, DEBUG4, DEBUG3, DEBUG2, DEBUG1, INFO, NOTICE, WARNING, ERROR, LOG, FATAL, and PANIC. Each severity level includes the subsequent levels mentioned above. ERROR is considered the best practice setting. Changes should only be made in accordance with the organizati
cis_gcp_6.2.5.remediation: Set 'log_min_messages' to at least 'WARNING' for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This ensures critical errors are logged for monitoring and alerting.

cis_gcp_6.2.6.description: The log_min_error_statement flag defines the minimum message severity level that are considered as an error statement. Messages for error statements are logged with the SQL statement. Valid values include (from lowest to highest severity) DEBUG5, DEBUG4, DEBUG3, DEBUG2, DEBUG1, INFO, NOTICE, WARNING, ERROR, LOG, FATAL, and PANIC. Each severity level includes the subsequent levels mentioned above. Ensure a value of ERROR or stricter is set.
cis_gcp_6.2.6.remediation: Set 'log_min_error_statement' to 'ERROR' or stricter (e.g., 'FATAL') for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, include it in 'database_flags'. This helps capture error details for troubleshooting and security analysis.

cis_gcp_6.2.7.description: The log_min_duration_statement flag defines the minimum amount of execution time of a statement in milliseconds where the total duration of the statement is logged. Ensure that log_min_duration_statement is disabled, i.e., a value of -1 is set.
cis_gcp_6.2.7.remediation: Disable 'log_min_duration_statement' by setting it to '-1' in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This prevents performance logging unless specifically needed, reducing log noise.

cis_gcp_6.2.8.description: Ensure cloudsql.enable_pgaudit database flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance is set to on to allow for centralized logging.
cis_gcp_6.2.8.remediation: Enable 'cloudsql.enable_pgaudit' by setting it to 'on' for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set it in 'database_flags'. This integrates with Cloud Audit Logs for centralized security monitoring and compliance.

cis_gcp_6.3.1.description: It is recommended to set external scripts enabled database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to off
cis_gcp_6.3.1.remediation: Disable 'external scripts enabled' by setting it to 'off' for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This prevents execution of external scripts, reducing attack surface.

cis_gcp_6.3.2.description: It is recommended to set cross db ownership chaining database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to off. This flag is deprecated for all SQL Server versions in CGP. Going forward, you can't set its value to on. However, if you have this flag enabled, we strongly recommend that you either remove the flag from your database or set it to off. For cross-database access, use the Microsoft tutorial for signing stored procedures with a certificate.
cis_gcp_6.3.2.remediation: Set 'cross db ownership chaining' to 'off' or remove it for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, ensure it's not in 'database_flags'. Implement certificate-based signing for cross-database access as per Microsoft guidelines.

cis_gcp_6.3.3.description: It is recommended to check the user connections for a Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to ensure that it is not artificially limiting connections.
cis_gcp_6.3.3.remediation: Configure 'user connections' to a non-limiting value (e.g., 0 for unlimited) for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set it appropriately in 'database_flags'. Monitor usage to avoid performance issues.

cis_gcp_6.3.4.description: The user options option specifies global defaults for all users. A list of default query processing options is established for the duration of a user's work session. The user options option allows you to change the default values of the SET options (if the server's default settings are not appropriate). A user can override these defaults by using the SET statement. You can configure user options dynamically for new logins. After you change the setting of user options, new login sessions use the
cis_gcp_6.3.4.remediation: Do not configure the 'user options' flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Leave it unset or remove it from 'database_flags' in IaC. Use session-level SET statements for user-specific configurations to maintain security and consistency.

cis_gcp_6.3.5.description: It is recommended to set remote access database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to off.
cis_gcp_6.3.5.remediation: Disable 'remote access' by setting it to 'off' for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This prevents remote server connections, enhancing network security.

cis_gcp_6.3.6.description: It is recommended to set 3625 (trace flag) database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to on.
cis_gcp_6.3.6.remediation: Enable trace flag 3625 by setting it to 'on' for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, include it in 'database_flags'. This suppresses error messages with sensitive data, improving security.

cis_gcp_6.3.7.description: A contained database includes all database settings and metadata required to define the database and has no configuration dependencies on the instance of the Database Engine where the database is installed. Users can connect to the database without authenticating a login at the Database Engine level. Isolating the database from the Database Engine makes it possible to easily move the database to another instance of SQL Server. Contained databases have some unique threats that should be understoo
cis_gcp_6.3.7.remediation: Set 'contained database authentication' to 'off' for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances. Configure via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, add it to 'database_flags'. This enforces instance-level authentication, reducing security risks.

cis_gcp_6.4.description: It is recommended to enforce all incoming connections to SQL database instance to use SSL.
cis_gcp_6.4.remediation: Require SSL for all incoming connections to Cloud SQL instances. Enable 'require_ssl' in the instance settings via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set 'require_ssl' to true. Use IAM to manage certificates and enforce encryption in transit.

cis_gcp_6.5.description: Database Server should accept connections only from trusted Network(s)/IP(s) and restrict access from public IP addresses.
cis_gcp_6.5.remediation: Configure authorized networks in Cloud SQL to whitelist specific IPs, not all public addresses. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set 'authorized_networks' with CIDR blocks. Implement VPC Service Controls and private IP for enhanced security.

cis_gcp_6.6.description: It is recommended to configure Second Generation Sql instance to use private IPs instead of public IPs.
cis_gcp_6.6.remediation: Use private IPs for Cloud SQL instances by configuring VPC peering or Private Services Access. Disable public IP in the instance settings via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set 'ip_configuration' to use private networks.

cis_gcp_6.7.description: It is recommended to have all SQL database instances set to enable automated backups.
cis_gcp_6.7.remediation: Enable automated backups for all Cloud SQL instances via the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set 'backup_configuration' with 'enabled' true and specify retention periods. Ensure backups are encrypted and stored securely.

cis_gcp_7.1.description: It is recommended that the IAM policy on BigQuery datasets does not allow anonymous and/or public access.
cis_gcp_7.1.remediation: Restrict IAM policies on BigQuery datasets to avoid 'allUsers' or 'allAuthenticatedUsers' roles. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, configure 'access' in 'google_bigquery_dataset' with specific roles. Regularly audit permissions with tools like Policy Analyzer.

cis_gcp_7.2.description: BigQuery by default encrypts the data as rest by employing Envelope Encryption using Google managed cryptographic keys. The data is encrypted using the data encryption keys and data encryption keys themselves are further encrypted using key encryption keys. This is seamless and do not require any additional input from the user. However, if you want to have greater control, Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) can be used as encryption key management solution for BigQuery Data Sets. If CMEK is
cis_gcp_7.2.remediation: Enable CMEK for BigQuery datasets by specifying a Cloud KMS key in dataset settings. Use the GCP console, gcloud, or IaC. In Terraform, set 'encryption_configuration' in 'google_bigquery_dataset'. Manage key rotation and IAM roles for 'cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter'.

cis_gcp_7.3.description: BigQuery by default encrypts the data as rest by employing Envelope Encryption using Google managed cryptographic keys. The data is encrypted using the data encryption keys and data encryption keys themselves are further encrypted using key encryption keys. This is seamless and do not require any additional input from the user. However, if you want to have greater control, Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) can be used as encryption key management solution for BigQuery Data Sets.
cis_gcp_7.3.remediation: Specify a default Customer-Managed Encryption Key (CMEK) for all BigQuery datasets to ensure encryption key management control. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Google Cloud Deployment Manager to enforce CMEK settings during dataset creation. Regularly audit datasets to confirm CMEK usage and revoke or rotate keys as needed via Google Cloud Key Management Service.

cis_gcp_7.4.description: BigQuery tables can contain sensitive data that for security purposes should be discovered, monitored, classified, and protected. Google Cloud's Sensitive Data Protection tools can automatically provide data classification of all BigQuery data across an organization.
cis_gcp_7.4.remediation: Enable and configure Google Cloud's Sensitive Data Protection tools to automatically classify all data in BigQuery. Set up data classification policies and scanning schedules to continuously monitor for sensitive data. Use IaC to deploy classification templates and ensure compliance across all datasets and projects.

cis_gcp_8.1.description: When you use Dataproc, cluster and job data is stored on Persistent Disks (PDs) associated with the Compute Engine VMs in your cluster and in a Cloud Storage staging bucket. This PD and bucket data is encrypted using a Google-generated data encryption key (DEK) and key encryption key (KEK). The CMEK feature allows you to create, use, and revoke the key encryption key (KEK). Google still controls the data encryption key (DEK).
cis_gcp_8.1.remediation: Encrypt Dataproc clusters using Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) to control key encryption keys. Configure CMEK settings during cluster creation via Google Cloud Console, gcloud CLI, or IaC tools like Terraform. Ensure associated Cloud Storage buckets also use CMEK and regularly audit encryption status and key rotation policies.

cis_ibm_1.1.description: The ability to pass through devices provides the capability to offload tasks from the CPU to the device itself.
cis_ibm_1.1.remediation: Establish a formal approval process for GPU and USB pass-through devices, including documentation and risk assessments. Use manual configuration reviews and RBAC to restrict device access to authorized personnel only. Regularly audit device usage and update policies to align with security requirements.

cis_ibm_1.1.1.description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.1.remediation: Manually set file permissions to 600 or more restrictive for the API server pod specification file using commands like chmod. Implement procedural checks during deployment and maintenance to verify permissions. Document and enforce this in security policies and conduct regular audits.

cis_ibm_1.1.10.description: Ensure that the Container Network Interface files have ownership set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.10.remediation: Use automated tools or scripts to set ownership of Container Network Interface files to root:root. Implement IaC configurations or configuration management tools like Ansible to enforce this during deployment. Regularly scan and remediate any deviations to maintain security posture.

cis_ibm_1.1.11.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory has permissions of 700 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.11.remediation: Automate the setting of etcd data directory permissions to 700 or more restrictive using configuration management or security scanning tools. Incorporate this into deployment pipelines and IaC templates. Conduct periodic audits to ensure compliance and address any misconfigurations promptly.

cis_ibm_1.1.12.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory ownership is set to etcd:etcd .
cis_ibm_1.1.12.remediation: Manually set ownership of the etcd data directory to etcd:etcd using commands like chown. Establish procedural guidelines for configuration and verification during setup and updates. Regularly review ownership settings as part of security audits and documentation.

cis_ibm_1.1.13.description: Ensure that the kubeconfig file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.13.remediation: Manually configure kubeconfig file permissions to 600 or more restrictive using chmod. Implement security policies that mandate strict file permissions and train staff on proper handling. Conduct routine checks to ensure compliance and update procedures as needed.

cis_ibm_1.1.14.description: Ensure that the kubeconfig file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.14.remediation: Manually set ownership of the kubeconfig file to root:root with chown commands. Develop and follow procedural steps for file management during deployments and maintenance. Regularly audit ownership settings and document any changes to maintain security controls.

cis_ibm_1.1.15.description: Ensure that the Scheduler kubeconfig file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.15.remediation: Manually adjust permissions of the Scheduler kubeconfig file to 600 or more restrictive using chmod. Establish manual review processes for file configurations and integrate checks into change management. Train personnel on security best practices and perform periodic validations.

cis_ibm_1.1.16.description: Ensure that the kubeconfig file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.16.remediation: Manually set ownership of the Scheduler kubeconfig file to root:root via chown. Implement procedural controls for file ownership during system setup and modifications. Conduct regular audits to verify compliance and update documentation accordingly.

cis_ibm_1.1.17.description: Ensure that the kubeconfig file mounted into the Controller Manager has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.17.remediation: Manually configure permissions to 600 or more restrictive for the Controller Manager kubeconfig file using chmod. Follow established procedures for file security during deployment and updates. Regularly review and audit permissions to ensure adherence to security policies.

cis_ibm_1.1.18.description: Ensure that the kubeconfig file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.18.remediation: Manually set ownership of the Controller Manager kubeconfig file to root:root with chown. Develop and enforce manual processes for verifying ownership during configuration changes. Conduct periodic security assessments to maintain compliance and document findings.

cis_ibm_1.1.19.description: Ensure that the OpenShift PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.19.remediation: Manually set ownership of OpenShift PKI directories and files to root:root using chown. Establish procedural guidelines for PKI management and include ownership checks in security reviews. Regularly audit and update ownership settings to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_ibm_1.1.2.description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.2.remediation: Manually set ownership of the API server pod specification file to root:root with chown. Implement manual verification steps during deployment and maintenance cycles. Document procedures and conduct regular audits to ensure ongoing compliance with ownership requirements.

cis_ibm_1.1.20.description: Ensure that OpenShift PKI certificate files have permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.20.remediation: Automate the setting of OpenShift PKI certificate file permissions to 644 or more restrictive using configuration management tools. Integrate checks into CI/CD pipelines and IaC deployments. Regularly scan for deviations and remediate to maintain secure file access controls.

cis_ibm_1.1.21.description: Ensure that the OpenShift PKI key files have permissions of 600 .
cis_ibm_1.1.21.remediation: Use automated tools to enforce permissions of 600 for OpenShift PKI key files. Implement security scanning and IaC configurations to apply these settings consistently. Conduct periodic audits to verify compliance and address any misconfigurations promptly.

cis_ibm_1.1.3.description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.3.remediation: Manually set permissions to 600 or more restrictive for the controller manager pod specification file using chmod. Follow procedural checks during system configuration and updates. Regularly review permissions as part of security audits and update policies as necessary.

cis_ibm_1.1.4.description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.4.remediation: Manually set ownership of the controller manager pod specification file to root:root with chown. Establish manual processes for ownership verification during deployments. Conduct routine audits to ensure compliance and document any changes to maintain security controls.

cis_ibm_1.1.5.description: Ensure that the scheduler pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.5.remediation: Manually configure permissions to 600 or more restrictive for the scheduler pod specification file using chmod. Implement manual review procedures for file security during setup and maintenance. Train staff on best practices and perform regular validations to uphold security standards.

cis_ibm_1.1.6.description: Ensure that the scheduler pod specification file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.6.remediation: Automate the setting of ownership for the scheduler pod specification file to root:root using configuration management or security tools. Incorporate this into IaC templates and deployment scripts. Regularly audit ownership settings to ensure consistency and compliance.

cis_ibm_1.1.7.description: Ensure that the /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.7.remediation: Manually set permissions of the etcd pod specification file to 600 or more restrictive using chmod. Follow established procedures for file configuration during deployments and updates. Conduct periodic checks to verify permissions and document any adjustments.

cis_ibm_1.1.8.description: Ensure that the /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_1.1.8.remediation: Manually set ownership of the etcd pod specification file to root:root with chown. Implement manual verification steps as part of security protocols. Regularly audit ownership settings and update documentation to reflect current configurations.

cis_ibm_1.1.9.description: Ensure that the Container Network Interface files have permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_1.1.9.remediation: Automate the enforcement of permissions set to 600 or more restrictive for Container Network Interface files using security scanning tools or IaC. Integrate checks into deployment pipelines to prevent misconfigurations. Regularly monitor and remediate any deviations to maintain secure access controls.

cis_ibm_1.10.description: Namespace administrators have the capability to perform live migrations, which can potentially impact other users of the system. Live migration is a process where virtual machines can be moved from one physical host to another without any downtime, making it a valuable tool for managing clusters and ensuring high availability. However, since live migration involves moving data between hosts, it can have an impact on other users who may have applications running on the same hosts. As such, it is
cis_ibm_1.10.remediation: Establish a formal process to review and approve namespace administrator roles, ensuring only authorized personnel have access to migration tools. Implement RBAC policies to restrict live migration permissions to specific users or groups, and regularly audit access logs to detect unauthorized usage.

cis_ibm_1.11.description: The ability to exec commands in a pod allows for arbitrary execution by users, including administrative functions which normally require elevation of privileges by an administrator, and could lead to unauthorized use of both security and non-approved administrative functions.
cis_ibm_1.11.remediation: Use RBAC to restrict pod exec access to only necessary users or service accounts, applying the principle of least privilege. Implement admission controllers or security policies in Kubernetes to block exec commands in pods, and monitor for unauthorized exec attempts using audit logs.

cis_ibm_1.12.description: Access to each workload’s virtual screen is provided via VNC. Permission to use VNC is granted via a role, which means users with role assigned can access the VNC console of all workloads in a namespace.
cis_ibm_1.12.remediation: Configure RBAC roles to limit VNC access to specific users or groups, avoiding broad role assignments. Use network policies or firewall rules to restrict VNC traffic to trusted IP ranges, and regularly review and revoke unnecessary VNC permissions.

cis_ibm_1.13.description: An instance type is a reusable object used to create VMs with sane defaults based on the desired type of VM. Since the object is used to create instances of VMs in the entire cluster, its creation and update needs to be carefully and securely managed.
cis_ibm_1.13.remediation: Apply RBAC policies to restrict create and modify permissions for Virtual Machine Cluster Instance and Preference Types to administrators only. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) templates to enforce standardized configurations and audit changes to these objects regularly.

cis_ibm_1.14.description: The Containerized Data Importer (CDI) CR contains sensitive configuration data and is not namespaced.
cis_ibm_1.14.remediation: Implement RBAC to restrict update access to the CDI CR to cluster administrators or specific service accounts. Use Kubernetes admission controllers to validate changes, and encrypt sensitive data within the CR using secrets management tools.

cis_ibm_1.16.description: IBM Cloud features the capability for users with specific access roles to create access policies that allow all users (authenticated and non-authenticated) to access resources in the account. This “all users” access in turn ends up in public (including non-authenticated) access to resources. Determine if this capability is required by your organization and disable if not required.
cis_ibm_1.16.remediation: Review and remove any IAM policies that grant public access to Cloud Object Storage buckets. Use bucket policies and ACLs to restrict access to specific IAM users or roles, and enable logging to monitor for public access attempts.

cis_ibm_1.17.description: Revoke access privileges for users in an IBM Cloud account that are inactive, typically defined as user accounts with no logins in a given time frame.
cis_ibm_1.17.remediation: Configure IAM settings to automatically suspend or delete user accounts after a period of inactivity, such as 90 days. Regularly audit user activity logs and manually disable unused accounts, ensuring to notify users before taking action.

cis_ibm_1.19.description: Allow users to log in to IBM Cloud by using their corporate Identity Provider (IdP) to authenticate.
cis_ibm_1.19.remediation: Set up identity federation in IBM Cloud IAM to integrate with a corporate IdP like Azure AD or Okta. Configure single sign-on (SSO) and enforce MFA through the IdP, then disable local user accounts to centralize authentication management.

cis_ibm_1.2.description: Monitor API key usage in your account and search for API keys that are unused or used infrequently.
cis_ibm_1.2.remediation: Use IAM tools to set up alerts for API keys unused for 180 days and automatically disable or rotate them. Implement a key management policy that includes regular reviews and deletion of unnecessary keys, storing them securely.

cis_ibm_1.2.1.description: When anonymous requests to the API server are allowed, they must be authorized.
cis_ibm_1.2.1.remediation: Configure the Kubernetes API server to disable anonymous access or set RBAC policies to strictly limit what anonymous users can do. Use admission controllers to enforce authorization rules and monitor logs for any anonymous request attempts.

cis_ibm_1.2.10.remediation: Ensure the AlwaysAdmit admission control plugin is disabled in the API server configuration. Use other plugins like NodeRestriction or PodSecurityPolicy to enforce security policies, and validate settings through automated checks or IaC.

cis_ibm_1.2.11.remediation: Establish a process to configure the AlwaysPullImages admission control plugin in Kubernetes, ensuring images are always pulled from trusted registries. Use image security scanning tools and enforce policies through CI/CD pipelines to prevent outdated or vulnerable images.

cis_ibm_1.2.12.remediation: Enable the ServiceAccount admission control plugin in the API server to automate service account token management. Use RBAC to assign minimal permissions to service accounts and regularly audit their usage to prevent privilege escalation.

cis_ibm_1.2.13.description: Reject creating objects in a namespace that is undergoing termination.
cis_ibm_1.2.13.remediation: Configure the NamespaceLifecycle admission control plugin in Kubernetes to prevent object creation in terminating namespaces. Use IaC to enforce this setting across clusters and monitor for any violations through audit logs.

cis_ibm_1.2.14.description: Reject creating pods that do not match Pod Security Policies.
cis_ibm_1.2.14.remediation: Enable the SecurityContextConstraint admission control plugin in OpenShift to enforce pod security standards. Define and apply security context constraints (SCCs) using RBAC, and use automated tools to scan for non-compliant pods.

cis_ibm_1.2.15.description: Limit the Node and Pod objects that a kubelet could modify.
cis_ibm_1.2.15.remediation: Set the NodeRestriction admission control plugin in the API server to restrict kubelet permissions. Apply RBAC roles to limit node access and use configuration management tools to enforce these settings across the cluster.

cis_ibm_1.2.16.description: Do not bind the insecure API service.
cis_ibm_1.2.16.remediation: Establish a procedure to verify that the --insecure-bind-address argument is not set in the API server configuration. Use secure ports and TLS encryption for all API communications, and regularly audit server settings for compliance.

cis_ibm_1.2.17.description: Do not bind to insecure port.
cis_ibm_1.2.17.remediation: Implement a process to ensure the --insecure-port argument is set to 0 in the API server configuration. Enforce the use of TLS-enabled secure ports and monitor network traffic to detect any insecure binding attempts.

cis_ibm_1.2.18.description: Do not disable the secure port.
cis_ibm_1.2.18.remediation: Create a policy to check that the --secure-port argument is not set to 0 in the API server configuration. Use configuration management tools to enforce secure port settings and conduct regular security audits to verify compliance.

cis_ibm_1.2.19.remediation: Define a procedure to protect the healthz endpoint with RBAC, disabling profiling unless required for debugging. Use network policies to restrict access to the endpoint and monitor logs for unauthorized access attempts.

cis_ibm_1.2.2.remediation: Configure the API server to not use the --basic-auth-file argument, relying on more secure methods like tokens or certificates. Use IAM integration for authentication and enable audit logging to track authentication events.

cis_ibm_1.2.20.description: Enable auditing on the Kubernetes API Server and set the desired audit log path.
cis_ibm_1.2.20.remediation: Set the --audit-log-path argument in the API server configuration to enable auditing and specify a secure log location. Use log management tools to collect and analyze audit logs, ensuring they are protected from tampering.

cis_ibm_1.2.21.description: Retain the logs for at least 30 days or as appropriate.
cis_ibm_1.2.21.remediation: Configure log forwarding to send audit logs off the cluster to a secure storage service like IBM Cloud Object Storage. Set retention policies to keep logs for at least 30 days and use encryption to protect log data at rest.

cis_ibm_1.2.22.description: Retain 10 or an appropriate number of old log files.
cis_ibm_1.2.22.remediation: Set the maximumRetainedFiles argument in the audit configuration to retain at least 10 old log files. Use log rotation policies and automated tools to manage file retention, ensuring compliance with organizational requirements.

cis_ibm_1.2.23.description: Audit logs are rotated upon reaching a maximum size, which is 100 MB by default.
cis_ibm_1.2.23.remediation: Configure the maximumFileSizeMegabytes argument to 100 MB in the audit settings to enable log rotation. Use monitoring tools to alert on log file sizes and ensure logs are properly archived without loss of data.

cis_ibm_1.2.24.description: The API server minimum request timeout defines the minimum number of seconds a handler must keep a request open before timing it out.
cis_ibm_1.2.24.remediation: Manually configure the --request-timeout argument in the API server configuration to set an appropriate timeout value, such as 30 seconds, to prevent resource exhaustion from hanging requests. Regularly review and update this setting as part of security audits to align with operational needs.

cis_ibm_1.2.25.description: Validate service account before validating token.
cis_ibm_1.2.25.remediation: Automate the configuration of the --service-account-lookup argument to true in the API server settings using Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Ansible. Ensure RBAC policies are in place to restrict service account usage and monitor for unauthorized access via audit logs.

cis_ibm_1.2.26.description: Explicitly set a service account public key file for service accounts on the apiserver.
cis_ibm_1.2.26.remediation: Manually set the --service-account-key-file argument to a secure public key file in the API server configuration. Implement a process for regular key rotation and store keys in encrypted storage to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_ibm_1.2.27.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections.
cis_ibm_1.2.27.remediation: Manually configure the --etcd-certfile and --etcd-keyfile arguments with valid TLS certificates and private keys in the etcd settings. Use encryption for data at rest and enforce strict access controls to protect etcd from unauthorized connections.

cis_ibm_1.2.28.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server.
cis_ibm_1.2.28.remediation: Manually set the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments with up-to-date TLS certificates in the API server configuration. Implement a certificate management process for regular renewal and use strong cryptographic ciphers to secure in-transit data.

cis_ibm_1.2.29.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server.
cis_ibm_1.2.29.remediation: Automate the configuration of the --client-ca-file argument to specify a client CA certificate for TLS mutual authentication using IaC. Enforce RBAC to limit client access and monitor TLS connections through audit logging for security compliance.

cis_ibm_1.2.3.description: Do not use token based authentication.
cis_ibm_1.2.3.remediation: Automate the removal of the --token-auth-file parameter from the API server configuration using tools like Kubernetes operators or configuration management. Replace with certificate-based authentication and implement MFA for administrative access to prevent authentication bypass.

cis_ibm_1.2.30.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections.
cis_ibm_1.2.30.remediation: Automate the setting of the --etcd-cafile argument to a trusted CA certificate in etcd configuration using IaC. Ensure encryption is enabled for both data in transit and at rest, and restrict network access to etcd endpoints.

cis_ibm_1.2.31.description: Where etcd encryption is used, appropriate providers should be configured.
cis_ibm_1.2.31.remediation: Manually configure encryption providers in etcd settings, such as AES-GCM, and document the encryption process. Regularly audit encryption status and rotate encryption keys as part of security policies to protect sensitive data.

cis_ibm_1.2.32.description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
cis_ibm_1.2.32.remediation: Automate the configuration of the API server to enforce TLS 1.2 or higher and disable weak ciphers using security policies or IaC. Regularly scan for cipher compliance and update configurations to meet industry standards like NIST guidelines.

cis_ibm_1.2.33.description: OpenShift supported an option called unsupportedConfigOverrides that allowed users to opt into unsupported behavior. This option is no longer supported by OpenShift and should not be used.
cis_ibm_1.2.33.remediation: Automate checks to ensure unsupportedConfigOverrides is not present in cluster configurations using compliance scanning tools. Remove any instances and enforce governance policies to prevent use of unsupported settings, documenting approved configurations.

cis_ibm_1.2.34.description: Where etcd encryption is used, appropriate providers should be configured.
cis_ibm_1.2.34.remediation: Automate the verification of etcd encryption providers using tools like OpenShift operators or custom scripts. Ensure encryption is enabled with strong algorithms and implement key management practices for regular rotation and secure storage.

cis_ibm_1.2.35.description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
cis_ibm_1.2.35.remediation: Automate the configuration of the API server's TLS security profile to use modern ciphers like TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 via IaC. Regularly audit cipher settings and update to comply with security benchmarks like CIS.

cis_ibm_1.2.4.remediation: Manually configure kubelet to use HTTPS by setting appropriate TLS certificates and disabling HTTP endpoints. Establish a process for certificate management and enforce network policies to restrict access to kubelet ports.

cis_ibm_1.2.5.remediation: Automate the configuration of kubelet to use client certificates for authentication instead of tokens using Kubernetes manifests or IaC. Implement RBAC to control kubelet access and monitor authentication logs for anomalies.

cis_ibm_1.2.6.description: Verify kubelet's certificate before establishing connection.
cis_ibm_1.2.6.remediation: Manually set the kubelet certificate authority (CA) in the API server configuration to validate kubelet certificates. Regularly audit certificate validity and rotation schedules, and use encrypted channels for all kubelet communications.

cis_ibm_1.2.7.description: Do not always authorize all requests.
cis_ibm_1.2.7.remediation: Automate the configuration of the --authorization-mode argument to use RBAC or other secure modes like Node, Webhook in the API server using IaC. Enforce least privilege access and regularly review authorization policies to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_ibm_1.2.8.description: Turn on Role Based Access Control.
cis_ibm_1.2.8.remediation: Automate the enablement of RBAC in the cluster configuration using tools like OpenShift operators or Kubernetes admission controllers. Define and apply granular RBAC roles, and audit permissions regularly to ensure compliance with security policies.

cis_ibm_1.2.9.description: Limit the rate at which the API server accepts requests.
cis_ibm_1.2.9.remediation: Automate the enablement of the APIPriorityAndFairness feature gate in the API server configuration using IaC. Configure rate limiting policies and monitor API usage to prevent denial-of-service attacks and ensure fair resource allocation.

cis_ibm_1.3.description: Replace production API keys with new API keys regularly, every 90 days for example, as a best practice to secure your account.
cis_ibm_1.3.remediation: Automate API key rotation using IAM tools or scripts to generate and deploy new keys every 90 days. Enforce RBAC to limit key permissions and monitor key usage via audit logs to detect unauthorized access.

cis_ibm_1.3.1.remediation: Manually configure RBAC to restrict access to controller manager healthz endpoints and disable profiling features in the controller manager settings. Document access controls and regularly review permissions as part of security audits.

cis_ibm_1.3.2.description: Use individual service account credentials for each controller.
cis_ibm_1.3.2.remediation: Automate the setting of the --use-service-account-credentials argument to true in the controller manager configuration using IaC. Assign unique service accounts with minimal permissions and monitor their usage through audit logging.

cis_ibm_1.3.3.description: Explicitly set a service account private key file for service accounts on the controller manager.
cis_ibm_1.3.3.remediation: Manually configure the --service-account-private-key-file argument to a secure private key file in the controller manager settings. Implement key rotation policies and store keys in encrypted storage to protect against credential exposure.

cis_ibm_1.3.4.description: Allow pods to verify the API server's serving certificate before establishing connections.
cis_ibm_1.3.4.remediation: Manually set the --root-ca-file argument to a trusted root CA certificate in the controller manager configuration. Ensure certificates are valid and regularly updated, and use encryption for all pod-to-API server communications.

cis_ibm_1.3.5.description: Do not bind the Controller Manager service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
cis_ibm_1.3.5.remediation: Automate the configuration of the --bind-address argument to 127.0.0.1 in the controller manager settings using IaC. Restrict network access to loopback interfaces and enforce firewall rules to prevent exposure to external networks.

cis_ibm_1.3.6.description: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation on controller-manager.
cis_ibm_1.3.6.remediation: Manually configure the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument to true in the controller-manager settings. Ensure this is documented in your security procedures and regularly audited to maintain compliance.

cis_ibm_1.3.7.description: Do not bind the Controller Manager service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
cis_ibm_1.3.7.remediation: Set the --bind-address argument to 127.0.0.1 in the controller-manager configuration. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Ansible to enforce this setting across all clusters.

cis_ibm_1.4.1.remediation: Manually review and disable profiling endpoints for the scheduler if not required. Update security policies to include regular checks and ensure RBAC is properly configured to restrict access.
