cis_ibm_1.4.2.description: Do not bind the scheduler service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
cis_ibm_1.4.2.remediation: Configure the scheduler to bind only to loopback addresses. Implement automated checks using CSPM tools and enforce RBAC policies to restrict API service access.

cis_ibm_1.5.description: Transport Layer Security (TLS) is a cryptographic protocol used in this context to protect data in transit. Restricting operations to the use of trusted registries ensures the use of approved container images.
cis_ibm_1.5.remediation: Configure container registries to use TLS and restrict to trusted sources. Use policy enforcement tools like Open Policy Agent (OPA) and update IaC templates to enforce TLS settings.

cis_ibm_1.6.description: provides a mechanism to customize hyperconverged (HCO) object - The kubevirt OpenShift Virtualization components through the control of patching operations. Although this approach itself does not inherently introduce security risks, enabling have unintended consequences and might not be officially experimental features may benefits of using these options against any supported. It is essential to weigh thepotential security implications before proceeding.
cis_ibm_1.6.remediation: Manually review and restrict patching operations in annotations for Hyperconverged. Establish a change management process to evaluate security implications before enabling experimental features.

cis_ibm_1.7.description: Kernel Samepage Merging (KSM) is a Linux kernel feature that merges identical memory pages across virtual machines running on a single host, potentially reducing memory usage and improving system performance.
cis_ibm_1.7.remediation: Disable KSM in hyperconverged configurations to mitigate potential security risks from memory sharing. Use automated configuration management tools to enforce this setting across all nodes.

cis_ibm_1.8.description: Requires account owner to provide multiple factors of login credentials to authenticate their identity and gain access to IBM Cloud resources.
cis_ibm_1.8.remediation: Enable MFA for the account owner in IBM Cloud IAM settings. Use automated policies to enforce MFA and regularly audit account configurations to ensure compliance.

cis_ibm_1.9.description: Requires users to provide multiple factors of login credentials to authenticate their identity and gain access to IBM Cloud resources.
cis_ibm_1.9.remediation: Enable MFA at the account level for all users in IBM Cloud IAM. Implement automated checks and use RBAC to enforce MFA policies across the organization.

cis_ibm_10.1.description: You can use the IBM Cloud™ Certificate Manager service dashboard to manage certificates that you obtain from third-party issuers, or order from Certificate Manager, to use with your IBM cloud-based apps or services. You can manually or automatically renew certificates that you order through Certificate Manager. You can also disable the auto-renewal feature after you enable it. You can set a certificate to automatically renew while you're ordering. Or, you can enable auto- renewal after it's conf
cis_ibm_10.1.remediation: Enable auto-renewal for certificates in IBM Cloud Certificate Manager. Configure alerts for certificate expiration and use automation scripts to ensure timely renewals.

cis_ibm_2.1.description: Configure TLS encryption for the etcd service.
cis_ibm_2.1.remediation: Set the --cert-file and --key-file arguments appropriately to enable TLS encryption for etcd. Use IaC to deploy these configurations and regularly audit TLS settings.

cis_ibm_2.1.1.1.description: Users can store objects in IBM Cloud Object Storage buckets by providing their own encryption keys which get applied at a per object level.
cis_ibm_2.1.1.1.remediation: Configure Cloud Object Storage to use customer-managed keys (CMK) for encryption. Use key management services like IBM Key Protect and enforce this via IaC policies.

cis_ibm_2.1.1.2.description: You can use IBM Cloud encryption key management service, for example Key Protect, to bring your own root key (BYOK) to IBM Cloud and use it to add envelope encryption for data that is stored in IBM Cloud Object Storage buckets.
cis_ibm_2.1.1.2.remediation: Enable BYOK encryption for Cloud Object Storage using IBM Key Protect. Automate key rotation and access policies to maintain security compliance.

cis_ibm_2.1.1.3.description: You can use IBM Cloud encryption key management services, for example, Hyper Protect Crypto Services to keep and manage exclusive control over the root keys used to add envelop encryption for data that is stored in IBM Cloud Object Storage buckets.
cis_ibm_2.1.1.3.remediation: Use KYOK encryption with Hyper Protect Crypto Services for Cloud Object Storage. Implement automated checks to ensure encryption is enabled and keys are properly managed.

cis_ibm_2.1.2.description: IBM Cloud Object Storage bucket firewall restricts all access to data unless the request originates from a list of allowed IP addresses.
cis_ibm_2.1.2.remediation: Restrict network access to Cloud Object Storage by configuring IP allowlists. Use network security groups and firewall rules in IaC to enforce these restrictions.

cis_ibm_2.1.3.description: IBM Cloud Object Storage bucket firewall restricts all access to data unless the request originates from a list of allowed IP addresses that are part of the private subnets only.
cis_ibm_2.1.3.remediation: Configure Cloud Object Storage to expose endpoints only on private networks. Use VPC settings and security policies to block public access and enforce private endpoint usage.

cis_ibm_2.1.4.description: Access controls on the Cloud Object Storage buckets are governed via IBM Identity and Access Management (IAM). However, some permissions can also be granted (or restricted) via S3 access controls.
cis_ibm_2.1.4.remediation: Use IAM and S3 access controls to restrict bucket access. Implement least-privilege principles and automate policy reviews to prevent excessive permissions.

cis_ibm_2.2.description: Enable client authentication on etcd service.
cis_ibm_2.2.remediation: Manually set the --client-cert-auth argument to true in etcd configuration. Document this in security procedures and conduct regular audits to ensure client authentication is enforced.

cis_ibm_2.2.1.1.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Services – Key Protect or Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS). Provider-managed encryption is turned on by default and cannot be turned off.
cis_ibm_2.2.1.1.remediation: Configure Block Storage to use customer-managed keys (CMK) via IBM Key Protect or HPCS. Use IaC to enforce encryption settings and regularly audit key management practices.

cis_ibm_2.2.1.2.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Service – Key Protect . The customer can chose to use BYOK instead of provider-managed keys for enhanced security
cis_ibm_2.2.1.2.remediation: Enable BYOK encryption for Block Storage using IBM Key Protect. Automate key deployment and rotation policies to ensure continuous encryption compliance.

cis_ibm_2.2.1.3.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Service – Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS). The customer can choose to use BYOK instead of provider-managed keys for enhanced security
cis_ibm_2.2.1.3.remediation: Use KYOK encryption with Hyper Protect Crypto Services for Block Storage. Implement automated checks to verify encryption status and key management configurations.

cis_ibm_2.2.2.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Services – Key Protect or Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS). Provider-managed encryption is turned on by default and cannot be turned off.
cis_ibm_2.2.2.remediation: Encrypt OS disks with customer-managed keys using IBM Key Protect or HPCS. Use IaC templates to enforce encryption and regularly review disk encryption settings.

cis_ibm_2.2.3.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Services – Key Protect or Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS). Provider-managed encryption is turned on by default and cannot be turned off.
cis_ibm_2.2.3.remediation: Encrypt data disks with customer-managed keys via IBM Key Protect or HPCS. Automate encryption policies in deployment scripts and audit disk configurations periodically.

cis_ibm_2.2.4.description: By default, IBM Cloud Block Storage provides provider-managed encryption for all data. For enhanced security, customers can bring their own encryption keys and manage them through IBM Key Management Services – Key Protect or Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS). Provider-managed encryption is turned on by default and cannot be turned off.
cis_ibm_2.2.4.remediation: Encrypt unattached disks with customer-managed keys using IBM Key Protect or HPCS. Implement automated cleanup policies and encryption checks for unused storage resources.

cis_ibm_2.3.description: Do not use self-signed certificates for TLS.
cis_ibm_2.3.remediation: Ensure the --auto-tls argument is not set to true in etcd configuration. Use trusted certificate authorities (CAs) and automate certificate management with tools like Cert-Manager.

cis_ibm_2.4.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for peer connections.
cis_ibm_2.4.remediation: Manually configure etcd with the --peer-cert-file and --peer-key-file arguments to specify TLS certificates and keys for peer connections. Ensure these files are securely stored and regularly rotated as part of your security procedures.

cis_ibm_2.5.description: etcd should be configured for peer authentication.
cis_ibm_2.5.remediation: Manually set the --peer-client-cert-auth argument to true in etcd configuration to enforce mutual TLS authentication between peers. Establish a process to manage and renew client certificates to maintain secure authentication.

cis_ibm_2.6.description: Do not use automatically generated self-signed certificates for TLS connections between peers.
cis_ibm_2.6.remediation: Manually ensure the --peer-auto-tls argument is not set to true; instead, use certificates from a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). Implement a procedure to procure and deploy CA-signed certificates for etcd peer communications.

cis_ibm_2.7.description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
cis_ibm_2.7.remediation: Automate the deployment of a unique CA for etcd using Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Ansible. Configure etcd to use this separate CA to limit the impact of a compromised certificate.

cis_ibm_2.8.description: ETCD is not enabled by default; enabling it can provide an additional layer of protection.
cis_ibm_2.8.remediation: Automate the enablement of etcd encryption via configuration management or cloud-native tools. Use encryption-at-rest features in your cloud environment to secure etcd data, and regularly audit encryption status.

cis_ibm_3.1.description: Collect audit events from IBM Cloud resources so that you can monitor activity in your IBM Cloud account.
cis_ibm_3.1.remediation: Automate the configuration of IBM Cloud Activity Tracker across all regions using scripts or IaC. Ensure logging is enabled for all critical resources and integrate with a centralized logging solution for continuous monitoring.

cis_ibm_3.1.1.description: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
cis_ibm_3.1.1.remediation: Manually avoid using client certificates for user authentication; instead, implement identity providers like OIDC or LDAP. Establish a procedure to manage user access through RBAC and regularly review authentication methods to ensure compliance.

cis_ibm_3.2.description: Sharable disks are shared between VMs, and they can be used only if a filesystem file system or the application using the device is - installed on top of it is a clusterdistributed and cloud aware. Their wrong usage might cause data corruption.
cis_ibm_3.2.remediation: Automate checks to disable shareable disks on VMs using cloud provider APIs or configuration management tools. Implement policies in IaC to prevent the creation of shareable disks unless explicitly required for clustered applications.

cis_ibm_3.2.1.description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server.
cis_ibm_3.2.1.remediation: Automate the creation of a minimal audit policy using Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts. Ensure the policy logs essential events and deploy it across all clusters to maintain consistent auditing.

cis_ibm_3.2.2.description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
cis_ibm_3.2.2.remediation: Automate the expansion of audit policies to include key security events like unauthorized access, privilege escalations, and resource modifications. Use policy-as-code tools to enforce and validate coverage across environments.

cis_ibm_3.3.description: Error policies for disks control how Input/Output (IO) errors are handled. If a read or write operation fails on the storage, an IO error occurs. Setting the error policy to recommended because it makes it difficult to determine the root cause of is not ignore any IO issues, which can lead to data loss if the data is not correctly written to the disk.
cis_ibm_3.3.remediation: Automate the configuration of disk error policies to not ignore IO errors using cloud provider settings or IaC. Implement monitoring to alert on disk failures and ensure data integrity through regular backups and redundancy.

cis_ibm_3.4.description: Events that you collect and centralize in the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service provide information about actions that take place on your account. You can define alerts to notify promptly of problems, anomalies, and abnormal situations.
cis_ibm_3.4.remediation: Automate the setup of alerts in IBM Cloud Activity Tracker for unauthorized requests, critical actions, and high-impact operations. Use scripting or cloud-native tools to define alert rules and integrate with notification channels like email or Slack.

cis_ibm_3.5.description: Monitor the account owner's access to the IBM Cloud account is done from authorized locations that are restricted by IP addresses.
cis_ibm_3.5.remediation: Automate IP restriction policies for account owner logins using IBM Cloud IAM or network security groups. Implement geofencing and IP allowlisting through IaC to enforce access controls and regularly update authorized ranges.

cis_ibm_3.6.description: Ensure Activity Tracker data is encrypted at rest.
cis_ibm_3.6.remediation: Automate the enablement of encryption-at-rest for Activity Tracker data using IBM Cloud Key Protect or similar services. Configure encryption keys and policies via IaC to ensure all audit logs are securely stored and compliant with data protection standards.

cis_ibm_3.7.description: Check whether Activity Tracker trails are integrated with LogDNA Logs.
cis_ibm_3.7.remediation: Automate the integration of Activity Tracker with LogDNA for centralized log management and archiving. Use cloud provider APIs or configuration scripts to set up log forwarding and ensure long-term retention for compliance and analysis.

cis_ibm_4.1.description: access each other's network interfaces, it is To ensure that virtual machines cannot crucial to segment them completely using separate VLANs. By connecting virtual logically separated VLANs, you enforce network segmentation at the L2 machines to machines from unauthorized/unintended data transmission, and level, preventing virtual breaches or data leaks. reducing the risk of security
cis_ibm_4.1.remediation: Automate the deployment of dedicated VLANs for network segmentation using IBM Cloud networking services or IaC tools like Terraform. Implement network policies and firewall rules to restrict traffic between segments and regularly audit configurations.

cis_ibm_4.1.1.description: Ensure that the kubelet service file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_4.1.1.remediation: Manually check and set the kubelet service file permissions to 644 or more restrictive on all nodes. Establish a routine procedure to verify file permissions as part of security audits and system hardening.

cis_ibm_4.1.10.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file is owned by root:root .
cis_ibm_4.1.10.remediation: Automate the verification and enforcement of root:root ownership for kubelet configuration files using configuration management tools like Ansible or Chef. Implement checks in CI/CD pipelines to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_ibm_4.1.2.description: Ensure that the kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_4.1.2.remediation: Automate the setting of kubelet service file ownership to root:root across all cluster nodes using scripts or orchestration tools. Regularly audit ownership settings to maintain security compliance.

cis_ibm_4.1.3.description: If kube-proxy is running, and if it is using a file-based configuration file, ensure that the file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_4.1.3.remediation: Automate the configuration of kube-proxy file permissions to 644 or more restrictive using configuration management or cloud-native security tools. Enforce these settings in IaC templates to ensure consistency.

cis_ibm_4.1.4.description: If kube-proxy is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_4.1.4.remediation: Manually verify and set the ownership of kube-proxy kubeconfig files to root:root on all relevant nodes. Document this step in operational procedures and include it in regular security reviews.

cis_ibm_4.1.5.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_4.1.5.remediation: Automate the enforcement of 644 permissions for kubelet.conf files using security scanning tools or configuration scripts. Integrate these checks into deployment pipelines to catch deviations early.

cis_ibm_4.1.6.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_4.1.6.remediation: Automate the setting of kubelet.conf file ownership to root:root across all nodes using orchestration or configuration management tools. Schedule regular audits to ensure ongoing compliance.

cis_ibm_4.1.7.description: Ensure that the certificate authorities file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_4.1.7.remediation: Manually review and adjust permissions for certificate authorities files to 644 or more restrictive. Implement a process for periodic checks and updates as part of certificate management procedures.

cis_ibm_4.1.8.description: Ensure that the certificate authorities file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_ibm_4.1.8.remediation: Manually set the ownership of certificate authorities files to root:root and document this in security guidelines. Conduct regular audits to verify ownership and maintain control over sensitive files.

cis_ibm_4.1.9.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_ibm_4.1.9.remediation: Manually review and set file permissions to 600 or stricter using commands like chmod 600 /path/to/kubelet-config.yaml. Implement a process to regularly audit file permissions in Kubernetes node configurations to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_ibm_4.18.description: The nonRoot feature gate restricts virtual machine access to non-root privileges, preventing full administrative access and reducing attack surface in OpenShift environments.
cis_ibm_4.18.remediation: Enable the nonRoot feature gate in OpenShift configurations via YAML files or cluster settings. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Ansible to enforce this setting across all virtual machines and nodes.

cis_ibm_4.2.description: MAC spoof filtering prevents man-in-the-middle attacks by verifying MAC addresses in network packets, ensuring only authorized devices communicate.
cis_ibm_4.2.remediation: Enable MAC spoof filtering in OpenShift virtual machine settings through configuration files or management consoles. Automate this using IaC scripts to apply the setting consistently across all network interfaces and nodes.

cis_ibm_4.2.1.description: Configure garbage collection for containers and images as appropriate to manage resource usage and prevent disk exhaustion in OpenShift Container Platform 4.
cis_ibm_4.2.1.remediation: Manually set up garbage collection policies in OpenShift cluster configurations based on workload needs. Establish a review process to adjust thresholds and schedules periodically to optimize performance and security.

cis_ibm_4.2.10.description: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation to prevent stale or compromised certificates from being used for authentication.
cis_ibm_4.2.10.remediation: Ensure the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false in kubelet configuration files. Use automated tools to monitor and enforce certificate rotation policies across all Kubernetes nodes.

cis_ibm_4.2.11.description: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation to maintain secure TLS connections and prevent certificate-based attacks.
cis_ibm_4.2.11.remediation: Set the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument to true in kubelet configurations. Implement IaC practices to automate this setting and use CSPM tools to audit for compliance regularly.

cis_ibm_4.2.12.description: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers to protect data in transit.
cis_ibm_4.2.12.remediation: Configure kubelet settings to restrict TLS ciphers to strong algorithms like AES-GCM. Use security policies and automated checks to enforce cipher suites across all cluster nodes.

cis_ibm_4.2.13.description: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers to protect data in transit.
cis_ibm_4.2.13.remediation: Apply TLS security profiles in OpenShift to enforce strong ciphers for kubelet communications. Utilize CSPM automation to validate configurations and remediate deviations via policy-as-code.

cis_ibm_4.2.2.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server to prevent unauthorized access and information disclosure.
cis_ibm_4.2.2.remediation: Set the --anonymous-auth argument to false in kubelet configuration files. Use RBAC and network policies to restrict access, and automate checks to ensure this setting is consistently applied.

cis_ibm_4.2.3.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization to enforce least privilege access controls.
cis_ibm_4.2.3.remediation: Ensure the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow; use modes like Webhook or RBAC. Configure authorization settings in API server manifests and audit with automated tools.

cis_ibm_4.2.4.description: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates to secure node-to-control plane communications.
cis_ibm_4.2.4.remediation: Set the --client-ca-file argument to a valid CA certificate path in kubelet configurations. Use certificate management systems and IaC to deploy and rotate certificates securely.

cis_ibm_4.2.5.description: Disable the read-only port to prevent unauthorized access to kubelet metrics and data.
cis_ibm_4.2.5.remediation: Verify the read-only port is set to 0 or not used in kubelet settings. Update configuration files and use automated scans to detect and fix any enabled read-only ports.

cis_ibm_4.2.6.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections to prevent resource exhaustion and denial-of-service attacks.
cis_ibm_4.2.6.remediation: Ensure the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not set to 0; set a reasonable timeout value. Implement configuration management tools to enforce timeout policies across nodes.

cis_ibm_4.2.7.description: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables for proper network security and pod communication controls.
cis_ibm_4.2.7.remediation: Set the --make-iptables-util-chains argument to true in kubelet configurations. Use IaC templates to apply this setting and validate with automated compliance checks.

cis_ibm_4.2.8.description: Set the --event-qps flag appropriately to balance event logging and prevent denial of service, ensuring security events are captured.
cis_ibm_4.2.8.remediation: Configure kubeAPIQPS to a non-zero value that allows adequate event capture without overwhelming the kubelet. Monitor and adjust settings based on cluster load using automated tools.

cis_ibm_4.2.9.description: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets using certificate files to encrypt communications and authenticate nodes.
cis_ibm_4.2.9.remediation: Set the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments to appropriate certificate paths. Use certificate authorities and automated deployment to manage TLS credentials securely.

cis_ibm_4.3.description: Use multi-network policies for micro-segmentation in VLANs to control traffic flow, contain breaches, and limit unauthorized access in OpenShift networks.
cis_ibm_4.3.remediation: Implement multi-network policies in OpenShift using YAML definitions to segment network traffic. Automate policy deployment with GitOps workflows and validate with network security tools.

cis_ibm_4.4.description: Data stored in IBM Cloud Databases need to be encrypted at rest at all times for client data security.
cis_ibm_4.4.remediation: Enable disk encryption for IBM Cloud Databases through the management console or API. Use IaC scripts like Terraform to provision encrypted databases and set up alerts for any unencrypted instances.

cis_ibm_5.1.description: Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) is a security-related CPU feature that is not currently leveraged by OpenShift or Openshift virtualization.
cis_ibm_5.1.remediation: Disable Intel TXT in BIOS or firmware settings if not needed. Document the procedure and use automated checks to ensure it remains disabled across all hardware nodes.

cis_ibm_5.1.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
cis_ibm_5.1.1.remediation: Restrict cluster-admin role assignments to essential users via RBAC policies. Implement regular audits using automated tools to review and revoke unnecessary admin privileges.

cis_ibm_5.1.2.description: Access to Kubernetes secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
cis_ibm_5.1.2.remediation: Use RBAC to limit secret access to specific service accounts and users. Automate policy enforcement with tools like OPA Gatekeeper and monitor access logs for anomalies.

cis_ibm_5.1.3.description: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles to prevent inadvertent access to new resources and enforce least privilege.
cis_ibm_5.1.3.remediation: Avoid using "*" in role definitions; specify explicit resources and verbs. Use automated scanners to detect wildcards and refactor roles with precise permissions via IaC.

cis_ibm_5.1.4.description: Restrict access to create pods to the smallest possible group of users to prevent privilege escalation and sensitive data exposure.
cis_ibm_5.1.4.remediation: Apply RBAC policies that limit pod creation permissions to authorized users or service accounts. Use Pod Security Policies or similar controls to enforce security contexts and automate compliance checks.

cis_ibm_5.1.5.description: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
cis_ibm_5.1.5.remediation: Create and assign specific service accounts for each application instead of using the default. Use Kubernetes manifests to define service accounts and automate audits to detect default account usage.

cis_ibm_5.1.6.description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload explicitly needs to communicate with the API server.
cis_ibm_5.1.6.remediation: Configure pod specifications to automountServiceAccountToken: false unless required. Use admission controllers and automated policies to enforce this setting and review token usage regularly.

cis_ibm_5.2.description: IBM Cloudant encrypts all client data at-rest by default. For customers using a Dedicated Hardware plan instance, it is optional to use the service's integration with IBM Key Protect for customers to bring their own encryption key at provision time for the instance.
cis_ibm_5.2.remediation: Provision a Cloudant Dedicated Hardware plan instance using Bring-Your-Own-Key with Key Protect. Ensure you are logged into your IBM Cloud account at https://cloud.ibm.com/ with a paid account. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) templates to enforce encryption with customer-managed keys during deployment.

cis_ibm_5.2.1.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true.
cis_ibm_5.2.1.remediation: Configure Kubernetes Pod Security Admission or Security Context Constraints (SCCs) to restrict privileged containers. Use RBAC to limit users who can deploy privileged pods and enforce policies via IaC tools like Terraform or Helm charts.

cis_ibm_5.2.10.description: OpenShift has the concept of Security Context Constraints (SCCs) that supplement the Pod Security Admission controller. SCCs allow you to group elevated container capabilities and assign those capabilities to users and groups.
cis_ibm_5.2.10.remediation: Define and apply restrictive SCCs in OpenShift to minimize access to privileged capabilities. Use RBAC to assign SCCs only to necessary users and groups, and audit SCC assignments regularly to ensure compliance.

cis_ibm_5.2.2.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
cis_ibm_5.2.2.remediation: Configure Kubernetes or OpenShift policies to restrict containers from sharing the host process ID namespace. Use Pod Security Standards or SCCs to enforce this, and monitor deployments with CSPM tools to detect violations.

cis_ibm_5.2.3.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostIPC flag set to true.
cis_ibm_5.2.3.remediation: Enforce restrictions on containers sharing the host IPC namespace using Kubernetes Pod Security Admission or OpenShift SCCs. Implement IaC policies to prevent hostIPC usage and conduct regular audits for compliance.

cis_ibm_5.2.4.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostNetwork flag set to true.
cis_ibm_5.2.4.remediation: Use Kubernetes Network Policies or OpenShift SCCs to restrict containers from using the host network namespace. Configure IaC templates to avoid hostNetwork settings and enable CSPM scanning to identify misconfigurations.

cis_ibm_5.2.5.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true.
cis_ibm_5.2.5.remediation: Set allowPrivilegeEscalation to false in pod security contexts. Apply this via Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or OpenShift SCCs, and use CSPM tools to continuously monitor and remediate any deviations in container configurations.

cis_ibm_5.2.6.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
cis_ibm_5.2.6.remediation: Configure pods to run as non-root users by setting runAsNonRoot to true in security contexts. Enforce this with Kubernetes Pod Security Admission or OpenShift SCCs, and use RBAC to restrict deployments that bypass this rule.

cis_ibm_5.2.7.description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
cis_ibm_5.2.7.remediation: Remove NET_RAW from container capabilities by configuring security contexts. Use Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or OpenShift SCCs to drop this capability, and audit container definitions in IaC to ensure compliance.

cis_ibm_5.2.8.description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
cis_ibm_5.2.8.remediation: Restrict additional capabilities in containers by using Kubernetes Pod Security Admission or OpenShift SCCs. Define minimal capability sets in security contexts and use CSPM tools to scan for unauthorized capability assignments.

cis_ibm_5.2.9.description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities.
cis_ibm_5.2.9.remediation: Configure containers to run without any added capabilities by dropping all in security contexts. Enforce this via Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or OpenShift SCCs, and implement IaC policies to prevent capability assignments.

cis_ibm_5.3.description: IBM Cloudant instances can only be connected to over TLS or HTTPS connections. This behavior is by default and is non-configurable.
cis_ibm_5.3.remediation: Ensure all connections to IBM Cloudant use HTTPS or TLS by verifying instance configurations. Use network policies and CSPM checks to confirm encryption in transit, and avoid any custom settings that might disable this default behavior.

cis_ibm_5.3.1.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
cis_ibm_5.3.1.remediation: Select a CNI plugin that supports Network Policies, such as Calico or Cilium. Configure the cluster during provisioning using IaC tools, and validate support through CSPM scans to ensure effective network traffic isolation.

cis_ibm_5.3.2.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
cis_ibm_5.3.2.remediation: Define and apply Network Policies for all namespaces to control ingress and egress traffic. Use IaC templates like Kubernetes manifests to enforce policies, and regularly audit with CSPM tools to ensure no namespace is left unprotected.

cis_ibm_5.4.1.description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
cis_ibm_5.4.1.remediation: Configure pods to mount secrets as files rather than environment variables. Update deployment manifests in IaC to use volume mounts for secrets, and use CSPM checks to identify and remediate pods using environment variable secrets.

cis_ibm_5.4.2.description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs.
cis_ibm_5.4.2.remediation: Integrate with external secret managers like AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault. Configure authentication, encryption, and auditing for secret access. Use IaC to deploy and manage these integrations, ensuring secrets are rotated regularly.

cis_ibm_5.5.1.description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
cis_ibm_5.5.1.remediation: Set up image provenance using OpenShift image controller configuration parameters to verify image sources and integrity. Use IaC to enforce trusted registries and enable CSPM scanning to detect images from untrusted sources.

cis_ibm_5.7.1.description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
cis_ibm_5.7.1.remediation: Create and use namespaces to separate resources by environment or team. Define namespace policies in IaC templates, apply RBAC to restrict cross-namespace access, and use CSPM tools to audit namespace usage and configurations.

cis_ibm_5.7.2.description: Enable default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
cis_ibm_5.7.2.remediation: Configure pods to use the default seccomp profile by setting securityContext.seccompProfile.type to RuntimeDefault. Enforce this via Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or OpenShift SCCs, and update IaC manifests to include seccomp settings.

cis_ibm_5.7.3.description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
cis_ibm_5.7.3.remediation: Define security contexts in pod and container specifications to set user IDs, capabilities, and other security settings. Use IaC templates to standardize security contexts across deployments and enable CSPM checks to validate configurations.

cis_ibm_5.7.4.description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
cis_ibm_5.7.4.remediation: Avoid using the default namespace by specifying namespaces in all Kubernetes manifests. Use IaC policies to enforce namespace assignments and implement RBAC to restrict access to the default namespace, improving security isolation.

cis_ibm_6.1.description: Enabling nested virtualization in OpenShift Virtualization allows virtual machines to run within a hypervisor that is itself running on top of an existing operating system.
cis_ibm_6.1.remediation: Disable nested virtualization in OpenShift Virtualization settings unless explicitly required. Configure cluster settings via IaC tools and use CSPM scans to detect and remediate enabled nested virtualization, reducing attack surface.

cis_ibm_6.1.2.description: The Web Application Firewall (WAF) protects against ISO Layer-7 attacks, which can be some of the most tricky.
cis_ibm_6.1.2.remediation: Enable the Web Application Firewall (WAF) in IBM Cloud Internet Services (CIS) for all proxied DNS records and applications. Configure WAF rules via the CIS dashboard or IaC templates, and regularly update rules to protect against emerging threats.

cis_ibm_6.1.3.description: IBM Cloud Internet services provides automatic DDoS protection for any proxied DNS records or a Range Application.
cis_ibm_6.1.3.remediation: Ensure DDoS protection is active by proxying DNS records and Global Load Balancers in IBM Cloud Internet Services. Configure allowlists to only permit IPs from CIS, and use IaC to enforce these settings across all network ingress points.

cis_ibm_6.2.description: Openshift Virtualization provides metrics that you can use to monitor the consumption of cluster infrastructure resources, including vCPU.
cis_ibm_6.2.remediation: Enable vCPU metrics by applying the kernel argument schedstats=enable in OpenShift Virtualization. Follow manual procedures to update cluster configurations, document the steps, and schedule regular reviews to ensure metrics are active for monitoring.

cis_ibm_6.2.1.description: VPC access control lists filter all incoming and outgoing traffic in IBM Cloud VPC. An ACL is a built-in, virtual firewall where ACL rules control traffic to and from the subnets, rather than to and from the virtual servers. It is recommended that no ACL allows unrestricted ingress access to port 22.
cis_ibm_6.2.1.remediation: Review and modify VPC ACL rules to deny inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22 (SSH). Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform to enforce default-deny policies and restrict access to specific IP ranges. Regularly audit ACL configurations to prevent exposure of administrative ports.

cis_ibm_6.2.2.description: VPC security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to Virtual Server. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to a Virtual Server. Unless modified, the default security group allows inbound traffic from all members of the group that is, all other virtual servers that are attached to this security group.
cis_ibm_6.2.2.remediation: Modify the default security group to restrict all inbound and outbound traffic by default. Implement least-privilege rules using IaC, and avoid using default security groups for production workloads. Use RBAC to control who can modify security group settings.

cis_ibm_6.2.3.description: VPC security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to Virtual Server Instances. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to port 3389.
cis_ibm_6.2.3.remediation: Update security group rules to block inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389 (RDP). Use IaC to enforce strict access controls and limit RDP access to trusted IP addresses. Enable logging and monitoring to detect unauthorized access attempts.

cis_ibm_6.2.4.description: VPC security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to Virtual Servers. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to port 22.
cis_ibm_6.2.4.remediation: Remove any security group rules that allow SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0. Implement network segmentation and use bastion hosts or VPNs for secure access. Automate compliance checks with CSPM tools to flag open SSH ports.

cis_ibm_6.2.5.description: VPC access control lists filter all incoming and outgoing traffic in IBM Cloud VPC. An ACL is a built-in, virtual firewall where ACL rules control traffic to and from the subnets, rather than to and from the virtual servers. It is recommended that no ACL allows unrestricted ingress access to port 3389.
cis_ibm_6.2.5.remediation: Adjust VPC ACLs to deny inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389. Use IaC to define and enforce ACL policies, and apply encryption for data in transit. Regularly review ACL configurations to ensure no public exposure of RDP ports.

cis_ibm_6.3.description: Ensure that all known CPU vulnerabilities are mitigated. This ensures that known attacks, such as Spectre, Meltdown, etc. either do not affect the node or are mitigated via software.
cis_ibm_6.3.remediation: Establish a process to regularly check for CPU vulnerability patches from IBM Cloud and apply them to virtual servers. Use manual procedures to update node images and verify mitigations through security advisories. Document patch management steps in organizational policies.

cis_ibm_7.1.1.1.description: IBM® Key Protect for IBM Cloud™ helps you provision encrypted keys for apps across IBM Cloud services. Use the IBM Key Protect for IBM Cloud key management service (KMS) to encrypt data in Kubernetes secrets and prevent unauthorized users from accessing sensitive app information (for example, credentials and keys). Keys are generated by FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified hardware security modules (HSMs) that are located in secure IBM Cloud data centers.
cis_ibm_7.1.1.1.remediation: Configure Kubernetes clusters to use IBM Key Protect with BYOK for encrypting secrets. Use IaC to automate key provisioning and enforce encryption policies. Regularly audit key usage and access controls to ensure secrets remain protected.

cis_ibm_7.1.1.2.description: IBM Cloud® Hyper Protect Crypto Services (Hyper Protect Crypto Services for short) is a dedicated key management service and hardware security module (HSM) based on IBM Cloud. This service allows you to take the ownership of the cloud HSM to fully manage your encryption keys and to perform cryptographic operations. Use Hyper Protect Crypt Services, a dedicated key management service (KMS) and hardware security module (HSM), to encrypt data in Kubernetes secrets and prevent unauthorized users fro
cis_ibm_7.1.1.2.remediation: Set up Kubernetes secrets encryption using Hyper Protect Crypto Services with KYOK. Implement IaC scripts to integrate HSM-based key management and enforce encryption at rest. Monitor key lifecycle and access logs for compliance.

cis_ibm_7.1.2.description: Ensure that all insecure (HTTP) client requests to applications and services hosted on IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service are redirected to secure TLS connections (HTTPS), and ensure that only TLS versions 1.2+ are supported. You set up your Ingress ALB to secure your domain with the IBM-provided TLS certificate or your custom TLS certificate. Some users might try to access your apps by using an insecure http request to your ALB domain, for example http://www.myingress.com, instead of using https. You
cis_ibm_7.1.2.remediation: Configure Ingress ALB to redirect HTTP to HTTPS and enforce TLS 1.2 or higher. Use IaC to define TLS policies and deploy custom certificates. Regularly scan for weak cipher suites and update configurations to maintain secure connections.

cis_ibm_7.1.3.description: Update the worker nodes in a cluster to the latest patch version so that security fixes are applied to those worker nodes.
cis_ibm_7.1.3.remediation: Automate worker node updates using IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service tools or IaC pipelines. Schedule regular patch cycles and enable auto-update features where available. Monitor cluster health and test updates in non-production environments first.

cis_ibm_7.1.4.description: Disable the public service endpoint so that communication to the master from both the worker nodes and cluster users is established over the private network through the private service endpoint.
cis_ibm_7.1.4.remediation: Disable the public endpoint for Kubernetes clusters and configure private endpoints only. Use IaC to enforce network isolation and apply RBAC to control access. Ensure VPN or direct connect is set up for secure administrative access.

cis_ibm_7.1.5.description: Image pull secrets are credentials that authorize your cluster to pull images from a private image registry. IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service integrates with IBM Cloud Container Registry and provides pull secrets for IBM Cloud Container Registry in the default Kubernetes namespace.
cis_ibm_7.1.5.remediation: Enable image pull secrets in Kubernetes clusters to authenticate with private registries. Use IaC to manage secret configurations and rotate credentials periodically. Implement RBAC to restrict who can modify pull secrets.

cis_ibm_7.1.6.description: Metrics help you monitor the health and performance of your clusters. Create a monitoring configuration to forward cluster and app metrics to IBM Cloud Monitoring with Sysdig. Use the IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service observability plug-in to create a monitoring configuration for IBM Cloud Monitoring with Sysdig in your cluster, and use this monitoring configuration to automatically collect and forward metrics to IBM Cloud Monitoring with Sysdig. With IBM Cloud Monitoring with Sysdig, you can collect
cis_ibm_7.1.6.remediation: Enable the monitoring service in IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service using the observability plug-in. Configure IaC to automate deployment of monitoring agents and set up alerts for anomalies. Regularly review metrics to detect security issues.

cis_ibm_7.1.7.description: Create a logging configuration to forward cluster and app logs to IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA. IBM Log Analysis with LogDNA offers administrators, DevOps teams, and developers advanced features to filter, search, and tail log data, define alerts, and design custom views to monitor application and system logs.
cis_ibm_7.1.7.remediation: Activate logging services in Kubernetes clusters to forward logs to IBM Log Analysis. Use IaC to set up log configurations and enable encryption for log data in transit. Establish alerting policies for critical log events.

cis_ibm_8.1.1.description: In Key Protect, you can set a rotation policy for a key or manually rotate the key. IBM Cloud Key Management Service (KMS): Key Protect (KP) allows customers to rotate the Data Encryption Key (DEK) which is key material stored within the KMS Hardware Security Module (HSM), which is tied to the key ID of the Customer Created customer master key (CMK). It is the DEK that is used to perform cryptographic operations such as encryption and decryption. When it's time to rotate the key based on the rot
cis_ibm_8.1.1.remediation: Enable automated key rotation policies in IBM Key Protect for customer-managed keys. Use IaC to define rotation schedules and audit key usage. Ensure backup and recovery procedures are in place to prevent data loss during rotation.

cis_ibm_8.1.2.description: IBM® Key Protect for IBM Cloud™ is a regional key management service with automatic features that help keep your applications secure and operational. Key Protect can be configured to provide high availability capabilities, such as IBM- owned infrastructure in multizone regions, to meet local access and low latency requirements for each supported region. Additionally, it can be configured to continuously back up keys in the region that the service operates in, and automatically recover and restar
cis_ibm_8.1.2.remediation: Configure IBM Key Protect for high availability by enabling multi-zone regions and automatic backups. Use IaC to deploy redundant instances and set up disaster recovery plans. Regularly test failover procedures to ensure service continuity.

cis_ibm_9.1.description: With IBM Cloud™ Security Advisor, you can instantly view the security posture of your IBM Cloud services through a single, centralized dashboard. The service receives security information from various sources and displays any security alerts or vulnerabilities that require your attention in the service dashboard. Out of the box, there are several pre-populated cards in your dashboard. These findings are from security services in IBM Cloud, but you can also add cards or custom partner solutions s
cis_ibm_9.1.remediation: Enable vulnerability alerts in IBM Container Registry and integrate with Security Advisor. Use IaC to automate image scanning and set up notifications for critical vulnerabilities. Establish a process to patch or replace vulnerable images promptly.

cis_k8s_1.1.1.description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.1.1.remediation: Set file permissions to 600 for the API server pod specification using configuration management tools. Implement IaC to enforce strict permissions and use RBAC to limit access. Regularly audit file permissions to prevent unauthorized modifications.

cis_k8s_1.1.10.description: Ensure that the Container Network Interface files have ownership set to root:root .
cis_k8s_1.1.10.remediation: Change ownership of CNI files to root:root using system commands or IaC scripts. Apply least-privilege principles and monitor for changes. Use automated checks to verify ownership settings periodically.

cis_k8s_1.1.11.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory has permissions of 700 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.1.11.remediation: Set permissions to 700 for the etcd data directory to restrict access. Use IaC to configure directory permissions during cluster setup. Regularly review and update permissions as part of security audits.

cis_k8s_1.1.12.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory ownership is set to etcd:etcd .
cis_k8s_1.1.12.remediation: Assign ownership of the etcd data directory to etcd:etcd using system tools. Implement IaC to automate ownership settings and enforce consistency. Monitor for unauthorized ownership changes.

cis_k8s_1.1.13.description: Ensure that the admin.conf file has permissions of 600 .
cis_k8s_1.1.13.remediation: Set permissions to 600 for the admin.conf file to protect Kubernetes configuration. Use IaC to manage file permissions and restrict access to administrators. Conduct regular audits to ensure compliance.

cis_k8s_1.1.14.description: Ensure that the admin.conf file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_k8s_1.1.14.remediation: Change ownership of admin.conf to root:root using configuration management. Apply IaC to enforce ownership policies and use RBAC to control file access. Verify ownership during security assessments.

cis_k8s_1.1.15.description: Ensure that the scheduler.conf file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.1.15.remediation: Set permissions to 600 or more restrictive for scheduler.conf. Use IaC to define and apply permission settings automatically. Regularly check file permissions to prevent security gaps.

cis_k8s_1.1.16.description: Ensure that the scheduler.conf file ownership is set to root:root .
cis_k8s_1.1.16.remediation: Assign ownership of scheduler.conf to root:root using system commands or IaC. Enforce ownership through automated compliance checks and limit access via RBAC. Monitor for deviations from set ownership.

cis_k8s_1.1.17.description: Ensure that the controller-manager.conf file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive configuration data.
cis_k8s_1.1.17.remediation: Use chmod 600 on the controller-manager.conf file to restrict permissions. In Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform or Ansible, enforce file permissions during deployment. Regularly audit file permissions with automated tools to maintain compliance.

cis_k8s_1.1.18.description: Ensure that the controller-manager.conf file ownership is set to root:root to prevent unauthorized modifications by non-root users.
cis_k8s_1.1.18.remediation: Use chown root:root on the controller-manager.conf file. Implement IaC scripts to set ownership during cluster setup. Use RBAC and security policies to restrict access to configuration files.

cis_k8s_1.1.19.description: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root to protect cryptographic keys and certificates from tampering.
cis_k8s_1.1.19.remediation: Set ownership to root:root for all PKI files and directories using chown. In IaC, define ownership in deployment manifests. Regularly monitor and enforce ownership with CSPM tools.

cis_k8s_1.1.2.description: Ensure that the API server pod specification file ownership is set to root:root to prevent unauthorized changes to critical Kubernetes components.
cis_k8s_1.1.2.remediation: Apply chown root:root to the API server pod specification file. Use IaC to enforce ownership during cluster provisioning. Implement audit logging to track ownership changes.

cis_k8s_1.1.20.description: Ensure that Kubernetes PKI certificate files have permissions of 600 or more restrictive to prevent unauthorized access and ensure encryption integrity.
cis_k8s_1.1.20.remediation: Set permissions to 600 on all PKI certificate files using chmod. In IaC, configure file permissions in security policies. Use encryption and key management services to protect certificates.

cis_k8s_1.1.21.description: Ensure that Kubernetes PKI key files have permissions of 600 to restrict access and protect private keys from exposure.
cis_k8s_1.1.21.remediation: Apply chmod 600 to all PKI key files. Use IaC to enforce strict permissions during deployment. Implement RBAC to limit access to key files and use encryption for storage.

cis_k8s_1.1.22.description: Verify kubelet's certificate before establishing connection to ensure secure and authenticated communication with the API server.
cis_k8s_1.1.22.remediation: Configure the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument in the API server settings. Use IaC to set this parameter in deployment manifests. Regularly validate certificates and use TLS encryption for all connections.

cis_k8s_1.1.23.description: Enable certificate-based kubelet authentication by setting --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key arguments to secure API server-kubelet communications.
cis_k8s_1.1.23.remediation: Configure the API server with appropriate certificate and key file paths. In IaC, specify these arguments in configuration files. Use encryption for key storage and rotate certificates periodically.

cis_k8s_1.1.24.description: Validate service account before validating token by setting --service-account-lookup to true to enhance security against token misuse.
cis_k8s_1.1.24.remediation: Set --service-account-lookup=true in the API server configuration. Use IaC to enforce this setting. Implement audit logging to monitor service account usage and review policies regularly.

cis_k8s_1.1.25.description: Reject creating pods that do not match Pod Security Policies by setting the admission control policy to PodSecurityPolicy to enforce security standards.
cis_k8s_1.1.25.remediation: Enable PodSecurityPolicy admission controller in the API server. Define and apply PSPs using IaC like Kubernetes manifests. Regularly audit and update policies to align with security best practices.

cis_k8s_1.1.26.description: Explicitly set a service account public key file for service accounts on the apiserver using the --service-account-key-file argument to ensure proper authentication.
cis_k8s_1.1.26.remediation: Configure the --service-account-key-file argument with the correct key file path. Use IaC to manage key files and permissions. Implement encryption for key storage and rotate keys as per policy.

cis_k8s_1.1.27.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections by setting --etcd-certfile and --etcd-keyfile arguments to protect data in transit.
cis_k8s_1.1.27.remediation: Configure etcd with TLS certificates and keys using the appropriate arguments. In IaC, specify these in etcd deployment configurations. Use encryption for certificate storage and enable audit logging.

cis_k8s_1.1.28.description: Automate service accounts management by setting the admission control policy to ServiceAccount to streamline and secure service account lifecycle.
cis_k8s_1.1.28.remediation: Enable ServiceAccount admission controller in the API server. Use IaC to define service account policies and roles. Regularly review and audit service account usage to prevent privilege escalation.

cis_k8s_1.1.29.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server by setting --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments to encrypt communications and prevent eavesdropping.
cis_k8s_1.1.29.remediation: Configure the API server with valid TLS certificate and key files. Use IaC to deploy these files with secure permissions. Implement certificate rotation and use encryption for key management.

cis_k8s_1.1.3.description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive to prevent unauthorized access to configuration details.
cis_k8s_1.1.3.remediation: Apply chmod 600 to the controller manager pod specification file. Use IaC to set permissions during cluster setup. Regularly audit file permissions with automated security scans.

cis_k8s_1.1.30.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server by setting the --client-ca-file argument to validate client certificates and secure authentication.
cis_k8s_1.1.30.remediation: Configure the --client-ca-file argument with the appropriate CA certificate file. Use IaC to manage certificate files and permissions. Enable encryption for data in transit and rotate CA certificates periodically.

cis_k8s_1.1.31.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections by setting the --etcd-cafile argument to validate server certificates.
cis_k8s_1.1.31.remediation: Set the --etcd-cafile argument in the API server to point to the CA certificate file. Use IaC to configure this in deployment manifests. Implement TLS encryption and regularly update certificates.

cis_k8s_1.1.4.description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file ownership is set to root:root to prevent unauthorized modifications and maintain integrity.
cis_k8s_1.1.4.remediation: Use chown root:root on the controller manager pod specification file. Enforce ownership in IaC scripts during provisioning. Apply RBAC policies to restrict access to configuration files.

cis_k8s_1.1.5.description: Ensure that the scheduler pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive to protect sensitive scheduling configurations.
cis_k8s_1.1.5.remediation: Set permissions to 600 on the scheduler pod specification file using chmod. In IaC, define file permissions in security policies. Use automated tools to monitor and enforce compliance.

cis_k8s_1.1.6.description: Ensure that the scheduler pod specification file ownership is set to root:root to prevent tampering and ensure secure operations.
cis_k8s_1.1.6.remediation: Apply chown root:root to the scheduler pod specification file. Use IaC to set ownership during deployment. Implement audit logging to track ownership changes and review regularly.

cis_k8s_1.1.7.description: Ensure that the /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive to protect etcd configuration from unauthorized access.
cis_k8s_1.1.7.remediation: Use chmod 600 on the etcd pod specification file. Configure permissions in IaC during cluster setup. Regularly scan file permissions with CSPM tools to ensure ongoing compliance.

cis_k8s_1.1.8.description: Ensure that the /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml file ownership is set to root:root to prevent unauthorized changes to etcd settings.
cis_k8s_1.1.8.remediation: Set ownership to root:root using chown on the etcd pod specification file. Enforce this in IaC deployment scripts. Use RBAC to limit access and monitor for ownership deviations.

cis_k8s_1.1.9.description: Ensure that the Container Network Interface files have permissions of 600 or more restrictive to secure network configuration and prevent breaches.
cis_k8s_1.1.9.remediation: Apply chmod 600 to all CNI configuration files. Use IaC to set permissions during network plugin installation. Implement security policies and audit logs to monitor file access.

cis_k8s_1.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the API server by setting --anonymous-auth to false to prevent unauthenticated access and potential attacks.
cis_k8s_1.2.1.remediation: Configure the API server with --anonymous-auth=false. Use IaC to enforce this setting in deployment manifests. Implement RBAC and audit logging to track authentication attempts.

cis_k8s_1.2.10.description: Do not allow all requests by ensuring the admission control plugin AlwaysAdmit is not set, to enforce security policies and prevent unauthorized actions.
cis_k8s_1.2.10.remediation: Remove or disable the AlwaysAdmit plugin from the API server admission control list. Use IaC to configure admission controllers securely. Regularly review and update admission policies to block insecure defaults.

cis_k8s_1.2.11.description: Ensure that the admission control plugin AlwaysPullImages is set to force pods to always pull images from the registry, preventing the use of stale or locally cached images that may have vulnerabilities.
cis_k8s_1.2.11.remediation: Configure the Kubernetes API server with the --enable-admission-plugins flag to include AlwaysPullImages. In Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests, set this in the kube-apiserver configuration. Regularly audit API server settings to ensure compliance.

cis_k8s_1.2.12.description: The SecurityContextDeny admission controller can be used to deny pods which make use of some SecurityContext fields which could allow for privilege escalation in the cluster. This should be used where PodSecurityPolicy is not in place within the cluster.
cis_k8s_1.2.12.remediation: Enable the SecurityContextDeny plugin in the API server configuration if PodSecurityPolicy is not deployed. Update kube-apiserver arguments to include --enable-admission-plugins=SecurityContextDeny. Use IaC to enforce this setting across all clusters.

cis_k8s_1.2.13.description: Ensure that the admission control plugin ServiceAccount is set to automate service accounts management and enforce service account policies in the cluster.
cis_k8s_1.2.13.remediation: Configure the API server with --enable-admission-plugins to include ServiceAccount. Implement this in cloud IaC templates for Kubernetes clusters. Regularly check admission plugin configurations to ensure ServiceAccount is enabled.

cis_k8s_1.2.14.description: Reject creating objects in a namespace that is undergoing termination to prevent resource conflicts and ensure clean namespace lifecycle management.
cis_k8s_1.2.14.remediation: Enable the NamespaceLifecycle admission plugin in the kube-apiserver by setting --enable-admission-plugins=NamespaceLifecycle. Use Kubernetes configuration management tools or IaC to apply this setting. Monitor API server logs for compliance.

cis_k8s_1.2.15.description: Limit the Node and Pod objects that a kubelet could modify to reduce the attack surface and prevent unauthorized changes in the cluster.
cis_k8s_1.2.15.remediation: Enable the NodeRestriction admission plugin in the API server configuration with --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction. Apply this via IaC for all cluster deployments. Regularly audit kubelet permissions and API server settings.

cis_k8s_1.2.16.description: Do not disable the secure port to ensure that the Kubernetes API server only accepts encrypted HTTPS connections, preventing unencrypted traffic exposure.
cis_k8s_1.2.16.remediation: Ensure the --secure-port argument is set to a non-zero value (e.g., 6443) in the kube-apiserver configuration. Use IaC to enforce this setting and implement network policies to block insecure ports. Regularly scan for open ports.

cis_k8s_1.2.17.description: Disable profiling on the API server if not needed to reduce performance overhead and minimize exposure of sensitive debugging information.
cis_k8s_1.2.17.remediation: Set the --profiling argument to false in the kube-apiserver configuration. Update cloud IaC templates to include this setting. Monitor API server performance and disable profiling in production environments.

cis_k8s_1.2.18.description: Enable auditing on the Kubernetes API Server and set the desired audit log path to track and log all API requests for security monitoring and compliance.
cis_k8s_1.2.18.remediation: Configure the API server with --audit-log-path to specify a log file location. Use IaC to set up audit logging and ensure logs are stored securely. Implement log rotation and retention policies.

cis_k8s_1.2.19.description: Retain the audit logs for at least 30 days or as appropriate to meet compliance requirements and facilitate forensic analysis.
cis_k8s_1.2.19.remediation: Set the --audit-log-maxage argument to 30 or a suitable value in the API server configuration. Use cloud storage solutions with lifecycle policies for log retention. Automate log management through IaC and monitoring tools.

cis_k8s_1.2.2.description: Do not use token based authentication via the --token-auth-file parameter to avoid insecure static token authentication methods.
cis_k8s_1.2.2.remediation: Ensure the --token-auth-file parameter is not set in the kube-apiserver configuration. Instead, use more secure authentication methods like client certificates or OIDC. Update IaC to remove this parameter and audit configurations regularly.

cis_k8s_1.2.20.description: Retain 10 or an appropriate number of old audit log files to ensure log availability and prevent data loss during rotation.
cis_k8s_1.2.20.remediation: Configure the --audit-log-maxbackup argument to 10 or a suitable number in the API server settings. Implement log rotation policies using IaC and cloud-native logging services. Regularly test log backup and recovery processes.

cis_k8s_1.2.21.description: Rotate audit log files on reaching 100 MB or as appropriate to manage log file sizes and maintain performance.
cis_k8s_1.2.21.remediation: Set the --audit-log-maxsize argument to 100 or an appropriate value in the kube-apiserver configuration. Use IaC to enforce log rotation settings. Monitor log storage and adjust sizes based on cluster activity.

cis_k8s_1.2.22.description: Set global request timeout for API server requests as appropriate to prevent resource exhaustion from long-running requests.
cis_k8s_1.2.22.remediation: Configure the --request-timeout argument to a suitable value (e.g., 30s) in the API server. Update cloud IaC templates to include this setting. Regularly review timeout values based on application requirements and performance.

cis_k8s_1.2.23.description: Validate service account before validating token to ensure that only active service accounts can authenticate, enhancing security.
cis_k8s_1.2.23.remediation: Set the --service-account-lookup argument to true in the kube-apiserver configuration. Implement this via IaC for all clusters. Regularly audit service accounts and their tokens to remove unused ones.

cis_k8s_1.2.24.description: Explicitly set a service account public key file for service accounts on the apiserver to ensure secure token validation and prevent unauthorized access.
cis_k8s_1.2.24.remediation: Configure the --service-account-key-file argument with the path to the public key file. Use IaC to manage key files and rotate them periodically. Ensure keys are stored securely and access is restricted.

cis_k8s_1.2.25.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections to protect sensitive data in transit between the API server and etcd.
cis_k8s_1.2.25.remediation: Set the --etcd-certfile and --etcd-keyfile arguments with appropriate TLS certificate and key paths. Use IaC to deploy TLS certificates and enforce encryption. Regularly rotate certificates and audit etcd configurations.

cis_k8s_1.2.26.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server using --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file to encrypt traffic and authenticate the server.
cis_k8s_1.2.26.remediation: Configure the API server with --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file pointing to valid TLS certificates. Use IaC to automate certificate deployment and renewal. Implement network policies to enforce TLS-only connections.

cis_k8s_1.2.27.description: Setup TLS connection on the API server by setting --client-ca-file to authenticate clients using certificates, enhancing access control.
cis_k8s_1.2.27.remediation: Set the --client-ca-file argument with the path to the CA certificate file. Use IaC to manage client certificates and ensure they are issued by a trusted CA. Regularly update and revoke client certificates as needed.

cis_k8s_1.2.28.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections, with --etcd-cafile set to verify etcd server certificates.
cis_k8s_1.2.28.remediation: Configure the --etcd-cafile argument with the CA certificate path for etcd TLS verification. Use IaC to deploy and manage CA certificates. Regularly audit etcd TLS settings and ensure certificates are valid.

cis_k8s_1.2.29.description: Encrypt etcd key-value store by setting --encryption-provider-config to protect sensitive data at rest, such as secrets and configurations.
cis_k8s_1.2.29.remediation: Create an encryption provider configuration file and set --encryption-provider-config to its path in the API server. Use IaC to manage encryption keys and configurations. Regularly rotate encryption keys and test decryption processes.

cis_k8s_1.2.3.description: This admission controller rejects all net-new usage of the Service field externalIPs to prevent unauthorized external access to services.
cis_k8s_1.2.3.remediation: Ensure the DenyServiceExternalIPs admission controller is not disabled; it should be enabled by default. Verify in the API server configuration that --enable-admission-plugins includes DenyServiceExternalIPs. Use IaC to enforce this setting and audit service definitions.

cis_k8s_1.2.30.description: Where etcd encryption is used, appropriate providers should be configured to ensure data is encrypted with strong algorithms and key management practices.
cis_k8s_1.2.30.remediation: Configure the encryption provider in the --encryption-provider-config file with strong providers like aesbc or secretbox. Use IaC to deploy and update encryption configurations. Regularly review and test encryption settings to ensure compliance.

cis_k8s_1.2.31.description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers for TLS connections to prevent weak cipher attacks.
cis_k8s_1.2.31.remediation: Set the --tls-cipher-suites argument to include only strong ciphers (e.g., TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256). Use IaC to enforce cipher suite configurations. Regularly scan and update cipher suites based on security best practices.

cis_k8s_1.2.32.description: Encrypt etcd key-value store by setting --encryption-provider-config to protect sensitive data at rest, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.
cis_k8s_1.2.32.remediation: Configure the API server with --encryption-provider-config pointing to a valid encryption configuration file. Use IaC to manage this file and encryption keys. Implement key rotation policies and audit encryption status regularly.

cis_k8s_1.2.33.description: Where etcd encryption is used, appropriate providers should be configured to ensure robust encryption of all data stored in etcd.
cis_k8s_1.2.33.remediation: Verify that the encryption provider configuration includes strong and supported providers. Use IaC to deploy and update the configuration. Conduct regular audits to ensure encryption is active and functioning correctly.

cis_k8s_1.2.34.description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
cis_k8s_1.2.34.remediation: Configure the API server to use only strong TLS cipher suites by setting the --tls-cipher-suites flag to approved ciphers like TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests to enforce this configuration across all clusters.

cis_k8s_1.2.4.remediation: Set the --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key arguments to valid certificate and key file paths on the API server. Use RBAC policies to manage access and ensure certificates are rotated regularly via automated processes.

cis_k8s_1.2.5.description: Verify kubelet's certificate before establishing connection.
cis_k8s_1.2.5.remediation: Configure the API server with the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument pointing to a CA file to validate kubelet certificates. Implement this in IaC templates and monitor for misconfigurations using CSPM tools.

cis_k8s_1.2.6.description: Do not always authorize all requests.
cis_k8s_1.2.6.remediation: Ensure the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow; use modes like Node, RBAC, or Webhook. Apply this via Kubernetes configuration files and audit logs to detect unauthorized access attempts.

cis_k8s_1.2.7.description: Restrict kubelet nodes to reading only objects associated with them.
cis_k8s_1.2.7.remediation: Include Node in the --authorization-mode argument to enforce node-level authorization. Use RBAC roles to define precise permissions and automate checks with CSPM scans to ensure compliance.

cis_k8s_1.2.8.description: Turn on Role Based Access Control.
cis_k8s_1.2.8.remediation: Set --authorization-mode to include RBAC for fine-grained access control. Define roles and role bindings in YAML files, and use policy-as-code tools to enforce least privilege principles across the cluster.

cis_k8s_1.2.9.description: Limit the rate at which the API server accepts requests.
cis_k8s_1.2.9.remediation: Enable the EventRateLimit admission control plugin by configuring it in the API server settings. Use IaC to deploy this configuration and monitor API server metrics to adjust limits as needed for performance and security.

cis_k8s_1.3.1.description: Activate garbage collector on pod termination, as appropriate.
cis_k8s_1.3.1.remediation: Set the --terminated-pod-gc-threshold argument to a suitable value to clean up terminated pods. Implement this in controller manager configurations and review regularly to optimize resource usage and prevent clutter.

cis_k8s_1.3.2.remediation: Set the --profiling argument to false in the controller manager to reduce attack surface. Use configuration management tools to enforce this setting and enable profiling only temporarily for debugging purposes.

cis_k8s_1.3.3.description: Use individual service account credentials for each controller.
cis_k8s_1.3.3.remediation: Configure the --use-service-account-credentials argument to true for the controller manager. Assign specific service accounts with minimal permissions using RBAC, and automate credential rotation with Kubernetes secrets management.

cis_k8s_1.3.4.description: Explicitly set a service account private key file for service accounts on the controller manager.
cis_k8s_1.3.4.remediation: Set the --service-account-private-key-file argument to a secure key file path. Use encrypted storage for keys, enforce file permissions, and integrate with CSPM for continuous validation of this configuration.

cis_k8s_1.3.5.description: Allow pods to verify the API server's serving certificate before establishing connections.
cis_k8s_1.3.5.remediation: Configure the --root-ca-file argument on the controller manager to provide a CA bundle for pod verification. Ensure certificates are issued by a trusted CA and use IaC to deploy this setting consistently across environments.

cis_k8s_1.3.6.description: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation on controller-manager.
cis_k8s_1.3.6.remediation: Set the RotateKubeletServerCertificate feature gate to true in the controller manager. Automate certificate lifecycle management with tools like cert-manager and monitor rotation status via CSPM alerts.

cis_k8s_1.3.7.description: Do not bind the Controller Manager service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
cis_k8s_1.3.7.remediation: Set the --bind-address argument to 127.0.0.1 to restrict access to localhost. Use network policies and firewall rules to block external access, and validate configurations with automated security scans.

cis_k8s_1.4.1.remediation: Set the --profiling argument to false in the scheduler to minimize exposure. Enforce this through IaC templates and enable profiling only during controlled troubleshooting sessions.

cis_k8s_1.4.10.description: Ensure that the flanneld file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_1.4.10.remediation: Manually check and set flanneld file ownership to root:root using chown commands. Establish a periodic review process and document ownership settings in security policies to prevent unauthorized modifications.

cis_k8s_1.4.11.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory has permissions of 700 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.4.11.remediation: Set etcd data directory permissions to 700 using chmod. Implement this in deployment scripts and use CSPM tools to continuously monitor and alert on any permission deviations.

cis_k8s_1.4.12.description: Ensure that the etcd data directory ownership is set to etcd:etcd.
cis_k8s_1.4.12.remediation: Configure etcd data directory ownership to etcd:etcd with chown. Automate this setup in IaC for etcd deployments and validate ownership regularly through automated compliance checks.

cis_k8s_1.4.2.description: Do not bind the scheduler service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
cis_k8s_1.4.2.remediation: Set the --bind-address argument to 127.0.0.1 for the scheduler. Use network segmentation and security groups to restrict access, and enforce this configuration via policy-as-code frameworks.

cis_k8s_1.4.3.description: Ensure that the config file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
