cis_k8s_1.4.3.remediation: Set config file permissions to 644 using chmod. Apply this across cloud providers via IaC templates and use CSPM to scan for overly permissive file settings in virtual machines or nodes.

cis_k8s_1.4.4.description: Ensure that the config file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_1.4.4.remediation: Set config file ownership to root:root with chown. Integrate this into node provisioning scripts and monitor ownership changes with file integrity monitoring tools.

cis_k8s_1.4.5.description: Ensure that the scheduler file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.4.5.remediation: Configure scheduler file permissions to 644 using chmod. Use configuration management tools to enforce this setting and regularly audit file permissions with automated security assessments.

cis_k8s_1.4.6.description: Ensure that the scheduler file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_1.4.6.remediation: Set scheduler file ownership to root:root with chown. Implement this in control plane deployment processes and validate through CSPM scans to ensure no unauthorized ownership changes.

cis_k8s_1.4.7.description: Ensure that the etcd.conf file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_1.4.7.remediation: Set etcd.conf file permissions to 644 using chmod. Automate this in etcd installation scripts and use CSPM tools to detect and remediate overly permissive permissions in real-time.

cis_k8s_1.4.8.description: Ensure that the etcd.conf file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_1.4.8.remediation: Manually verify and set etcd.conf file ownership to root:root using chown. Document this requirement in operational procedures and conduct regular audits to maintain compliance with security policies.

cis_k8s_1.4.9.description: Ensure that the flanneld file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive to prevent unauthorized modifications.
cis_k8s_1.4.9.remediation: Manually review and set file permissions on the flanneld configuration file to 644 using commands like chmod. Establish a procedure for regular file permission audits in Kubernetes node configurations.

cis_k8s_1.5.1.description: Configure TLS encryption for the etcd service to secure data in transit.
cis_k8s_1.5.1.remediation: Set the --cert-file and --key-file arguments in etcd configuration to use valid TLS certificates. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests to enforce TLS settings, and ensure certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA).

cis_k8s_1.5.2.description: Enable client authentication on etcd service to restrict access to authorized clients.
cis_k8s_1.5.2.remediation: Configure the --client-cert-auth argument to true in etcd settings. Use RBAC and TLS client certificates to enforce authentication, and automate this via configuration management tools or Kubernetes operators.

cis_k8s_1.5.3.description: Do not use self-signed certificates for TLS to ensure proper certificate validation and trust.
cis_k8s_1.5.3.remediation: Ensure the --auto-tls argument is not set to true in etcd configuration. Use certificates from a trusted CA, and implement policies in IaC to block self-signed certificates, with regular audits for compliance.

cis_k8s_1.5.4.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for peer connections to secure inter-node communication.
cis_k8s_1.5.4.remediation: Set the --peer-cert-file and --peer-key-file arguments in etcd configuration for TLS on peer connections. Enforce this through Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts, and use encryption policies to mandate TLS for all etcd traffic.

cis_k8s_1.5.5.description: etcd should be configured for peer authentication to verify the identity of connecting peers.
cis_k8s_1.5.5.remediation: Configure the --peer-client-cert-auth argument to true in etcd settings. Implement mutual TLS (mTLS) for peer authentication using RBAC and certificate management, and automate checks with CSPM tools.

cis_k8s_1.5.6.description: Do not use automatically generated self-signed certificates for TLS connections between peers to maintain security standards.
cis_k8s_1.5.6.remediation: Ensure the --peer-auto-tls argument is not set to true in etcd configuration. Use trusted CA-issued certificates for peer TLS, and enforce this via security policies and automated configuration validation in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_1.5.7.description: Store etcd logs separately from etcd data to improve security and manageability.
cis_k8s_1.5.7.remediation: Manually set the --wal-dir argument to a dedicated directory for write-ahead logs. Establish procedures for log management and storage segregation in etcd deployment documentation, and conduct regular reviews.

cis_k8s_1.5.8.description: Do not auto rotate logs to ensure audit trails are preserved for compliance and troubleshooting.
cis_k8s_1.5.8.remediation: Manually configure the --max-wals argument to 0 in etcd settings. Implement log retention policies and manual log rotation processes, and document procedures for maintaining etcd log integrity.

cis_k8s_1.5.9.description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes to limit blast radius in case of CA compromise.
cis_k8s_1.5.9.remediation: Deploy a unique CA for etcd certificates separate from the Kubernetes CA. Use IaC to manage CA segregation, and enforce this through security policies with regular audits of certificate issuance.

cis_k8s_1.6.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed to minimize privilege escalation risks.
cis_k8s_1.6.1.remediation: Restrict cluster-admin role assignments to essential personnel only. Use RBAC policies with least privilege principles, automate role reviews with CSPM tools, and implement approval workflows for role changes.

cis_k8s_1.6.2.description: Create and enforce Pod Security Policies for your cluster to control pod security settings and reduce attack surface.
cis_k8s_1.6.2.remediation: Define and apply Pod Security Policies (PSPs) or use Pod Security Standards in Kubernetes. Automate policy enforcement via admission controllers, and use IaC to manage PSP configurations across environments.

cis_k8s_1.6.3.description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects for better access control and resource management.
cis_k8s_1.6.3.remediation: Organize resources into logical namespaces based on teams or applications. Implement RBAC to restrict cross-namespace access, and use IaC templates to standardize namespace creation and policies.

cis_k8s_1.6.4.description: Use network policies to isolate your cluster network and control traffic flow between pods.
cis_k8s_1.6.4.remediation: Define and apply Network Policies with default-deny ingress and egress rules. Use Kubernetes manifests or CNI-specific tools to automate policy deployment, and regularly audit network configurations for compliance.

cis_k8s_1.6.5.description: Avoid using Kubernetes Secrets to reduce risk of secret exposure; use external secret management systems instead.
cis_k8s_1.6.5.remediation: Integrate with external secret managers like AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault. Use sidecar injectors or CSI drivers for secret retrieval, and enforce policies to block plain-text secrets in Kubernetes manifests.

cis_k8s_1.6.6.description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions to restrict syscall access and enhance container security.
cis_k8s_1.6.6.remediation: Configure seccomp profiles in pod security contexts or use runtime defaults. Automate this via Kubernetes admission controllers or IaC policies, and validate profiles during CI/CD pipelines.

cis_k8s_1.6.7.description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers to set security parameters like user IDs and capabilities.
cis_k8s_1.6.7.remediation: Define securityContext fields in pod and container specifications to enforce non-root users and drop capabilities. Use policy-as-code tools like OPA/Gatekeeper to automate compliance, and review configurations regularly.

cis_k8s_1.6.8.description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment to ensure only trusted container images are used.
cis_k8s_1.6.8.remediation: Enable the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller and integrate with image registries for signature verification. Use tools like Notary or Cosign for image signing, and enforce policies in CI/CD to block unsigned images.

cis_k8s_2.1.description: Configure TLS encryption for the etcd service to protect sensitive data in transit.
cis_k8s_2.1.remediation: Set the --cert-file and --key-file arguments in etcd configuration with valid TLS certificates. Use IaC to enforce encryption settings, and ensure certificates are rotated regularly and issued by a trusted CA.

cis_k8s_2.1.1.description: Do not allow privileged containers to prevent excessive host access and reduce security risks.
cis_k8s_2.1.1.remediation: Set the --allow-privileged argument to false in Kubernetes API server configuration. Enforce this via Pod Security Policies or security contexts, and use CSPM tools to detect and remediate privileged container deployments.

cis_k8s_2.1.10.description: Do not override node hostnames to maintain consistent node identification and security logging.
cis_k8s_2.1.10.remediation: Ensure the --hostname-override argument is not set in kubelet configuration. Use IaC to standardize node naming, and implement policies to block hostname overrides, with regular audits of kubelet settings.

cis_k8s_2.1.11.description: Do not limit event creation to ensure comprehensive audit logging and monitoring.
cis_k8s_2.1.11.remediation: Set the --event-qps argument to 0 in Kubernetes API server configuration. Configure logging and monitoring tools to capture all events, and use IaC to enforce unlimited event rates for compliance with audit requirements.

cis_k8s_2.1.12.description: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets to secure communication with the API server.
cis_k8s_2.1.12.remediation: Configure the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments in kubelet settings with valid certificates. Use certificate management solutions for automatic rotation, and enforce TLS via security policies in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_2.1.13.description: Disable cAdvisor to reduce attack surface and resource usage on nodes.
cis_k8s_2.1.13.remediation: Set the --cadvisor-port argument to 0 in kubelet configuration. Use IaC to disable cAdvisor by default, and implement monitoring alternatives like Prometheus node exporters for metrics collection.

cis_k8s_2.1.2.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server to prevent unauthorized access.
cis_k8s_2.1.2.remediation: Set the --anonymous-auth argument to false in kubelet configuration. Enforce authentication via TLS client certificates or webhook tokens, and use CSPM tools to automate checks and remediate misconfigurations.

cis_k8s_2.1.3.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
cis_k8s_2.1.3.remediation: Configure the Kubernetes API server with --authorization-mode set to RBAC, Node, or Webhook instead of AlwaysAllow. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests to enforce this setting, and regularly audit RBAC policies to ensure least privilege access.

cis_k8s_2.1.4.remediation: Set the --client-ca-file argument to a valid Certificate Authority file for Kubelet authentication. Use IaC to deploy Kubernetes clusters with proper TLS certificates, and ensure certificate management processes include rotation and validation to maintain secure authentication.

cis_k8s_2.1.5.remediation: Configure Kubelet with --read-only-port set to 0 to disable the insecure read-only port. Implement this via IaC in Kubernetes node configurations, and use network policies to restrict access to Kubelet ports, ensuring only authorized services can communicate with Kubelet.

cis_k8s_2.1.6.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
cis_k8s_2.1.6.remediation: Set --streaming-connection-idle-timeout to a non-zero value, such as 30 minutes, to prevent resource exhaustion. Use IaC to define this in Kubernetes API server configurations, and monitor connection timeouts to balance security and performance in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_2.1.7.description: Protect tuned kernel parameters from overriding kubelet default kernel parameter values.
cis_k8s_2.1.7.remediation: Enable --protect-kernel-defaults by setting it to true in Kubelet configurations. Deploy this via IaC tools, and enforce kernel parameter policies through security benchmarks and regular compliance checks to prevent unauthorized modifications.

cis_k8s_2.1.8.remediation: Set --make-iptables-util-chains to true in Kubelet settings to ensure proper network policy enforcement. Use IaC to configure this in Kubernetes clusters, and verify iptables rules are correctly applied through automated security scans and network audits.

cis_k8s_2.1.9.description: Unmount volumes from the nodes on pod termination.
cis_k8s_2.1.9.remediation: Configure Kubelet with --keep-terminated-pod-volumes set to false to automatically clean up volumes. Implement this via IaC in Kubernetes manifests, and use storage policies to enforce encryption and access controls for persistent volumes in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_2.2.description: Enable client authentication on etcd service.
cis_k8s_2.2.remediation: Set --client-cert-auth to true for etcd to require TLS client certificates. Use IaC to deploy etcd with proper certificate authorities, and enforce RBAC and encryption policies to secure etcd communications and prevent unauthorized access.

cis_k8s_2.2.1.description: Ensure that the config file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_2.2.1.remediation: Set file permissions to 644 or more restrictive for Kubernetes configuration files using OS-level commands or IaC scripts. Regularly audit file permissions with automated tools and enforce least privilege access through security policies and monitoring.

cis_k8s_2.2.2.description: Ensure that the config file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_2.2.2.remediation: Change file ownership to root:root for Kubernetes configuration files using chown commands. Implement this via IaC or configuration management tools, and conduct periodic audits to ensure ownership settings are maintained and not altered by unauthorized users.

cis_k8s_2.2.3.description: Ensure that the kubelet file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_2.2.3.remediation: Set kubelet configuration file permissions to 644 or more restrictive using OS-level controls. Use IaC to enforce these settings during cluster deployment, and monitor for deviations with file integrity monitoring tools in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_2.2.4.description: Ensure that the kubelet file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_2.2.4.remediation: Assign ownership of kubelet files to root:root using chown commands. Deploy this configuration via IaC or automated scripts, and verify ownership regularly through security audits to prevent unauthorized modifications and maintain system integrity.

cis_k8s_2.2.5.description: Ensure that the proxy file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_2.2.5.remediation: Set proxy file permissions to 644 or more restrictive using file system commands. Implement this through IaC in Kubernetes node setups, and use access control policies to restrict write access, ensuring only authorized processes can modify proxy configurations.

cis_k8s_2.2.6.description: Ensure that the proxy file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_2.2.6.remediation: Since automation_type is manual, follow a procedural approach: establish a documented process to regularly check and set proxy file ownership to root:root using manual audits or scheduled scripts. Train operations teams on these procedures and maintain logs of ownership changes for compliance tracking.

cis_k8s_2.3.description: Do not use self-signed certificates for TLS.
cis_k8s_2.3.remediation: Disable --auto-tls by setting it to false and use certificates from a trusted Certificate Authority. Deploy TLS certificates via IaC with tools like cert-manager, and enforce encryption policies to ensure all etcd communications are securely encrypted with valid certificates.

cis_k8s_2.4.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for peer connections.
cis_k8s_2.4.remediation: Set --peer-cert-file and --peer-key-file arguments to appropriate TLS certificate and key files for etcd peer connections. Use IaC to configure etcd clusters with encrypted peer communications, and regularly rotate certificates and keys to maintain security.

cis_k8s_2.5.description: etcd should be configured for peer authentication.
cis_k8s_2.5.remediation: Enable --peer-client-cert-auth by setting it to true to require client certificates for etcd peer authentication. Implement this via IaC in etcd configurations, and use RBAC and network policies to restrict peer access, ensuring only authenticated nodes can participate in the cluster.

cis_k8s_2.6.description: Do not use automatically generated self-signed certificates for TLS connections between peers.
cis_k8s_2.6.remediation: Set --peer-auto-tls to false and provide manually configured TLS certificates from a trusted CA for etcd peer connections. Use IaC to deploy etcd with proper certificate management, and audit TLS settings regularly to prevent the use of insecure self-signed certificates.

cis_k8s_2.7.description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
cis_k8s_2.7.remediation: Configure a unique Certificate Authority for etcd separate from the Kubernetes CA. Use IaC to manage distinct CAs, and implement certificate lifecycle management processes to issue, rotate, and revoke certificates independently for etcd and Kubernetes components.

cis_k8s_2.8.description: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
cis_k8s_2.8.remediation: Ensure etcd uses a separate CA by configuring --trusted-ca-file and related arguments appropriately. Deploy this via IaC with tools like HashiCorp Vault for CA management, and enforce separation of duties through policy controls to prevent cross-component certificate misuse.

cis_k8s_3.1.1.description: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
cis_k8s_3.1.1.remediation: Avoid using client certificates for user authentication; instead, use identity providers like OIDC or integrate with cloud IAM services. Implement RBAC policies to manage user access, and use IaC to enforce authentication methods that support revocation, such as token-based systems, in cloud environments.

cis_k8s_3.1.10.description: Enable auditing on kubernetes federation apiserver and set the desired audit log path as appropriate.
cis_k8s_3.1.10.remediation: Configure --audit-log-path to a secure directory for storing audit logs. Use IaC to set up auditing in Kubernetes API server configurations, and ensure log files are encrypted at rest and access is restricted through file permissions and IAM policies in cloud storage.

cis_k8s_3.1.11.description: Retain the logs for at least 30 days or as appropriate.
cis_k8s_3.1.11.remediation: Set --audit-log-maxage to 30 or higher to retain audit logs for compliance periods. Implement this via IaC in Kubernetes setups, and use cloud log management services with retention policies to automatically archive or delete logs based on regulatory requirements.

cis_k8s_3.1.12.description: Retain 10 or an appropriate number of old log files.
cis_k8s_3.1.12.remediation: Configure --audit-log-maxbackup to 10 or a suitable number to manage log file backups. Use IaC to define these settings, and implement log rotation policies with tools like logrotate or cloud-native services to prevent disk space issues and ensure log availability.

cis_k8s_3.1.13.description: Rotate log files on reaching 100 MB or as appropriate.
cis_k8s_3.1.13.remediation: Set --audit-log-maxsize to 100 or an appropriate value to control log file size. Deploy this configuration via IaC, and use automated log management solutions to handle rotation, compression, and storage, ensuring logs are accessible for security monitoring and compliance audits.

cis_k8s_3.1.14.description: Do not always authorize all requests.
cis_k8s_3.1.14.remediation: Configure the Kubernetes API server with a proper authorization mode such as RBAC, Node, or ABAC instead of AlwaysAllow. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests to enforce this setting, and regularly audit API server configurations to ensure compliance.

cis_k8s_3.1.15.description: Do not use token based authentication.
cis_k8s_3.1.15.remediation: Disable the --token-auth-file parameter and use more secure authentication methods like client certificates, OIDC, or service account tokens. Implement RBAC for access control and use IaC to manage API server configurations, ensuring no static token files are present.

cis_k8s_3.1.16.description: Validate service account before validating token.
cis_k8s_3.1.16.remediation: Set the --service-account-lookup argument to true in the API server configuration to ensure service accounts are validated. Use Kubernetes admission controllers and IaC to enforce this setting, and monitor for any misconfigurations in audit logs.

cis_k8s_3.1.17.description: Explicitly set a service account public key file for service accounts on the federation apiserver.
cis_k8s_3.1.17.remediation: Configure the --service-account-key-file argument with a valid public key file for service account token validation. Use IaC to manage this configuration, ensure the file is securely stored with proper permissions, and rotate keys periodically.

cis_k8s_3.1.18.description: etcd should be configured to make use of TLS encryption for client connections.
cis_k8s_3.1.18.remediation: Set the --etcd-certfile and --etcd-keyfile arguments to use TLS certificates for etcd client connections. Use IaC to deploy etcd with TLS enabled, ensure certificates are valid and encrypted, and restrict access to etcd endpoints.

cis_k8s_3.1.19.description: Setup TLS connection on the federation API server.
cis_k8s_3.1.19.remediation: Configure the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments to enable TLS on the federation API server. Use IaC to manage TLS certificates, enforce encryption in transit, and regularly update certificates to prevent expiration.

cis_k8s_3.1.2.remediation: Remove the --basic-auth-file argument from the API server configuration and use secure authentication methods like client certificates or OIDC. Implement RBAC for authorization and use IaC to enforce this change across all clusters.

cis_k8s_3.1.3.description: Do not allow any insecure tokens.
cis_k8s_3.1.3.remediation: Ensure the --insecure-allow-any-token argument is not set to prevent insecure token usage. Use secure token-based authentication with validation, implement RBAC, and audit API server flags using IaC and compliance tools.

cis_k8s_3.1.4.description: Do not bind to insecure addresses.
cis_k8s_3.1.4.remediation: Remove the --insecure-bind-address argument or set it to a secure local address. Configure the API server to use TLS and bind only to secure ports, using IaC to enforce network policies and restrict access to internal networks.

cis_k8s_3.1.5.description: Do not bind to insecure port.
cis_k8s_3.1.5.remediation: Set the --insecure-port argument to 0 to disable insecure HTTP access. Use TLS-enabled secure ports for all API server communications, implement network policies, and use IaC to ensure this configuration is applied consistently.

cis_k8s_3.1.6.description: Do not disable the secure port.
cis_k8s_3.1.6.remediation: Ensure the --secure-port argument is set to a non-zero value (e.g., 443) to enable TLS. Configure proper certificates and encryption, use IaC to manage port settings, and monitor for any attempts to disable secure access.

cis_k8s_3.1.7.remediation: Set the --profiling argument to false in the API server configuration to reduce attack surface. Use IaC to enforce this setting, and if profiling is required for debugging, ensure it is enabled temporarily and in secure environments only.

cis_k8s_3.1.8.remediation: Configure admission control policies to use plugins like PodSecurityPolicy or ResourceQuota instead of AlwaysAdmit. Use IaC to set appropriate admission controllers, implement RBAC, and regularly audit admission control settings.

cis_k8s_3.1.9.description: Reject creating objects in a namespace that is undergoing termination.
cis_k8s_3.1.9.remediation: Set the admission control policy to include NamespaceLifecycle to prevent object creation in terminating namespaces. Use IaC to configure admission plugins, enforce namespace management policies, and monitor for compliance in audit logs.

cis_k8s_3.2.1.description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit-policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
cis_k8s_3.2.1.remediation: Create and configure a minimal audit policy file and set the --audit-policy-file argument on the API server. Use IaC to deploy the audit policy, ensure logs are stored securely, and regularly review audit logs for security incidents.

cis_k8s_3.2.2.description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
cis_k8s_3.2.2.remediation: Define an audit policy that includes events for authentication, authorization, and resource changes. Use IaC to manage the policy file, integrate with SIEM tools for monitoring, and periodically update the policy to address new threats.

cis_k8s_4.1.1.description: Ensure that the kubelet service file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_4.1.1.remediation: Set file permissions to 600 or more restrictive on the kubelet service file using commands like chmod. Use IaC tools to enforce these permissions during deployment, and regularly audit file permissions across nodes.

cis_k8s_4.1.10.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file is owned by root:root.
cis_k8s_4.1.10.remediation: Set ownership of the kubelet config.yaml file to root:root using chown. Use IaC to configure file ownership during node provisioning, and implement monitoring to detect unauthorized changes to configuration files.

cis_k8s_4.1.2.description: Ensure that the kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_4.1.2.remediation: Change ownership of the kubelet service file to root:root using chown. Use IaC to enforce ownership settings, restrict access to sensitive files, and perform regular security scans on node file systems.

cis_k8s_4.1.3.description: If kube-proxy is running, and if it is using a file-based kubeconfig file, ensure that the proxy kubeconfig file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_4.1.3.remediation: Set permissions to 600 or more restrictive on the kube-proxy kubeconfig file using chmod. Use IaC to manage file permissions, encrypt sensitive configurations, and audit access to kubeconfig files regularly.

cis_k8s_4.1.4.description: If kube-proxy is running, ensure that the file ownership of its kubeconfig file is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_4.1.4.remediation: Set ownership of the kube-proxy kubeconfig file to root:root using chown. Use IaC to enforce ownership, implement least privilege access, and monitor for any unauthorized modifications to configuration files.

cis_k8s_4.1.5.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_4.1.5.remediation: Set permissions to 600 or more restrictive on the kubelet.conf file using chmod. Use IaC to configure file permissions during cluster setup, and regularly check file integrity with security tools.

cis_k8s_4.1.6.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_4.1.6.remediation: Set ownership of the kubelet.conf file to root:root using chown. Use IaC to manage ownership settings, restrict access to root users, and audit file ownership changes in system logs.

cis_k8s_4.1.7.description: Ensure that the certificate authorities file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_4.1.7.remediation: Set permissions to 600 or more restrictive on the certificate authorities file using chmod. Use IaC to enforce permissions, store certificates in secure locations, and rotate them periodically to maintain security.

cis_k8s_4.1.8.description: Ensure that the certificate authorities file ownership is set to root:root.
cis_k8s_4.1.8.remediation: Set ownership of the client certificate authorities file to root:root using chown. Use IaC to configure ownership, implement encryption for certificate storage, and monitor for unauthorized access attempts.

cis_k8s_4.1.9.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
cis_k8s_4.1.9.remediation: Set file permissions to 600 using chmod 600 /path/to/kubelet/config.yaml. In Infrastructure as Code (IaC) like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests, define strict file permissions during deployment. Regularly audit kubelet configuration files with automated tools to enforce least privilege access.

cis_k8s_4.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
cis_k8s_4.2.1.remediation: Configure the kubelet with --anonymous-auth=false in its startup arguments or configuration file. Use IaC to enforce this setting across all nodes. Implement RBAC to ensure only authenticated users and service accounts can access kubelet endpoints, and monitor logs for unauthorized attempts.

cis_k8s_4.2.10.description: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
cis_k8s_4.2.10.remediation: Set --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments to valid certificate and key paths in the kubelet configuration. Use automated certificate management tools like cert-manager for rotation. Ensure TLS is enforced in all kubelet communications to encrypt data in transit and prevent eavesdropping.

cis_k8s_4.2.11.remediation: Ensure --rotate-certificates is not set to false; it should be true or unspecified to allow automatic rotation. Configure certificate authorities and use tools like Kubernetes CA or external PKI for issuance. Regularly review kubelet logs to confirm certificate rotation is occurring without issues.

cis_k8s_4.2.12.remediation: Set RotateKubeletServerCertificate to true in the kubelet configuration or as a feature gate. Use automated certificate management to handle issuance and renewal. Monitor certificate expiration dates and ensure rotation processes are integrated into cluster maintenance procedures.

cis_k8s_4.2.13.description: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
cis_k8s_4.2.13.remediation: Configure the kubelet with --tls-cipher-suites argument specifying strong ciphers like TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256. Use IaC to enforce this across all nodes. Regularly update cipher suite configurations to align with security best practices and disable weak ciphers.

cis_k8s_4.2.14.description: Ensure that the Kubelet enforces the use of the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile
cis_k8s_4.2.14.remediation: Set --seccomp-default=true in the kubelet configuration to apply the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile to all pods unless overridden. Use Pod Security Standards or Admission Controllers to enforce seccomp profiles. Regularly audit pod configurations to ensure compliance with seccomp policies.

cis_k8s_4.2.15.description: Ensuring that --IPAddressDeny is set to "Any" will facilitate allowlisting of only IP addresses that are explicitly set with the --IPAddressAllow parameter which will block unspecified IP addresses from communicating with the kubelet component.
cis_k8s_4.2.15.remediation: Manually configure the kubelet with --IPAddressDeny=any and specify allowed IPs using --IPAddressAllow. Establish a procedure to review and update allowlists regularly based on network requirements. Document this configuration in security policies and train administrators on its importance.

cis_k8s_4.2.2.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
cis_k8s_4.2.2.remediation: Set --authorization-mode to a value other than AlwaysAllow, such as Webhook or Node,RBAC. Configure RBAC policies to define precise permissions. Use automated tools to scan and remediate misconfigurations, ensuring authorization is enforced for all kubelet requests.

cis_k8s_4.2.3.remediation: Set --client-ca-file to a valid CA certificate file path in the kubelet configuration. Use a trusted CA to issue client certificates and implement certificate rotation. Enforce this setting via IaC and regularly audit authentication logs to ensure only authorized clients access the kubelet.

cis_k8s_4.2.4.remediation: Set --read-only-port=0 in the kubelet configuration to disable the read-only port. Use secure ports and authentication for all kubelet communications. Implement network policies to block access to port 10255 and monitor for any unauthorized attempts to access disabled ports.

cis_k8s_4.2.5.description: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
cis_k8s_4.2.5.remediation: Ensure --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to a positive value, such as 5m, to prevent indefinite connections. Configure this in the kubelet startup arguments or config file. Regularly review timeout settings to balance security and functionality, and use automated checks to enforce compliance.

cis_k8s_4.2.6.description: Protect tuned kernel parameters from overriding kubelet default kernel parameter values.
cis_k8s_4.2.6.remediation: Set --protect-kernel-defaults=true in the kubelet configuration. Use IaC to apply this setting across all nodes. Monitor kernel parameter changes and ensure kubelet defaults are maintained to prevent security bypasses or performance issues.

cis_k8s_4.2.7.remediation: Set --make-iptables-util-chains=true in the kubelet configuration to enable proper network policy enforcement. Use IaC to ensure consistency across the cluster. Verify iptables rules are correctly applied and audit network configurations regularly to prevent misconfigurations.

cis_k8s_4.2.8.remediation: Avoid setting --hostname-override in the kubelet configuration to prevent hostname spoofing and ensure consistent node identification. Use IaC to enforce this and regularly audit kubelet arguments. Implement logging to detect any unauthorized hostname changes in the cluster.

cis_k8s_4.2.9.description: Security relevant information should be captured. The --event-qps flag on the Kubelet can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
cis_k8s_4.2.9.remediation: Set --event-qps to a balanced value, such as 5, to capture security events without causing denial of service. Configure this in the kubelet settings and use monitoring tools to adjust based on cluster load. Regularly review event logs to ensure critical security events are being recorded.

cis_k8s_4.3.1.description: Do not bind the kube-proxy metrics port to non-loopback addresses.
cis_k8s_4.3.1.remediation: Configure kube-proxy with --metrics-bind-address=127.0.0.1 to restrict metrics access to localhost. Use IaC to enforce this setting and implement network policies to block external access. Regularly audit kube-proxy configurations and monitor for any exposure of metrics endpoints.

cis_k8s_5.1.1.description: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
cis_k8s_5.1.1.remediation: Limit cluster-admin role assignments to essential users and service accounts. Use RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings with least privilege principles. Regularly audit RBAC configurations with automated tools and implement just-in-time access controls to reduce standing privileges.

cis_k8s_5.1.10.description: Users with access to the Proxy sub-resource of Node objects automatically have permissions to use the kubelet API, which may allow for privilege escalation or bypass cluster security controls such as audit logs. The kubelet provides an API which includes rights to execute commands in any container running on the node. Access to this API is covered by permissions to the main Kubernetes API via the node object. The proxy sub-resource specifically allows wide ranging access to the kubelet API. Dire
cis_k8s_5.1.10.remediation: Restrict access to nodes/proxy sub-resource using RBAC policies. Assign permissions only to specific roles that require it, and avoid wildcard usage. Regularly review and audit RBAC configurations to ensure minimal access, and use automated tools to detect and remediate excessive permissions.

cis_k8s_5.1.11.description: Users with access to the update the approval sub-resource of CertificateSigningRequests objects can approve new client certificates for the Kubernetes API effectively allowing them to create new high-privileged user accounts. This can allow for privilege escalation to full cluster administrator, depending on users configured in the cluster
cis_k8s_5.1.11.remediation: Limit access to certificatesigningrequests/approval sub-resource to trusted administrators only. Use RBAC to define precise roles and avoid broad permissions. Implement automated monitoring for CSR approvals and regularly audit certificate issuance logs to prevent unauthorized privilege escalation.

cis_k8s_5.1.12.description: Users with rights to create/modify/delete validatingwebhookconfigurations or mutatingwebhookconfigurations can control webhooks that can read any object admitted to the cluster, and in the case of mutating webhooks, also mutate admitted objects. This could allow for privilege escalation or disruption of the operation of the cluster.
cis_k8s_5.1.12.remediation: Restrict access to webhook configuration objects using RBAC with least privilege. Assign permissions only to specific service accounts or users that manage webhooks. Regularly audit webhook configurations and use automated tools to detect unauthorized changes or excessive permissions.

cis_k8s_5.1.13.description: Users with rights to create new service account tokens at a cluster level, can create long-lived privileged credentials in the cluster. This could allow for privilege escalation and persistent access to the cluster, even if the users account has been revoked.
cis_k8s_5.1.13.remediation: Limit access to service account token creation using RBAC policies. Use automated token rotation and short-lived tokens where possible. Regularly audit service account tokens and their permissions, and implement monitoring to detect anomalous token creation activities.

cis_k8s_5.1.2.description: The Kubernetes API stores secrets, which may be service account tokens for the Kubernetes API or credentials used by workloads in the cluster. Access to these secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
cis_k8s_5.1.2.remediation: Implement RBAC to restrict secret access to specific roles and namespaces. Use encryption at rest for secrets and avoid storing sensitive data in plaintext. Regularly audit secret permissions with automated tools and enforce least privilege access through policy as code.

cis_k8s_5.1.3.description: Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles provide access to resources based on sets of objects and actions that can be taken on those objects. It is possible to set either of these to be the wildcard "*" which matches all items. Use of wildcards is not optimal from a security perspective as it may allow for inadvertent access to be granted when new resources are added to the Kubernetes API either as CRDs or in later versions of the product.
cis_k8s_5.1.3.remediation: Avoid using wildcards (*) in Roles and ClusterRoles; specify exact resources and verbs instead. Use automated scanning to detect and remediate wildcard usage. Regularly review and update RBAC policies to align with least privilege principles as new resources are added to the cluster.

cis_k8s_5.1.4.description: The ability to create pods in a namespace can provide a number of opportunities for privilege escalation, such as assigning privileged service accounts to these pods or mounting hostPaths with access to sensitive data (unless Pod Security Policies are implemented to restrict this access) As such, access to create new pods should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users.
cis_k8s_5.1.4.remediation: Restrict pod creation permissions using RBAC, limiting access to specific users or service accounts. Implement Pod Security Standards or Admission Controllers to enforce security policies on pods. Regularly audit pod creation events and use automated tools to detect and remediate excessive permissions.

cis_k8s_5.1.5.description: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
cis_k8s_5.1.5.remediation: Create and assign custom service accounts for each application workload, ensuring they have only necessary permissions. Use RBAC policies to restrict the default service account and regularly audit service account usage via Kubernetes audit logs or CSPM tools.

cis_k8s_5.1.6.description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server.
cis_k8s_5.1.6.remediation: Configure pods to disable automatic mounting of service account tokens by setting automountServiceAccountToken to false in pod specs. Only enable token mounting for pods that require API server access, and use least-privilege service accounts.

cis_k8s_5.1.7.description: The special group system:masters should not be used to grant permissions to any user or service account, except where strictly necessary (e.g. bootstrapping access prior to RBAC being fully available).
cis_k8s_5.1.7.remediation: Avoid assigning users or service accounts to the system:masters group. Use RBAC roles with specific permissions instead. If used temporarily for bootstrapping, revoke access immediately after RBAC is configured and audit for any lingering assignments.

cis_k8s_5.1.8.description: Cluster roles and roles with the impersonate, bind or escalate permissions should not be granted unless strictly required. Each of these permissions allow a particular subject to escalate their privileges beyond those explicitly granted by cluster administrators.
cis_k8s_5.1.8.remediation: Review and restrict RBAC roles to remove impersonate, bind, and escalate permissions unless absolutely necessary. Use policy-as-code tools to enforce least privilege and regularly audit role bindings for excessive permissions.

cis_k8s_5.1.9.description: The ability to create persistent volumes in a cluster can provide an opportunity for privilege escalation, via the creation of hostPath volumes. As persistent volumes are not covered by Pod Security Admission, a user with access to create persistent volumes may be able to get access to sensitive files from the underlying host even where restrictive Pod Security Admission policies are in place.
cis_k8s_5.1.9.remediation: Limit RBAC permissions to create persistent volumes to trusted administrators only. Implement admission controllers or policy engines to restrict hostPath volume usage and regularly audit persistent volume creation events.

cis_k8s_5.2.1.description: Every Kubernetes cluster should have at least one policy control mechanism in place to enforce the other requirements in this section. This could be the in-built Pod Security Admission controller, or a third party policy control system.
cis_k8s_5.2.1.remediation: Enable and configure Pod Security Admission or deploy a third-party policy engine like OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno. Define and enforce security policies as code, and integrate with CI/CD pipelines to prevent non-compliant deployments.

cis_k8s_5.2.10.description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities.
cis_k8s_5.2.10.remediation: Use Pod Security Standards or admission controllers to block containers with added capabilities. Configure securityContext to drop all capabilities by default and only add specific ones if absolutely required, following least privilege principles.

cis_k8s_5.2.11.description: Do not generally permit Windows containers to be run with the hostProcess flag set to true.
cis_k8s_5.2.11.remediation: Implement admission control policies to restrict Windows HostProcess containers. Use policy engines to enforce that hostProcess is set to false unless explicitly approved for administrative tasks, and audit container configurations regularly.

cis_k8s_5.2.12.description: Do not generally admit containers which make use of hostPath volumes.
cis_k8s_5.2.12.remediation: Configure admission controllers to deny pods with hostPath volumes unless explicitly allowed. Use persistent volumes with controlled access instead, and implement RBAC to restrict creation of hostPath volumes.

cis_k8s_5.2.13.description: Do not generally permit containers which require the use of HostPorts.
cis_k8s_5.2.13.remediation: Use Network Policies and Services instead of HostPorts for exposing applications. Configure admission controllers to block pods with hostPort settings, and audit existing deployments to migrate away from HostPort usage.

cis_k8s_5.2.2.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true.
cis_k8s_5.2.2.remediation: Enforce policies via Pod Security Admission or third-party tools to deny privileged containers. Use securityContext with non-root users and limited capabilities, and regularly scan for privileged pods in the cluster.

cis_k8s_5.2.3.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
cis_k8s_5.2.3.remediation: Configure admission controllers to restrict pods with hostPID enabled. Use namespaces and RBAC to isolate workloads, and audit pod specifications to ensure hostPID is only used where necessary for debugging or system tasks.

cis_k8s_5.2.4.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostIPC flag set to true.
cis_k8s_5.2.4.remediation: Implement policy controls to block containers with hostIPC enabled. Use secure inter-process communication methods within pods, and review existing deployments to disable hostIPC unless required for specific legacy applications.

cis_k8s_5.2.5.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostNetwork flag set to true.
cis_k8s_5.2.5.remediation: Use Network Policies and Services instead of hostNetwork for network isolation. Configure admission controllers to deny pods with hostNetwork, and migrate existing workloads to use cluster-internal networking with proper ingress controls.

cis_k8s_5.2.6.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
cis_k8s_5.2.6.remediation: Set allowPrivilegeEscalation to false in pod securityContext by default. Use admission controllers to enforce this policy, and audit container images to ensure they do not require privilege escalation for normal operation.

cis_k8s_5.2.7.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
cis_k8s_5.2.7.remediation: Configure pods to run as non-root users by setting runAsNonRoot and runAsUser in securityContext. Use policy engines to enforce this, and build container images with non-root users to minimize security risks.

cis_k8s_5.2.8.description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
cis_k8s_5.2.8.remediation: Drop the NET_RAW capability by default in pod securityContext. Use admission controllers to block containers with NET_RAW, and audit existing pods to remove this capability unless required for specific network debugging tools.

cis_k8s_5.2.9.description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
cis_k8s_5.2.9.remediation: Enforce policies to restrict added capabilities using Pod Security Standards or admission controllers. Regularly review and audit container configurations to ensure capabilities are minimized and aligned with least privilege.

cis_k8s_5.3.1.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
cis_k8s_5.3.1.remediation: Select a CNI plugin that supports Network Policies, such as Calico, Cilium, or Weave Net. Deploy and configure Network Policies to enforce traffic rules, and test policy enforcement to ensure isolation between namespaces and pods.

cis_k8s_5.3.2.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
cis_k8s_5.3.2.remediation: Define and apply Network Policies for all namespaces to enforce ingress and egress rules. Use default deny-all policies and then allow specific traffic as needed. Regularly audit and update policies to align with application requirements.

cis_k8s_5.4.1.description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
cis_k8s_5.4.1.remediation: Mount secrets as files using volumes rather than environment variables to reduce exposure. Update deployment manifests to use volumeMounts for secrets, and audit existing configurations to migrate away from environment variable secrets.

cis_k8s_5.4.2.description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
cis_k8s_5.4.2.remediation: Integrate with external secret managers like AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault. Configure authentication and encryption for secret access, enable audit logging, and implement automatic secret rotation policies to enhance security.

cis_k8s_5.5.1.description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
cis_k8s_5.5.1.remediation: Enable and configure the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller to validate container images against trusted registries and signatures. Use tools like Notary or Cosign for image signing, and enforce policies to block unsigned or untrusted images in CI/CD pipelines.

cis_k8s_5.6.1.description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
cis_k8s_5.6.1.remediation: Organize resources into logical namespaces based on teams, environments, or applications. Implement RBAC and Network Policies per namespace to enforce boundaries, and regularly review namespace usage to ensure proper isolation and resource management.

cis_k8s_5.6.2.description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
cis_k8s_5.6.2.remediation: Configure pods to use the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile by setting securityContext.seccompProfile.type to RuntimeDefault. Use admission controllers to enforce this policy, and audit existing pods to apply seccomp profiles for enhanced container security.

cis_k8s_5.6.3.description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
cis_k8s_5.6.3.remediation: Define security contexts in pod and container specifications using Kubernetes YAML or Helm charts to enforce security settings like runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and capabilities. Use admission controllers like Pod Security Standards or OPA/Gatekeeper to automate enforcement across the cluster.

cis_k8s_5.6.4.description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
cis_k8s_5.6.4.remediation: Avoid using the default namespace for workloads; create and use custom namespaces for all applications. Implement namespace policies via Kubernetes RBAC and network policies to restrict access and enforce isolation. Use tools like kube-score or kube-bench to scan for resources in the default namespace.

cis_k8s_5.7.1.description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
cis_k8s_5.7.1.remediation: Create dedicated namespaces for different applications, teams, or environments to enforce logical separation. Apply RBAC roles and network policies per namespace to limit cross-namespace access. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform or Kubernetes manifests to define and manage namespaces consistently.

cis_k8s_5.7.2.description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
cis_k8s_5.7.2.remediation: Specify the seccomp profile in pod security contexts using annotations or the securityContext field, e.g., seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod: runtime/default. Use Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or admission controllers to enforce seccomp profiles across all pods. Update existing deployments via kubectl edit or CI/CD pipelines.

cis_k8s_5.7.3.description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
cis_k8s_5.7.3.remediation: Define security contexts in pod and container specifications using Kubernetes YAML or Helm charts to enforce security settings like runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and capabilities. Use admission controllers like Pod Security Standards or OPA/Gatekeeper to automate enforcement across the cluster.

cis_k8s_5.7.4.description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
cis_k8s_5.7.4.remediation: Avoid using the default namespace for workloads; create and use custom namespaces for all applications. Implement namespace policies via Kubernetes RBAC and network policies to restrict access and enforce isolation. Use tools like kube-score or kube-bench to scan for resources in the default namespace.

cis_oci_1.1.description: To apply least-privilege security principle, one can create service-level administrators in corresponding groups and assigning specific users to each service-level administrative group in a tenancy. This limits administrative access in a tenancy. It means service-level administrators can only manage resources of a specific service. Example policies for global/tenant level service-administrators Allow group VolumeAdmins to manage volume-family in tenancy Allow group ComputeAdmins to manage instan
cis_oci_1.1.remediation: Create IAM groups for each service (e.g., ComputeAdmins, VolumeAdmins) and assign users with minimal required permissions using OCI policies. Define policies in Terraform or OCI Resource Manager to automate group and policy management. Regularly audit group memberships and permissions using OCI IAM or third-party CSPM tools.

cis_oci_1.10.description: Auth tokens are authentication tokens generated by Oracle. You use auth tokens to authenticate with APIs that do not support the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure signature- based authentication. If the service requires an auth token, the service-specific documentation instructs you to generate one and how to use it.
cis_oci_1.10.remediation: Implement automated rotation of auth tokens every 90 days using OCI IAM policies or scripts via OCI CLI/SDK. Monitor token usage with OCI Audit logs and set up alerts for tokens nearing expiration. Use OCI Vault for secure storage and management of tokens if needed.

cis_oci_1.11.description: Users can create and manage their database password in their IAM user profile and use that password to authenticate to databases in their tenancy. An IAM database password is a different password than an OCI Console password. Setting an IAM database password allows an authorized IAM user to sign in to one or more Autonomous Databases in their tenancy. An IAM database password is a different password than an OCI Console password. Setting an IAM database password allows an authorized IAM user to s
cis_oci_1.11.remediation: Enforce IAM database password rotation within 90 days using OCI IAM password policies or automated scripts. Educate users on password best practices and use OCI Audit to track password changes. Consider integrating with identity providers for centralized password management.

cis_oci_1.12.description: Tenancy administrator users have full access to the organization's OCI tenancy. API keys associated with user accounts are used for invoking the OCI APIs via custom programs or clients like CLI/SDKs. The clients are typically used for performing day-to- day operations and should never require full tenancy access. Service-level administrative users with API keys should be used instead.
cis_oci_1.12.remediation: Prohibit API key creation for tenancy administrator users by enforcing IAM policies that restrict key management to service-level admins. Use OCI IAM to audit and revoke any existing admin API keys. Implement least-privilege access by creating separate service accounts with limited permissions for API usage.

cis_oci_1.13.description: All OCI IAM local user accounts have an email address field associated with the account. It is recommended to specify an email address that is valid and current. If you have an email address in your user profile, you can use the Forgot Password link on the sign on page to have a temporary password sent to you.
cis_oci_1.13.remediation: Ensure all IAM user accounts have valid and current email addresses configured via OCI Console or API. Regularly review and update email addresses as part of user lifecycle management processes. Use automation scripts to validate email formats and send reminders for updates.

cis_oci_1.14.description: OCI instances, OCI database and OCI functions can access other OCI resources either via an OCI API key associated to a user or via Instance Principal. Instance Principal authentication can be achieved by inclusion in a Dynamic Group that has an IAM policy granting it the required access or using an OCI IAM policy that has request.principal added to the where clause. Access to OCI Resources refers to making API calls to another OCI resource like Object Storage, OCI Vaults, etc.
cis_oci_1.14.remediation: Use Instance Principal authentication for OCI instances, databases, and functions by assigning them to Dynamic Groups with appropriate IAM policies. Avoid using user API keys for these resources. Configure policies in Terraform or OCI Console to grant minimal permissions based on resource tags or compartments.

cis_oci_1.15.description: To apply the separation of duties security principle, one can restrict service-level administrators from being able to delete resources they are managing. It means service- level administrators can only manage resources of a specific service but not delete resources for that specific service. Example policies for global/tenant level for block volume service-administrators: Allow group VolumeUsers to manage volumes in tenancy where request.permission!='VOLUME_DELETE' Allow group VolumeUsers to ma
cis_oci_1.15.remediation: Create IAM policies that explicitly deny delete permissions for service-level admin groups, using conditions like request.permission!='DELETE'. Implement these policies via OCI Console or IaC tools like Terraform. Regularly audit policies to ensure separation of duties is maintained and adjust as needed.

cis_oci_1.16.description: OCI IAM Local users can access OCI resources using different credentials, such as passwords or API keys. It is recommended that credentials that have been unused for 45 days or more be deactivated or removed.
cis_oci_1.16.remediation: Monitor credential usage with OCI Audit logs and set up automated alerts for inactivity beyond 45 days. Deactivate or remove unused credentials via OCI IAM Console or scripts using OCI CLI. Implement periodic reviews of user accounts and credentials as part of security hygiene processes.

cis_oci_1.17.description: API Keys are long-term credentials for an OCI IAM user. They can be used to make programmatic requests to the OCI APIs directly or via, OCI SDKs or the OCI CLI.
cis_oci_1.17.remediation: Enforce a policy that each IAM user has only one active API key at a time. Use OCI IAM to audit and revoke excess keys. Automate key management with scripts that check and enforce this rule, and educate users on the risks of multiple keys.

cis_oci_1.2.description: There is a built-in OCI IAM policy enabling the Administrators group to perform any action within a tenancy. In the OCI IAM console, this policy reads: Allow group Administrators to manage all-resources in tenancy Administrators create more users, groups, and policies to provide appropriate access to other groups. Administrators should not allow any-other-group full access to the tenancy by writing a policy like this - Allow group any-other-group to manage all-resources in tenancy The access sho
cis_oci_1.2.remediation: Ensure only the tenancy administrator group has manage all-resources permissions; avoid granting similar broad access to other groups. Review and modify IAM policies using OCI Console or Terraform to apply least-privilege principles. Regularly audit policies with OCI IAM or CSPM tools to detect and remediate over-permissive grants.

cis_oci_1.3.description: Tenancy administrators can create more users, groups, and policies to provide other service administrators access to OCI resources. For example, an IAM administrator will need to have access to manage resources like compartments, users, groups, dynamic-groups, policies, identity-providers, tenancy tag- namespaces, tag-definitions in the tenancy. The policy that gives IAM-Administrators or any other group full access to 'groups' resources should not allow access to the tenancy 'Administrators' gr
cis_oci_1.3.remediation: Create IAM policies that explicitly exclude the tenancy Administrators group from updates by other groups, using conditions or deny statements. Implement these policies via OCI Console or IaC tools. Conduct regular policy audits to ensure IAM administrators cannot modify the Administrators group, maintaining separation of duties.

cis_oci_1.4.description: Password policies are used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure passwords are at least a certain length and are composed of certain characters. It is recommended the password policy require a minimum password length 14 characters and contain 1 non-alphabetic character (Number or “Special Character”).
cis_oci_1.4.remediation: Configure OCI IAM password policy to enforce a minimum length of 14 characters and include at least one non-alphabetic character. Use OCI Console or APIs to set these requirements globally. Regularly review and update the policy as needed, and educate users on strong password practices.

cis_oci_1.5.description: IAM password policies can require passwords to be rotated or expired after a given number of days. It is recommended that the password policy expire passwords after 365 and are changed immediately based on events.
cis_oci_1.5.remediation: Set OCI IAM password policy to expire passwords within 365 days and enforce immediate changes after security events. Configure this via OCI Console or using Terraform for automation. Monitor compliance with audit logs and send reminders to users before expiration.

cis_oci_1.6.description: IAM password policies can prevent the reuse of a given password by the same user. It is recommended the password policy prevent the reuse of passwords.
cis_oci_1.6.remediation: Enable password reuse prevention in OCI IAM password policy, typically by setting a history limit (e.g., last 24 passwords). Apply this policy through OCI Console or automated scripts. Combine with other password controls like length and complexity for enhanced security.

cis_oci_1.7.description: Multi-factor authentication is a method of authentication that requires the use of more than one factor to verify a user’s identity. With MFA enabled in the IAM service, when a user signs in to Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, they are prompted for their user name and password, which is the first factor (something that they know). The user is then prompted to provide a verification code from a registered MFA device, which is the second factor (something that they have). The two factors work together
cis_oci_1.7.remediation: Enable MFA for all IAM users with console passwords via OCI IAM settings, requiring hardware or software tokens. Use OCI Console or APIs to enforce MFA enrollment and monitor compliance. Educate users on MFA setup and consider integrating with identity providers for centralized MFA management.

cis_oci_1.8.description: API keys are used by administrators, developers, services and scripts for accessing OCI APIs directly or via SDKs/OCI CLI to search, create, update or delete OCI resources. The API key is an RSA key pair. The private key is used for signing the API requests and the public key is associated with a local or synchronized user's profile.
cis_oci_1.8.remediation: Implement automated rotation of user API keys every 90 days using OCI IAM policies or custom scripts with OCI CLI. Track key usage and expiration with OCI Audit logs. Encourage the use of service accounts with limited permissions instead of user accounts for API access.

cis_oci_1.9.description: Object Storage provides an API to enable interoperability with Amazon S3. To use this Amazon S3 Compatibility API, you need to generate the signing key required to authenticate with Amazon S3. This special signing key is an Access Key/Secret Key pair. Oracle generates the Customer Secret key to pair with the Access Key.
cis_oci_1.9.remediation: Rotate customer secret keys every 90 days by generating new keys in OCI Object Storage settings and updating applications accordingly. Use OCI Vault for secure key management and automation. Monitor key usage and set up alerts for keys nearing expiration to ensure timely rotation.

cis_oci_2.1.description: Security lists provide stateful and stateless filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to OCI resources on a subnet level. It is recommended that no security list allows unrestricted ingress access to port 22.
cis_oci_2.1.remediation: Modify security lists to remove rules that allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22 (SSH). Restrict SSH access to specific IP ranges or use bastion hosts. Implement these changes via OCI Console, Terraform, or OCI CLI, and regularly audit security lists with network scanning tools.

cis_oci_2.1.2.description: OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure IAM for authentication and authorization, for all interfaces (the Console, SDK or CLI, and REST API). To create, update, and delete clusters and node pools, users must be in groups and those group must have the necessary access. Certain cluster operations created by Container Engine for Kubernetes may require additional permissions granted via a Kubernetes RBAC role or cluster role. One example is the installing the metrics server which requires ad
cis_oci_2.1.2.remediation: Create dedicated IAM groups for OKE service-level admins with minimal permissions required for cluster management. Define policies in OCI IAM using Terraform or OCI Console. Combine with Kubernetes RBAC roles for fine-grained access within clusters, and regularly review group memberships and permissions.

cis_oci_2.2.description: Security lists provide stateful and stateless filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to OCI resources on a subnet level. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to port 3389.
cis_oci_2.2.remediation: Modify security lists in OCI to remove any ingress rules that allow traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform to enforce restricted access, and implement network security policies that limit RDP access to specific IP ranges or VCNs.

cis_oci_2.2.1.description: The audit logs are part of the OKE managed Kubernetes control plane logs managed by OKE. OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. All operations performed by the Kubernetes API server are visible as log events on the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service.
cis_oci_2.2.1.remediation: Enable and configure OCI Audit service logging for OKE clusters to capture all Kubernetes API server activities. Use IaC to automate audit log setup, ensure logs are stored securely with encryption, and set up alerts for critical events to maintain compliance and monitoring.

cis_oci_2.2.2.description: OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure IAM for authentication and authorization, for all interfaces (the Console, SDK or CLI, and REST API). To create, update, and delete clusters and node pools, users must be in groups and those group must have the necessary access. Certain cluster operations created by Container Engine for Kubernetes may require additional permissions granted via a Kubernetes RBAC role or cluster role. One example is the installing the metrics server which requires ad
cis_oci_2.2.2.remediation: Create dedicated IAM groups with least-privilege policies for OKE service-level admins. Use RBAC in Kubernetes to assign necessary roles, and automate user management through IaC to ensure consistent access controls and audit permissions regularly.

cis_oci_2.3.description: Network security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to OCI resources. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress to port 22.
cis_oci_2.3.remediation: Update network security groups in OCI to block ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22. Implement IaC policies to restrict SSH access to trusted IPs, use VPNs or bastion hosts for secure connections, and regularly audit NSG rules for compliance.

cis_oci_2.3.1.description: The audit logs are part of the OKE managed Kubernetes control plane logs managed by OKE. OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. All operations performed by the Kubernetes API server are visible as log events on the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service.
cis_oci_2.3.1.remediation: Ensure OCI Audit service is enabled for OKE and logs are retained per compliance requirements. Use automation tools to configure audit settings, encrypt log data, and integrate with SIEM systems for real-time monitoring and analysis.

cis_oci_2.3.2.description: Ensure that the audit policy created for the cluster covers key security concerns.
cis_oci_2.3.2.remediation: Define and apply audit policies in OKE that include logging for authentication, authorization, and resource changes. Use IaC to deploy these policies consistently, review logs regularly, and update policies to address emerging security threats.

cis_oci_2.4.description: Network security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to OCI resources. It is recommended that no security group allows unrestricted ingress access to port 3389.
cis_oci_2.4.remediation: Remove any NSG rules in OCI that permit ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 3389. Enforce strict network policies via IaC, use private subnets and VPNs for RDP access, and conduct periodic scans to detect misconfigurations.

cis_oci_2.5.description: A default security list is created when a Virtual Cloud Network (VCN) is created and attached to the public subnets in the VCN. Security lists provide stateful or stateless filtering of ingress and egress network traffic to OCI resources in the VCN. It is recommended that the default security list does not allow unrestricted ingress and egress access to resources in the VCN.
cis_oci_2.5.remediation: Modify the default security list in each VCN to restrict all traffic except ICMP within the VCN. Use IaC templates to define secure default rules, apply network segmentation, and audit VCN configurations to prevent open access.

cis_oci_2.6.description: Oracle Integration (OIC) is a complete, secure, but lightweight integration solution that enables you to connect your applications in the cloud. It simplifies connectivity between your applications and connects both your applications that live in the cloud and your applications that still live on premises. Oracle Integration provides secure, enterprise- grade connectivity regardless of the applications you are connecting or where they reside. OIC instances are created within an Oracle managed se
cis_oci_2.6.remediation: Restrict OIC access by configuring allowed source IPs or VCNs in OCI. Use IAM policies and network security groups to enforce least-privilege access, implement encryption for data in transit, and regularly review access controls.

cis_oci_2.7.description: Oracle Analytics Cloud (OAC) is a scalable and secure public cloud service that provides a full set of capabilities to explore and perform collaborative analytics for you, your workgroup, and your enterprise. OAC instances provide ingress filtering of network traffic or can be deployed with in an existing Virtual Cloud Network VCN. It is recommended that all new OAC instances be deployed within a VCN and that the Access Control Rules are restricted to your corporate IP Addresses or VCNs for exis
cis_oci_2.7.remediation: Deploy OAC instances within a VCN and configure access control rules to limit ingress to corporate IPs or trusted VCNs. Use IaC to automate VCN deployment, apply network security policies, and encrypt data to protect sensitive analytics information.

cis_oci_2.8.description: Oracle Autonomous Database Shared (ADB-S) automates database tuning, security, backups, updates, and other routine management tasks traditionally performed by DBAs. ADB-S provide ingress filtering of network traffic or can be deployed within an existing Virtual Cloud Network (VCN). It is recommended that all new ADB-S databases be deployed within a VCN and that the Access Control Rules are restricted to your corporate IP Addresses or VCNs for existing ADB-S databases.
cis_oci_2.8.remediation: Place ADB-S databases within a VCN and restrict access control rules to specific IPs or VCNs. Use IaC for consistent deployment, enable encryption for data at rest and in transit, and regularly audit network settings to prevent public exposure.

cis_oci_3.1.description: Compute Instances that utilize Legacy MetaData service endpoints (IMDSv1) are susceptible to potential SSRF attacks. To bolster security measures, it is strongly advised to reconfigure Compute Instances to adopt Instance Metadata Service v2, aligning with the industry's best security practices.
cis_oci_3.1.remediation: Disable IMDSv1 and enable IMDSv2 on all OCI compute instances. Use IaC to enforce this configuration, update instance templates, and apply security policies that require IMDSv2 for new deployments to mitigate SSRF risks.

cis_oci_3.1.1.description: If kubelet is running, and if it is using a file-based oke_kubelet_conf.json file, ensure that the proxy oke_kubelet_conf.json file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_oci_3.1.1.remediation: Set file permissions for oke_kubelet_conf.json to 644 or stricter on OKE nodes. Use configuration management tools like Ansible or IaC scripts to automate permission settings, and regularly audit file permissions to prevent unauthorized access.

cis_oci_3.1.2.description: If kubelet is running, ensure that the file ownership of its oke_kubelet_conf.json file is set to root:root.
cis_oci_3.1.2.remediation: Manually verify and set ownership of oke_kubelet_conf.json to root:root on each OKE node. Establish a procedure for regular checks, document the steps in security policies, and use audit tools to monitor file ownership changes.

cis_oci_3.1.3.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
cis_oci_3.1.3.remediation: Configure kubelet configuration files with permissions set to 644 or more restrictive. Use IaC to enforce these settings across clusters, implement role-based access controls, and schedule periodic reviews to maintain security compliance.

cis_oci_3.1.4.description: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file is owned by root:root.
cis_oci_3.1.4.remediation: Set ownership of kubelet configuration files to root:root. Automate this with configuration management tools, apply security policies that mandate root ownership, and audit file ownership regularly to prevent tampering.

cis_oci_3.2.description: Shielded Instances with Secure Boot enabled prevents unauthorized boot loaders and operating systems from booting. This prevent rootkits, bootkits, and unauthorized software from running before the operating system loads. Secure Boot verifies the digital signature of the system's boot software to check its authenticity. The digital signature ensures the operating system has not been tampered with and is from a trusted source. When the system boots and attempts to execute the software, it will fi
cis_oci_3.2.remediation: Enable Secure Boot on all OCI compute instances to protect against boot-level attacks. Use IaC to configure Secure Boot in instance templates, update existing instances via automation, and enforce this setting through security policies for compliance.

cis_oci_3.2.1.description: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
cis_oci_3.2.1.remediation: Set the --anonymous-auth argument to false in kubelet configuration. Use IaC to deploy this setting across OKE nodes, implement RBAC for authentication, and monitor logs to detect any anonymous access attempts.

cis_oci_3.2.10.remediation: Configure the --rotate-server-certificates argument to true in kubelet settings. Automate this with IaC tools, ensure certificates are managed securely, and schedule regular rotations to enhance security and compliance.

cis_oci_3.2.11.remediation: Ensure the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false in kubelet configuration. Use IaC to enforce certificate rotation policies, automate renewal processes, and audit certificate management to prevent expiration issues.

cis_oci_3.2.12.remediation: Set the --rotate-server-certificates argument to true in kubelet configuration. Implement automation via IaC, monitor certificate expiration, and use secure key management services to handle rotations efficiently.

cis_oci_3.2.2.description: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
